works.title: 18 Poems
works.shortDescription: Thomas's first collection of poetry, which established his reputation as a major new voice[reference:64].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Collection – Thomas's debut collection. (2) The Style – the poems are characterized by their intensity, complexity, and rich romanticism[reference:65]. (3) The Themes – themes include procreation, birth, death, sensuality, love, and religion[reference:66]. PYQ trends: asked about Thomas's early work; the style and themes.

works.shortDescription: A sonnet sequence of five poems, including 'The Soldier', written shortly after the outbreak of World War I[reference:50].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Sonnet Sequence – a collection of five sonnets. (2) The Theme of War – the poems reflect an early idealism about the war. (3) The Contrast – the sequence contrasts sharply with the disillusionment of later war poetry[reference:51]. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the sonnet sequence; the idealism of early war poetry; contrasted with Owen and Sassoon.

works.title: A Beginning
works.shortDescription: Moraes's first collection of poetry, published when he was just nineteen years old. It won the Hawthornden Prize.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Young Poet – the collection was published when the poet was only nineteen. (2) The Hawthornden Prize – the collection won the Hawthornden Prize in 1958. (3) The Style – the poems are characterized by their lyrical and romantic style. PYQ trends: asked about Moraes's first collection; the Hawthornden Prize; the poet's early success.

works.title: A Dance of the Forests
works.shortDescription: Soyinka's first major play, a complex and symbolic work written for Nigeria's independence celebrations. It explores themes of history, myth, and the cyclical nature of human folly.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Symbolism – the play is highly symbolic, drawing on Yoruba mythology. (2) The Historical Theme – the play critiques the celebration of independence. (3) The Structure – the play uses a complex, non-linear structure. (4) The Language – the play is written in poetic and elevated language. PYQ trends: asked about the symbolism; the historical critique; the Yoruba mythology; the play's structure.
works.references: Edition: 1960 (Ibadan: Oxford University Press). Criticism: Soyinka (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: A Dictionary of the English Language
works.shortDescription: Johnson's monumental dictionary, first published in 1755. It was the pre-eminent English dictionary until the Oxford English Dictionary appeared in the 19th century.[reference:28]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Lexicography – Johnson's dictionary set a new standard for English lexicography. (2) The Definitions – Johnson's definitions are often witty and personal. (3) The Quotations – the dictionary includes quotations from English literature, making it a literary anthology as well as a dictionary. (4) The Preface – Johnson's preface is a masterpiece of English prose. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Johnson's Dictionary; the lexicography; the definitions; the preface.

works.title: A Farewell to Arms
works.shortDescription: Hemingway's novel about an American ambulance driver in World War I and his love affair with a British nurse.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The War Novel – the novel is a critique of war. (2) The Love Story – the love story is overshadowed by tragedy. (3) The Famous Ending – the novel ends with a famous scene of loss. PYQ trends: asked about the war novel; the love story; the tragic ending.

works.title: A Few Late Chrysanthemums
works.shortDescription: Betjeman's collection that won him an appreciative readership.[reference:22]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Breakthrough Collection – the collection that established Betjeman's popularity. (2) The Themes – reflects nostalgia and social commentary.[reference:23] (3) The Style – celebrates the nuances of English class structure and cultural heritage.[reference:24] PYQ trends: asked about Betjeman's popular success; the themes of nostalgia and social commentary.

works.title: A Fine Balance
works.shortDescription: Mistry's novel, set against the backdrop of the Emergency years. It recounts the lives and struggles of four unlikely characters, revealing the distress of the ordinary citizens of India during the despotic rule of Indira Gandhi.[reference:61]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Emergency – the novel is set during the Emergency years.[reference:62] (2) The Characters – the novel follows four unlikely characters.[reference:63] (3) The Parsi Experience – the novel reveals the vicissitudes of Parsi life.[reference:64] PYQ trends: asked about the novel's publication year (1995); the Emergency setting; and the characters.
works.references: Edition: 1995 (London: Faber and Faber). Criticism: Mistry (2000).

works.title: A Good Time Was Had by All
works.shortDescription: Smith's first collection of verse, published in 1937.[reference:29][reference:30]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Collection – Smith's debut poetry collection. (2) The Ironic Title – the title is typical of Smith's ironic, offbeat sensibility. (3) The Style – the poems are characterized by their simplicity, wit, and dark undertones. PYQ trends: asked about Smith's first collection; the ironic tone.

works.title: A Grain of Wheat
works.shortDescription: Ngũgĩ's novel set during the Mau Mau Uprising in Kenya, exploring the personal and political dimensions of the struggle for independence.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Mau Mau Uprising – the novel is set during the Kenyan independence struggle. (2) The Personal and Political – the novel explores the intersection of personal lives and political events. (3) The Characters – the novel follows a group of characters whose lives are affected by the uprising. (4) The Narrative Structure – the novel uses flashbacks and multiple perspectives. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the Mau Mau Uprising; the personal and political themes; the narrative structure.

works.title: A Handful of Dust
works.shortDescription: Waugh's satirical novel about the breakdown of a marriage and the emptiness of upper-class life.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Satire of Marriage – the novel is a savage satire of marriage and upper-class life. (2) The Title – from T.S. Eliot's The Waste Land. PYQ trends: asked about the satire of marriage; the title's allusion.

works.title: A History of Indian English Literature
works.shortDescription: Naik's comprehensive history of Indian English literature, covering the major authors and movements from the colonial period to the present.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The History – a comprehensive history of Indian English literature. (2) The Survey – covers major authors and movements. (3) The Chronology – provides a chronological framework. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Naik's history; the survey of Indian English literature; the chronological framework.
works.references: Edition: 1982 (New Delhi: Sahitya Akademi). Criticism: Naik (2000).

works.title: A House for Mr. Biswas
works.shortDescription: Naipaul's most famous novel, a tragicomic story of a man's quest for a house of his own in Trinidad.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Quest – the novel is about a man's quest for a house of his own. (2) The Trinidad Setting – the novel is set in Trinidad. (3) The Comic Tragedy – the novel blends comedy and tragedy. (4) The Themes – explores identity, belonging, and the search for home. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the quest; the Trinidad setting; the comic tragic tone; the themes of identity and belonging.
works.references: Edition: 1961 (London: Andre Deutsch). Criticism: Naipaul (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: A Literature of Their Own
works.shortDescription: Showalter's foundational work of feminist literary criticism, which traces the tradition of women's writing in England from the Brontës to the present.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Women's Tradition – a distinct tradition of women's writing. (2) The Phases – feminine, feminist, and female. (3) Gynocriticism – the study of women's writing as a distinct tradition. PYQ trends: frequently asked about gynocriticism; the phases of women's writing; compared with other feminist literary critics.
works.references: Edition: 1977 (Princeton: Princeton University Press). Criticism: Showalter (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: A Midsummer Night's Dream
works.shortDescription: Shakespeare's comedy of love, magic, and mistaken identity. The play intertwines the stories of Athenian lovers, fairy royalty, and a group of amateur actors in a moonlit forest.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Genre – one of Shakespeare's most popular comedies, blending romance, fantasy, and farce. (2) The Fairy World – Oberon, Titania, and Puck represent the magical realm. (3) Play-within-a-Play – the performance of Pyramus and Thisbe by the 'rude mechanicals' is a comic parody of tragic love. (4) Theme of Love – love is portrayed as irrational and fickle. PYQ trends: asked about the play's structure; the character of Puck; the play-within-a-play; the theme of love.
works.references: Editions: Arden Shakespeare (3rd ed., Foakes, 1984). Criticism: Kott (1964), Wells (1990).

works.title: A Modest Proposal
works.shortDescription: Swift's most famous prose satire, a shocking proposal that the Irish should eat their children to solve the problem of poverty.[reference:25]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Satirical Proposal – Swift's ironic proposal is a savage indictment of English exploitation of Ireland. (2) The Irony – the speaker is reasonable and logical, which makes the proposal even more shocking. (3) The Rhetoric – the work is a masterpiece of rhetorical persuasion. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Swift's A Modest Proposal; the irony and satire; compared with Gulliver's Travels.

works.title: A Musical Instrument
works.shortDescription: Barrett Browning's poem about the god Pan and the creation of the first musical instrument.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Mythological Subject – based on the Greek myth of Pan and Syrinx. (2) The Theme of Creation – the poem explores the relationship between nature and art. PYQ trends: asked about the mythological sources; the theme of creation.

works.title: A Myrrour for Magistrates
works.shortDescription: A collection of tragic narratives of historical figures. Sackville contributed the most famous part, the 'Induction', which describes the poet's visit to the infernal regions.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Mirror Tradition – follows the 'mirror for princes' tradition, using history to provide moral and political instruction. (2) Sackville's 'Induction' – the most celebrated part of the work, described as the best poetry written in English between Chaucer and Spenser. (3) Infernal Vision – the 'Induction' describes a journey to the underworld, anticipating Spenser's Faerie Queene and Dante's Inferno. (4) Influence on Spenser – Sackville's work is considered a model for some of Spenser's poetry. PYQ trends: asked about Sackville's contribution; the 'Induction' as a poetic achievement; the influence on Spenser.

works.title: A Passage to India
works.shortDescription: Forster's most famous novel, set in British India and exploring the tensions between the British colonizers and the Indian people.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Colonial Theme – the novel critiques British colonialism in India. (2) The Cave Scene – the incident in the Marabar Caves is a pivotal moment. (3) The Characters – includes Dr. Aziz, Adela Quested, and Cyril Fielding. (4) The Theme of Friendship – the novel explores the possibility of friendship across racial and cultural divides. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the colonial theme; the cave scene; the character of Dr. Aziz.

works.title: A Philosophical Enquiry into the Origin of Our Ideas of the Sublime and Beautiful
works.shortDescription: Burke's treatise on aesthetics, exploring the concepts of the sublime and the beautiful.[reference:36]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Sublime – Burke defines the sublime as that which inspires awe, terror, and astonishment. (2) The Beautiful – Burke defines the beautiful as that which inspires love and pleasure. (3) The Transition to Romanticism – the work anticipates the Romantic movement's interest in the sublime. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Burke's Enquiry; the sublime and the beautiful; the influence on Romanticism.

works.title: A Question of Power
works.shortDescription: Head's autobiographical novel about a woman's struggle with madness and racism in South Africa.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Autobiographical Element – the novel draws on Head's own experiences. (2) The Theme of Madness – the novel explores the protagonist's struggle with mental illness. (3) The Theme of Racism – the novel explores the psychological effects of racism. PYQ trends: asked about the autobiographical elements; the theme of madness; the theme of racism.

works.title: A Retrospect
works.shortDescription: Pound's collection of essays on poetry, including 'A Few Don'ts by an Imagiste' and 'Prolegomena'[reference:24].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Imagist Manifesto – the essays define the principles of Imagism. (2) A Few Don'ts – Pound's famous list of rules for poets. PYQ trends: asked about Pound's essays; the Imagist manifesto.

works.title: A Room of One's Own
works.shortDescription: Woolf's extended essay on women's writing and the obstacles women face in achieving artistic and financial independence. It introduces the concept of 'Shakespeare's sister'.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Famous Dictum – 'A woman must have money and a room of her own if she is to write fiction'. (2) Shakespeare's Sister – a hypothetical figure representing the lost potential of women writers. (3) The Feminist Argument – the essay explores the history of women's writing. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the famous dictum; Shakespeare's sister; compared with other feminist texts.
works.references: Edition: 1929 (London: Hogarth Press). Criticism: Woolf (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: A Room with a View
works.shortDescription: Forster's novel about Lucy Honeychurch, a young Englishwoman who travels to Italy and experiences a liberating awakening.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Contrast – the novel contrasts the repressed English society with the liberating spirit of Italy. (2) The Theme of Awakening – Lucy undergoes a journey of self-discovery. PYQ trends: asked about the contrast between England and Italy; the theme of awakening.

works.title: A Sentimental Journey
works.shortDescription: Sterne's last work, a sentimental travel narrative about the journey of Parson Yorick through France and Italy.[reference:33]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Sentimental Novel – the novel explores the emotions and sympathies of the narrator. (2) Parson Yorick – the narrator is a continuation of the character from Tristram Shandy. (3) The Travel Narrative – the novel is a series of episodes and reflections on the journey. PYQ trends: asked about Sterne's later works; the sentimental novel.

works.title: A Streetcar Named Desire
works.shortDescription: Williams's most famous play, about Blanche DuBois, a fading Southern belle who moves in with her sister Stella and her brutish husband Stanley Kowalski.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Southern Belle – Blanche represents the fading aristocratic South. (2) The Brutal Husband – Stanley represents the modern, working-class, brutal world. (3) The Psychological Drama – the play explores themes of desire, delusion, and madness. (4) The Pulitzer Prize – the play won the Pulitzer Prize in 1948. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the character of Blanche; the contrast between Blanche and Stanley; the theme of desire; the Pulitzer Prize.

works.title: A Suitable Boy
works.shortDescription: Seth's first prose novel, a sprawling epic set in post-independence India. It follows the story of Lata and her mother's search for a suitable husband for her.[reference:56][reference:57]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Realist Tradition – the novel appropriates the nineteenth-century realist tradition.[reference:58] (2) The Nehruvian Idea of India – the novel features the Nehruvian idea of India as a 'unity within diversity'.[reference:59] (3) The Commonwealth Writers Prize – the novel won the 1994 Commonwealth Writers Prize.[reference:60] PYQ trends: asked about the novel's publication year (1993); the realist style; and the Commonwealth Writers Prize.

works.title: A Supermarket in California
works.shortDescription: A poem in which Ginsberg imagines meeting Walt Whitman in a supermarket.[reference:10]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Whitman Connection – the poem is a tribute to Walt Whitman. (2) The Supermarket – the supermarket symbolizes modern American consumer culture. PYQ trends: asked about Ginsberg's Whitman connection; the critique of consumer culture.
works.references: Edition: 1956 (in Howl and Other Poems). Criticism: Ginsberg (2000).

works.title: A System of Logic
works.shortDescription: Mill's major work on logic and scientific method, presenting a systematic account of inductive reasoning. It was a landmark in the philosophy of science.[reference:38][reference:39]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Inductive Logic – Mill presents a systematic account of inductive reasoning. (2) The Methods of Induction – Mill outlines his famous 'Methods of Induction'. (3) Philosophy of Science – the work is a landmark in the philosophy of science. PYQ trends: asked about Mill's logic; the Methods of Induction; compared with other philosophers of science.

works.title: A Tale of a Tub
works.shortDescription: Swift's early satirical work, a complex and often baffling allegory that attacks religious excess and literary pretension.[reference:24]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Religious Satire – the work satirizes the corruption of the Church. (2) The Three Brothers – Peter, Martin, and Jack represent the Roman Catholic, Anglican, and Dissenting churches. (3) The Digressions – the work is notable for its digressions, which are themselves satirical. PYQ trends: asked about Swift's early works; the religious satire.

works.title: A Tale of Two Cities
works.shortDescription: Dickens's historical novel set in London and Paris before and during the French Revolution.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Historical Novel – the novel is set during the French Revolution. (2) The Famous Opening – 'It was the best of times, it was the worst of times'. (3) The Theme of Resurrection – the novel explores the theme of resurrection and redemption. (4) The Characters – includes Sydney Carton, Charles Darnay, and Madame Defarge. PYQ trends: asked about the historical novel; the famous opening; the theme of resurrection.

works.title: A Treatise of Human Nature
works.shortDescription: Hume's major philosophical work, an ambitious attempt to introduce the experimental method of reasoning into moral subjects.[reference:37]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Empiricism – Hume is a key figure in the empiricist tradition. (2) Impressions and Ideas – Hume distinguishes between impressions (sensations) and ideas (thoughts). (3) Causation – Hume argues that causation is not a necessary connection but a habit of the mind. (4) The Self – Hume argues that the self is a bundle of perceptions. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Hume's Treatise; the empiricist philosophy; the theory of causation; the self.

works.title: A Vindication of the Rights of Woman
works.shortDescription: Wollstonecraft's foundational feminist text, which argues for the education and rights of women. It is one of the earliest works of feminist philosophy.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Feminist Argument – Wollstonecraft argues for the education and rights of women. (2) The Critique of Marriage – marriage is described as 'legal prostitution'. (3) The Enlightenment Context – the work is a response to Rousseau and other Enlightenment thinkers. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the feminist arguments; the critique of marriage; compared with other feminist texts.
works.references: Edition: 1792 (London: J. Johnson). Criticism: Wollstonecraft (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: A Vision Upon this Concept of the Faery Queene
works.shortDescription: A commendatory sonnet written by Raleigh and published at the beginning of Spenser's The Faerie Queene (1590).
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Commendatory Verse – a sonnet praising Spenser's epic. (2) Spenser-Raleigh Connection – Raleigh and Spenser met in Ireland; Spenser modeled the character Timias after Raleigh. (3) Elizabeth as Belphoebe – Raleigh's poem reflects the allegorical representation of Elizabeth. PYQ trends: asked about the literary circle around Spenser; Raleigh's role as a poet and patron.
works.references: Edition: 1590 (published with The Faerie Queene). Criticism: May (1997).

works.title: A Woman of No Importance
works.shortDescription: Wilde's comedy of manners about Mrs. Arbuthnot, who is revealed to be the mother of the hero, Gerald, and the former mistress of the villainous Lord Illingworth.[reference:13]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Double Standard – the play critiques the different standards applied to men and women. (2) The 'Woman of No Importance' – the title character is ultimately revealed to be a figure of moral authority. (3) The Wit – the play is filled with Wildean epigrams. PYQ trends: asked about the critique of the double standard; compared with other Wilde comedies.

works.title: Absalom, Absalom!
works.shortDescription: Faulkner's novel about Thomas Sutpen, a man who rises from poverty to build a plantation in Mississippi, and the tragic consequences of his ambition.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Southern Epic – the novel is a complex epic of the American South. (2) The Multiple Narrators – the story is told through multiple perspectives. (3) The Theme of Race – the novel explores the racial tensions of the South. PYQ trends: asked about the Southern epic; the multiple narrators; the theme of race.

works.title: Absalom and Achitophel
works.shortDescription: Dryden's famous satirical poem, published in 1681, using the biblical story of Absalom's rebellion against King David to satirize the political situation in Restoration England, particularly the Exclusion Crisis.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Political Satire – a powerful satire on the Exclusion Crisis and the Whig opposition to Charles II. (2) Biblical Allegory – the biblical story of Absalom and David is used as an allegory for contemporary politics. (3) Characters – Absalom represents the Duke of Monmouth, Achitophel represents the Earl of Shaftesbury. (4) Dryden's Style – the poem is written in heroic couplets and demonstrates Dryden's mastery of satire. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Dryden's Absalom and Achitophel; the political satire; the use of biblical allegory.

works.title: Abt Vogler
works.shortDescription: Browning's dramatic monologue about the composer Georg Joseph Vogler, reflecting on the purpose and value of his life.[reference:8]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Dramatic Monologue – the poem is a classic example of the dramatic monologue[reference:9]. (2) The Artist – the poem explores the nature of art and the role of the artist[reference:10]. (3) The Historical Context – the poem is based on the historical figure of Georg Joseph Vogler[reference:11]. PYQ trends: asked about the dramatic monologue; the theme of art; appeared in UGC NET Paper 2, 13 Oct 2022[reference:12].

works.title: Adam Bede
works.shortDescription: Eliot's first novel, set in the rural community of Hayslope and exploring themes of love, jealousy, and moral responsibility.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Novel – Eliot's debut novel. (2) The Rural Setting – the novel is set in a rural community. (3) The Moral Theme – the novel explores themes of moral responsibility. PYQ trends: asked about Eliot's first novel; the rural setting.

works.title: Adonais
works.shortDescription: Shelley's pastoral elegy for John Keats, written in Spenserian stanzas.[reference:27]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Pastoral Elegy – the poem follows the classical tradition of pastoral mourning. (2) The Subject – the poem mourns the death of John Keats. (3) The Spenserian Stanza – Shelley uses the nine-line stanza. PYQ trends: asked about Shelley's Adonais; the pastoral elegy tradition; compared with other elegies (e.g., Milton's Lycidas).

works.title: Adventures of Huckleberry Finn
works.shortDescription: A picaresque novel by Mark Twain, first published in the United Kingdom in December 1884 and in the United States in February 1885. It is a scathing satire on entrenched attitudes, particularly racism, set in a Southern antebellum society.[reference:0]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Vernacular Language – the novel is written in vernacular English, characterized by local color regionalism. (2) The Mississippi River – serves as a symbol of freedom and adventure. (3) The Theme of Race – the novel explores the relationship between Huck and Jim, a runaway slave. (4) The Controversy – the novel has been controversial for its use of racial slurs. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the vernacular style; the theme of race; the symbolism of the river.

works.title: Aesthetic Theory
works.shortDescription: Adorno's comprehensive work on aesthetics, which argues that art is a critique of the culture industry and a form of negative dialectics.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Culture Industry – mass culture is a system of domination. (2) Art and Critique – art is a critique of the culture industry. (3) Negative Dialectics – art is a form of negative dialectics. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the culture industry; the critique of mass culture; compared with other Marxist aesthetic theories.

works.title: After Amnesia
works.shortDescription: Devy's work on the critical tradition in Indian English literature, arguing for the need to recover the lost memory of Indian literary traditions.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Critical Tradition – the work explores the critical tradition in Indian English literature. (2) The Amnesia – the forgetting of Indian literary traditions. (3) The Recovery – the need to recover lost traditions. PYQ trends: asked about Devy's critical work; the amnesia of Indian traditions; the recovery.
works.references: Edition: 1992 (New Delhi: Orient Longman). Criticism: Devy (2000).

works.title: Agnes Grey
works.shortDescription: Anne Brontë's first novel, a realistic account of the life of a governess based on Brontë's own experiences.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Governess Novel – the novel offers a realistic portrayal of the life of a governess. (2) The Autobiographical Elements – the novel draws on Brontë's own experiences as a governess. PYQ trends: asked about the governess novel; the autobiographical elements.

works.title: Alias Grace
works.shortDescription: Atwood's historical fiction novel about the real-life figure of Grace Marks, a convicted murderer.[reference:38]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Historical Fiction – the novel is based on a true story. (2) The Theme of Identity – the novel explores questions of identity and truth. PYQ trends: asked about Atwood's historical fiction.

works.title: All About H. Hatterr
works.shortDescription: Desani's most famous novel, which cast an absurdist, comedic light on the plight of a common man in a multicultural world.[reference:96]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Absurdist Novel – the novel is an absurdist comedy.[reference:97] (2) The Multicultural Theme – the novel explores a pan-ethnic world.[reference:98] PYQ trends: asked about the novel's publication year (1948); and the absurdist style.
works.references: Edition: 1948 (London: George Allen & Unwin). Criticism: Desani (2000).

works.title: All for Love
works.shortDescription: Dryden's tragedy, often considered his greatest play. It tells the story of the love between Mark Antony and Cleopatra, written in blank verse.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Restoration Tragedy – one of the greatest tragedies of the Restoration period. (2) Blank Verse – Dryden abandons rhyme in this play, using blank verse instead. (3) Source – based on Shakespeare's Antony and Cleopatra and Plutarch's Lives. (4) Theme of Love – the play explores the destructive power of love. PYQ trends: asked about Dryden's All for Love; compared with Shakespeare's Antony and Cleopatra; the use of blank verse.

works.title: Amoretti
works.shortDescription: 89 sonnets chronicling Spenser's courtship of Elizabeth Boyle, Published with Epithalamion.
works.analysis:
Spenserian Sonnet – a distinct sonnet form with the rhyme scheme ABAB BCBC CDCD EE (interlocking quatrains). unlike Petrarch's unrequited love, Spenser's sonnets celebrate successful courtship. 
#Three phases of passion
**Section I**(Sonnet 1-36)- Complaint and frustration
**Section II**(Sonnet 37-69)- lover appears more aware about his feelings and of his mistress
**Section III**(Sonnet 70-87)- Joy, Faith, and Transcendence
# Sonnet 34
in the tone of complaint and confusion depicts his beloved as remote and almost inaccessible. Belovedf as the star that guides him through the sea of life but gets hidden in the storm, when this strom of desire pass, she will shine again.
# Sonnet 67
lover as a hunter tired of chasing his beloved (prey), she comes only when he breaks the chase. The wild beast could only be tamed by her own will.
# Sonnet 77
acknowledges the purity of their love which has transcended the physical desires. Shows the fusion of senual and spiritual.

works.title: Amours de Voyage
works.shortDescription: Clough's epistolary verse-novel about a love affair set against the backdrop of the 1849 Roman Republic[reference:62].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Epistolary Novel – the poem is an epistolary verse-novel[reference:63]. (2) The Ironic Tone – the poem is notable for its wry, detached, and ironic tone[reference:64]. (3) The Classical Hexameters – the poem is written in classical hexameters[reference:65]. PYQ trends: asked about the epistolary form; the ironic tone; the use of classical hexameters.

works.title: An Area of Darkness
works.shortDescription: Naipaul's non-fiction work about his first visit to India, exploring themes of identity, displacement, and the experience of diaspora.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Non-Fiction – the work is a travel memoir. (2) The Diasporic Theme – the work explores the experience of diaspora. (3) The Themes – explores identity, displacement, and the experience of India. PYQ trends: asked about the non-fiction; the diasporic themes; the exploration of India.

works.title: An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding
works.shortDescription: Hume's more accessible version of his philosophy, originally titled Philosophical Essays Concerning Human Understanding.[reference:38]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Problem of Induction – Hume explores the problem of induction and the limits of human knowledge. (2) The Miracles Argument – Hume argues against the validity of miracles. PYQ trends: asked about Hume's Enquiry; the problem of induction.

works.title: An Essay on Criticism
works.shortDescription: Pope's didactic poem in heroic couplets, offering guidelines for critics and poets. It is one of the most quoted works in English literature.[reference:1]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Didactic Poetry – the poem is a guide to criticism, drawing on classical sources like Horace, Aristotle, and Longinus. (2) Famous Lines – contains some of the most quoted lines in English poetry, including 'To err is human, to forgive divine' and 'A little learning is a dangerous thing'. (3) The Three Parts – the poem is divided into three parts: general rules for criticism, the causes of faulty criticism, and the qualities of a good critic. (4) The Ideal Critic – Pope advocates for a critic who is humble, learned, and guided by nature and reason. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Pope's didactic poetry; the famous quotations; the poem's structure.

works.title: An Essay on Man
works.shortDescription: Pope's philosophical poem in heroic couplets, exploring the nature of humanity and the universe. It was published in four epistles.[reference:3]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Philosophical Poetry – the poem explores themes of theodicy, the nature of humanity, and the order of the universe. (2) The Great Chain of Being – Pope uses the concept of the Great Chain of Being to argue for the perfection of the universe. (3) Famous Lines – contains the famous line 'Whatever is, is right'. (4) Influence of Leibniz – the poem draws on the philosophical optimism of Leibniz. PYQ trends: asked about Pope's philosophical poetry; the Great Chain of Being; the poem's sources.

works.title: An Ideal Husband
works.shortDescription: Wilde's comedy of manners about Sir Robert Chiltern, a respected politician who is blackmailed over a past indiscretion. The play explores themes of political morality and marital ideals.[reference:12]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Political Morality – the play explores the gap between public image and private behavior. (2) The Ideal Husband – the title is ironic: the 'ideal' husband is revealed to have a flawed past. (3) The Wit – Wilde's characteristic epigrams are on full display. PYQ trends: asked about the political satire; the theme of the ideal; compared with other Wilde comedies.

works.title: Anatomy of Criticism
works.shortDescription: Frye's monumental work of literary theory, which argues for a systematic, scientific approach to criticism based on archetypes and the structure of literature.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Archetypal Criticism – Frye argues that literature is structured by recurring archetypes and myths. (2) The Four Modes – myth, romance, high mimetic, low mimetic, and ironic. (3) The Mythos of Spring, Summer, Autumn, Winter – comedy, romance, tragedy, and satire/irony. PYQ trends: frequently asked about archetypal criticism; the four modes; the mythos; compared with other critical approaches.
works.references: Edition: 1957 (Princeton: Princeton University Press). Criticism: Frye (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: And death shall have no dominion
works.shortDescription: A poem celebrating the power of life over death, written in a biblical style[reference:73].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Title – a reference to St. Paul's Epistle to the Romans. (2) The Theme of Death – the poem asserts that death has no ultimate power. (3) The Biblical Style – the poem echoes the language of the King James Bible. PYQ trends: asked about the biblical style; the theme of death.

works.title: Animal Farm
works.shortDescription: Orwell's allegorical novella about a group of farm animals who revolt against their human farmer, only to have their revolution betrayed.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Allegory – the novel is an allegory of the Russian Revolution. (2) The Seven Commandments – the animals' principles are gradually corrupted. (3) The Famous Line – 'All animals are equal, but some animals are more equal than others.' PYQ trends: frequently asked about the allegory; the Seven Commandments; the famous line.

works.title: Annie John
works.shortDescription: Kincaid's novel about a young girl growing up in Antigua, exploring themes of identity, colonialism, and the relationship between mother and daughter.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Coming-of-Age – the novel is a bildungsroman. (2) The Mother-Daughter Relationship – the novel explores the complex relationship between Annie and her mother. (3) The Colonial Theme – the novel explores the legacy of colonialism. PYQ trends: asked about the coming-of-age; the mother-daughter relationship; the colonial themes.
works.references: Edition: 1985 (New York: Farrar, Straus and Giroux). Criticism: Kincaid (2000).

works.title: Another Time
works.shortDescription: Auden's collection of poems, including 'September 1, 1939' and 'In Memory of W. B. Yeats'[reference:31].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Collection – the volume contains some of Auden's most famous poems. (2) In Memory of W. B. Yeats – Auden's elegy for Yeats, which famously begins 'He disappeared in the dead of winter.' PYQ trends: asked about Auden's middle period; the elegies.

works.title: Anthem for Doomed Youth
works.shortDescription: A sonnet that transforms the traditional anthem of worship into a lament for the young soldiers killed in war[reference:40].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Sonnet Form – a Petrarchan sonnet, subverting the traditional love poem. (2) The Military Imagery – the sounds of war replace the sounds of the church: the 'stuttering rifles' rapid rattle' and the 'monstrous anger of the guns'. (3) The Theme of Mourning – the poem mourns the young soldiers who died without proper burial. PYQ trends: asked about the sonnet form; the war imagery; the theme of mourning.

works.title: Appreciations
works.shortDescription: A collection of essays on literature, including the important essay 'Style'. Pater defined nonfiction prose as 'the special and opportune art of the modern world' in this work.[reference:19]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Prose as Art – Pater argues for the artistic value of prose, calling it 'the special and opportune art of the modern world'.[reference:20] (2) The Essay on Style – Pater writes in defence of prose in his essay on 'Style'.[reference:21] (3) Literary Criticism – the work is a collection of literary criticism. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Pater's essay on 'Style'; the definition of nonfiction prose; compared with other prose stylists.

works.title: Archetypal Patterns in Poetry
works.shortDescription: Bodkin's foundational work of archetypal criticism, which applies Jungian psychology to the study of poetry.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Archetypal Patterns – Bodkin identifies recurring archetypes in poetry. (2) The Jungian Influence – the work draws on Jung's theory of the collective unconscious. (3) The Psychological Approach – the work applies psychology to literary analysis. PYQ trends: asked about archetypal criticism; the Jungian influence; Bodkin's contribution to archetypal theory.
works.references: Edition: 1934 (London: Oxford University Press). Criticism: Bodkin (2000).

works.title: Areopagitica
works.shortDescription: Milton's prose pamphlet, published in 1644, arguing against censorship and for the freedom of the press. It is one of the most influential works on free speech in the English language.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Freedom of the Press – a powerful defence of the freedom to publish without censorship. (2) Rhetorical Power – the pamphlet is notable for its passionate and eloquent argument. (3) Historical Context – written in response to the Licensing Order of 1643. (4) Famous Passage – 'Give me the liberty to know, to utter, and to argue freely according to conscience, above all liberties.' PYQ trends: frequently asked about Milton's prose; the defence of free speech; compared with other works on liberty.

works.title: Ariel
works.shortDescription: Plath's most famous collection, published posthumously in 1965. It contains many of her best-known poems, including 'Lady Lazarus', 'Daddy', and 'Ariel'.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Confessional Poetry – the collection is a landmark of confessional poetry. (2) The Poems – includes 'Lady Lazarus', 'Daddy', 'Tulips', and 'Ariel'. (3) The Posthumous Publication – the collection was published after Plath's death. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Ariel; the confessional style; individual poems like 'Lady Lazarus' and 'Daddy'.

works.title: Arms and the Man
works.shortDescription: Shaw's first public success, a satirical comedy set during the 1885 Serbo-Bulgarian War. It subverts the romantic conventions of war and heroism.[reference:5][reference:6]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Anti-Romantic Comedy – the play debunks the romantic idealization of war. (2) The Chocolate Cream Soldier – Captain Bluntschli, who carries chocolates instead of ammunition, represents Shaw's critique. (3) The Title – from Virgil's Aeneid: 'Arms and the man I sing'. PYQ trends: asked about the satire of war; the character of Bluntschli; compared with other Shavian plays.

works.title: Ars Poetica
works.shortDescription: "Epistle to the Pisos" offers advice to aspiring poets, key text of classical literary criticism, used by Romantics
works.analysis:
NET/SET Focus: (1) Utile et Dulce – poetry should both instruct and delight. (2) The Poet as Craftsman – the poet should be careful and skilled in his craft. (3) Decorum – the style and content should be appropriate to the subject. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the principle of utile et dulce; the poet as craftsman; the concept of decorum.
# Summary
1-188 Use of unity and simplicity, abstain from extremes, add new words with care, kinds of poetry and style suiting them, words-characters relation, follow the tradition of muse, characteristics of each age of man, a good model is always find in HOMER.
189-360 Role of chorus, different taste of different people, origin and development of tragedy, role of critic. knowledge is backbone of all writings, poetry should give Utile et Dulce (instruction and delight), poetry not perfect but written with care and pains
361-476 A short comparison between poetry and painting, mediocrity in poetry is insufferable, origin of poetry in early days, role of genius accompanied by art and study, avoid flatter, don't be arrogant as Empedocles refusing to accept help.
# Key Quotes
"in medias res (l. 148)", or "into the middle of things"- Begin the story in the middle as per GREEKS
"ab ovo (l. 147)", or "from the beginning"- from the very beginning (ovo-egg) as per HOMER
"quandoque bonus dormitat Homerus (l. 359)" or "sometimes even good Homer nods off"-even the most skilled poet can make continuity errors
"ut pictura poesis (l. 361)", or "as is painting so is poetry"
"Desinit in piscem (l. 4)," or "it ends with the tail of a fish,"-literary works must be composed with unity to fulfill their promises to the reader not end abruptly.
# Literary Concepts
Decorum/Appropriateness-criticism of purple prose (highly ornamented diction)
deus ex machina- a plot device where an unsolvable problem is abruptly solved by some Olympian God. "That a god not intervene, unless a knot show up that be worthy of such an untangler"

works.title: Art as Technique
works.shortDescription: Shklovsky's foundational essay of Russian Formalism, which introduces the concept of defamiliarization (ostranenie). It argues that the purpose of art is to make objects unfamiliar and to renew perception.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Defamiliarization – the central concept of the essay; art makes the familiar strange. (2) The Purpose of Art – art renews perception by making objects unfamiliar. (3) The Formalist Method – the essay is a key example of the Formalist approach. PYQ trends: one of the most frequently asked concepts in NET; defamiliarization; compared with other formalist concepts.
works.references: Edition: 1917 (in Russian), 1965 (English translation). Criticism: Shklovsky (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: As I Lay Dying
works.shortDescription: Faulkner's novel about the Bundren family's journey to bury their mother, told through 59 interior monologues.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Multiple Narrators – the novel is told through 59 different interior monologues. (2) The Quest – the journey to bury the mother is a symbolic quest. (3) The Theme of Endurance – the family endures hardship. PYQ trends: asked about the multiple narrators; the symbolic quest; the theme of endurance.

works.title: Astrophel
works.shortDescription: A pastoral elegy written on the death of Sir Philip Sidney. Spenser mourned Sidney as a fellow poet and friend.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Pastoral Elegy – follows the classical tradition of pastoral mourning (Theocritus, Virgil). (2) Astrophel – the name itself is a tribute to Sidney's sonnet sequence Astrophel and Stella. (3) Friendship and Tribute – reflects the close literary friendship between Spenser and Sidney. PYQ trends: asked about the pastoral elegy tradition; the relationship between Spenser and Sidney.
works.references: Edition: 1595 (published in Colin Clouts Come Home Againe). Criticism: O'Connell (2002).

works.title: Astrophel and Stella
works.shortDescription: Sidney's famous sonnet sequence, published posthumously in 1591. It consists of 108 sonnets and 11 songs, chronicling the love of Astrophel (the star-lover) for Stella (the star).
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Major English Sonnet Sequence – predates Shakespeare's sonnets and Spenser's Amoretti. (2) Petrarchan and Anti-Petrarchan – follows Petrarchan conventions while also subverting them; Sidney explores the psychology of love with greater psychological depth. (3) Autobiographical Reading – often read as reflecting Sidney's own love for Penelope Devereux. (4) Metrical Innovation – uses the English sonnet form (Shakespearean) but with variations. PYQ trends: frequently asked as the first major English sonnet sequence; compared with Spenser's Amoretti and Shakespeare's Sonnets; the Petrarchan tradition in English poetry.

works.title: Atalanta in Calydon
works.shortDescription: Swinburne's verse drama based on the Greek myth of the hunt for the Calydonian boar.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Greek Drama – the work is a verse drama in the classical Greek style. (2) The Mythological Subject – based on the Greek myth of Atalanta. (3) The Famous Chorus – 'When the hounds of spring are on winter's traces'. PYQ trends: asked about the Greek influence; the mythological subject.

works.title: Atonement
works.shortDescription: McEwan's novel about a young girl's false accusation that changes the lives of her family forever. It explores themes of guilt, memory, and the power of storytelling.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Metafictional Element – the novel is self-conscious about its status as fiction. (2) The Theme of Guilt – the protagonist's false accusation haunts her for life. (3) The Historical Setting – the novel is set before, during, and after World War II. (4) The Twist – the ending reveals that the story is a fictionalized account. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the metafictional element; the theme of guilt; the World War II setting.

works.title: Auld Lang Syne
works.shortDescription: Burns's most famous song, a celebration of friendship and old times. It is one of the most widely sung songs in the English language.[reference:19]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Folk Song – Burns collected and adapted traditional Scottish songs. (2) The Theme of Friendship – the song celebrates old friendships and shared memories. (3) Global Reach – the song is sung around the world on New Year's Eve. PYQ trends: asked about Burns's songs; the Scottish folk tradition.

works.title: Aurora Leigh
works.shortDescription: Barrett Browning's epic novel in verse, a celebration of woman's potential to stand on her own[reference:14].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Epic – the poem is a long narrative in blank verse. (2) The Feminist Theme – the poem celebrates the potential of women[reference:15]. (3) The Aurora Leigh – the heroine is a poet who struggles to find her voice. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the feminist themes; the epic form; compared with other Victorian epics.

works.title: Autobiography of an Unknown Indian
works.shortDescription: Chaudhuri's classic autobiography, which offers a rich and insightful account of life in colonial India.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Autobiography – the work is a classic autobiography of life in colonial India. (2) The Style – Chaudhuri's prose is elegant and incisive. (3) The Themes – explores the experience of colonialism, cultural identity, and modernization. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the autobiography; the colonial setting; the prose style.

works.title: Bartholomew Fair
works.shortDescription: Jonson's comedy set in the annual fair at Smithfield, London. It is a sprawling, carnivalesque play that depicts the chaos and corruption of urban life[reference:34][reference:35].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Carnivalesque – the play depicts the fair as a place of moral inversion and social chaos. (2) Sati re of Puritanism – the play satirizes the Puritan characters who hypocritically condemn the fair while participating in its pleasures[reference:36]. (3) The Apprentices – the play features a subplot involving apprentices and their masters. (4) The Contract Scene – a famous scene in which the puppet shows are used to satirize religious and legal debates. PYQ trends: asked about the satire of Puritanism; the carnivalesque elements; compared with Jonson's other comedies[reference:37][reference:38].
works.references: Editions: Norton Critical Edition. Criticism: Donaldson (2011), Jonson (1967).

works.title: Beloved
works.shortDescription: Morrison's most famous novel, a powerful exploration of the legacy of slavery in America. It tells the story of Sethe, a former slave who is haunted by the ghost of her daughter.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Legacy of Slavery – the novel explores the trauma of slavery. (2) The Supernatural – the novel uses the ghost of Beloved as a symbol. (3) The Historical Context – the novel is set in post-Civil War America. (4) The Pulitzer Prize – the novel won the Pulitzer Prize in 1988. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the legacy of slavery; the supernatural elements; the Pulitzer Prize.

works.title: Beowulf
works.shortDescription: Old English epic poem in the Germanic heroic legend tradition, consisting of 3,182 alliterative lines. Set in pagan Scandinavia, it follows the Geatish hero Beowulf as he defeats Grendel, Grendel's mother, and later dies fighting a dragon.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Oral-Formulaic Theory – the poem is a product of oral tradition, built with recurring epithets and themes (Parry-Lord hypothesis). (2) Christian-Pagan Synthesis – a defining tension: pagan Germanic heroic ethos (wyrd/fate, vengeance) juxtaposed with a Christian narrator's moral commentary (divine providence, humility). (3) Digressive Structure – frequent embedded narratives (Finnsburh episode, Sigemund's lay) that function as thematic parallels to Beowulf's own life. (4) Manuscript – sole surviving copy in the Nowell Codex (British Library Cotton Vitellius A.xv), dating c. 975-1025. (5) Critical Landmark – J.R.R. Tolkien's 1936 lecture 'Beowulf: The Monsters and the Critics' shifted criticism from historical-search to literary appreciation. PYQ trends: often asked about the elegiac tone, the role of the scop, the three monsters as symbolic threats, and the poem's treatment of gender (Wealhtheow, Grendel's mother).
works.references: MS: Nowell Codex (Cotton Vitellius A.xv). Editions: Klaeber (4th ed., 2008). Criticism: Tolkien (1936), Brodeur (1959), Orchard (1995).

works.title: Binsey Poplars
works.shortDescription: Hopkins's poem lamenting the felling of a row of poplar trees[reference:50].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Theme of Nature – the poem mourns the destruction of nature[reference:51]. (2) The Sprung Rhythm – the poem is written in sprung rhythm[reference:52]. PYQ trends: asked about the theme of nature; the sprung rhythm.

works.title: Biographia Literaria
works.shortDescription: Coleridge's major prose work, a blend of autobiography, philosophy, and literary criticism. It is the most significant work of literary criticism produced in the English Romantic period.[reference:14]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Primary and Secondary Imagination – Coleridge's famous distinction: the primary imagination is the 'living Power and prime Agent' of human perception, while the secondary imagination is the creative power of the artist.[reference:15] (2) Fancy vs. Imagination – another key distinction. (3) The Definition of a Poem – Coleridge defines a poem as 'that species of composition, which is opposed to works of science, by proposing for its immediate object pleasure, not truth'. PYQ trends: one of the most frequently asked critical texts in NET; the distinction between imagination and fancy; compared with Wordsworth's Preface.

works.title: Birthday Letters
works.shortDescription: Hughes's final collection, a sequence of poems addressed to his first wife, Sylvia Plath. It won the Whitbread Book of the Year.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Autobiographical Element – the poems are addressed to Plath and reflect on their marriage. (2) The Revelation – the collection offers Hughes's perspective on their relationship. (3) The Prize – the collection won the Whitbread Book of the Year. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Birthday Letters; the autobiographical element; the relationship with Plath.

works.title: Black Skin, White Masks
works.shortDescription: Fanon's psychoanalytic and philosophical examination of the psychology of colonialism and the experience of black people in a white-dominated world.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Psychology of Colonialism – explores how colonialism shapes the psyche of both colonized and colonizer. (2) The Black-White Dynamic – analyzes the desire of black people to be recognized as white. (3) The Concept of Alienation – the colonized individual is alienated from their own identity. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the psychology of colonialism; the black-white dynamic; Fanon's psychoanalytic approach.

works.title: Black Water
works.shortDescription: A 1992 novella by Joyce Carol Oates, a roman à clef based on the Chappaquiddick incident. It was a finalist for the Pulitzer Prize.[reference:12]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Roman à Clef – the novella is based on a real-life incident. (2) The Psychological Depth – the novella explores the intense narrative and psychological depth. (3) The Pulitzer Prize – the novella was a finalist for the Pulitzer Prize. PYQ trends: asked about the roman à clef; the psychological depth; the Pulitzer Prize nomination.

works.title: Blasted
works.shortDescription: Kane's controversial debut play, a shocking exploration of violence, sex, and survival in a wartime setting. It established her as a major voice of 'In-Yer-Face' theatre.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) In-Yer-Face Theatre – the play is a key example of the 'In-Yer-Face' theatre movement. (2) The Violence – the play is notorious for its graphic violence. (3) The Setting – the play begins in a hotel room and descends into a war zone. (4) The Controversy – the play was controversial for its subject matter. PYQ trends: frequently asked about 'In-Yer-Face' theatre; the graphic violence; the play's controversy; compared with other contemporary British plays.

works.title: Bleak House
works.shortDescription: Dickens's novel about the interminable lawsuit of Jarndyce and Jarndyce, critiquing the British legal system and Victorian society.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Social Critique – the novel is a savage critique of the legal system and social inequality. (2) The Dual Narrative – the novel alternates between an omniscient third-person narrator and Esther Summerson's first-person account. (3) The Fog – the fog symbolizes the confusion and corruption of the legal system. PYQ trends: asked about the dual narrative; the social critique; the symbol of the fog.

works.title: Brave New World
works.shortDescription: Huxley's dystopian novel about a future society where humans are genetically engineered and conditioned to be happy in a world of consumerism and pleasure.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Dystopian Fiction – the novel is a landmark of dystopian fiction. (2) The World State – the society is controlled through genetic engineering and psychological conditioning. (3) The Savage – John the Savage represents the critique of the society. (4) The Famous Line – 'I want to know what passion is. I want to feel something strongly.' PYQ trends: frequently asked about the dystopian genre; the themes of consumerism and conditioning; compared with Nineteen Eighty-Four.

works.title: Brideshead Revisited
works.shortDescription: Waugh's novel about the aristocratic Flyte family and their Catholic faith, narrated by Charles Ryder.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Theme of Faith – the novel explores the Catholic faith of the Flyte family. (2) The Narrator – Charles Ryder is a nostalgic narrator. (3) The Setting – Brideshead is a symbol of aristocratic decline. PYQ trends: asked about the theme of faith; the narrator; the symbolism of Brideshead.

works.title: Brighton Rock
works.shortDescription: Greene's novel about a young gangster in Brighton, exploring themes of good and evil.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Thriller – the novel is a thriller with serious themes. (2) The Theme of Good and Evil – the novel explores the nature of evil. PYQ trends: asked about the thriller genre; the theme of good and evil.

works.title: Bussy D'Ambois
works.shortDescription: Chapman's best-known tragedy, a two-part play about the historical figure Bussy d'Ambois, a French soldier and courtier known for his duels and his relationship with the mistress of the Duke of Guise[reference:57].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Historical Source – based on the life of Louis de Clermont, seigneur de Bussy d'Amboise, a 16th-century French courtier. (2) The Tragic Hero – Bussy is a man of integrity who is destroyed by the corrupt court. (3) The Play's Language – known for its memorable lines, such as 'Man is a torch borne in the wind; a dream / But of a shadow, summ'd with all his substance'[reference:58]. (4) The School of Night – Chapman has been associated with the 'School of Night', a group of free-thinkers including Marlowe and Raleigh[reference:59]. PYQ trends: asked about Chapman's tragedies; compared with Shakespeare's tragedies; the play's historical context[reference:60].
works.references: Editions: Norton Critical Edition. Criticism: Chapman (2002), Kermode (2000).

works.title: Caedmon's Hymn
works.shortDescription: A short, nine-line Old English alliterative poem of praise to God the Creator. It is the earliest known Old English poem and one of the oldest surviving samples of Germanic alliterative verse.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Foundation Text – often called 'the first English poem'; establishes the earliest known named English poet. (2) Oral to Written Transmission – preserved in Bede's Latin Historia Ecclesiastica (8th c.) with both Latin paraphrase and the Old English original, showing a rare bilingual transmission. (3) Metrical Mastery – uses the standard OE alliterative line (four stresses, medial caesura) despite the poet's illiteracy, proving the poem's oral origin. (4) Kennings – dense use of compound metaphors (e.g. 'heofonrices Weard' – heaven-kingdom's Guardian, 'Wuldor-Fæder' – Glory-Father). (5) Textual Variants – survives in two dialectal versions: Northumbrian (older, closer to original) and West Saxon (later, more standardized). PYQ trends: asked alongside Bede's account, and often compared with other creation hymns (e.g., the Genesis poem).
works.references: Source: Bede, HE IV.24. MS: St. Petersburg, NL, Q.v.I.18 (earliest, Northumbrian c. 737). Editions: Dobbie (1942), O'Donnell (2005).

works.title: Campaspe
works.shortDescription: Lyly's first play, performed at court by the Children of the Chapel and Children of Paul's. It tells the story of Alexander the Great who surrenders his mistress Campaspe to the painter Apelles[reference:67].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First English Drama – one of the first English dramas based on historical background; possibly the first romantic drama[reference:68]. (2) Court Entertainment – performed at Whitehall Palace for Queen Elizabeth[reference:69]. (3) The Love Triangle – Alexander, Campaspe, and Apelles form a classic love triangle. (4) The Play's Ending – Alexander gives up Campaspe to Apelles, demonstrating his magnanimity. PYQ trends: asked about Lyly's plays; the development of English comedy; the court context[reference:70].
works.references: Editions: Norton Critical Edition. Criticism: Hunter (1962), Lyly (2002).

works.title: Can the Subaltern Speak?
works.shortDescription: Spivak's seminal essay, which argues that the subaltern (the oppressed and marginalized) cannot speak because their voice is always mediated by colonial and patriarchal power structures.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Subaltern – the oppressed and marginalized subject. (2) The Problem of Representation – the subaltern's voice is always mediated. (3) The Case of the Sati – Spivak uses the example of the Sati to illustrate her argument. PYQ trends: one of the most frequently asked essays in NET; the subaltern; the problem of representation; the Sati example.
works.references: Edition: 1988 (in Marxism and the Interpretation of Culture). Criticism: Spivak (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Carrion Comfort
works.shortDescription: Hopkins's sonnet about despair and the struggle to find comfort in the face of overwhelming grief[reference:53].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Sonnet – the poem is a Petrarchan sonnet. (2) The Theme of Despair – the poem explores the theme of despair[reference:54]. (3) The Sprung Rhythm – the poem is written in sprung rhythm[reference:55]. PYQ trends: asked about the theme of despair; the sprung rhythm.

works.title: Cathleen ni Houlihan
works.shortDescription: Yeats's one-act play about a poor old woman who represents Ireland and inspires a young man to sacrifice himself for the nationalist cause.[reference:33]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Nationalist Allegory – the old woman represents Ireland. (2) The One-Act Form – the play is a powerful one-act drama. (3) The Influence – the play had a significant impact on the Irish nationalist movement. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the nationalist allegory; the play's influence on the Easter Rising.

works.title: Cecilia
works.shortDescription: Burney's second novel, a more complex and ambitious work than Evelina.[reference:39]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Social Satire – the novel satirizes the marriage market and social pretensions. (2) Financial Themes – the novel explores the financial constraints faced by women. (3) Greater Complexity – the novel is more complex than Evelina, with a larger cast of characters and a more intricate plot. PYQ trends: asked about Burney's later works.

works.title: Centuries of Meditations
works.shortDescription: Traherne's most famous work, a collection of short paragraphs reflecting on Christian life, philosophy, happiness, desire, and childhood. It was not published until 1908, long after his death.[reference:30][reference:31]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Spiritual Autobiography – the Centuries are a record of Traherne's spiritual experiences and reflections. (2) Mystical Vision – Traherne celebrates the wonder and beauty of creation and the presence of God in all things. (3) Childhood – Traherne glorifies childhood as a state of innocence and spiritual purity. (4) Prose Style – Traherne's prose is characterized by its exuberance, simplicity, and visionary quality.[reference:32] PYQ trends: asked about Traherne's prose; the mystical element; the celebration of childhood.
works.references: Edition: 1908 (published by Bertram Dobell). Criticism: Stewart (2006), Smith (2000).

works.title: Chandalika
works.shortDescription: Tagore's one-act play about an untouchable girl who falls in love with a Buddhist monk. It is a powerful exploration of caste, love, and redemption.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Caste Theme – the play explores the injustice of the caste system. (2) The One-Act Play – the play is a one-act drama. (3) The Themes – explores love, redemption, and the human spirit. PYQ trends: asked about the caste theme; the one-act form; the themes of love and redemption.

works.title: Childe Harold's Pilgrimage
works.shortDescription: Byron's narrative poem in Spenserian stanzas, published in four cantos between 1812 and 1818. It established Byron's reputation as a major poet.[reference:18]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Byronic Hero – the poem introduces the archetypal Byronic hero: a brooding, rebellious, and melancholy figure. (2) The Spenserian Stanza – Byron uses the nine-line stanza invented by Spenser. (3) The Grand Tour – the poem follows Harold's travels through Europe. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the Byronic hero; the Spenserian stanza; compared with other Romantic epics.

works.title: Christ II
works.shortDescription: A lyrical adaptation of a homily on the Ascension written by Pope Gregory I. It is the second part of a trilogy on Christ, though parts I and III are by different anonymous poets.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Part of the 'Christ' triptych in the Exeter Book – only Cynewulf's section (Christ II) contains the runic signature. (2) Gregorian Source – closely translates Pope Gregory's homily, but innovatively reworks it into OE alliterative verse with added imagery (e.g., Christ's leap into heaven). (3) The 'Leap' Metaphor – Cynewulf uses the verb 'hlēapan' (to leap) to describe Christ's Ascension, a vivid and original kenning-like conceptualization. (4) Epilogue – the runic passage here (spelling CYNWULF) focuses on the soul's journey to judgment, a common Cynewulfian theme. PYQ trends: asked about the structure of the Exeter Book and the attribution of anonymous poems.
works.references: MS: Exeter Book (Exeter Cathedral Library, MS 3501). Editions: Krapp & Dobbie (1936), Muir (2000).

works.title: Christabel
works.shortDescription: Coleridge's unfinished Gothic poem about a young woman who encounters a mysterious stranger named Geraldine.[reference:13]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Gothic Elements – the poem uses Gothic conventions of mystery and terror. (2) The Unfinished State – the poem remains incomplete. (3) The Symbolism – Christabel represents innocence, Geraldine represents corruption. PYQ trends: asked about the Gothic elements; the poem's unfinished status.

works.title: Church Going
works.shortDescription: One of Larkin's most famous poems, a meditation on the decline of religious faith in modern Britain.[reference:19]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Theme of Faith – the poem explores the loss of religious faith. (2) The Church – the church is a symbol of a disappearing way of life. (3) The Speaker – the speaker is an agnostic who nonetheless feels drawn to the church. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Church Going; the theme of faith; the poem's ambivalent tone.
works.references: Edition: 1955 (in The Less Deceived). Criticism: Larkin (2000).

works.title: Clarissa
works.shortDescription: Richardson's masterpiece, an epistolary novel about a young woman who is seduced and abandoned by a rake. It is one of the longest and most psychologically complex novels in English.[reference:28]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Epistolary Novel – the novel is written in the form of letters, allowing for multiple perspectives. (2) Psychological Depth – the novel explores the inner lives of its characters with unprecedented depth. (3) The Tragic Ending – unlike Pamela, Clarissa ends tragically. (4) Clarissa and Lovelace – the conflict between the virtuous heroine and the charming rake is one of the great dramatic confrontations in the novel. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Richardson's Clarissa; the epistolary form; the psychological depth; compared with Pamela.

works.title: Clear Light of Day
works.shortDescription: Desai's finest novel, which evokes the world in which she grew up. It is set in a crumbling mansion in Old Delhi and explores themes of memory, identity, and the impact of societal changes on familial relationships.[reference:70][reference:71]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Autobiographical Work – the novel is Desai's most autobiographical work.[reference:72] (2) The Partition – the novel is a partition novel which depicts the influence of the Partition on the Indian family.[reference:73] (3) The Themes – the novel explores memory, identity, and societal changes.[reference:74] PYQ trends: asked about the novel's publication year (1980); the autobiographical elements; and the themes of memory and identity.
works.references: Edition: 1980 (New York: Harper & Row). Criticism: Desai (2000).

works.title: Cloud Nine
works.shortDescription: Churchill's play about colonialism and gender, set in Victorian Africa and 1970s London. It uses cross-casting to explore the construction of identity.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Cross-Casting – characters are played by actors of different genders and races. (2) The Colonial Theme – the play critiques colonialism. (3) The Two Acts – the first act is set in colonial Africa, the second in 1970s London. PYQ trends: asked about the cross-casting; the colonial theme; the two-act structure.

works.title: Collected Poems
works.shortDescription: Betjeman's collected poems, which contain his best-known works including 'Slough' and 'A Subaltern's Love-Song'. His poetry is characterized by its wit, nostalgia, and social observation.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Wit – Betjeman's poetry is known for its wit and humour. (2) The Nostalgia – the poems are suffused with nostalgia for a vanishing England. (3) The Social Observation – Betjeman satirizes the English class structure and suburban life. (4) 'Slough' – a famously satirical poem about the town of Slough. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Betjeman's wit and social satire; the poem 'Slough'; his popularity as Poet Laureate.

works.title: Colonial Transactions
works.shortDescription: Trivedi's study of the exchange between English and Indian literatures during the colonial period.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Exchange – the literary exchange between England and India. (2) The Colonial Period – the period of colonial rule. (3) The Transactions – the mutual influence of literatures. PYQ trends: asked about the exchange between English and Indian literatures; compared with other postcolonial studies.

works.title: Confessio Amantis
works.shortDescription: A 33,000-line Middle English poem by John Gower, which uses the confession made by an ageing lover to the chaplain of Venus as a frame story for a collection of shorter narrative poems on various moral and political themes.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Frame Story – uses the confession frame to structure a series of exempla. (2) Commission – composed at the request of Richard II (later revised for Henry IV). (3) Genre – usually considered a poem of consolation, inspired by Boethius's Consolation of Philosophy. (4) Popularity – one of the most-often copied manuscripts (59 surviving copies) before the printing press. (5) Three Languages – Gower wrote in French (Mirour de l'Omme), Latin (Vox Clamantis), and English (Confessio), showcasing his trilingual mastery. PYQ trends: compared with Chaucer's Canterbury Tales as both share several stories; asked about Gower as a contemporary of Chaucer; the tripartite structure.
works.references: MS: 59 surviving copies. Edition: Macaulay (1900-01). Criticism: Nicholson (1991), Watt (2003).

works.title: Confessions of an English Opium Eater
works.shortDescription: De Quincey's most famous work, an autobiographical account of his addiction to opium.[reference:44]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Autobiographical Prose – the work is a confessional and introspective account of addiction. (2) The Opium Dream – the work explores the visionary and hallucinatory experiences induced by opium. (3) The Influence – the work influenced later writers on drugs and consciousness. PYQ trends: frequently asked about De Quincey's Confessions; the autobiographical prose; compared with other Romantic prose.

works.title: Considerations on Representative Government
works.shortDescription: Mill's work on political theory, exploring the nature and benefits of representative democracy.[reference:53][reference:54]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Representative Democracy – Mill argues for the value of representative government. (2) Proportional Representation – Mill advocates for proportional representation. (3) Plural Voting – Mill controversially argues for plural voting for the educated. PYQ trends: asked about Mill's political theory; representative government; compared with other political theorists.

works.title: Continual Dew
works.shortDescription: Betjeman's early collection, which helped to establish his reputation.[reference:21]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Early Collection – Betjeman's second collection. (2) The Style – the collection showcases Betjeman's wit and social observation. PYQ trends: asked about Betjeman's early collections.

works.title: Coolie
works.shortDescription: Anand's second novel, which reinforced his position as a leading Indian English author. It follows the life of a young boy who works as a coolie and exposes the exploitation of the working class.[reference:8][reference:9]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Social Realism – a critique of the exploitation of the working class under British rule.[reference:10] (2) The Caste System – the novel addresses the rigid caste system that structured Indian society.[reference:11] PYQ trends: asked about the novel's publication year (1936); the themes of exploitation and the caste system.[reference:12]
works.references: Edition: 1936 (London: Lawrence & Wishart). Criticism: Anand (2000), King (1987).

works.title: Counter-Attack
works.shortDescription: Sassoon's major poetry collection, containing some of his most powerful war poems[reference:48].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Collection – the volume contains Sassoon's most powerful war poetry. (2) The Realism – the poems depict the horrors of war with brutal realism. PYQ trends: asked about Sassoon's major collection; the realism of the poems.

works.title: Course in General Linguistics
works.shortDescription: Saussure's foundational text of structural linguistics, which introduces the key concepts of langue and parole, the signifier and the signified, and the arbitrary nature of the linguistic sign.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Langue vs. Parole – langue is the underlying system of language; parole is individual speech acts. (2) Signifier and Signified – the signifier is the sound-image; the signified is the concept. (3) The Arbitrary Sign – the relationship between signifier and signified is arbitrary. (4) Synchronic vs. Diachronic – synchronic analysis focuses on a language at a given time; diachronic analysis focuses on change over time. PYQ trends: one of the most frequently asked texts in NET; langue and parole; the signifier and signified; the arbitrary sign.

works.title: Cranford
works.shortDescription: Gaskell's comic novel about the lives of a group of elderly women in a small provincial town.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Comic Novel – the novel is a gentle comedy of manners. (2) The Provincial Setting – the novel is set in a small provincial town. (3) The Female Community – the novel celebrates the community of women. PYQ trends: asked about the comic novel; the provincial setting.

works.title: Critique of Judgment
works.shortDescription: Kant's major work on aesthetics, exploring the nature of beauty, the sublime, and the judgment of taste.[reference:53]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Aesthetics – Kant's work is a foundational text in the philosophy of art. (2) The Beautiful – Kant defines beauty as that which pleases universally without a concept. (3) The Sublime – Kant explores the experience of the sublime. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Kant's aesthetics; the beautiful and the sublime; the judgment of taste.

works.title: Critique of Practical Reason
works.shortDescription: Kant's major work on ethics, exploring the nature of moral law and the concept of duty.[reference:52]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Categorical Imperative – Kant's famous moral principle: 'Act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law.' (2) The Good Will – Kant argues that only a good will is good without qualification. (3) Duty – the concept of duty is central to Kant's ethics. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Kant's ethics; the categorical imperative; the good will.

works.title: Critique of Pure Reason
works.shortDescription: Kant's most famous philosophical work, a foundational text of modern philosophy. It examines the nature of human knowledge and the limits of reason.[reference:51]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Transcendental Idealism – Kant's philosophy is known as transcendental idealism. (2) The Categories – Kant argues that the mind imposes categories on experience. (3) The Thing-in-Itself – Kant distinguishes between the phenomenal world (as it appears) and the noumenal world (as it is in itself). (4) The Copernican Revolution – Kant compares his philosophy to the Copernican revolution in astronomy. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Kant's Critique of Pure Reason; transcendental idealism; the categories; the thing-in-itself.

works.title: Crome Yellow
works.shortDescription: Huxley's first novel, a satire of the intellectual circles of post-World War I England.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Novel – Huxley's debut novel. (2) The Satire – the novel satirizes the intellectual pretensions of the age. PYQ trends: asked about Huxley's first novel; the satire.

works.title: Crow: From the Life and Songs of the Crow
works.shortDescription: One of Hughes's most influential works, a sequence of poems featuring the figure of Crow as a trickster and survivor.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Crow Figure – Crow is a trickster, a survivor, and a darkly comic figure. (2) The Mythological Framework – the poems draw on mythology and folklore. (3) The Themes – explores themes of creation, destruction, and the nature of evil. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Crow; the trickster figure; the mythological elements.

works.title: Cultural Identity and Diaspora
works.shortDescription: Hall's essay on cultural identity, which argues that identity is not fixed but is a process of becoming, shaped by experience and history.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Cultural Identity – identity is not fixed but is a process. (2) The Two Positions – identity as a shared culture and identity as a process of becoming. (3) The Diaspora – the experience of displacement. PYQ trends: frequently asked about cultural identity; diaspora; compared with other identity theorists.
works.references: Edition: 1990 (in Identity: Community, Culture, Difference). Criticism: Hall (2000).

works.title: Culture and Anarchy
works.shortDescription: Arnold's major prose work, a social and cultural critique arguing for the importance of culture as a force for order and perfection.[reference:60]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Definition of Culture – Arnold defines culture as 'the study of perfection'.[reference:61] (2) The Three Classes – Arnold identifies the Barbarians (aristocracy), the Philistines (middle class), and the Populace (working class).
