(3) Sweetness and Light – culture represents 'sweetness and light'.[reference:62] (4) Anarchy – anarchy results from 'doing as one likes'.[reference:63] PYQ trends: frequently asked about the definition of culture; the three classes; the concept of 'sweetness and light'; compared with other social critiques.

works.title: Culture and Society, 1780-1950
works.shortDescription: Williams's foundational work of cultural studies, which traces the development of the concept of culture in the modern period.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Concept of Culture – culture as a social and political construct. (2) The Structure of Feeling – the lived experience of a community. (3) The Cultural Materialism – culture is a material practice. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the structure of feeling; cultural materialism; compared with other cultural theorists.
works.references: Edition: 1958 (London: Chatto & Windus). Criticism: Williams (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Daniel Deronda
works.shortDescription: Eliot's last novel, exploring Jewish identity and the Zionist movement through the character of Daniel Deronda.[reference:22]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Jewish Identity – the novel explores Jewish identity and the Zionist movement. (2) The Dual Plot – the novel interweaves the stories of Daniel Deronda and Gwendolen Harleth. (3) The Social Critique – the novel critiques Victorian society. PYQ trends: asked about the exploration of Jewish identity; the dual plot.

works.title: Das Kapital
works.shortDescription: Marx's foundational work of political economy, which analyzes the capitalist mode of production and introduces the concept of surplus value.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Critique of Capitalism – capitalism is based on the exploitation of labor. (2) Surplus Value – the value created by labor beyond the cost of labor. (3) The Commodity – the analysis of the commodity form. PYQ trends: frequently asked about surplus value; the commodity form; the critique of capitalism.

works.title: David Copperfield
works.shortDescription: Dickens's most autobiographical novel, following the life of David Copperfield from childhood to maturity. Dickens called it his 'favourite child'.[reference:1]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Autobiographical Elements – the novel draws on Dickens's own experiences. (2) Bildungsroman – the novel traces David's moral and social development. (3) The Characters – includes Uriah Heep, Mr. Micawber, and Betsey Trotwood. (4) The Theme of Innocence – the novel explores the loss and preservation of innocence. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the autobiographical elements; the characters; compared with other bildungsromans.

works.title: Death of a Naturalist
works.shortDescription: Heaney's first major collection, drawn from his childhood experiences and environments. It depicts a tough, predominantly rural life with remarkable precision.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Collection – Heaney's debut collection, which established his reputation. (2) The Rural Setting – the poems are set in the countryside of Northern Ireland. (3) The Title Poem – 'Death of a Naturalist' explores the loss of childhood innocence. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Heaney's early work; the rural setting; the title poem.

works.title: Death of a Salesman
works.shortDescription: Miller's most famous play, a tragedy about Willy Loman, a traveling salesman who is unable to accept the failure of his life and dreams.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The American Dream – the play critiques the American Dream. (2) The Tragic Hero – Willy Loman is a modern tragic hero. (3) The Structure – the play uses flashbacks to explore Willy's past. (4) The Pulitzer Prize – the play won the Pulitzer Prize in 1949. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the critique of the American Dream; the character of Willy Loman; the tragic hero; the Pulitzer Prize.

works.title: Deaths and Entrances
works.shortDescription: Thomas's most famous collection, containing some of his best-known poems[reference:67].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Collection – the volume contains Thomas's most celebrated poems. (2) The Themes – the poems explore life, death, and the cycle of existence. PYQ trends: asked about Thomas's major collection; the themes of life and death.

works.title: Decline and Fall
works.shortDescription: Waugh's first novel, a satirical comedy about a young man who is wrongly expelled from Oxford and enters a series of absurd adventures.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Satirical Comedy – the novel is a sharp satire of British society. (2) The Picaresque – the novel follows the picaresque tradition. PYQ trends: asked about Waugh's satirical comedies; the picaresque form.

works.title: Decolonising the Mind
works.shortDescription: Ngũgĩ's most important non-fiction work, arguing for the decolonization of the mind and the importance of writing in African languages.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Language Debate – Ngũgĩ argues that African writers should write in their native languages. (2) The Decolonization – the work is a call for the decolonization of the mind. (3) The Politics of Language – the work explores the political implications of language choice. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the language debate; the decolonization of the mind; compared with other postcolonial theorists.

works.title: Deirdre of the Sorrows
works.shortDescription: Synge's unfinished play, a poetic drama based on the Irish myth of Deirdre and the Sons of Usna.[reference:24][reference:25]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Mythological Subject – based on the Ulster Cycle of Irish mythology. (2) The Unfinished Play – Synge died before completing it. (3) The Poetic Language – the play is written in Synge's characteristic poetic prose. PYQ trends: asked about Synge's mythological plays; the unfinished status.

works.title: Dejection: An Ode
works.shortDescription: Coleridge's ode on the loss of his creative powers, written in a state of mental torment.[reference:16]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Ode Form – a Pindaric ode in irregular meter. (2) The Loss of Imagination – the poem laments Coleridge's loss of creative power. (3) The Epigraph – the poem opens with an epigraph from the ballad of Sir Patrick Spence.[reference:17] PYQ trends: asked about the theme of creative loss; compared with Wordsworth's 'Intimations Ode'.

works.title: Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion
works.shortDescription: Hume's posthumously published work, a philosophical dialogue on the nature of religion and the existence of God.[reference:39]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Design Argument – the work critiques the design argument for the existence of God. (2) The Dialogic Form – the work uses the dialogue form to explore different philosophical positions. PYQ trends: asked about Hume's Dialogues; the critique of natural religion.

works.title: Difficult Daughters
works.shortDescription: Kapur's first novel, set around the time of Partition. It explores a woman's journey through pre and post-Partition India as she navigates societal expectations and family pressures.[reference:85][reference:86]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Partition – the novel is set around the time of Partition.[reference:87] (2) The Woman's Journey – the novel explores a woman's journey.[reference:88] (3) The Commonwealth Writers' Prize – the novel won the Commonwealth Writers' Prize in 1999.[reference:89] PYQ trends: asked about the novel's publication year (1998); the Partition setting; and the Commonwealth Writers' Prize.
works.references: Edition: 1998 (London: Faber and Faber). Criticism: Kapur (2000).

works.title: Dipsychus
works.shortDescription: Clough's incomplete dramatic poem, which most fully expresses his doubts about the social and spiritual developments of his era[reference:66].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Dramatic Poem – the poem is a dramatic work in verse[reference:67]. (2) The Theme of Doubt – the poem expresses Clough's doubts about the Victorian era[reference:68]. (3) The Incomplete Work – the poem remains unfinished[reference:69]. PYQ trends: asked about the theme of doubt; the incomplete status.

works.title: Discipline and Punish
works.shortDescription: Foucault's study of the history of punishment, which introduces the concept of the 'panopticon' as a model of disciplinary power.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Panopticon – a model of disciplinary power that subjects individuals to surveillance. (2) The History of Punishment – the shift from physical punishment to discipline. (3) The Body – the body is the target of disciplinary power. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the panopticon; disciplinary power; the history of punishment.

works.title: Discourse on Colonialism
works.shortDescription: Césaire's foundational essay, which argues that colonialism is a system of dehumanization and is inextricably linked to European civilization. It is a key text of Négritude.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Critique of Colonialism – colonialism is a system of dehumanization. (2) Négritude – the movement that asserts the value of black culture. (3) The West Indian Context – Césaire's analysis of the Caribbean experience. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Négritude; the critique of colonialism; Césaire's role in the movement.

works.title: Disgrace
works.shortDescription: Coetzee's novel about David Lurie, a South African professor who is disgraced after a sexual relationship with a student. It explores themes of power, race, and redemption.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Post-Apartheid Setting – the novel is set in post-apartheid South Africa. (2) The Theme of Power – the novel explores the dynamics of power in the new South Africa. (3) The Redemption – the novel explores the possibility of redemption. (4) The Booker Prize – the novel won the Booker Prize in 1999. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the post-apartheid setting; the theme of power; the Booker Prize.

works.title: Do not go gentle into that good night
works.shortDescription: Thomas's most famous poem, a villanelle urging resistance against death, written for his dying father[reference:68].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Villanelle – the poem is a nineteen-line villanelle[reference:69]. (2) The Theme of Resistance – the poem urges the reader to resist death[reference:70]. (3) The Addressee – the poem is addressed to the poet's father, who was dying[reference:71]. (4) The Famous Lines – 'Do not go gentle into that good night, / Old age should burn and rave at close of day.' PYQ trends: frequently asked about the villanelle form; the theme of resistance; the poem's address to the father.

works.title: Doctor Faustus
works.shortDescription: Marlowe's most famous play, based on the legend of a scholar who sells his soul to the devil. It explores themes of ambition, knowledge, sin, and damnation[reference:10].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Source – based on the German Faustbuch (1587), translated into English as The History of the Damnable Life and Deserved Death of Doctor John Faustus. (2) Morality Play Framework – uses the dramatic framework of a morality play in its presentation of temptation, fall, and damnation[reference:11]. (3) The Tragic Hero – Faustus is an ambitious figure who strives beyond human limits[reference:12]. (4) The Seven Deadly Sins – the pageant of the Seven Deadly Sins is a key scene. (5) The Final Speech – 'O, I'll leap up to my God! Who pulls me down?' PYQ trends: frequently asked about the character of Faustus; the theme of knowledge and damnation; the influence of the morality play; the play's sources[reference:13].
works.references: Sources: German Faustbuch, English translation (1592). Editions: Norton Critical Edition (2nd ed., 2005). Criticism: Bevington (1988), Greenblatt (2001).

works.title: Don Juan
works.shortDescription: Byron's satirical epic, an unfinished poem in ottava rima. It is one of the longest and most ambitious poems in English.[reference:19]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Satirical Epic – the poem is a satire on contemporary society, politics, and literature. (2) The Byronic Hero Subverted – Don Juan is not a heroic figure but a passive and easily manipulated character. (3) The Isles of Greece – a famous song within the poem.[reference:20] (4) The Dedication – Byron dedicates the poem to Robert Southey, a rival poet. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the satire; the ottava rima form; compared with other Romantic epics.

works.title: Door into the Dark
works.shortDescription: Heaney's second collection, which points to his belief that poems emerge from what he called 'the buried life of the feelings'.[reference:13]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Second Collection – Heaney's follow-up to Death of a Naturalist. (2) The Buried Life – the poems explore the hidden depths of experience. (3) The Title – 'Door into the Dark' suggests a journey into the unknown. PYQ trends: asked about Heaney's second collection; the concept of the 'buried life'; the title's significance.

works.title: Dover Beach
works.shortDescription: Arnold's most famous poem, a meditation on the loss of faith in the modern world. It is often considered the emblematic poem of the Victorian crisis of faith.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Crisis of Faith – the poem expresses the Victorian loss of faith[reference:16]. (2) The Sea of Faith – the famous image of the withdrawing tide. (3) The Famous Line – 'And we are here as on a darkling plain'[reference:17]. (4) The Poetic Form – the poem is a dramatic lyric in irregular meter. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the theme of faith; the 'Sea of Faith' image; compared with other Victorian poems of doubt.

works.title: Down and Out in Paris and London
works.shortDescription: Orwell's non-fiction work about his experiences of poverty in Paris and London.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Non-Fiction – the work is a blend of memoir and social commentary. (2) The Theme of Poverty – Orwell explores the experience of poverty. PYQ trends: asked about Orwell's non-fiction; the theme of poverty.

works.title: Dream on Monkey Mountain
works.shortDescription: Walcott's play, which explores Caribbean identity and the postcolonial experience.[reference:35]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Play – a dramatic work exploring Caribbean identity. (2) The Postcolonial Theme – the play addresses the legacy of colonialism. PYQ trends: asked about Walcott's plays; the postcolonial theme.

works.title: Dubliners
works.shortDescription: A collection of 15 short stories depicting the lives of ordinary people in Dublin. The collection explores themes of paralysis, epiphany, and the Irish condition.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Paralysis – the stories depict characters who are trapped in their lives. (2) Epiphany – each story contains a moment of revelation or insight. (3) The Stories – includes 'The Dead', 'Araby', 'Eveline', and 'The Sisters'. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the theme of paralysis; the concept of epiphany; 'The Dead' as the culminating story.

works.title: Dulce et Decorum Est
works.shortDescription: Owen's most famous poem, written in response to pro-war poems and exposing the horrors of gas warfare[reference:34]. The title is from Horace: 'it is sweet and fitting to die for one's country'[reference:35].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Anti-War Theme – the poem is a fierce critique of the romantic glorification of war[reference:36]. (2) The Horrors of War – Owen describes the gas attack in graphic, brutal detail[reference:37]. (3) The Latin Title – the title is a bitter irony, exposing the lie of 'dulce et decorum est pro patria mori'[reference:38]. (4) The Address – the poem is addressed to 'Jessie Pope' and other pro-war poets[reference:39]. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the anti-war theme; the irony of the title; the graphic imagery.

works.title: Easter 1916
works.shortDescription: A poem reflecting on the Easter Rising, a pivotal event in Irish history. It explores the transformation of ordinary people into heroes and the nature of sacrifice.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Terrible Beauty – 'All changed, changed utterly: / A terrible beauty is born.' (2) The Rebels – Yeats names the leaders of the Rising. (3) The Theme of Sacrifice – the poem explores the meaning of sacrifice for a cause. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the phrase 'terrible beauty'; the historical context of the Easter Rising; Yeats's ambivalent attitude.
works.references: Edition: 1916 (in Michael Robartes and the Dancer). Criticism: Yeats (2000).

works.title: Eastward Ho!
works.shortDescription: A comedy written in collaboration with Ben Jonson and John Marston. The play satirizes the get-rich-quick mentality of Londoners and contains anti-Scottish jokes that landed the authors in prison[reference:61][reference:62].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Collaboration – one of the most famous collaborations in English drama[reference:63]. (2) Satire of Greed – the play satirizes the pursuit of wealth and social climbing. (3) The Anti-Scottish Jokes – the play contained jokes about Scots that offended King James I, leading to the imprisonment of Jonson and Chapman[reference:64][reference:65]. (4) The Play's Title – 'Eastward Ho!' was a cry used by Thames watermen to indicate a journey downstream. PYQ trends: asked about Jonson's collaborations; the play's political satire; the imprisonment of the authors[reference:66].
works.references: Editions: Norton Critical Edition. Criticism: Donaldson (2011), Jonson (1967).

works.title: Edward II
works.shortDescription: Marlowe's history play focusing on the weak king's downfall due to his favoritism and political failures. It is one of the earliest English plays to depict homosexual love[reference:15][reference:16].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Historical Play – based on the reign of King Edward II of England (1307-1327). (2) Homosexuality – the play openly depicts the relationship between Edward and his favorite Piers Gaveston. (3) Deposition and Murder – the play dramatizes the king's deposition and gruesome murder. (4) Influence on Shakespeare – influenced Shakespeare's history plays, particularly Richard II. PYQ trends: asked about Marlowe's history plays; the treatment of homosexuality; the play's influence on Shakespeare[reference:17].
works.references: Editions: Norton Critical Edition. Criticism: Cartelli (2002), Wilson (1969).

works.title: Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard
works.shortDescription: Gray's masterpiece, one of the most famous and best-loved poems in the English language. It is a meditative elegy on the lives of the humble and forgotten dead buried in a rural churchyard.[reference:5]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Elegy – the poem is a formal elegy, mourning the deaths of the rural poor. (2) The Graveyard School – the poem is a key example of the Graveyard School of poetry, which focused on mortality and melancholy. (3) The Universal Theme – the poem reflects on the universal human experience of death. (4) The Epitaph – the poem concludes with an epitaph for the poet himself. (5) The Pindaric Odes – Gray's other major works, The Progress of Poesy and The Bard, are Pindaric odes that mark a shift towards the sublime.[reference:6] PYQ trends: frequently asked about Gray's Elegy; the Graveyard School; the poem's structure and themes; compared with other elegies.

works.title: Elene
works.shortDescription: The longest of Cynewulf's works at 1,321 lines. It tells the story of Saint Helena, mother of Constantine, and her quest to find the True Cross in Jerusalem. The poem blends epic battle imagery with hagiographical narrative.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Runic Signature – contains Cynewulf's full runic acrostic (CYNEWULF) woven into the epilogue, a unique authorship claim. (2) Latin Source – adapted from the Acta Cyriaci, but Cynewulf expands it with Germanic heroic diction (battle, sea-voyage) to elevate a saint's life into epic grandeur. (3) The 'Exile' Motif – a thematic link across Cynewulf's works; Helena's journey is framed as a spiritual exile that leads to redemption. (4) Dialectal Evidence – the presence of rhymed passages helps scholars identify it as Anglian in origin. PYQ trends: asked specifically for the runic signature technique and compared with other signed poems (Juliana, Christ II, Fates).
works.references: MS: Vercelli Book (Biblioteca Capitolare, Vercelli, Italy – Codex CXVII). Editions: Krapp (1932), Gradon (1958).

works.title: Eloisa to Abelard
works.shortDescription: Pope's verse epistle, a dramatic monologue in which the medieval nun Eloisa expresses her conflicted love for Abelard.[reference:4]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Verse Epistle – the poem is written in the form of a letter. (2) Dramatic Monologue – the poem is a psychological exploration of Eloisa's conflicted emotions. (3) Theme of Conflict – the poem explores the conflict between love and religious duty. PYQ trends: asked about Pope's verse epistles; the dramatic monologue; the poem's psychological depth.

works.title: Emma
works.shortDescription: Austen's novel about the clever and self-assured Emma Woodhouse, who meddles in the love lives of others while remaining oblivious to her own feelings.[reference:5]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Heroine – Emma is one of Austen's most complex heroines, flawed but ultimately sympathetic. (2) The Theme of Self-Discovery – the novel traces Emma's journey towards self-awareness. (3) The Social World – the novel is set in the small village of Highbury. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the character of Emma; the theme of self-discovery; compared with other Austen heroines.

works.title: Encoding and Decoding in the Television Discourse
works.shortDescription: Hall's seminal essay, which argues that meaning in media is not simply transmitted but is encoded and decoded by audiences in different ways.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Encoding and Decoding – meaning is encoded by producers and decoded by audiences. (2) The Three Positions – dominant-hegemonic, negotiated, and oppositional readings. (3) The Active Audience – audiences are active participants in the creation of meaning. PYQ trends: frequently asked about encoding and decoding; the three positions; compared with other cultural studies theorists.

works.title: Endgame
works.shortDescription: Beckett's one-act play about four characters trapped in a room at the end of the world. It is a bleak and comic exploration of human dependency and decay.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The One-Act Play – the play is a single act. (2) The Characters – Hamm (the master), Clov (the servant), and Nagg and Nell (Hamm's parents). (3) The Theme of Dependency – Hamm and Clov are dependent on each other. (4) The Bleak Setting – the play is set in a bare room at the end of the world. PYQ trends: asked about the one-act form; the theme of dependency; the characters; compared with Waiting for Godot.

works.title: Endymion
works.shortDescription: Keats's narrative poem, subtitled 'A Poetic Romance'. It tells the story of the shepherd Endymion's love for the moon goddess Cynthia.[reference:35]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Narrative Poem – a long romantic narrative in four books. (2) The Famous Line – 'A thing of beauty is a joy for ever'.[reference:36] (3) The Mythological Subject – the poem draws on Greek mythology. (4) The Reception – the poem received mixed reviews. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Keats's Endymion; the famous line; the poem's sources.

works.title: Englands Heroicall Epistles
works.shortDescription: A series of pairs of letters exchanged between famous lovers in English history. One of Drayton's most original poems.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Epistolary Form – uses the letter form to explore historical relationships. (2) English Historical Figures – draws on figures from English history. (3) Originality – considered among Drayton's most original works. PYQ trends: asked about Drayton's original contributions; the epistolary form in poetry.

works.title: English, August: An Indian Story
works.shortDescription: Chatterjee's debut novel, a comic story about a young civil servant who is posted to a remote town in India.[reference:90][reference:91]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Comic Novel – the novel is known for its comic tone. (2) The Civil Service – the novel explores the life of a young civil servant. PYQ trends: asked about the novel's publication year (1988); and the comic style.
works.references: Edition: 1988 (London: Faber and Faber). Criticism: Chatterjee (2000).

works.title: Epithalamion
works.shortDescription: A marriage ode celebrating Spenser's own wedding to Elizabeth Boyle. It is one of the finest wedding poems in the English language.
works.analysis:
Sexual and Spiritual Union – celebrates both romantic and sexual love within the framework of Christian marriage.
# Structure
24 Stanza- 24 Hours
Each stanza decided in day and night
365 lines representing 365 days of the year

works.title: Erewhon
works.shortDescription: Butler's satirical utopian novel, whose title is 'Nowhere' backwards. It satirizes Victorian society and anticipates the collapse of the Victorian illusion of eternal progress.[reference:46][reference:47]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Satirical Utopia – the novel is a satire of Victorian society. (2) The Title – 'Erewhon' is 'Nowhere' backwards. (3) The Theme of Progress – the novel critiques the Victorian belief in progress. PYQ trends: asked about the satirical utopia; the title; the critique of Victorian society.

works.title: Essays in Criticism
works.shortDescription: Arnold's first collection of critical essays, including the famous 'The Function of Criticism at the Present Time'. It is a landmark in English literary criticism.[reference:55]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Function of Criticism – Arnold defines criticism as 'a disinterested endeavor to learn and propagate the best that is known and thought of in the world'.[reference:56][reference:57] (2) Disinterestedness – the essence of criticism is disinterestedness.[reference:58] (3) The 'Object as in Itself it Really Is' – Arnold famously argues for seeing 'the object as in itself it really is'.[reference:59] PYQ trends: frequently asked about the function of criticism; the concept of disinterestedness; compared with other critical works.

works.title: Essays of Elia
works.shortDescription: Lamb's most famous collection of essays, written under the pseudonym 'Elia'. They are known for their personal, humorous, and nostalgic tone.[reference:45]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Familiar Essay – Lamb is considered a master of the familiar essay. (2) The Pseudonym – Lamb wrote under the name 'Elia'.[reference:46] (3) The Personal Voice – the essays are characterized by their personal and reflective style. (4) Key Essays – includes 'The South-Sea House', 'New Year's Eve', and 'Christ's Hospital'.[reference:47] PYQ trends: frequently asked about Lamb's Essays of Elia; the familiar essay; the pseudonym Elia.

works.title: Euphues: The Anatomy of Wit
works.shortDescription: Lyly's breakthrough prose work, a didactic romance that established the euphuistic style. It tells the story of a young Athenian who learns the folly of love and the value of wisdom[reference:71][reference:72].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Euphuism – the ornate prose style characterized by alliteration, balanced antithetical phrases, classical allusions, and 'unnatural natural history'[reference:73]. (2) Immediate Fame – the work made Lyly the most fashionable English writer of the 1580s[reference:74]. (3) Didactic Romance – the work is a moral tale about the education of a young man. (4) The Sequel – Euphues and His England (1580) continued the story[reference:75]. PYQ trends: frequently asked about euphuism; the prose style; the influence on Elizabethan prose[reference:76][reference:77].
works.references: Editions: Norton Critical Edition. Criticism: Hunter (1962), Lyly (2002).

works.title: Evam Indrajit
works.shortDescription: Sircar's famous play, an absurdist drama that explores the existential crisis of a young Bengali intellectual named Indrajit.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Absurdist Drama – the play is a key example of absurdist theatre in India. (2) The Existential Crisis – the play explores the meaninglessness of modern life. (3) The Influence – the play is influenced by the Theatre of the Absurd. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the absurdist style; the existential crisis; the influence of Western theatre.
works.references: Edition: 1963 (Calcutta: Oxford University Press). Criticism: Sircar (2000), Dharwadker (2005).

works.title: Evelina
works.shortDescription: Burney's first novel, an epistolary novel about a young woman's entry into London society. It was published anonymously.[reference:36]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Epistolary Novel – the novel is written in the form of letters. (2) Social Satire – the novel satirizes the pretensions and foibles of London society.[reference:37] (3) Female Perspective – the novel offers a female perspective on the social world. (4) Influence on Austen – Jane Austen admired Burney's novels.[reference:38] PYQ trends: frequently asked about Burney's Evelina; the epistolary form; the social satire; compared with Austen's novels.

works.title: Every Man in His Humour
works.shortDescription: Jonson's first major success, a comedy based on the theory of humours. The play follows the fortunes of a group of characters whose actions are determined by their dominant personality traits[reference:28][reference:29].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Theory of Humours – based on the medieval/ Renaissance medical theory that personality is determined by the balance of four bodily fluids (blood, phlegm, choler, melancholy)[reference:30]. (2) Influence of Shakespeare – Shakespeare acted in the play and they became close friends[reference:31]. (3) The First Quarto – the first version (1598) was set in Italy; the Folio version (1616) is set in England. (4) Social Satire – the play satirizes the pretensions of characters who are ruled by their humours. PYQ trends: asked about Jonson's theory of humours; compared with Shakespeare's comedies; the play's sources[reference:32][reference:33].
works.references: Editions: Norton Critical Edition. Criticism: Donaldson (2011), Jonson (1967).

works.title: Fern Hill
works.shortDescription: A nostalgic poem about the poet's childhood on a farm in Wales[reference:72].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Theme of Childhood – the poem celebrates the innocence and wonder of childhood. (2) The Passage of Time – the poem reflects on the loss of childhood and the inevitability of aging. (3) The Welsh Setting – the poem is set in the Welsh countryside. PYQ trends: asked about the theme of childhood; the nostalgic tone; the Welsh setting.

works.title: Final Solutions
works.shortDescription: Dattani's play that explores the aftermath of communal violence in India and the persistence of communal prejudice.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Communal Theme – the play explores communal violence and prejudice. (2) The Structure – the play alternates between past and present. (3) The Guilt – the play explores the guilt of the older generation. PYQ trends: asked about the communal themes; the structure; the exploration of guilt.
works.references: Edition: 1993 (in Collected Plays). Criticism: Dattani (2000), Dharwadker (2005).

works.title: Finnegans Wake
works.shortDescription: Joyce's final novel, a complex and experimental work written in a unique, dream-like language. It follows the dreams of a Dublin publican, Humphrey Chimpden Earwicker.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Experimental Language – the novel is written in a multilingual, dream-like language. (2) The Circular Structure – the novel ends with the first sentence, creating a cycle. (3) The Title – refers to an Irish folk ballad. PYQ trends: asked about the experimental language; the circular structure; the dream-like narrative.

works.title: For Whom the Bell Tolls
works.shortDescription: Hemingway's novel about an American dynamiter fighting in the Spanish Civil War.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Spanish Civil War – the novel is set during the Spanish Civil War. (2) The Theme of Sacrifice – the novel explores the theme of sacrifice. (3) The Famous Line – 'For whom the bell tolls' from John Donne. PYQ trends: asked about the Spanish Civil War; the theme of sacrifice; the title's source.

works.title: Fors Clavigera
works.shortDescription: A series of letters addressed to the workmen and labourers of Great Britain, published from 1871 to 1884. It is a sprawling, personal, and often eccentric work reflecting Ruskin's social and political views.[reference:10]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Letters to Workmen – the work is addressed to the working class. (2) Social Critique – the letters offer a wide-ranging critique of Victorian society. (3) Eccentric Style – the work is known for its personal and often idiosyncratic style. PYQ trends: asked about Ruskin's later works; the social critique; compared with Carlyle's social criticism.

works.title: Four Quartets
works.shortDescription: Eliot's last major poem, a sequence of four meditations on time, history, and spiritual redemption. It is considered his greatest work alongside The Waste Land[reference:11].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Four Quartets – 'Burnt Norton,' 'East Coker,' 'The Dry Salvages,' and 'Little Gidding.' (2) Time – the poem explores the nature of time and eternity. (3) The Turning Point – 'In my beginning is my end' and 'In my end is my beginning.' PYQ trends: asked about the structure; the theme of time; the religious dimension.

works.title: Fra Lippo Lippi
works.shortDescription: Browning's dramatic monologue in which the Renaissance painter Fra Lippo Lippi defends his artistic vision.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Dramatic Monologue – the poem is a classic example of the dramatic monologue[reference:7]. (2) The Artist – the poem explores the nature of art and the role of the artist. (3) The Historical Context – the poem is based on the historical figure of Fra Filippo Lippi. PYQ trends: asked about the dramatic monologue; the theme of art; compared with other Browning monologues.

works.title: Fragmenta Aurea
works.shortDescription: Suckling's posthumous collection of poems, plays, and letters. It includes his famous 'Ballad upon a Wedding' and 'A Session of the Poets'. Suckling is known for his elegant, witty, and often cynical verse.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Cavalier Poet – Suckling is one of the leading Cavalier poets, known for his elegance and wit. (2) 'Ballad upon a Wedding' – a light-hearted and humorous poem celebrating a wedding. (3) 'A Session of the Poets' – a satirical poem in which the poets of the age are judged by Apollo. (4) Cribbage – Suckling is credited with inventing the game of cribbage. PYQ trends: asked about Suckling's Cavalier poetry; his wit and elegance; the 'Ballad upon a Wedding'.

works.title: Frankenstein
works.shortDescription: Mary Shelley's most famous novel, subtitled 'The Modern Prometheus'. It tells the story of Victor Frankenstein, a scientist who creates a living creature and is horrified by his creation.[reference:27][reference:28]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Gothic Novel – the novel uses Gothic conventions. (2) The Science Fiction Novel – often considered the first science fiction novel.[reference:29] (3) The Subtitle – 'The Modern Prometheus' references the Greek myth of Prometheus.[reference:30] (4) The Framed Narrative – the novel uses a framed narrative structure. (5) The Theme of Creation – the novel explores the dangers of unchecked ambition and scientific hubris. (6) The Preface – the preface was written by Percy Bysshe Shelley.[reference:31] PYQ trends: frequently asked about the Gothic and science fiction elements; the subtitle; the framed narrative; the theme of creation.

works.title: The History of Friedrich II of Prussia, Called Frederick the Great
works.shortDescription: Carlyle's six-volume biography of Frederick the Great, a monumental work of historical scholarship and one of his most ambitious projects.[reference:36][reference:37]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Monumental History – the work is a massive biography of Frederick the Great. (2) The Hero as King – the work exemplifies Carlyle's 'Great Man' theory. (3) Historical Scholarship – the work is a major work of historical scholarship. PYQ trends: asked about Carlyle's biographical works; the 'Great Man' theory in practice.

works.title: Futility
works.shortDescription: A poem about the futility of war, using the image of the sun to contrast the life-giving power of nature with the death-dealing power of war[reference:42].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Sun – the sun is portrayed as a life-giving force that cannot awaken the dead soldier. (2) The Theme of Futility – the poem questions the purpose of war. PYQ trends: asked about the symbolism of the sun; the theme of futility.

works.title: Gender Trouble
works.shortDescription: Butler's foundational work of gender theory, which argues that gender is not a natural identity but a performative act.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Gender Performativity – gender is a performative act. (2) The Critique of Identity – gender is not a stable identity. (3) The Subversive Power – performativity can be used subversively. PYQ trends: one of the most frequently asked texts in NET; gender performativity; the critique of identity; compared with other gender theories.
works.references: Edition: 1990 (New York: Routledge). Criticism: Butler (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Ghashiram Kotwal
works.shortDescription: Tendulkar's most famous play, a historical drama set in Pune during the Peshwa period. It is a powerful exploration of power, corruption, and social hypocrisy.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Historical Drama – the play is set in the Peshwa period. (2) The Political Theme – the play explores the nature of power and corruption. (3) The Musical Form – the play is written in the form of a musical. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the play; the historical setting; the political themes; the musical form.
works.references: Edition: 1972 (Delhi: Oxford University Press). Criticism: Tendulkar (2000), Dharwadker (2005).

works.title: Gitanjali
works.shortDescription: Tagore's most famous collection of poetry, which won him the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1913. The poems are deeply spiritual and devotional.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Nobel Prize – the collection won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1913. (2) The Spiritual Theme – the poems are deeply spiritual and devotional. (3) The Song Offerings – the title means 'song offerings'. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the Nobel Prize; the spiritual themes; the poems' devotional nature.
works.references: Edition: 1910 (Calcutta: Indian Society of Oriental Art). Criticism: Tagore (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Glengarry Glen Ross
works.shortDescription: Mamet's Pulitzer Prize-winning play about a group of desperate real estate salesmen. It is a powerful critique of capitalism and American business culture.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Critique of Capitalism – the play critiques the cutthroat nature of capitalism. (2) The Language – the play is famous for its distinctive, rhythmic, and profane dialogue (Mamet-speak). (3) The Salesmen – the characters are desperate men trying to close a deal. (4) The Pulitzer Prize – the play won the Pulitzer Prize in 1984. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the critique of capitalism; Mamet's distinctive dialogue; the Pulitzer Prize; compared with other American plays.

works.title: Goblin Market
works.shortDescription: Rossetti's most famous work, a narrative poem about two sisters who are tempted by goblin merchants[reference:25]. It is a rich allegory of temptation, sacrifice, and redemption.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Narrative Poem – the poem is a long narrative in irregular meter[reference:26]. (2) The Allegory – the poem is a rich allegory of temptation, sacrifice, and redemption[reference:27]. (3) The Symbolism – the goblin fruit represents temptation and desire[reference:28]. (4) The Feminist Reading – the poem is often read as a feminist work about sisterhood and resistance[reference:29]. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the allegory; the symbolism; the feminist reading; compared with other Pre-Raphaelite poems.

works.title: God's Grandeur
works.shortDescription: Hopkins's sonnet celebrating the grandeur of God in nature[reference:42].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Sonnet – the poem is a Petrarchan sonnet. (2) The Theme of Nature – the poem celebrates the presence of God in nature[reference:43]. (3) The Sprung Rhythm – the poem is written in Hopkins's sprung rhythm[reference:44]. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Hopkins's poetry; the sprung rhythm; the theme of nature; appeared in UGC NET / SET / PGTRB[reference:45].

works.title: Good-Bye to All That
works.shortDescription: Graves's autobiographical classic of World War I, a brutally honest account of his experiences in the trenches[reference:57].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Autobiography – a classic of war memoir literature. (2) The Brutal Honesty – the work is noted for its unflinching portrayal of the horrors of war. (3) The Title – the title reflects Graves's rejection of his past life. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Graves's war memoir; the brutal honesty; compared with other war memoirs.

works.title: Gorboduc
works.shortDescription: Also known as Ferrex and Porrex, this is the first English tragedy and the first English play to employ blank verse. Written jointly by Thomas Norton and Thomas Sackville and first acted in 1562.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First English Tragedy – a landmark in English drama. (2) First Blank Verse – the first English play to use blank verse (unrhymed iambic pentameter). (3) Senecan Model – follows the Senecan tragedy model with rhetorical speeches, choruses, and moralizing tone. (4) Dynastic Dispute – presents the rivalry between sons Ferrex and Porrex over succession, leading to fratricide and civil collapse. (5) Sidney's Praise – Philip Sidney, in his Defence of Poesie, praised its classical restraint and tragic dignity. PYQ trends: frequently asked as the first English tragedy and first blank verse drama; the Senecan influence; compared with later Elizabethan tragedies.
works.references: Edition: 1570 (as Ferrex and Porrex). Criticism: Linton (2003).

works.title: Great Expectations
works.shortDescription: Dickens's thirteenth novel, a bildungsroman following the orphan Pip's journey from humble beginnings to gentlemanly aspirations, exploring themes of class, ambition, and moral redemption.[reference:0]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Bildungsroman – the novel traces Pip's moral and social development. (2) The Great Expectations – Pip's expectations of wealth and social status are revealed as hollow. (3) The Theme of Class – the novel critiques the Victorian class system. (4) The Characters – includes Miss Havisham, Estella, and Abel Magwitch. (5) The Twist – the revelation that Magwitch is Pip's benefactor is a key turning point. (6) First-Person Narration – the novel is narrated by Pip in retrospect. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the bildungsroman form; the theme of class; Miss Havisham as a symbol; the novel's ending (original vs. revised).

works.title: Gulliver's Travels
works.shortDescription: Swift's masterpiece, a satirical fantasy in which Lemuel Gulliver travels to four fantastical lands. It is a savage indictment of human pride and corruption.[reference:22]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Satire – Swift uses the travel narrative to satirize human folly and corruption. (2) The Four Voyages – Lilliput (smallness), Brobdingnag (giants), Laputa (scientists), and the Houyhnhnms (horses). (3) The Yahoos – the Yahoos represent the worst aspects of humanity, while the Houyhnhnms represent reason. (4) The Irony – Swift's use of irony is masterful; the reader is often unsure of the narrator's reliability. (5) A Modest Proposal – Swift's other famous satirical work, a proposal to eat Irish babies.[reference:23] PYQ trends: frequently asked about Swift's Gulliver's Travels; the satirical targets; the Houyhnhnms and Yahoos; compared with other satires.

works.title: Guthlac B
works.shortDescription: A poem about the life of the Anglo-Saxon saint Guthlac. It is attributed to Cynewulf by some scholars due to similarities in style and vocabulary.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Attribution Debate – considered 'probably Cynewulfian' because its stylistic markers (diction, themes of spiritual warfare, exile) closely match his signed works, but the epilogue with the runic signature is lost because the manuscript's ending is damaged. (2) Thematic Parallel – like Juliana, it features intense spiritual combat with demons. (3) Exeter Book context – Guthlac A and Guthlac B are paired; only Guthlac B is debated. PYQ trends: asked as a case study in attribution methodology and stylistic analysis in OE studies.
works.references: MS: Exeter Book (Exeter Cathedral Library, MS 3501). Editions: Roberts (1979), Bjork (1998).

works.title: Hamlet
works.shortDescription: Shakespeare's most famous tragedy, believed to have been written around 1601. It tells the story of Prince Hamlet who seeks revenge against his uncle Claudius for the murder of his father, the King. The play is set in Denmark and explores themes of revenge, madness, mortality, and the nature of truth.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Source – based on the legend of Amleth in Saxo Grammaticus's Historia Danica (c. 1200)[reference:0]. (2) Revenge Tragedy – follows the conventions established by Thomas Kyd's The Spanish Tragedy but elevates the genre with psychological depth. (3) To be or not to be – the most quoted soliloquy in English literature. (4) Character Complexity – Hamlet is the most psychologically complex character in English drama; the 'Hamlet problem' of his delay in action is a major critical debate. (5) Theme of Appearance vs Reality – reflected in the play-within-a-play and the theme of spying. PYQ trends: one of the most frequently asked plays; the soliloquies; the character of Hamlet; the play's sources; the play's treatment of madness (real vs feigned).
works.references: Source: Saxo Grammaticus's Historia Danica. Editions: Arden Shakespeare (3rd ed., Thompson & Taylor, 2006). Criticism: Bradley (1904), Kermode (2000).

works.title: Hard Times
works.shortDescription: Dickens's novel critiquing utilitarianism and the dehumanizing effects of industrialization.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Critique of Utilitarianism – the novel attacks the philosophy of 'Facts, facts, facts'. (2) The Industrial Landscape – the novel is set in the fictional industrial town of Coketown. (3) The Characters – includes Thomas Gradgrind, Josiah Bounderby, and Stephen Blackpool. PYQ trends: asked about the critique of utilitarianism; the industrial setting; the characters.

works.title: Hayavadana
works.shortDescription: Karnad's most famous play, based on a story from the Kathasaritsagara. It explores themes of identity, perfection, and the nature of love.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Mythological Source – the play is based on a story from the Kathasaritsagara. (2) The Themes – explores themes of identity, perfection, and the nature of love. (3) The Structure – the play uses a play-within-a-play structure. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the play; the mythological source; the themes of identity and love; the play-within-a-play structure.
works.references: Edition: 1971 (Delhi: Oxford University Press). Criticism: Karnad (2000), Dharwadker (2005).

works.title: Heart of Darkness
works.shortDescription: Conrad's novella about Charles Marlow's journey up the Congo River to meet Kurtz, an ivory trader who has become a tyrant. It is a profound exploration of colonialism and the darkness of the human soul.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Frame Narrative – the story is narrated by Marlow to a group of listeners. (2) The Theme of Colonialism – the novel is a critique of European colonialism. (3) The Darkness – the 'heart of darkness' represents both the Congo and the human soul. (4) The Famous Line – 'The horror! The horror!' PYQ trends: frequently asked about the frame narrative; the critique of colonialism; the symbolism of darkness.
works.references: Edition: 1899 (in Blackwood's Magazine), 1902 (book). Criticism: Conrad (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: The History of Henry Esmond
works.shortDescription: Thackeray's historical novel set in the early 18th century, exploring the life of Henry Esmond, a soldier and gentleman.[reference:34][reference:35]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Historical Novel – the novel is set in the early 18th century. (2) The First-Person Narration – the novel is narrated by Henry Esmond. (3) The Theme of Identity – the novel explores questions of identity and belonging. PYQ trends: asked about the historical novel; the first-person narration.

works.title: Hesperides
works.shortDescription: Herrick's major collection of lyric poetry, containing approximately 1,130 secular poems. It was published together with his religious poems, Noble Numbers, in 1648. The collection includes his most famous poem, 'To the Virgins, to Make Much of Time'.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Carpe Diem Theme – Herrick is famous for his celebration of the fleeting nature of life and love, best exemplified in 'Gather ye rosebuds while ye may'. (2) Cavalier Poet – Herrick is one of the leading Cavalier poets, known for his light-hearted, lyrical, and often sensual verse. (3) Tribe of Ben – Herrick was influenced by Ben Jonson and was part of the 'Tribe of Ben', a group of poets who admired Jonson's classical style. (4) Secular and Sacred – the collection includes both secular love poems and religious poems (Noble Numbers). PYQ trends: asked about Herrick's carpe diem theme; the Cavalier poets; the influence of Ben Jonson.

works.title: High Windows
works.shortDescription: Larkin's last major collection, which includes some of his most provocative poems.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Last Major Collection – Larkin's final major work. (2) The Themes – explores themes of sex, death, and the loss of faith. (3) The Provocative Poems – includes poems like 'High Windows' and 'This Be the Verse'. PYQ trends: asked about Larkin's later work; the provocative themes.

works.title: Holy Sonnets
works.shortDescription: A collection of nineteen sonnets dealing with themes of death, divine judgment, and the poet's relationship with God. The most famous is 'Batter my heart, three-personed God'.[reference:18]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Religious Poetry – Donne's devotional poetry explores his intense and often conflicted relationship with God. (2) Dramatic Address – the sonnets often address God directly in a dramatic, colloquial style. (3) Paradox and Contradiction – Donne uses paradox to express the complexities of faith. (4) The Sonnet Form – Donne adapts the Petrarchan sonnet form to religious themes. PYQ trends: asked about Donne's religious poetry; individual sonnets like 'Batter my heart'; the metaphysical style in devotional poetry.

works.title: Howards End
works.shortDescription: Forster's novel about the social and cultural conflicts of Edwardian England, symbolized by the house Howards End.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Social Novel – the novel critiques the social divisions of Edwardian England. (2) The Symbolism – Howards End represents the ideal of English culture. (3) The Famous Line – 'Only connect...' PYQ trends: asked about the social critique; the symbolism of the house; the famous line.

works.title: Howl
works.shortDescription: Ginsberg's most famous poem, a free verse work that became a manifesto of the Beat Generation. The famous opening line is: 'I saw the best minds of my generation destroyed by madness...'
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Beat Generation – the poem is a key text of the Beat movement. (2) The Obscenity Trial – the poem was the subject of a major obscenity trial in 1957. (3) The Themes – explores madness, sexuality, drug use, and capitalism. (4) The Whitman Influence – the poem is inspired by Walt Whitman. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Howl; the Beat Generation; the obscenity trial; the free verse form.

works.title: Hudibras
works.shortDescription: Butler's satirical poem, published in three parts (1663, 1664, 1678). It is a mock-heroic satire on the Puritans and the English Civil War, following the adventures of the Presbyterian knight Sir Hudibras.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Mock-Heroic Satire – a brilliant satire on the Puritans, using the mock-heroic style. (2) Character of Hudibras – Sir Hudibras is a pompous and hypocritical Presbyterian knight. (3) Verse Form – the poem is written in octosyllabic couplets. (4) Political and Religious Satire – the poem satirizes the excesses of Puritanism and the Civil War. (5) Immediate Success – the poem was an immediate success and made Butler famous. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Butler's Hudibras; the mock-heroic satire; the satire on the Puritans; compared with other satirical works.
works.references: Edition: 1663 (Part I), 1664 (Part II), 1678 (Part III). Criticism: Butler (2000), Weidhorn (2002).

works.title: Hugh Selwyn Mauberley
works.shortDescription: Pound's first major work, a long poem that serves as a 'portrait' of British literary culture and a critique of the artistic scene[reference:15]. Pound referred to it as an attempt 'to condense a [Henry] James novel'[reference:16].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Quatrain Form – Pound uses traditional quatrains in parts 1-3, discarding free verse[reference:17]. (2) The Title Character – Mauberley represents the artist who fails to find a receptive audience[reference:18]. (3) The Critique – the poem critiques the commercialization of art and the decline of culture. PYQ trends: asked about Pound's major works; the critique of British literary culture; the use of traditional form.

works.title: Humphry Clinker
works.shortDescription: Smollett's last novel, an epistolary novel about the travels of a Welsh family through England and Scotland.[reference:35]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Epistolary Novel – the novel is written in the form of letters from different characters. (2) Multiple Perspectives – the letters offer different perspectives on the journey. (3) Satire and Sentiment – the novel combines satire with sentiment. PYQ trends: asked about Smollett's later works; the epistolary form.

works.title: Hyperion
works.shortDescription: Keats's unfinished epic poem, based on the fall of the Titans in Greek mythology.[reference:37]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Epic Fragment – the poem remains unfinished. (2) The Mythological Subject – the poem draws on Greek mythology. (3) The Miltonic Style – the poem is written in blank verse and shows the influence of Milton. PYQ trends: asked about Keats's Hyperion; the Miltonic influence; the unfinished status.

works.title: I, Claudius
works.shortDescription: Graves's most famous novel, a historical fiction narrated by the Roman emperor Claudius[reference:58].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Historical Novel – the novel is a work of historical fiction. (2) The Narrator – the novel is narrated by Claudius, who presents himself as a stammering fool who survives the intrigues of the imperial court. (3) The Sequel – Claudius the God (1943) continues the story[reference:59]. PYQ trends: asked about Graves's novels; the historical fiction; the narrator.

works.title: Ibis Trilogy
works.shortDescription: Non-linear narrative historical fiction of first anglo-chinese opium war.
works.analysis:
**In Sea of Poppies(2008)**, the Ibis sets off from Calcutta carrying indentured servants and convicts destined for Mauritius, but runs into a major storm and faces a mutiny.
**River of Smoke(2011)** is set in China — particularly around the Thirteen Factories where tensions between local authorities and international traders begin to escalate. two more ships caught in the same storm as the Ibis — the Anahita, a vessel carrying opium to Canton, and the Redruth, which is on a botanical expedition.
**Flood of Fire(2015)** culminates in the outbreak of the First Opium War and its impact across the Indian Ocean region, leading to the foundation of Hong Kong.
# Themes
**Colonialism and globalisation**
**Water**- "I’m from West Bengal. Water, rivers etc., are an important aspect of life there, even in Bengali art and cinema. Personally, water is an essential part of my imaginative landscape."
**Language**-ses different languages in the series to touch on themes of diaspora, globalisation

works.title: Ideology and Ideological State Apparatuses
works.shortDescription: Althusser's essay, which introduces his theory of ideology and the concept of interpellation.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Ideological State Apparatuses – institutions that reproduce ideology. (2) Interpellation – the process by which individuals are hailed as subjects. (3) The Function of Ideology – ideology ensures the reproduction of the social order. PYQ trends: one of the most frequently asked concepts in NET; interpellation; ideological state apparatuses.

works.title: Idylls of the King
works.shortDescription: Tennyson's cycle of twelve narrative poems on the Arthurian legend, published between 1859 and 1885. It is a somber vision of an idealistic community in decay.[reference:0]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Arthurian Legend – based largely on Sir Thomas Malory's Le Morte Darthur[reference:1]. (2) The Decline of the Round Table – the poem attributes the decline to Guinevere's betrayal[reference:2]. (3) The Idylls – includes 'Enid', 'Vivien', 'Elaine', and 'Guinevere' published in 1859[reference:3]. (4) The Somber Vision – the poem offers a tragic vision of an ideal community in decay[reference:4]. PYQ trends: asked about the Arthurian legend; the poem's structure; compared with Malory's Le Morte Darthur.

works.title: Illiterate Heart
works.shortDescription: Alexander's collection of poems, which explores themes of memory, loss, love, and the experience of being a woman and a poet.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Feminist Theme – the collection explores the experience of women. (2) The Theme of Memory – the poems explore the nature of memory. PYQ trends: asked about the feminist themes; the themes of memory and loss.
works.references: Edition: 2002 (Evanston: Northwestern University Press). Criticism: Alexander (2000).

works.title: In a Station of the Metro
works.shortDescription: Pound's most famous Imagist poem, a two-line masterpiece that captures a fleeting moment of beauty[reference:19].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Imagism – the poem is the quintessential Imagist poem. (2) The Superposition – Pound presents two images without comment: the faces in the crowd and the petals on a wet black bough. (3) The Economy of Language – the poem uses language with extreme economy. PYQ trends: frequently asked as the exemplar of Imagist poetry[reference:20]; the technique of superposition; the economy of language.

works.title: In Memoriam A.H.H.
works.shortDescription: Tennyson's great elegy for his friend Arthur Henry Hallam, composed over seventeen years. It is one of the greatest poems of the Victorian era, exploring grief, doubt, and faith.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Elegy – one of the greatest elegies in English literature, mourning the death of a friend. (2) The Prologue – a powerful statement of faith: 'Strong Son of God, immortal Love'. (3) The Sections – the poem is divided into 133 cantos, tracing the poet's journey through grief. (4) The Famous Lines – includes ' 'Tis better to have loved and lost / Than never to have loved at all'. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the elegy form; the theme of doubt and faith; the prologue; compared with other elegies.

works.title: In the Castle of My Skin
works.shortDescription: Lamming's semi-autobiographical novel about growing up in Barbados and the experience of colonialism and cultural displacement.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Colonial Experience – the novel explores life in colonial Barbados. (2) The Autobiographical Element – the novel draws on Lamming's own childhood. (3) The Coming-of-Age – the novel is a bildungsroman. PYQ trends: asked about the colonial experience; the autobiographical elements; the bildungsroman.
works.references: Edition: 1953 (London: Michael Joseph). Criticism: Lamming (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: In the Shadow of the Glen
works.shortDescription: Synge's first performed play, a one-act comedy about a woman whose husband pretends to be dead to test her loyalty.[reference:21]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Performed Play – Synge's debut at the Abbey Theatre. (2) The Folk Tale – the play is based on an Irish folk tale. (3) The Theme of Marriage – the play explores the unhappiness of a rural marriage. PYQ trends: asked about Synge's early works; compared with Riders to the Sea.

works.title: Indian Writing in English
works.shortDescription: Iyengar's foundational survey of Indian English literature, one of the earliest comprehensive studies of the field.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Survey – an early comprehensive survey of Indian English literature. (2) The Pioneering Work – one of the first studies of the field. (3) The Chronology – provides a historical framework. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Iyengar's survey; the pioneering work; the chronological framework.
works.references: Edition: 1962 (New Delhi: Asia Publishing House). Criticism: Iyengar (2000).

works.title: Intentions
works.shortDescription: Wilde's collection of four dialogues on aesthetics, including 'The Decay of Lying', 'Pen, Pencil and Poison', 'The Critic as Artist', and 'The Truth of Masks'.[reference:65]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) 'The Decay of Lying' – Wilde argues that 'As a method, realism is a complete failure'.[reference:66] (2) 'The Critic as Artist' – Wilde argues that criticism is a creative endeavor, equal to art.[reference:67] (3) Aesthetic Philosophy – the collection contains the most extensive statements of Wilde's aesthetic philosophy.[reference:68] PYQ trends: frequently asked about Wilde's aesthetic philosophy; 'The Decay of Lying'; 'The Critic as Artist'; the dialogue form.

works.title: Is There a Text in This Class?
works.shortDescription: Fish's collection of essays that argues for the centrality of the reader's interpretive community in the construction of meaning.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Interpretive Communities – meaning is created by interpretive communities. (2) The Reader's Role – the reader is central to the construction of meaning. (3) The Text – the text is not a fixed object. PYQ trends: frequently asked about interpretive communities; the reader's role; compared with other reader-response theorists.
works.references: Edition: 1980 (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press). Criticism: Fish (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Ivanhoe
works.shortDescription: Scott's most famous novel, set in 12th-century England during the reign of Richard I. It tells the story of the Saxon knight Wilfred of Ivanhoe.[reference:43]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Historical Novel – the novel is set in medieval England. (2) The Norman-Saxon Conflict – the novel explores the tensions between the Norman rulers and the Saxon subjects. (3) The Characters – includes Robin Hood, King Richard, and the knight Templar. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Scott's Ivanhoe; the historical novel; the characters.

works.title: Jane Eyre
works.shortDescription: Charlotte Brontë's most famous novel, a bildungsroman about the orphan Jane Eyre who overcomes hardship to find love and independence.[reference:10][reference:11]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Bildungsroman – the novel traces Jane's development from childhood to adulthood. (2) The Feminist Theme – the novel celebrates female independence and self-respect. (3) The Famous Line – 'Reader, I married him.' (4) The Characters – includes Mr. Rochester, Bertha Mason, and St. John Rivers. (5) The Gothic Elements – the novel uses Gothic conventions (the madwoman in the attic). (6) The Pseudonym – Brontë published under the pseudonym Currer Bell.[reference:12] PYQ trends: frequently asked about the bildungsroman; the feminist themes; the famous line; the Gothic elements.

works.title: Jejuri
works.shortDescription: Kolatkar's most famous collection, a cycle of poems about a visit to the temple town of Jejuri. The poems explore faith, doubt, and the meaning of religious experience.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Religious Theme – the collection explores the experience of visiting a temple town. (2) The Irony – the poems combine reverence with irony and doubt. (3) The Commonwealth Poetry Prize – the collection won the Commonwealth Poetry Prize in 1977. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the collection; the religious themes; the irony; the Commonwealth Poetry Prize.
works.references: Edition: 1976 (Bombay: Clearing House). Criticism: Kolatkar (2000), King (1987).

works.title: Jerusalem
works.shortDescription: Blake's epic prophetic poem, one of his most complex and ambitious works. It is also the title of his famous hymn, 'And did those feet in ancient time'.[reference:41]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Prophetic Book – Jerusalem is one of Blake's major prophetic books. (2) The Myth of Albion – the poem explores the myth of Albion, representing England. (3) The Hymn – 'And did those feet in ancient time' is one of the most famous hymns in English. PYQ trends: asked about Blake's prophetic books; the Jerusalem hymn.

works.title: Joseph Andrews
works.shortDescription: Fielding's first novel, a parody of Pamela but also a work of great originality. It tells the story of Joseph Andrews, a servant who resists the advances of his mistress.[reference:30]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Parody of Pamela – Joseph is the brother of Pamela and resists the advances of his mistress, Lady Booby. (2) Comic Epic in Prose – Fielding called his work a 'comic epic in prose'. (3) The Picaresque – the novel follows the picaresque tradition, with Joseph and his companion Parson Adams traveling through the countryside. (4) The Character of Parson Adams – one of the great comic characters in English literature. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Fielding's Joseph Andrews; the parody of Pamela; the character of Parson Adams; the 'comic epic in prose'.

works.title: Jude the Obscure
works.shortDescription: Hardy's last novel, a bleak tragedy about Jude Fawley, a working-class man who aspires to a university education and is destroyed by the forces of social convention.[reference:24]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Tragedy – the novel is a dark tragedy of frustrated ambition. (2) The Critique of Marriage – the novel critiques the institution of marriage. (3) The Theme of Class – the novel explores the barriers of social class. PYQ trends: asked about the tragedy; the critique of marriage; the theme of class.

works.title: Juliana
works.shortDescription: A 731-line retelling of the Latin prose life of St. Juliana. A maiden who refuses the suit of a Roman prefect because of her Christian faith, she endures numerous torments.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Dramatic Dialogue – the poem features an extended, sophisticated verbal duel between Juliana and a demon sent to torment her; the force of her rhetoric defeats him – a rare psychological depth in OE poetry. (2) Female Agency – Juliana is one of the few strong female protagonists in OE literature; her unwavering resolve defies pagan authority. (3) Runic Signature – the runes (CYNEWULF) appear in the epilogue, where he reflects on the Last Judgment and asks for prayers. (4) Manuscript Context – preserved uniquely in the Exeter Book. PYQ trends: often asked about the representation of women in Old English literature, and the role of the demonic debate.
works.references: MS: Exeter Book (Exeter Cathedral Library, MS 3501). Editions: Krapp & Dobbie (1936), Woolf (1955).

works.title: July's People
works.shortDescription: Gordimer's novel set in a near-future South Africa where the apartheid system has collapsed. It follows a white family who are taken in by their former servant.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Post-Apartheid Setting – the novel imagines a future after the collapse of apartheid. (2) The Role Reversal – the white family is dependent on their black servant. (3) The Theme of Power – the novel explores the shifting power dynamics between races. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the post-apartheid setting; the role reversal; the theme of power.
works.references: Edition: 1981 (London: Jonathan Cape). Criticism: Gordimer (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Juno and the Paycock
works.shortDescription: O'Casey's best-known play, a tragicomedy set in Dublin during the Irish Civil War. It follows the Boyle family, who believe they have inherited money and spend it on credit.[reference:26][reference:27]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Irish Civil War – the play is set against the backdrop of the Civil War. (2) The Paycock – the Captain is a braggart and drinker who lives in a fantasy world. (3) Juno – the mother is the moral center of the play. (4) The Tragicomedy – the play blends comedy and tragedy. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the Civil War setting; the character of the Captain; the tragicomic form.

works.title: Kaddish
works.shortDescription: Ginsberg's long poem of mourning for his mother, Naomi Ginsberg. It is one of his most powerful and personal works.[reference:9]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Elegy – the poem is an elegy for Ginsberg's mother. (2) The Personal Element – the poem draws on Ginsberg's own life. (3) The Kaddish – the title refers to the Jewish prayer for the dead. PYQ trends: asked about Ginsberg's Kaddish; the elegy form; the personal element.
works.references: Edition: 1961 (in Kaddish and Other Poems). Criticism: Ginsberg (2000).

works.title: Kanthapura
works.shortDescription: Rao's first novel, considered one of the foundational texts of Indian English literature. It tells the story of how the independence movement becomes a tragic reality in a tiny and secluded village in South India.[reference:26][reference:27][reference:28]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Independence Movement – the novel depicts the impact of the independence movement on a rural village.[reference:29] (2) The Narrative Style – the novel uses a traditional Indian storytelling style. (3) The Publication Year – a key fact for UGC NET chronology.[reference:30][reference:31] PYQ trends: asked about the novel's publication year (1938); the theme of nationalism; and its status as a foundational text.[reference:32][reference:33]
works.references: Edition: 1938 (London: George Allen & Unwin). Criticism: Rao (2000), Narayan (1983).

works.title: King Lear
works.shortDescription: Shakespeare's tragedy of filial ingratitude and madness. The play tells the story of King Lear who divides his kingdom among his three daughters based on their professions of love, disowning the honest Cordelia and suffering the consequences.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Source – based on the legend of Leir of Britain in Geoffrey of Monmouth's Historia Regum Britanniae. (2) Theme of Madness – Lear's descent into madness is paralleled by the subplot of Gloucester and his sons. (3) The Storm Scene – Lear on the heath is a powerful symbol of human vulnerability. (4) The Fool – a crucial character who speaks truth to power. (5) Two Versions – the play exists in two versions: the Quarto (1608) and the Folio (1623), with significant differences. PYQ trends: asked about the theme of madness; the character of the Fool; the storm scene; the play's sources; the difference between the Quarto and Folio versions.
works.references: Sources: Geoffrey of Monmouth, Holinshed's Chronicles. Editions: Arden Shakespeare (3rd ed., Foakes, 1997). Criticism: Bradley (1904), Kott (1964).

works.title: Kubla Khan
works.shortDescription: Coleridge's fragmentary visionary poem, subtitled 'A Vision in a Dream'. It was composed after an opium-induced dream and published in 1816.[reference:12]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Dream Vision – the poem is presented as a fragment of a dream. (2) The 'Person from Porlock' – the famous story of the interruption that prevented its completion. (3) The Imagery – exotic and sensual images of Xanadu. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the poem's composition; the dream vision; the exotic imagery.

works.title: La Belle Dame sans Merci
works.shortDescription: Keats's ballad about a knight who is seduced and abandoned by a fairy woman.[reference:39]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Ballad Form – the poem follows the traditional ballad form. (2) The Supernatural – the poem uses supernatural elements. (3) The Theme of Love – the poem explores the dangers of love and illusion. PYQ trends: asked about Keats's La Belle Dame sans Merci; the ballad form; the theme of love.

works.title: Lady Chatterley's Lover
works.shortDescription: Lawrence's most controversial novel, about the love affair between Constance Chatterley, an upper-class woman, and Oliver Mellors, her gamekeeper.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Controversy – the novel was banned for obscenity and was the subject of a famous obscenity trial in 1960. (2) The Theme of Sexuality – the novel celebrates physical love. (3) The Class Conflict – the novel explores the class divide. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the obscenity trial; the theme of sexuality; the class conflict.

works.title: Lady Lazarus
works.shortDescription: One of Plath's most famous poems, a dramatic monologue in which the speaker compares herself to Lazarus, rising from the dead.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Lazarus Figure – the speaker is a female Lazarus who has survived multiple suicide attempts. (2) The Holocaust Imagery – the poem uses imagery from the Holocaust. (3) The Confessional Style – the poem is a key example of confessional poetry. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Lady Lazarus; the Holocaust imagery; the confessional style.

works.title: Lady Windermere's Fan
works.shortDescription: Wilde's first major success as a playwright, a social comedy about a woman who discovers her husband's past and considers leaving him. The play features the famous line: 'We are all in the gutter, but some of us are looking at the stars.'[reference:10][reference:11]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Well-Made Play – the play follows the French formula with a carefully constructed plot. (2) The Fan – the fan is a key symbol of Lady Windermere's marital fidelity. (3) The 'Gutter' Line – one of Wilde's most famous epigrams. PYQ trends: asked about the well-made play; the use of the fan as a symbol; compared with other Wilde comedies.

works.title: Land's End
works.shortDescription: Jussawalla's first collection of poetry, which was part of the movement to modernize Indian poetry in English.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Modernist Movement – the collection was part of the movement to modernize Indian poetry in English. (2) The Themes – explores themes of alienation, identity, and the urban experience. PYQ trends: asked about Jussawalla's contribution to Indian English poetry; the modernist movement.
works.references: Edition: 1962 (London: George Allen & Unwin). Criticism: Jussawalla (2000).

works.title: Le Morte d'Arthur
works.shortDescription: A 15th-century Middle English prose compilation and reworking by Sir Thomas Malory of tales about King Arthur, Guinevere, Lancelot, Merlin, and the Knights of the Round Table. It is the definitive English prose rendering of the Arthurian legend.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Compilation – Malory put together, rearranged, interpreted, and modified material from various French (Old French romances) and English sources. (2) Publication – completed around 1469–1470 and first published in a printed edition in 1485 by William Caxton, who divided it into 21 books. (3) Winchester Manuscript – discovered in 1934 at Winchester College; considered closer to Malory's original and differs from Caxton's edition. (4) Written in Prison – apparently written in prison at the end of the medieval English era. PYQ trends: asked about Malory's sources, the Arthurian legend, Caxton's role in publishing, and the differences between the Caxton and Winchester versions.
works.references: MS: Winchester Manuscript (c. 1470, BL Add. 59678). First Edition: Caxton (1485). Editions: Vinaver (1947), Field (2013). Criticism: Benson (1976), McCarthy (1991).

works.title: Leaves of Grass
works.shortDescription: Whitman's monumental collection of poetry, celebrating the self, the body, and the American spirit. He spent his life revising and expanding it.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Free Verse – the collection is written in free verse. (2) The American Spirit – the poems celebrate American democracy and nature. (3) The Self – the poet celebrates the self and the body. (4) The Revision – Whitman revised the collection throughout his life. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the free verse form; the celebration of the self; the American spirit; the revisions.

works.title: Light in August
works.shortDescription: Faulkner's novel about a pregnant woman and the two men who intersect with her life, exploring themes of race, identity, and redemption.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Theme of Race – the novel explores racial identity. (2) The Theme of Redemption – the novel explores the possibility of redemption. PYQ trends: asked about the theme of race; the theme of redemption.

works.title: Literary Theory: An Introduction
works.shortDescription: Eagleton's accessible introduction to literary theory, which argues that theory is a political and ideological practice.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Introduction – an accessible overview of literary theory. (2) The Political Dimension – theory is a political practice. (3) The Historical Context – theory is shaped by historical context. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Eagleton's introduction; the political dimension of theory; compared with other introductions.

works.title: Lives of Girls and Women
works.shortDescription: A collection of interconnected short stories by Alice Munro, forming a novel-like narrative. It explores the lives of girls and women in a small Canadian town.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Short Story Cycle – the book is a collection of interconnected stories. (2) The Small-Town Setting – the book is set in a small Canadian town. (3) The Female Experience – the book explores the lives of girls and women. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the short story cycle; the small-town setting; the exploration of female experience.
works.references: Edition: 1971 (Toronto: McGraw-Hill Ryerson). Criticism: Munro (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Lives of the Poets
