works.shortDescription: Johnson's last major work, a series of biographical and critical essays on English poets from the 17th and 18th centuries.[reference:32]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Literary Biography – Johnson's Lives are a landmark in literary biography and criticism. (2) The Critical Judgments – Johnson's judgments on poets like Milton, Pope, and Dryden are famous and influential. (3) The Prefaces – the work includes the famous preface to Shakespeare. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Johnson's Lives of the Poets; the critical judgments; compared with other literary biographies.

works.title: Lolita
works.shortDescription: Nabokov's most famous novel, a controversial work about Humbert Humbert, a middle-aged professor who becomes obsessed with a 12-year-old girl.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Unreliable Narrator – Humbert Humbert is a classic unreliable narrator. (2) The Theme of Obsession – the novel explores obsessive love. (3) The Prose Style – Nabokov's prose is ornate and allusive. (4) The Controversy – the novel was controversial for its subject matter. (5) The Famous Opening – 'Lolita, light of my life, fire of my loins.' PYQ trends: frequently asked about the unreliable narrator; the theme of obsession; the prose style; the famous opening.
works.references: Edition: 1955 (Paris, Olympia Press). Criticism: Nabokov (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Look Back in Anger
works.shortDescription: Osborne's play that revolutionized English theatre and defined the 'Angry Young Men' movement. It features Jimmy Porter, a working-class intellectual who rages against the establishment.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Angry Young Men – the play is the defining work of the Angry Young Men movement. (2) The Character of Jimmy – Jimmy Porter is the archetypal Angry Young Man. (3) The Setting – the play is set in a squalid attic flat. (4) The Social Critique – the play critiques the British class system and the establishment. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the Angry Young Men; the character of Jimmy Porter; the play's social critique.

works.title: Lord Jim
works.shortDescription: Conrad's novel about Jim, a young seaman who abandons his ship and spends his life trying to redeem his honor.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Theme of Honor – the novel explores the meaning of honor and redemption. (2) The Multiple Narrators – the story is told through multiple perspectives. (3) The Setting – the novel is set in the Malay Archipelago. PYQ trends: asked about the theme of honor; the multiple narrators; compared with Heart of Darkness.

works.title: Love for Love
works.shortDescription: Congreve's comedy about a spendthrift gentleman who tries to secure his inheritance by pretending to be mad. It is one of his most popular and enduring plays.[reference:2]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Comic Plot – Valentine's feigned madness allows him to outwit his father and secure his fortune. (2) Characters – the play features memorable characters including the witty servant Jeremy and the pedantic schoolmaster. (3) The Contract Scene – a famous scene in which Valentine draws up a 'bond' to secure his love. PYQ trends: asked about Congreve's comedies; compared with The Way of the World.

works.title: Lucasta
works.shortDescription: Lovelace's major collection of poetry, published in 1649. It includes his most famous poems, 'To Althea, from Prison' and 'To Lucasta, Going to the Wars', which reflect his experiences as a Cavalier and Royalist during the English Civil War.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Cavalier Poetry – Lovelace is one of the leading Cavalier poets, known for his elegant and refined verse. (2) Political Context – the poems reflect Lovelace's Royalist sympathies and his experiences of imprisonment and war. (3) 'To Althea, from Prison' – a famous poem celebrating the freedom of the mind even in physical captivity ('Stone walls do not a prison make, / Nor iron bars a cage'). (4) 'To Lucasta, Going to the Wars' – a poem in which the poet bids farewell to his beloved to go and fight for the king. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Lovelace's Cavalier poetry; the theme of freedom and loyalty; individual poems like 'To Althea' and 'To Lucasta'.

works.title: Lucky Jim
works.shortDescription: Amis's comic masterpiece, a satire of academic life and the English class system. It follows Jim Dixon, a young lecturer at a provincial university.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Campus Novel – the novel is a key example of the campus novel genre. (2) The Anti-Hero – Jim Dixon is a classic anti-hero who rebels against academic pretension. (3) The Comic Style – the novel is known for its comic, satirical style. (4) The 1950s Context – the novel reflects the social changes of post-war Britain. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the campus novel; the character of Jim Dixon; the satire of academic life.

works.title: Lupercal
works.shortDescription: Hughes's second collection, which includes some of his most famous poems, including 'The Bull Moses' and 'An Otter'.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Title – refers to the Roman festival of Lupercalia. (2) The Animal Poems – the collection continues Hughes's exploration of the animal world. (3) The Hawkins – the collection features the hawk poems that would become central to his œuvre. PYQ trends: asked about Hughes's second collection; the animal poems; the title's significance.

works.title: Lycidas
works.shortDescription: Milton's pastoral elegy, published in 1638, mourning the death of his friend Edward King, who drowned at sea. It is one of the greatest elegies in the English language.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Pastoral Elegy – follows the classical tradition of pastoral mourning. (2) The Speaker – the poem is spoken by a shepherd, representing Milton himself. (3) Theme of Death – the poem explores the meaning of death and the hope of resurrection. (4) Critical Acclaim – considered one of the finest elegies in English literature. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Milton's Lycidas; the pastoral elegy tradition; compared with other elegies (e.g., Spenser's Astrophel, Shelley's Adonais).
works.references: Edition: 1638 (published in Justa Edouardo King). Criticism: Milton (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Lyrical Ballads
works.shortDescription: A collection of poems by William Wordsworth and Samuel Taylor Coleridge, first published in 1798. It is considered the foundational text of the English Romantic movement. The second edition (1800) included Wordsworth's famous 'Preface'.[reference:0]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Founding Text – Lyrical Ballads heralded the English Romantic movement.[reference:1] (2) Preface (1800) – Wordsworth's manifesto on poetry, defining it as the 'spontaneous overflow of powerful feelings' and advocating for the use of 'language really used by men'.[reference:2] (3) Key Poems – includes Coleridge's 'The Rime of the Ancient Mariner' and Wordsworth's 'Tintern Abbey'.[reference:3] PYQ trends: frequently asked about the Preface; the collaboration between Wordsworth and Coleridge; the shift from Augustan to Romantic poetics.

works.title: Macbeth
works.shortDescription: Shakespeare's shortest tragedy, often called 'the Scottish play'. It dramatizes the damaging physical and psychological effects of political ambition on those who seek power for its own sake.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Source – based on Holinshed's Chronicles (1587)[reference:1]. (2) The Supernatural – the three witches and their prophecies are central to the plot. (3) Theme of Ambition – 'Vaulting ambition, which o'erleaps itself'. (4) The 'Scottish Play' – theatrical superstition surrounds the play; actors consider it unlucky to mention the name. (5) Psychological Depth – the play explores guilt and madness (Lady Macbeth's sleepwalking scene). PYQ trends: asked about the supernatural elements; the theme of ambition; the character of Lady Macbeth; the play's historical context.
works.references: Source: Holinshed's Chronicles. Editions: Arden Shakespeare (3rd ed., Muir, 1984). Criticism: Bradley (1904), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Major Barbara
works.shortDescription: A play about Barbara Undershaft, a Salvation Army major, and her father, a wealthy arms manufacturer. It explores themes of poverty, charity, and the ethics of wealth.[reference:7]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Salvation Army – Shaw critiques the charity's dependence on money from questionable sources. (2) The Undershaft Paradox – the idea that poverty is a worse evil than the arms trade. (3) The Ending – Barbara accepts her father's philosophy. PYQ trends: asked about the ethics of charity; the character of Undershaft; the play's social critique.

works.title: Man and Superman
works.shortDescription: A philosophical comedy in four acts, subtitled 'A Comedy and a Philosophy'. The play explores the concept of the 'Life Force' and features the 'Don Juan in Hell' dream sequence.[reference:1][reference:2]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Life Force – Shaw's concept of a creative energy driving evolution. (2) The Don Juan in Hell – a dream sequence in the third act, often performed separately. (3) The Revolutionist's Handbook – a fictional appendix included in the published text. (4) The Theme of Marriage – the play satirizes conventional attitudes towards marriage. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the 'Don Juan in Hell' scene; the Life Force concept; Shaw's philosophical drama.

works.title: Mansfield Park
works.shortDescription: Austen's novel about the timid and morally upright Fanny Price, who is sent to live with her wealthy relatives at Mansfield Park.[reference:7]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Heroine – Fanny Price is often seen as Austen's most morally serious heroine. (2) The Theme of Morality – the novel explores questions of morality and integrity. (3) The Controversy – Somerset Maugham called Fanny 'a little prig'. PYQ trends: asked about the character of Fanny; the theme of morality; compared with other Austen heroines.

works.title: Maria: or, The Wrongs of Woman
works.shortDescription: Wollstonecraft's unfinished novel, published posthumously, which continues her feminist arguments through fiction.[reference:43]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Feminist Novel – the novel continues the arguments of the Vindication through fiction. (2) The Wrongs of Woman – the title refers to the legal and social wrongs suffered by women. (3) Unfinished – the novel was left unfinished at her death. PYQ trends: asked about Wollstonecraft's fiction.

works.title: Marius the Epicurean
works.shortDescription: Pater's philosophical novel, a spiritual biography of a young Roman during the time of Marcus Aurelius. It explores the search for meaning and the relationship between aestheticism and Christianity.[reference:18]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Philosophical Novel – the novel is a work of philosophical fiction. (2) Spiritual Biography – the novel traces the spiritual development of the protagonist. (3) Aestheticism vs. Christianity – the novel explores the relationship between aestheticism and religion. PYQ trends: asked about Pater's philosophical novel; the exploration of aestheticism and Christianity.

works.title: Mary Barton
works.shortDescription: Gaskell's first novel, a social novel about the lives of the working class in Manchester during the Industrial Revolution.[reference:41]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Social Novel – the novel is a critique of the social conditions of the working class. (2) The Industrial Setting – the novel is set in Manchester during the Industrial Revolution. (3) The Theme of Class Conflict – the novel explores the tensions between the working class and the factory owners. PYQ trends: asked about the social novel; the industrial setting; the theme of class conflict.

works.title: Masks of Conquest
works.shortDescription: Viswanathan's study of the role of English literary studies in the colonial education system in India, arguing that literature was a tool of colonial control.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Colonial Education – the role of English education in India. (2) The Mask – literature as a mask for colonial control. (3) The Macaulay Minute – the role of Macaulay's education policy. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the colonial education system; the role of English literature; compared with other postcolonial studies.
works.references: Edition: 1989 (New York: Columbia University Press). Criticism: Viswanathan (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Mean Time
works.shortDescription: Duffy's collection which won the Whitbread Poetry Award.[reference:45]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Whitbread Prize – the collection won the Whitbread Poetry Award. (2) The Themes – explores time, loss, and memory. PYQ trends: asked about Duffy's award-winning collection.

works.title: Memoirs of a Fox-Hunting Man
works.shortDescription: Sassoon's first volume of his fictionalized autobiography, covering his pre-war life[reference:49].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Autobiographical Novel – the work is a fictionalized autobiography. (2) The Pre-War World – the work depicts the Edwardian world of fox-hunting and privilege. PYQ trends: asked about Sassoon's prose works; the autobiographical novels.

works.title: Middlemarch
works.shortDescription: Eliot's masterpiece, subtitled 'A Study of Provincial Life'. It is a complex portrait of life in a provincial town, exploring the lives of its inhabitants and their interconnected fates.[reference:19]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Multi-Plot Novel – the novel interweaves several storylines. (2) The Provincial Setting – the novel is set in the fictional town of Middlemarch. (3) The Theme of Ideal vs. Reality – the novel explores the gap between ideals and the compromises of real life. (4) The Characters – includes Dorothea Brooke, Tertius Lydgate, and Rosamond Vincy. (5) The Narrator – the omniscient narrator offers commentary and moral judgments. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the multi-plot structure; the provincial setting; the theme of idealism; the narrator.

works.title: Midnight's Children
works.shortDescription: Rushdie's most famous novel, a magical realist work about Saleem Sinai, who is born at the moment of India's independence. It won the Booker Prize and the 'Booker of Bookers'.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Magical Realism – the novel is a key example of magical realism. (2) The Historical Context – the novel follows the history of India from independence to the Emergency. (3) The Narrator – Saleem Sinai is an unreliable narrator. (4) The Booker Prize – the novel won the Booker Prize in 1981 and the 'Booker of Bookers' in 1993 and 2008. PYQ trends: frequently asked about magical realism; the historical context; the narrator; the Booker Prize.

works.title: Moby-Dick
works.shortDescription: Melville's epic novel about Captain Ahab's obsessive quest to kill the white whale Moby-Dick. It is a profound exploration of obsession, fate, and the nature of evil.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Obsession – Ahab's obsession with the whale. (2) The Symbolism – the white whale is a powerful symbol. (3) The Narrative – the novel is narrated by Ishmael. (4) The Genre – the novel blends adventure, philosophy, and allegory. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the character of Ahab; the symbolism of the white whale; the narrator Ishmael; the novel's structure.

works.title: Modern Painters
works.shortDescription: Ruskin's five-volume magnum opus, defending the work of J.M.W. Turner and arguing for the superiority of modern landscape painters over the Old Masters.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Truth to Nature – Ruskin elevates 'truth' as the highest aim of art. (2) The Beautiful – Volume II explores the physical, spiritual, and intellectual dimensions of beauty. (3) Mountain Aesthetics – Volume IV contains a detailed analysis of mountain beauty. PYQ trends: asked about the concept of 'truth to nature'; the aesthetic philosophy; the defence of Turner.

works.title: Moll Flanders
works.shortDescription: Defoe's novel about a woman who becomes a thief and a prostitute to survive in 18th-century London. It is a picaresque and morally ambiguous work.[reference:21]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Picaresque Novel – the novel follows the adventures of a rogue heroine. (2) Moral Ambiguity – the novel does not moralize; instead, it presents Moll's life as a series of survival strategies. (3) Realism – Defoe's detailed and realistic narration creates a sense of authenticity. (4) Female Voice – the novel is narrated by a woman, offering a rare female perspective in early fiction. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Defoe's Moll Flanders; the picaresque genre; compared with other early novels.

works.title: Mount Zion
works.shortDescription: Betjeman's first collection, which began his poetic output.[reference:20]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Collection – Betjeman's debut collection. (2) The Early Style – the collection reflects Betjeman's developing voice. PYQ trends: asked about Betjeman's early work.

works.title: Mrs Dalloway
works.shortDescription: Woolf's novel about a day in the life of Clarissa Dalloway, a high-society woman in post-World War I London, as she prepares for a party.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Stream of Consciousness – the novel explores the inner lives of its characters. (2) The Single Day – the action takes place over a single day. (3) The Theme of Mental Illness – the novel explores Septimus Warren Smith's shell shock. (4) The Party – the party symbolizes Clarissa's attempt to connect people. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the stream of consciousness technique; the theme of mental illness; the single-day structure.

works.title: Murder in the Cathedral
works.shortDescription: Eliot's most successful play, a verse drama about the murder of Thomas Becket, Archbishop of Canterbury, in 1170.[reference:35][reference:36]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Verse Drama – the play is written in blank verse. (2) The Chorus – Eliot uses a Greek-style chorus of women. (3) The Temptation – Becket is tempted by four tempters. (4) The Martyrdom – the play explores the meaning of martyrdom. (5) The Modern Miracle Play – the play is a modern reworking of the medieval miracle play.[reference:37] PYQ trends: frequently asked about the verse drama; the chorus; the temptation scene; the theme of martyrdom.

works.title: My Last Duchess
works.shortDescription: Browning's most famous dramatic monologue, in which the Duke of Ferrara reveals his jealousy and violence as he shows a portrait of his late wife to a visitor.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Dramatic Monologue – the poem is the supreme example of Browning's use of the dramatic monologue[reference:5]. (2) The Unreliable Narrator – the Duke reveals his own guilt through his words. (3) The Irony – the Duke's pride in his possession of the portrait is undercut by his confession of murder. (4) The Historical Context – the poem is based on the historical figure of Alfonso II, Duke of Ferrara. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the dramatic monologue; the unreliable narrator; compared with other Browning monologues.

works.title: My Story
works.shortDescription: Das's controversial autobiography, which candidly explores her life as a woman, wife, and poet. It is one of the first confessional autobiographies in Indian English literature.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Confessional Mode – the autobiography is candid and confessional. (2) The Feminist Theme – the work explores the life of a woman in Indian society. (3) The Controversy – the work was controversial for its frank depiction of sexuality. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the confessional mode; the feminist themes; the controversy.
works.references: Edition: 1976 (Delhi: DC Books). Criticism: Das (2000), King (1987).

works.title: Mythologies
works.shortDescription: Barthes's collection of essays that analyze everyday cultural phenomena as myths. It introduces the concept of myth as a system of signification that naturalizes cultural values.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Concept of Myth – myth is a system of signification that naturalizes cultural values. (2) Semiological Analysis – Barthes applies semiotics to everyday culture. (3) Denotation and Connotation – myth operates on the level of connotation. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the concept of myth; the semiological analysis; denotation and connotation.

works.title: Nagamandala
works.shortDescription: Karnad's play based on a Kannada folk tale. It explores the lives of women and the nature of love and desire.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Folk Source – the play is based on a Kannada folk tale. (2) The Feminist Theme – the play explores the lives of women. (3) The Themes – explores love, desire, and the relationship between human and animal worlds. PYQ trends: asked about the folk source; the feminist themes; the play's exploration of love and desire.
works.references: Edition: 1988 (Delhi: Oxford University Press). Criticism: Karnad (2000).

works.title: Nectar in a Sieve
works.shortDescription: Markandaya's first novel, a bestseller that depicts the suffering of the rural poor in India.[reference:78][reference:79]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Rural India – the novel depicts rural India and the suffering of its poor.[reference:80] (2) The Commercial Success – the novel was a huge commercial success.[reference:81] PYQ trends: asked about the novel's publication year (1954); and the depiction of rural India.
works.references: Edition: 1954 (New York: John Day). Criticism: Markandaya (2000).

works.title: Night of the Scorpion
works.shortDescription: One of Ezekiel's most famous poems, which recounts the night his mother was stung by a scorpion and the village's superstitious response to the event.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Indian Context – the poem depicts rural Indian life and superstition. (2) The Narrative – the poem is a first-person narrative of a childhood memory. (3) The Theme of Suffering – explores the mother's suffering and the father's rational response. PYQ trends: frequently asked about this poem; the Indian context; the contrast between superstition and rationality.
works.references: Edition: 1965 (in The Exact Name). Criticism: Ezekiel (2000), King (1987).

works.title: Nineteen Eighty-Four
works.shortDescription: Orwell's dystopian novel about a totalitarian society where the government, led by Big Brother, controls every aspect of life.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Dystopian Fiction – the novel is a landmark of dystopian fiction. (2) The Party – the ruling party controls all aspects of life. (3) The Famous Lines – 'Big Brother is watching you' and 'War is Peace. Freedom is Slavery. Ignorance is Strength.' (4) Newspeak – the invented language designed to limit thought. (5) The Ending – Winston's final submission. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the dystopian genre; Newspeak; the themes of totalitarianism and surveillance.

works.title: Noble Numbers
works.shortDescription: Herrick's collection of 272 religious poems, published together with Hesperides in 1648. The poems reflect Herrick's Anglican faith and his devotion to God.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Religious Poetry – Herrick's sacred verse explores themes of sin, repentance, and divine grace. (2) Contrast with Hesperides – the religious poems contrast with the secular sensuality of Hesperides, showing Herrick's range as a poet. (3) Anglican Devotion – Herrick was an Anglican priest, and his poetry reflects his commitment to the Church of England. PYQ trends: asked about the relationship between Herrick's secular and religious poetry.
works.references: Edition: 1648 (published with Hesperides). Criticism: Herrick (2002), Summers (1998).

works.title: North
works.shortDescription: Heaney's collection that addresses the Troubles in Northern Ireland through the metaphor of the bog bodies.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Bog Poems – the collection includes the famous 'bog poems', including 'Punishment' and 'Strange Fruit'. (2) The Troubles – the poems respond to the political violence in Northern Ireland. (3) The Archaeological Metaphor – Heaney uses the bog bodies as a metaphor for the violence of the present. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the bog poems; the Troubles; the archaeological metaphor.

works.title: North and South
works.shortDescription: Gaskell's novel about Margaret Hale, who moves from the rural South of England to the industrial North, and the clash of cultures she encounters.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Industrial Novel – the novel explores the social and economic conditions of the industrial North. (2) The Clash of Cultures – the novel contrasts the rural South with the industrial North. (3) The Social Critique – the novel critiques the social inequalities of the Industrial Revolution. PYQ trends: asked about the industrial novel; the clash of cultures; the social critique.

works.title: Northanger Abbey
works.shortDescription: Austen's satire of Gothic fiction, following the naive Catherine Morland's adventures in Bath and at Northanger Abbey.[reference:8]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Satire of Gothic Fiction – the novel parodies the conventions of Gothic novels. (2) The Heroine – Catherine is naive but ultimately learns to distinguish between fiction and reality. PYQ trends: asked about the satire of Gothic fiction; the character of Catherine.

works.title: Nostromo
works.shortDescription: Conrad's novel set in the fictional South American country of Costaguana, exploring themes of revolution, greed, and the nature of heroism.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Political Novel – the novel explores the politics of a fictional South American country. (2) The Theme of Silver – the silver mine represents greed and corruption. (3) The Character of Nostromo – the title character is a complex hero. PYQ trends: asked about the political themes; the symbolism of silver; the character of Nostromo.

works.title: Not Waving but Drowning
works.shortDescription: Smith's most famous collection, which includes the title poem that appears in many anthologies. The title poem explores the failure of communication and the loneliness of the individual.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Title Poem – 'Not Waving but Drowning' is one of Smith's most famous poems. (2) The Themes – explores loneliness, death, and the failure of communication. (3) The Illustrations – the collection features Smith's own drawings. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Not Waving but Drowning; the title poem; the themes of loneliness and death.

works.title: Novel on Yellow Paper
works.shortDescription: Smith's first novel, published in 1936.[reference:31]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Novel – Smith's debut novel. (2) The Style – the novel is written in a distinctive, idiosyncratic style. PYQ trends: asked about Smith's prose works.

works.title: Novum Organum
works.shortDescription: Bacon's major work on scientific method, published in 1620 as part of his Instauratio Magna. It presents a new system of logic based on induction and empirical observation.[reference:2]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Scientific Method – Bacon presents a new system of logic (induction) as opposed to the old Aristotelian system (deduction). (2) The Idols – further develops the concept of the Idols that hinder human understanding. (3) Influence – laid the foundation for modern scientific inquiry. PYQ trends: asked about Bacon's contribution to the scientific method; the Idols; the inductive method.

works.title: Nymphidia
works.shortDescription: A delightful fairy poem with mock-heroic undertones, written in Drayton's old age.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Fairy Poetry – part of the Elizabethan/Jacobean tradition of fairy poetry (along with Shakespeare's A Midsummer Night's Dream and Spenser's Faerie Queene). (2) Mock-Heroic – combines fairy mythology with mock-heroic tone. (3) Late Work – written in Drayton's old age, showcasing his enduring creativity. PYQ trends: asked about the fairy poetry tradition; Drayton's late works.

works.title: Ode: Intimations of Immortality from Recollections of Early Childhood
works.shortDescription: One of Wordsworth's greatest odes, reflecting on the loss of childhood vision and the consolation offered by memory and mature reflection.[reference:10]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Ode Form – a Pindaric ode in irregular meter. (2) The Theme of Loss – 'The things which I have seen I now can see no more'. (3) The Child as Father of the Man – the famous line from the poem. PYQ trends: asked about the theme of childhood; the ode form; the phrase 'trailing clouds of glory'.
works.references: Edition: 1807 (in Poems, in Two Volumes). Criticism: Wordsworth (2000), Abrams (1973).

works.title: Ode on a Distant Prospect of Eton College
works.shortDescription: Gray's early poem, reflecting on the fate of the schoolboys at Eton College.[reference:7]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The 'Little Victims' – Gray reflects on the future fate of the schoolboys, who will face the trials of adult life. (2) The Theme of Mortality – the poem anticipates the themes of the Elegy. PYQ trends: asked about Gray's early poems.

works.title: Ode on a Grecian Urn
works.shortDescription: Keats's ode reflecting on the beauty and permanence of art, as represented by a Greek urn.[reference:31]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Ode Form – a Pindaric ode. (2) The Urn – the urn represents the permanence of art. (3) The Famous Line – 'Beauty is truth, truth beauty, – that is all / Ye know on earth, and all ye need to know.'[reference:32] PYQ trends: frequently asked about Keats's Ode on a Grecian Urn; the theme of art; the famous line; compared with other odes.

works.title: Ode to a Nightingale
works.shortDescription: Keats's most famous ode, celebrating the song of the nightingale and reflecting on mortality, beauty, and the nature of art.[reference:30]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Ode Form – a Pindaric ode in ten-line stanzas. (2) The Nightingale – the bird represents the ideal of art, which is immortal. (3) The Theme of Mortality – the poem reflects on the transience of human life. (4) The Famous Line – 'Thou wast not born for death, immortal Bird!' (5) The Influence of Shakespeare – Keats's imagery is drawn from Shakespeare's plays. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Keats's Ode to a Nightingale; the theme of mortality; compared with other odes.
works.references: Edition: 1819 (in Lamia, Isabella, The Eve of St. Agnes, and Other Poems). Criticism: Keats (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Ode to Psyche
works.shortDescription: Keats's ode to the goddess Psyche, celebrating the power of the imagination.[reference:33]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Ode Form – a Pindaric ode. (2) The Goddess Psyche – Psyche represents the soul and the power of the imagination. (3) The Opening Line – 'O Goddess! hear these tuneless numbers, wrung / By sweet enforcement and remembrance dear'.[reference:34] PYQ trends: asked about Keats's Ode to Psyche; the theme of the imagination.

works.title: Ode to the West Wind
works.shortDescription: Shelley's most famous ode, a powerful poem that uses the wind as a symbol of inspiration and revolution.[reference:24]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Ode Form – a Pindaric ode in terza rima. (2) The West Wind – the wind represents the power of nature and the spirit of revolution. (3) The Famous Line – 'If Winter comes, can Spring be far behind?'[reference:25] (4) The Structure – the poem is divided into five sections, each addressing the wind in different aspects. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Shelley's Ode to the West Wind; the symbolism of the wind; compared with other Romantic odes.

works.title: Of Dramatick Poesie
works.shortDescription: Dryden's first major critical work, a dialogue in which four speakers debate the merits of ancient and modern drama, and the use of rhyme in plays.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Literary Criticism – one of the most important works of English literary criticism. (2) Dialogue Form – the work is a dialogue between four speakers: Crites, Eugenius, Lisideius, and Neander (representing Dryden). (3) Defence of Modern Drama – Dryden argues for the superiority of modern English drama over classical drama. (4) Defence of Rhyme – Dryden defends the use of rhyme in serious drama. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Dryden as a critic; Of Dramatick Poesie; the arguments for and against rhyme; compared with other critical works.

works.title: Of Grammatology
works.shortDescription: Derrida's foundational text of deconstruction. It argues for the primacy of writing over speech and introduces the concept of différance.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Deconstruction – the method of reading that challenges binary oppositions. (2) Différance – the idea that meaning is endlessly deferred and differs. (3) The Primacy of Writing – Derrida argues that writing is not secondary to speech. PYQ trends: one of the most frequently asked texts in NET; deconstruction; différance; the primacy of writing.

works.title: Of Human Bondage
works.shortDescription: Maugham's most famous novel, a semi-autobiographical bildungsroman about the life of Philip Carey, an orphan who struggles to find meaning in life.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Bildungsroman – the novel traces Philip's development from childhood to adulthood. (2) The Autobiographical Elements – the novel draws on Maugham's own life. (3) The Title – from Spinoza's Ethics: 'Of human bondage' refers to the constraints of human existence. PYQ trends: asked about the bildungsroman; the autobiographical elements; the title's meaning.

works.title: Of the Laws of Ecclesiastical Polity
works.shortDescription: Hooker's masterpiece, a monumental work of English theology and philosophy defending the Church of England against Puritan attacks. The first four books were published in 1593, the fifth in 1597, and the rest posthumously.[reference:3][reference:4]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Anglican Apologetic – a defence of the Church of England and its Elizabethan settlement. (2) Natural Law – Hooker bases his defence on the natural law tradition and Aristotelianism.[reference:5] (3) Prose Style – Hooker is considered one of the greatest prose stylists of the Elizabethan age.[reference:6] (4) Political Theory – the work addresses issues of church-state relations and the nature of authority. PYQ trends: asked about Hooker as a theologian and prose stylist; the defence of the Church of England; the natural law tradition.
works.references: Editions: 1593 (Books I-IV), 1597 (Book V). Criticism: Hooker (2002), McGrade (1998).

works.title: Oliver Twist
works.shortDescription: Dickens's second novel, the story of an orphan who escapes from a workhouse and falls in with a gang of thieves in London.[reference:2]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Social Critique – the novel critiques the workhouse system and the criminal underworld. (2) The Characters – includes Fagin, Bill Sikes, the Artful Dodger, and Nancy. (3) The Theme of Innocence – Oliver represents innocence in a corrupt world. PYQ trends: asked about the social critique; the characters; the novel's treatment of crime.

works.title: Omeros
works.shortDescription: Walcott's Homeric epic poem, which many critics view as his major achievement. It is set in his native St. Lucia and explores the Caribbean cultural experience.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Homeric Epic – the poem is a Caribbean reworking of the Homeric epic. (2) The Caribbean Setting – the poem is set in St. Lucia. (3) The Postcolonial Theme – the poem explores the postcolonial experience. (4) The Nobel Prize – Walcott won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1992. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Walcott's Omeros; the Homeric epic; the postcolonial theme; the Nobel Prize.

works.title: On Heroes, Hero-Worship, and the Heroic in History
works.shortDescription: A series of six lectures on the nature of heroism and its role in history. Carlyle argues that history is shaped by great individuals.[reference:33][reference:34]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Hero – Carlyle defines his view of the heroic and its role throughout history.[reference:35] (2) The Great Man Theory – the work is a classic statement of the 'Great Man' theory of history. (3) Types of Heroes – Carlyle identifies different types of heroes: the Prophet, the Poet, the Priest, the Man of Letters, and the King. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the 'Great Man' theory; the types of heroes; compared with other theories of history.

works.title: On Liberty
works.shortDescription: Mill's most famous work, a philosophical treatise on individual liberty and the limits of state authority. It is a classic text of liberal political philosophy.[reference:42][reference:43]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Harm Principle – Mill argues that the only purpose for which power can be exercised over an individual against his will is to prevent harm to others.[reference:44] (2) Liberty of Thought and Expression – Mill defends freedom of thought and expression strongly.[reference:45] (3) Self-Regarding vs. Other-Regarding Actions – Mill distinguishes between actions that concern only the individual and those that concern others.[reference:46] (4) Individual Sovereignty – Mill argues that over himself, over his own body and mind, the individual is sovereign.[reference:47] PYQ trends: frequently asked about the Harm Principle; the defence of free speech; the distinction between self-regarding and other-regarding actions; compared with other political philosophers.

works.title: On the Sublime
works.shortDescription: A treatise on style and aesthetics, attributed to Longinus. It argues that the sublime is a quality of elevated thought and language that transports the reader.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Sublime – the quality that transports the reader and elevates the soul. (2) The Sources of the Sublime – includes grandeur of thought, strong emotion, figures of speech, noble diction, and dignified composition. (3) The Influence – the work was highly influential in the Romantic period. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the sublime; the sources of the sublime; the work's influence on Romanticism.
works.references: Edition: c. 1st century CE. Criticism: Longinus (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Orientalism
works.shortDescription: Said's foundational work of postcolonial theory, which argues that Western representations of the 'Orient' are a form of knowledge that serves the interests of colonial power.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Orientalism – the Western construction of the Orient. (2) Knowledge and Power – knowledge is a form of power. (3) The Western Gaze – the Orient is represented through a Western lens. PYQ trends: one of the most frequently asked texts in NET; Orientalism; knowledge and power; the Western gaze.
works.references: Edition: 1978 (New York: Pantheon). Criticism: Said (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Oroonoko
works.shortDescription: Behn's prose novella, often considered one of the earliest English novels. It tells the story of an African prince who is enslaved and taken to Suriname, where he leads a rebellion.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Early Novel – one of the earliest examples of the novel form in English. (2) Anti-Slavery Theme – the novella is a powerful critique of slavery and colonialism. (3) The Noble Savage – Oroonoko is portrayed as a noble and dignified figure. (4) Narrative Voice – the story is told by a female narrator who claims to have witnessed the events. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Behn's Oroonoko; the early novel; the anti-slavery theme; compared with other early novels.

works.title: Othello
works.shortDescription: Shakespeare's tragedy of jealousy and betrayal, set in Venice and Cyprus. The play tells the story of Othello, a Moorish general in the Venetian army, and his ensign Iago, who manipulates him into believing his wife Desdemona has been unfaithful.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Source – based on Cinthio's Gli Hecatommithi (1565)[reference:2]. (2) Theme of Jealousy – 'the green-eyed monster' – Iago manipulates Othello's trust. (3) Race and Otherness – Othello is a Moor in a white Venetian society; the play engages with Renaissance attitudes towards race. (4) Iago – one of Shakespeare's most villainous characters, motivated by 'motiveless malignity' (Coleridge). (5) Domestic Tragedy – unlike most Shakespearean tragedies, this is a domestic tragedy. PYQ trends: asked about the character of Iago; the theme of jealousy; the treatment of race; the handkerchief symbol.
works.references: Source: Cinthio's Gli Hecatommithi. Editions: Arden Shakespeare (3rd ed., Honigmann, 1997). Criticism: Bradley (1904), Greenblatt (2001).

works.title: Ozymandias
works.shortDescription: Shelley's famous sonnet about the ruins of a statue of the Egyptian pharaoh Ramesses II.[reference:26]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Sonnet Form – a Petrarchan sonnet. (2) The Theme of Transience – the poem reflects on the impermanence of power and empires. (3) The Irony – the inscription 'Look on my works, ye Mighty, and despair!' is ironically contrasted with the ruins. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Shelley's Ozymandias; the theme of transience; the sonnet form.

works.title: Pamela
works.shortDescription: Richardson's first novel, an epistolary novel about a virtuous servant girl who resists her master's advances and eventually marries him.[reference:26]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Epistolary Novel – the novel is written in the form of letters, a form that Richardson pioneered.[reference:27] (2) Virtue Rewarded – the novel's subtitle, 'Virtue Rewarded', reflects its moral message. (3) The 'Pamela' Debate – the novel sparked a debate about its morality, with Henry Fielding writing a parody, Shamela. (4) Sentimental Novel – the novel is an early example of the sentimental novel. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Richardson's Pamela; the epistolary novel; compared with Fielding's Joseph Andrews.

works.title: Paradise Lost
works.shortDescription: Milton's epic masterpiece, a Christian epic in blank verse, telling the story of the Fall of Man, the temptation of Adam and Eve by Satan, and their expulsion from the Garden of Eden.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Epic Genre – Milton's epic is one of the greatest in the English language, modeled on classical epics like Homer's Iliad and Virgil's Aeneid. (2) Blank Verse – Milton's use of blank verse is considered one of the most accomplished in English poetry. (3) The Hero – the epic's hero is often debated: is it Adam, Christ, or Satan? (4) Theological Themes – the poem explores themes of free will, obedience, redemption, and the nature of evil. (5) Satan's Character – Satan is one of the most complex and compelling characters in English literature, often seen as a sympathetic rebel. (6) Structure – the poem is divided into twelve books. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Milton's epic; the character of Satan; the theme of free will; the use of blank verse.
works.references: Edition: 1667 (10 books), 1674 (12 books). Criticism: Milton (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Paradise Regained
works.shortDescription: Milton's epic poem, published in 1671, telling the story of the temptation of Christ in the wilderness. It is a sequel to Paradise Lost and focuses on the theme of redemption.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Sequel to Paradise Lost – a shorter epic that focuses on Christ's temptation. (2) Theme of Redemption – the poem explores how Christ resists temptation and achieves redemption for humanity. (3) Contrast with Paradise Lost – Paradise Regained is more meditative and less dramatic than Paradise Lost. PYQ trends: asked about the relationship between Paradise Lost and Paradise Regained; the theme of redemption.

works.title: Peace
works.shortDescription: One of Brooke's sonnets from the 1914 sequence, celebrating the spiritual renewal brought by the war[reference:56].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Theme of Peace – the poem suggests that war brings a kind of spiritual peace. (2) The Idealistic Vision – the poem reflects the early idealism of the war. PYQ trends: asked about Brooke's sonnets; the theme of peace.

works.title: Pearl
works.shortDescription: A Middle English alliterative poem found in the same manuscript as Sir Gawain and the Green Knight. It is a dream vision and elegy, likely for the death of a small girl, exploring the nature of grief and the pearl of great price.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Dream Vision – uses the dream vision genre for philosophical and theological discussions on divine grace and salvation. (2) Elegy – an elegy for a deceased child, often interpreted as the poet's own daughter. (3) Same Author – attributed to the same anonymous 'Pearl Poet'. (4) Symbolism – the pearl represents purity, innocence, and the soul's salvation. (5) Numerical Structure – has a highly structured verse form with 101 stanzas and repeated linking words, showing mathematical precision. PYQ trends: asked in context of the Gawain manuscript and the Pearl Poet's corpus; the poem's intricate numerical structure.
works.references: MS: Cotton Nero A.x. Editions: Andrew & Waldron (2007), Putter (1996).

works.title: Pen Rhythm
works.shortDescription: Zephaniah's first collection of poems, which established his reputation as a dub poet. The poems are written in the dub poetry style, influenced by reggae.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Collection – Zephaniah's debut collection. (2) Dub Poetry – the poems are written in the dub poetry style, influenced by reggae. (3) The Themes – explores political and social issues. PYQ trends: asked about Zephaniah's first collection; the dub poetry style.

works.title: Personae
works.shortDescription: Pound's early collection of poems, which established his reputation as a modernist voice[reference:23].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Persona – the poems are written in various voices or personae. (2) The Early Style – the collection shows Pound's early influences and his developing modernist style. PYQ trends: asked about Pound's early works.

works.title: Persuasion
works.shortDescription: Austen's last completed novel, about Anne Elliot who, persuaded by a friend to break off an engagement, meets her former love Captain Wentworth years later.[reference:9]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Theme of Persuasion – the novel explores the dangers of persuasion and the importance of following one's own judgment. (2) The Heroine – Anne is Austen's most mature and introspective heroine. (3) The Ending – the novel's ending is more romantic than Austen's other works. PYQ trends: asked about the theme of persuasion; the character of Anne; compared with other Austen novels.

works.title: The Pickwick Papers
works.shortDescription: Dickens's first novel, a series of loosely connected adventures of Mr. Pickwick and his club members.[reference:3]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Novel – Dickens's debut novel, published in serial form. (2) The Comic Novel – the novel is a comic masterpiece. (3) The Characters – includes Mr. Pickwick, Sam Weller, and Mr. Jingle. PYQ trends: asked about Dickens's first novel; the comic elements.

works.title: Pierce Penilesse His Supplication to the Divell
works.shortDescription: Nashe's most famous prose satire, in which the impoverished author Pierce Penilesse appeals to the devil for money. The work is a sharp satire on the social and economic conditions of Elizabethan England[reference:85].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Satire of Society – the work satirizes the greed and corruption of Elizabethan society[reference:86]. (2) The Seven Deadly Sins – the work uses the seven deadly sins as a framework for its satire[reference:87]. (3) Nashe's Style – characterized by its free, colloquial style, full of newly coined words and fantastic idiosyncrasies[reference:88]. (4) The Feud with Harvey – the work was part of Nashe's ongoing feud with Gabriel Harvey[reference:89]. PYQ trends: asked about Nashe's satires; the style and themes; the Harvey feud[reference:90][reference:91].
works.references: Editions: Norton Critical Edition. Criticism: Nashe (2002), Kinney (2005).

works.title: Piers Plowman
works.shortDescription: A Middle English alliterative poem in the form of a series of dream visions dealing with the social and spiritual predicament of late 14th-century England. It is one of the greatest examples of Middle English alliterative poetry.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Three Versions – A-text (c. 1360–70), B-text (c. 1377–79), and C-text (c. 1380s) – this textual complexity is a major scholarly issue. (2) Satire – a sharp satire on the corruption of the clergy and the social order. (3) Religious Allegory – rich in religious allegory, translating monastic language into symbols accessible to the layman. (4) Piers as Christ – Piers appears finally as Jesus, embodying the ideal of the suffering servant. (5) Structure – mirrors the complexity of its themes with recurring concepts of Do-wel, Do-bet, and Do-best (ethical progression). PYQ trends: asked about the representation of Piers, the allegorical meaning, the satire on clergy, and the comparison with Chaucer's contemporary social critique.
works.references: MS: Multiple (14th c., 50+ manuscripts). Editions: Schmidt (1995), Pearsall (2008). Criticism: Kane (1965), Simpson (1990).

works.title: Plato and Platonism
works.shortDescription: Pater's last major work, a study of Plato and his philosophy. Pater considered this his greatest contribution to literature.[reference:22]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Platonic Philosophy – the work offers a study of Plato and his influence. (2) Pater's Late Style – the work represents Pater's mature critical style. (3) Pater's Own Assessment – Pater said of this work: 'If there is anything of mine which has a chance of surviving, I should say was my Plato'.[reference:23] PYQ trends: asked about Pater's later works; his study of Plato.

works.title: Poems
works.shortDescription: Carew's collection of poems, published posthumously in 1640. It includes love lyrics, occasional poems, and his famous masque Coelum Britannicum (1634). His most notorious poem is 'A Rapture', an erotic celebration of physical love.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Cavalier Poet – Carew is a leading Cavalier poet, known for his polished and elegant verse. (2) 'A Rapture' – one of the most notorious erotic poems of the 17th century, celebrating physical love in frank and sensuous language. (3) Coelum Britannicum – Carew's masque, performed before Charles I in 1634, is considered one of the finest Caroline masques. (4) Influence of Jonson – Carew was influenced by Ben Jonson and was part of the 'Tribe of Ben'. PYQ trends: asked about Carew's erotic poetry; his masque; the Cavalier poets.

works.title: Poems (1930)
works.shortDescription: Auden's first book of poems, which brought him to wide public attention[reference:25].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The 1930s Poet – the collection established Auden as a leading poet of the 1930s. (2) The Political Dimension – the poems reflect Auden's left-wing political commitments. PYQ trends: asked about Auden's early work; the 1930s political context.

works.title: Poems and Ballads
works.shortDescription: Swinburne's first major collection, which caused a scandal for its pagan themes and sensuous imagery.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Scandal – the collection caused a scandal for its pagan themes and sensuous imagery. (2) The Pagan Themes – Swinburne celebrates pagan gods and myths. (3) The Metrical Mastery – Swinburne is known for his mastery of meter and rhythm. PYQ trends: asked about Swinburne's poetry; the scandal; the metrical mastery.

works.title: Poems, Chiefly in the Scottish Dialect
works.shortDescription: Burns's first major collection of poetry, published in Kilmarnock in 1786. It established him as the national poet of Scotland.[reference:17]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Scottish Dialect – Burns wrote in the Scots language, celebrating Scottish culture and identity. (2) The Kilmarnock Edition – the first edition of his poems, which made him famous. (3) The Poems – includes 'To a Mouse', 'The Cotter's Saturday Night', and 'Address to the Deil'. (4) The National Poet – Burns is widely regarded as the national poet of Scotland.[reference:18] PYQ trends: frequently asked about Burns's poetry; the use of Scots dialect; the national poet of Scotland; individual poems like 'To a Mouse'.

works.title: Poetics
works.shortDescription: Aristotle's foundational work of literary criticism, which defines poetry as a form of mimesis (imitation) and introduces the concepts of catharsis and hamartia.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Mimesis – poetry is an imitation of action and life. (2) Catharsis – the purgation of pity and fear through tragedy. (3) Hamartia – the tragic flaw of the hero. (4) The Six Parts – plot, character, thought, diction, melody, and spectacle. (5) The Unities – unity of action, time, and place (though Aristotle only explicitly mentions unity of action). PYQ trends: one of the most frequently asked texts in NET; the definition of tragedy; catharsis; hamartia; the six parts.
works.references: Edition: c. 335 BCE. Criticism: Aristotle (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Point Counter Point
works.shortDescription: Huxley's novel about a group of intellectuals in 1920s London, using a counterpoint structure inspired by music.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Counterpoint Structure – the novel uses a musical counterpoint structure. (2) The Intellectual Circle – the novel satirizes the intellectual circles of the 1920s. PYQ trends: asked about the counterpoint structure; the satire of intellectuals.

works.title: Poly-Olbion
works.shortDescription: Drayton's most ambitious work, a topographical poem on England. Written in alexandrines (12-syllable lines), it is among the longest poems in English. Part I published in 1612, Part II in 1622.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Topographical Poem – a poetic survey of the geography, history, and legends of England. (2) Alexandrine Meter – written in 12-syllable lines, a French meter unusual for English poetry. (3) Elizabethan Discovery of England – intended to record the beauty of the English countryside, its history, legend, and present life. (4) Monumental Achievement – among the longest poems in English, though rarely read today. PYQ trends: asked as Drayton's major work; the topographical poem genre; the use of alexandrines.

works.title: Porphyria's Lover
works.shortDescription: Browning's dramatic monologue in which a man strangles his lover with her own hair.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Dramatic Monologue – the poem is a classic example of the dramatic monologue[reference:6]. (2) The Madness – the speaker reveals his madness through his rationalization of the murder. PYQ trends: asked about the dramatic monologue; the theme of madness; compared with My Last Duchess.

works.title: Portnoy's Complaint
works.shortDescription: A 1969 American novel by Philip Roth. It is a monologue by a neurotic young man, Alexander Portnoy, recounted to his analyst. The novel turned Roth into a major celebrity and sparked controversy over its explicit treatment of sexuality.[reference:10]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Postmodernism – the novel is a key example of Postmodernist fiction. (2) The Confessional Mode – the novel is a long, confessional monologue. (3) The Controversy – the novel was controversial for its candid treatment of sexuality. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Postmodernism; the confessional mode; the controversy.

works.title: A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man
works.shortDescription: Joyce's semi-autobiographical bildungsroman about Stephen Dedalus, a young Irishman who rebels against his family, his religion, and his country to become an artist.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Bildungsroman – the novel traces Stephen's development from childhood to young adulthood. (2) The Artist's Vow – Stephen vows to 'forge in the smithy of my soul the uncreated conscience of my race'. (3) The Style – the novel's style develops with Stephen, from childhood prose to mature artistic prose. PYQ trends: asked about the bildungsroman; the theme of the artist; the developing style.

works.title: Possession
works.shortDescription: Byatt's novel about two scholars who discover a hidden romance between two Victorian poets. It explores the nature of scholarship, creativity, and obsession.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Academic Novel – the novel is a key example of the academic novel. (2) The Victorian Theme – the novel explores Victorian poetry and culture. (3) The Metafictional Element – the novel is about scholarship and research. (4) The Booker Prize – the novel won the Booker Prize in 1990. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the academic novel; the Victorian theme; the metafictional element.

works.title: Postmodernism, or, The Cultural Logic of Late Capitalism
works.shortDescription: Jameson's comprehensive work on postmodernism, which argues that postmodernism is the cultural logic of late capitalism.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Late Capitalism – the current stage of capitalism. (2) The Cultural Logic – postmodernism is the cultural logic of late capitalism. (3) The Characteristics – pastiche, schizophrenic temporality, and the waning of affect. PYQ trends: frequently asked about postmodernism; late capitalism; compared with other theories of postmodernism.
works.references: Edition: 1991 (Durham: Duke University Press). Criticism: Jameson (2000).

works.title: Practical Criticism
works.shortDescription: A study of the problems of literary interpretation, based on Richards's experiments in which students were given poems without any identifying information.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Experiment – Richards gave students poems without any context and analyzed their responses. (2) The Four Kinds of Misunderstanding – reading problems: sense, feeling, tone, and intention. (3) The Method – close reading without reference to the author or historical context. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the Practical Criticism experiment; the four kinds of misunderstanding; the method of close reading.

works.title: Praeterita
works.shortDescription: Ruskin's unfinished autobiography, published in three volumes from 1885 to 1889. It is a vivid and introspective account of his early life and development.[reference:11]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Autobiography – the work is an introspective account of Ruskin's early life. (2) Vivid Prose – the work is known for its vivid and evocative prose. (3) Unfinished – the work remains incomplete. PYQ trends: asked about Ruskin's autobiography; the prose style.

works.title: Preface to Shakespeare
works.shortDescription: Johnson's influential preface to his edition of Shakespeare's plays, offering a critical assessment of Shakespeare's genius.[reference:33]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Shakespeare Criticism – Johnson's preface is one of the most important works of Shakespeare criticism. (2) The Defence of Shakespeare – Johnson defends Shakespeare against the charges of his critics. (3) The 'Just Representation' – Johnson argues that Shakespeare's characters are a 'just representation of general nature'. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Johnson's Preface to Shakespeare; the critical arguments; compared with other Shakespeare critics.

works.title: Pride and Prejudice
works.shortDescription: Austen's most famous novel, a romantic comedy of manners about the Bennet family and the relationship between Elizabeth Bennet and Mr. Darcy.[reference:4]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Comedy of Manners – the novel satirizes the social conventions of Regency England. (2) The Title – 'pride' refers to Darcy, 'prejudice' to Elizabeth. (3) The Opening Line – 'It is a truth universally acknowledged, that a single man in possession of a good fortune, must be in want of a wife.' (4) The Marriage Plot – the novel explores the institution of marriage and the social pressures on women. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the comedy of manners; the opening line; the characters; the theme of marriage.

works.title: Principles of Literary Criticism
works.shortDescription: Richards's foundational text of New Criticism. He argues for a psychological and scientific approach to literature, focusing on the reader's response and the nature of poetic language.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Scientific Approach – Richards advocates for a scientific, empirical approach to literature. (2) The Reader's Response – the critic should focus on the reader's mental processes. (3) Poetic Language – Richards distinguishes between scientific and emotive language. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Richards's scientific approach; the distinction between scientific and emotive language; the influence on New Criticism.

works.title: Principles of Political Economy
works.shortDescription: Mill's comprehensive work on economics, which was the standard economics textbook for several decades.[reference:40][reference:41]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Economic Theory – the work is a comprehensive exposition of economic theory. (2) Standard Textbook – the work was the standard economics textbook for decades. (3) Social Philosophy – the work combines economic theory with social philosophy. PYQ trends: asked about Mill's economic thought; compared with other economists.

works.title: Prometheus Unbound
works.shortDescription: Shelley's lyrical drama, a reworking of the Prometheus myth. It is one of his most ambitious works, celebrating human freedom and the power of love.[reference:22]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Lyrical Drama – the work is a dramatic poem rather than a stage play. (2) The Myth of Prometheus – Shelley subverts the Greek myth, ending with Prometheus's liberation. (3) The Theme of Freedom – the poem celebrates human freedom and the power of love. (4) The Influence of Aeschylus – Shelley draws on Aeschylus's Prometheus Bound.[reference:23] PYQ trends: frequently asked about Shelley's Prometheus Unbound; the theme of freedom; compared with other Romantic dramas.

works.title: Prothalamion
works.shortDescription: A wedding song written for the marriage of the daughters of the Duke of Somerset. Strategically written to gain favor at court.
works.analysis: Prothalamion Genre – a pre-nuptial song.'Sweet Thames, run softly, till I end my song'. Lack in vigour and detail as opposed to Epithalmon.

works.title: Purdah
works.shortDescription: Dharker's first collection of poetry, which explores themes of identity, displacement, and the experience of women. The title refers to the practice of secluding women.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Feminist Theme – the collection explores the experience of women and the practice of purdah. (2) The Theme of Displacement – the collection explores the experience of being displaced. (3) The Title – 'Purdah' is a metaphor for the silencing and seclusion of women. PYQ trends: asked about the feminist themes; the theme of displacement; the symbolism of the title.
works.references: Edition: 1989 (New Delhi: Rupa & Co). Criticism: Dharker (2000).

works.title: Pygmalion
works.shortDescription: Shaw's most popular play, a comedy about phonetics professor Henry Higgins who bets he can transform Cockney flower girl Eliza Doolittle into a duchess through speech training. It explores class, identity, and the power of language.[reference:0]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The 'Pygmalion' Myth – the play subverts the myth by having Eliza assert her independence. (2) Social Class – Shaw critiques the British class system. (3) The Ending – Higgins and Eliza do not marry, subverting audience expectations. (4) The Play's Title – the title refers to the Greek myth of Pygmalion and Galatea. (5) The Epilogue – Shaw wrote an epilogue explaining the characters' fates. PYQ trends: asked about the Pygmalion myth; the theme of class; the unconventional ending.

works.title: Rapture
works.shortDescription: Duffy's collection which won the T.S. Eliot Prize.[reference:44]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The T.S. Eliot Prize – the collection won the prestigious prize. (2) The Theme of Love – the poems explore the experience of love. PYQ trends: asked about Duffy's award-winning collection; the theme of love.

works.title: Reflections on the Revolution in France
works.shortDescription: Burke's most famous work, a powerful critique of the French Revolution and a defence of tradition and gradual reform.[reference:34]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Political Philosophy – the work is a landmark in political thought, defending conservatism and tradition. (2) The Sublime and Beautiful – Burke's earlier work, A Philosophical Enquiry into the Origin of Our Ideas of the Sublime and Beautiful (1757), is also a key text.[reference:35] (3) The Attack on the Revolution – Burke argues that the French Revolution is a dangerous experiment that will lead to chaos and tyranny. (4) The Defence of Tradition – Burke defends the value of tradition, custom, and gradual reform. (5) The 'Age of Chivalry' – Burke famously laments the passing of the 'age of chivalry'. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Burke's Reflections; the political philosophy; compared with Paine's Rights of Man.

works.title: Refugee Boy
works.shortDescription: Zephaniah's novel about a young Ethiopian refugee in England.[reference:48]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Novel – a work of fiction for young adults. (2) The Theme of Refuge – explores the experience of refugees in Britain. PYQ trends: asked about Zephaniah's prose works.

works.title: Relationship
works.shortDescription: Mahapatra's collection of poems that explores the poet's relationship with his father and the larger relationship between man and his environment.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Autobiographical Element – the collection explores the poet's relationship with his father. (2) The Theme of Nature – the collection explores man's relationship with the natural world. (3) The Significance – the collection is considered one of Mahapatra's most important works. PYQ trends: asked about the autobiographical elements; the theme of nature; the collection's significance.
works.references: Edition: 1980 (New York: Greenfield Review Press). Criticism: Mahapatra (2000).

works.title: Remember
works.shortDescription: Rossetti's sonnet about death and remembrance[reference:30].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Sonnet – the poem is a Petrarchan sonnet. (2) The Theme of Death – the poem explores the theme of death and remembrance[reference:31]. (3) The Speaker – the speaker asks to be remembered after death, but also to be forgotten if it brings pain. PYQ trends: asked about the sonnet form; the theme of death; compared with other Victorian sonnets.

works.title: Revolution in Poetic Language
works.shortDescription: Kristeva's work that introduces the concepts of the semiotic and the symbolic, and her theory of intertextuality.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Semiotic vs. Symbolic – the semiotic is the pre-linguistic, rhythmic, and instinctual realm; the symbolic is the realm of language and order. (2) Intertextuality – every text is a product of other texts. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the semiotic and symbolic; intertextuality; compared with other poststructuralist theories.

works.title: Rich Like Us
works.shortDescription: Sahgal's most ambitious and complex novel, set in New Delhi during the chaotic time between 1932 and the mid-1970s. It follows the lives of two female protagonists.[reference:65][reference:66]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Historical and Political Fiction – the novel is a historical and political fiction.[reference:67] (2) The Female Protagonists – the novel follows two female protagonists.[reference:68] (3) The Sahitya Akademi Award – the novel won the Sahitya Akademi Award in 1986.[reference:69] PYQ trends: asked about the novel's publication year (1985); the political themes; and the Sahitya Akademi Award.

works.title: Riders to the Sea
works.shortDescription: Synge's one-act tragedy about a mother who loses her last son to the sea. It is a stark and powerful portrait of the harsh life of the Aran Islands.[reference:18][reference:19]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The One-Act Play – the play is a masterpiece of the one-act form. (2) The Setting – the play is set on the Aran Islands, based on Synge's observations there. (3) The Theme of Fate – the mother's tragic acceptance of her fate is the play's emotional core. (4) The Language – the dialogue is spare and poetic.[reference:20] PYQ trends: asked about the one-act form; the Aran Islands setting; the theme of fate.

works.title: Robinson Crusoe
works.shortDescription: Defoe's most famous novel, often regarded as one of the first modern novels. It tells the story of a castaway who survives on a desert island for 28 years.[reference:19]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Modern Novel – often regarded as one of the earliest examples of the modern novel.[reference:20] (2) Realism – Defoe's detailed and realistic narration creates a sense of authenticity. (3) Theme of Survival – the novel explores themes of self-reliance, colonialism, and the relationship between man and nature. (4) Spiritual Autobiography – the novel can be read as a spiritual autobiography, with Crusoe's journey representing a search for God. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Defoe's Robinson Crusoe; the novel's realism; compared with other early novels.

works.title: Roderick Random
works.shortDescription: Smollett's first novel, a picaresque adventure following the fortunes of a Scottish adventurer.[reference:34]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Picaresque Novel – the novel follows the picaresque tradition. (2) Autobiographical Elements – the novel draws on Smollett's own experiences as a surgeon's apprentice. (3) Satire of Society – the novel satirizes the social and political institutions of 18th-century England. PYQ trends: asked about Smollett's novels; the picaresque genre.

works.title: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern Are Dead
works.shortDescription: Stoppard's absurdist comedy, a retelling of Shakespeare's Hamlet from the perspective of the two minor characters. It is a metatheatrical exploration of identity, fate, and the nature of theatre.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Metatheatrical Play – the play is self-conscious about its status as a play. (2) The Perspective – the play is from the perspective of minor characters. (3) The Themes – explores themes of identity, fate, and the meaning of life. (4) The Absurd – the play is influenced by the Theatre of the Absurd. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the metatheatrical elements; the Absurdist style; the relationship to Hamlet.

works.title: Rough Passage
works.shortDescription: Parthasarathy's long poem, which traces the journey of the poet from adolescence to adulthood. It is a meditation on identity, language, and the experience of being an Indian writing in English.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Autobiographical Poem – the poem traces the poet's journey through life. (2) The Theme of Identity – the poem explores the poet's identity as an Indian writing in English. (3) The Significance – the poem is considered a landmark in Indian English poetry. PYQ trends: frequently asked about this poem; the theme of identity; the autobiographical elements.
works.references: Edition: 1977 (Madras: Oxford University Press). Criticism: Parthasarathy (2000).

works.title: S/Z
works.shortDescription: Barthes's detailed analysis of Balzac's short story 'Sarrasine'. It introduces the concept of the 'readerly' and the 'writerly' text.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Readerly vs. Writerly – the readerly text is passive and consumable; the writerly text is active and productive. (2) The Five Codes – Barthes identifies five codes in the text. PYQ trends: asked about the readerly and writerly texts; the five codes; compared with other Barthes works.

works.title: Sailing to Byzantium
works.shortDescription: A meditation on old age, art, and the transcendence of the physical world, set in the ancient city of Byzantium.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Byzantium – symbolizes the ideal world of art and eternity. (2) The Golden Bird – represents the eternal work of art, contrasted with mortal life. (3) The Theme of Aging – the poet seeks to escape the decay of old age through art. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the symbolism of Byzantium; the golden bird; the theme of art vs. nature.
works.references: Edition: 1928 (in The Tower). Criticism: Yeats (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Saint Joan
works.shortDescription: Shaw's historical drama about Joan of Arc, often regarded as his greatest play. It explores faith, nationalism, and the conflict between individual conviction and institutional authority.[reference:3][reference:4]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Historical Joan – Shaw presents Joan as a pragmatic, intelligent figure rather than a mystical saint. (2) The Trial Scene – a powerful courtroom drama. (3) The Epilogue – a dream sequence in which Joan returns to the modern world. (4) The Prologue – the play opens with a prologue set in the French countryside. PYQ trends: asked about the historical accuracy; the trial scene; the play's place in Shaw's œuvre.

works.title: Salome
works.shortDescription: Wilde's Symbolist tragedy, originally written in French, about the biblical figure Salome, who demands the head of John the Baptist.[reference:14]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Symbolist Drama – the play is a key example of Symbolist theatre. (2) The French Language – Wilde wrote the play in French and oversaw its translation into English. (3) The 'Dance of the Seven Veils' – a famous scene in the play. PYQ trends: asked about the Symbolist movement; the play's French origins; compared with Wilde's comedies.

works.title: Samson Agonistes
works.shortDescription: Milton's tragic drama in blank verse, published in 1671. It tells the story of Samson, the biblical hero, who is blinded and imprisoned by the Philistines.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Dramatic Poem – a tragedy modeled on Greek classical drama. (2) Autobiographical Parallels – Milton, who was blind, may have identified with Samson. (3) Theme of Faith – the play explores Samson's struggle with faith and his eventual redemption. (4) The Chorus – uses a Greek-style chorus. PYQ trends: asked about Milton's Samson Agonistes; the autobiographical elements; the theme of faith.

works.title: Sartor Resartus
works.shortDescription: Carlyle's satirical and philosophical novel, ostensibly a treatise on the symbolism and philosophy of clothes. It blends fiction, philosophy, and autobiography.[reference:24][reference:25]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Satirical Philosophy – the work is a biting critique of German Idealism.[reference:26] (2) The Hero – the book follows the life of Diogenes Teufelsdröckh.[reference:27] (3) 'The Everlasting No' – the work contains the famous passage on spiritual crisis. (4) The 'Clothes Philosophy' – the work uses clothes as a symbol for the spiritual and material world.[reference:28] PYQ trends: frequently asked about the 'Clothes Philosophy'; the satire of German Idealism; the autobiographical elements.

works.title: Saturday
works.shortDescription: McEwan's novel set on a single day (Saturday, February 15, 2003) in the life of a London neurosurgeon. It explores themes of domesticity, violence, and the post-9/11 world.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Single-Day Structure – the novel takes place over 24 hours. (2) The Post-9/11 Setting – the novel reflects the anxieties of the post-9/11 world. (3) The Theme of Violence – the protagonist is confronted by violence. PYQ trends: asked about the single-day structure; the post-9/11 context; the theme of violence.

works.title: Schindler's Ark
works.shortDescription: Keneally's novel about Oskar Schindler, a German businessman who saved the lives of over a thousand Jewish refugees during the Holocaust.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Holocaust Novel – the novel is a powerful account of the Holocaust. (2) The Historical Novel – the novel is based on a true story. (3) The Booker Prize – the novel won the Booker Prize in 1982. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the Holocaust theme; the historical fiction; the Booker Prize.

works.title: Scrutiny
works.shortDescription: A literary periodical edited by F.R. Leavis from 1932 to 1953. It was a major forum for the New Criticism in Britain.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Periodical – Scrutiny was a major forum for literary criticism. (2) The Methods – Leavis promoted close reading and moral seriousness. (3) The Influence – the periodical had a significant influence on English literary studies. PYQ trends: asked about Scrutiny; Leavis's role; compared with other critical periodicals.

works.title: Self-Reliance
works.shortDescription: Emerson's famous essay, which argues for the importance of self-reliance, non-conformity, and trusting one's own instincts. It is a key text of Transcendentalism.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Essay – the essay is a key text of Transcendentalism. (2) The Message – Emerson argues for self-reliance and non-conformity. (3) The Famous Line – 'Trust thyself: every heart vibrates to that iron string.' (4) The Influence – the essay influenced many writers. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the Transcendentalist ideas; the theme of self-reliance; the famous line; compared with Thoreau.
works.references: Edition: 1841 (in Essays: First Series). Criticism: Emerson (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Sense and Sensibility
works.shortDescription: Austen's first published novel, contrasting the rational Elinor Dashwood with the emotional Marianne Dashwood.[reference:6]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Title – 'sense' represents Elinor's rationality, 'sensibility' represents Marianne's emotionalism. (2) The Contrast – the novel explores the tension between reason and emotion. PYQ trends: asked about the title; the contrasting heroines; compared with other Austen novels.

works.title: September 1, 1939
works.shortDescription: Auden's famous poem written on the outbreak of World War II, reflecting on the state of the world and the role of the poet[reference:30].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Historical Context – written on the day Germany invaded Poland. (2) The Famous Line – 'We must love one another or die.' (3) The Poet's Role – the poet reflects on the failure of the intellectual class. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the historical context; the famous line; the theme of love and death.

works.title: Seven Types of Ambiguity
works.shortDescription: Empson's groundbreaking work, which identifies and analyzes seven types of ambiguity in poetry. It is a key text of New Criticism.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Seven Types – Empson classifies ambiguity into seven categories. (2) Close Reading – the work is a model of close reading.
