(3) The Influence – the work was highly influential on New Criticism. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the seven types of ambiguity; the close reading method; the influence on New Criticism.
works.references: Edition: 1930 (London: Chatto & Windus). Criticism: Empson (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Shamela
works.shortDescription: Fielding's parody of Richardson's Pamela, in which the virtuous heroine is revealed to be a scheming opportunist.[reference:29]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Parody – Fielding's work is a direct parody of Richardson's sentimental novel. (2) The Shamela Character – the title character is a hypocrite who uses her virtue to manipulate men. (3) The Anti-Sentimental – the novel is a reaction against the sentimentalism of Richardson. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Fielding's Shamela; the parody of Richardson; compared with Joseph Andrews.

works.title: She Stoops to Conquer
works.shortDescription: Goldsmith's most famous play, a comedy of manners that was a great success on the London stage.[reference:13]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Comedy of Manners – the play is a comic satire of social pretensions. (2) The Title – the title refers to the heroine's strategy of 'stooping' to conquer her lover. (3) The Mistaken Identity Plot – the play uses the classic mistaken identity plot. PYQ trends: asked about Goldsmith's She Stoops to Conquer; the comedy of manners; compared with Sheridan's The Rivals.

works.title: She Walks in Beauty
works.shortDescription: One of Byron's most famous short poems, a lyric celebrating the beauty of a woman.[reference:21]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Lyric Form – a short lyric poem. (2) The Theme of Beauty – the poem celebrates the harmony of inner and outer beauty. (3) The Occasion – the poem was inspired by a woman in mourning. PYQ trends: asked about Byron's shorter lyrics.

works.title: She Would If She Could
works.shortDescription: Etherege's comedy about two young women who set out to enjoy the pleasures of London. It is a light-hearted and witty portrayal of Restoration society.[reference:8]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Female Agency – the play focuses on the desires of two young women. (2) Restoration Comedy Conventions – the play follows the conventions of the genre. PYQ trends: asked about Etherege's comedies.

works.title: Silas Marner
works.shortDescription: Eliot's novel about a reclusive weaver who is transformed by the arrival of a golden-haired child.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Redemption Theme – the novel explores the redemption of Silas Marner through love. (2) The Contrast – the novel contrasts the rural community of Raveloe with the industrialized world. PYQ trends: asked about the redemption theme; the contrast between rural and industrial.

works.title: Silex Scintillans
works.shortDescription: Vaughan's major collection of religious poetry, published in two parts (1650 and 1655). The title means 'The Flaming Flint' or 'Sparkling Flint'. Vaughan acknowledges his debt to George Herbert in the second edition.[reference:23][reference:24]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Religious Poetry – Vaughan's poetry explores themes of spiritual longing, mystical experience, and the search for God. (2) Mystical Vision – Vaughan is a mystical poet, seeking a direct experience of the divine.[reference:25] (3) Nature Poetry – Vaughan uses nature imagery to express spiritual truths. (4) Influence of Herbert – Vaughan acknowledges his debt to Herbert, but his style is more mystical and visionary. (5) 'The Retreat' – one of his best-known poems, glorifying the state of infancy as the purest state of life.[reference:26] PYQ trends: asked about Vaughan's religious poetry; the mystical element; compared with Herbert.
works.references: Editions: 1650 (Part I), 1655 (Part II). Criticism: Dickson (2005), Rudrum (1998).

works.title: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight
works.shortDescription: A Middle English alliterative poem written in the late 14th century. It tells the story of Sir Gawain, a knight of King Arthur's Round Table, who accepts a challenge from a mysterious Green Knight and embarks on a journey of honor, temptation, and self-discovery.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Anonymous Author – known as the 'Gawain Poet' or 'Pearl Poet'. (2) Manuscript – known from a single surviving manuscript, the Cotton MS Nero A.x. (3) Alliterative Revival – a prime example of the Alliterative Revival of the 14th century. (4) Ricardian Poets – the poet is a contemporary of Chaucer, Gower, and Langland. (5) Symmetry and Pentangle – the poem is highly structured, with five sections and the pentangle emblem representing Gawain's virtues. (6) Temptation and Deception – the hunting and seduction scenes are meticulously paralleled. PYQ trends: asked about the Gawain manuscript (contains Sir Gawain, Patience, Cleanness, and Pearl); the poet's possible origin in east Cheshire or west Staffordshire; the pentangle's symbolism.
works.references: MS: Cotton Nero A.x. Editions: Tolkien & Gordon (1925), Andrew & Waldron (2007). Criticism: Borroff (1962), Putter (1996).

works.title: Sister Carrie
works.shortDescription: A 1900 novel by Theodore Dreiser about a young woman who moves to the big city to realize her American Dream, first becoming a mistress and later a famous actress.[reference:1]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Naturalism – the novel is a key example of American Naturalism. (2) The American Dream – the novel critiques the American Dream. (3) The Controversy – the novel was controversial for its frank depiction of sexuality. PYQ trends: asked about Naturalism; the critique of the American Dream; the controversial reception.

works.title: Sister Helen
works.shortDescription: Rossetti's dramatic ballad about a woman who uses a wax figure to curse her faithless lover[reference:41].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Ballad Form – the poem follows the traditional ballad form. (2) The Supernatural – the poem uses supernatural elements. (3) The Theme of Revenge – the poem explores the theme of revenge. PYQ trends: asked about the ballad form; the supernatural elements.

works.title: Slaughterhouse-Five
works.shortDescription: A 1969 semi-autobiographic science fiction-infused anti-war novel by Kurt Vonnegut. It blends science fiction with historical facts, notably Vonnegut's experience as a prisoner of war during the firebombing of Dresden.[reference:14]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Postmodernism – the novel is a key example of Postmodernist fiction. (2) The Anti-War Theme – the novel is a powerful anti-war statement. (3) The Nonlinear Narrative – the novel uses a nonlinear, absurdist structure. (4) The Black Humor – the novel is filled with black humor. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Postmodernism; the anti-war theme; the nonlinear narrative; the black humor.

works.title: So Many Hungers!
works.shortDescription: Bhattacharya's first novel, set during the Bengal Famine of 1943-44.[reference:82][reference:83]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Bengal Famine – the novel is set during the Bengal Famine.[reference:84] (2) The Anti-Colonial Theme – the novel explores the impact of British rule. PYQ trends: asked about the novel's publication year (1947); and the Bengal Famine setting.

works.title: Song of Myself
works.shortDescription: The central poem of Leaves of Grass, a long, ecstatic celebration of the self and the universe.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Self – the poem celebrates the self and its connection to the universe. (2) The Free Verse – the poem is written in free verse. (3) The Transcendentalist Influence – the poem reflects Transcendentalist ideas. PYQ trends: asked about the celebration of the self; the free verse; the Transcendentalist influence.
works.references: Edition: 1855 (in Leaves of Grass). Criticism: Whitman (2000).

works.title: Songs and Sonnets
works.shortDescription: Donne's collection of love poetry, published posthumously in 1633. It includes some of his most famous poems, such as 'The Good Morrow', 'The Canonization', 'The Flea', and 'A Valediction: Forbidding Mourning'.[reference:15]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Metaphysical Poetry – Donne is the founder of the metaphysical school of poetry, characterized by intellectual wit, conceits, and paradoxes.[reference:16] (2) Love Poetry – the poems explore the complexities of love, both physical and spiritual.[reference:17] (3) Dramatic Monologue – many poems employ a dramatic, conversational style. (4) Conceits – Donne is famous for his elaborate extended metaphors (e.g., the compass in 'A Valediction'). PYQ trends: frequently asked about Donne's love poetry; the metaphysical conceit; individual poems like 'The Flea' and 'The Canonization'.

works.title: Songs of Innocence and of Experience
works.shortDescription: Blake's most famous collection, pairing poems of innocence with poems of experience to explore the contrasting states of the human soul.[reference:40]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Innocence and Experience – the collection presents paired poems that explore the contrasting states of the human soul. (2) The Lamb and The Tyger – the most famous pair, representing innocence and experience respectively. (3) Illuminated Printing – Blake created his own method of illuminated printing, combining text and image. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Blake's Songs; the Lamb and the Tyger; the illuminated printing.

works.title: Sonnets from the Portuguese
works.shortDescription: A sonnet sequence of 44 poems celebrating Elizabeth Barrett Browning's love for her husband Robert Browning. It is one of the most famous sequences of love poems in English.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Sonnet Sequence – the poems are written in the Petrarchan sonnet form. (2) The Autobiographical Context – the poems chronicle Browning's courtship of Elizabeth Barrett. (3) The Famous Sonnet – Sonnet 43: 'How do I love thee? Let me count the ways'. (4) The Title – the title suggests a translation, but the poems are original[reference:13]. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the sonnet sequence; the Petrarchan sonnet; compared with other sonnet sequences.
works.references: Edition: 1850 (in Poems). Criticism: Barrett Browning (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Sons and Lovers
works.shortDescription: Lawrence's semi-autobiographical novel about Paul Morel, a young man struggling to break free from his mother's emotional grip and find love.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Oedipal Theme – the novel explores the intense bond between Paul and his mother. (2) Working-Class Life – the novel offers a vivid portrait of working-class life in Nottinghamshire. (3) The Bildungsroman – the novel traces Paul's development. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the Oedipal theme; the autobiographical elements; the portrait of working-class life.

works.title: Speculum of the Other Woman
works.shortDescription: Irigaray's work, which argues that Western philosophy is based on a phallocentric logic that excludes women. It is a key text of French feminism.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Critique of Phallocentrism – Western philosophy is based on a phallocentric logic. (2) The Speculum – a metaphor for the way women are reflected in and excluded from patriarchal discourse. (3) The Feminine Economy – a new way of thinking about women's relationship to the world. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the critique of phallocentrism; the speculum; compared with other French feminist theories.

works.title: Standing Female Nude
works.shortDescription: Duffy's first collection of poetry, which won a Scottish Arts Council Award.[reference:39][reference:40]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Collection – Duffy's debut collection. (2) The Title Poem – 'Standing Female Nude' explores the relationship between the artist and the model. (3) The Feminist Perspective – the collection is informed by a feminist perspective. PYQ trends: asked about Duffy's first collection; the feminist themes.

works.title: Steps to the Temple
works.shortDescription: Crashaw's major collection of sacred poetry, published in 1646. The title echoes Herbert's The Temple, indicating Crashaw's debt to Herbert. A second edition was published in 1648.[reference:27]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Sacred Poetry – Crashaw's poetry is intensely religious, often focusing on the suffering of Christ and the saints. (2) Baroque Style – Crashaw's style is characterized by its ornate, sensuous, and emotional language, influenced by Continental Baroque poetry. (3) Influence of Herbert – Crashaw was strongly influenced by George Herbert, but his style is more elaborate and passionate.[reference:28] (4) Carmen Deo Nostro – a later collection of religious poems published in 1652.[reference:29] PYQ trends: asked about Crashaw's religious poetry; the baroque style; compared with Herbert.
works.references: Editions: 1646 (Steps to the Temple), 1652 (Carmen Deo Nostro). Criticism: Cousins (2004), White (2006).

works.title: Strange Meeting
works.shortDescription: A poem about a soldier who encounters an enemy soldier he killed, exploring the shared humanity of soldiers[reference:41].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Encounter – the narrator meets the soldier he killed in a dream. (2) The Theme of Brotherhood – the poem emphasizes the shared humanity of soldiers. (3) The Famous Line – 'I am the enemy you killed, my friend.' PYQ trends: asked about the theme of brotherhood; the dream structure; the encounter with the enemy.

works.title: Structural Anthropology
works.shortDescription: Levi-Strauss's collection of essays, which applies structuralist methods to anthropology. It introduces the concept of the 'elementary structure of kinship' and the analysis of myth.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Structuralism – Levi-Strauss applies structuralist methods to anthropology. (2) The Analysis of Myth – myth is analyzed as a system of binary oppositions. (3) The Elementary Structure of Kinship – kinship is understood as a system of exchange. PYQ trends: asked about Levi-Strauss's structuralism; the analysis of myth; compared with other structuralists.

works.title: Structuralist Poetics
works.shortDescription: Culler's introduction to structuralism and its application to literary studies.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Structuralist Poetics – the application of structuralism to literary studies. (2) The Linguistic Model – the use of linguistics as a model for literary analysis. (3) The Competence – the reader's knowledge of literary conventions. PYQ trends: asked about structuralist poetics; the linguistic model; compared with other introductions.

works.title: Structure, Sign, and Play in the Discourse of the Human Sciences
works.shortDescription: Derrida's seminal lecture, which argues that Western thought is based on a 'metaphysics of presence' and introduces the concept of 'play' in the system of signification.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Critique of Structuralism – Derrida argues that structuralism is based on a 'metaphysics of presence'. (2) The Play of Signification – meaning is the product of 'play' in the system of signs. (3) The Absence of a Center – there is no fixed center of meaning. PYQ trends: one of the most frequently asked essays in NET; the critique of structuralism; the play of signification; the absence of a center.

works.title: Studies in the History of the Renaissance
works.shortDescription: Pater's most influential work, a collection of essays on the Renaissance that is considered a manifesto of Aestheticism. It includes the famous 'Conclusion'.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Aestheticism – the work is considered a manifesto of Aestheticism. (2) The 'Conclusion' – urges readers to live with a 'hard, gem-like flame'. (3) Impressionistic Criticism – the critic should explore his own aesthetic responses. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the 'Conclusion'; the manifesto of Aestheticism; impressionistic criticism.

works.title: Summoned by Bells
works.shortDescription: Betjeman's verse autobiography, which was an immediate success.[reference:25] Philip Larkin praised it for its 'astonishing command of detail'.[reference:26]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Verse Autobiography – Betjeman's account of his own life in verse. (2) The Success – the work was an immediate success. (3) The Detail – the work is noted for its vivid and circumstantial detail. PYQ trends: asked about Betjeman's verse autobiography; the Larkin praise.

works.title: Swami and Friends
works.shortDescription: Narayan's debut novel, which introduced the world to the fictional town of Malgudi. It follows the innocent adventures of a schoolboy named Swaminathan.[reference:13][reference:14]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Fictional Town of Malgudi – the novel introduced the iconic setting of Malgudi.[reference:15] (2) Graham Greene's Influence – the novel was published under the care of Graham Greene.[reference:16] (3) The Bildungsroman – the novel traces the development of a young boy. PYQ trends: asked about the novel's publication year (1935); the character of Swaminathan; and the creation of Malgudi.[reference:17][reference:18]
works.references: Edition: 1935 (London: Hamish Hamilton). Criticism: Narayan (2000), Walsh (1982).

works.title: Tales from Shakespeare
works.shortDescription: A collection of prose adaptations of Shakespeare's plays, written by Charles and Mary Lamb for children.[reference:48]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Adaptation – the work adapts Shakespeare's plays for a young audience. (2) The Collaboration – the work was written with his sister Mary. PYQ trends: asked about Lamb's prose works.

works.title: Tamburlaine the Great
works.shortDescription: Marlowe's first major play, a two-part heroic tragedy about a Scythian shepherd who rises to become a world conqueror. The play defied medieval theatrical conventions and replaced divine fate with sheer human will[reference:5].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Major Success – performed in 1587, it was Marlowe's breakthrough play and established him as the dominant voice of the new professional theatre[reference:6]. (2) Blank Verse – the play is famous for its 'mighty lines' and elevated blank verse, revolutionizing English drama[reference:7]. (3) Tragic Hero – Tamburlaine is an ambitious figure who strives beyond human limits[reference:8]. (4) Rejection of Morality Play – the play shifted attention from morality plays to psychological exploration[reference:9]. PYQ trends: asked about Marlowe's contribution to blank verse; the character of Tamburlaine as a tragic hero; the play's influence on Elizabethan drama.
works.references: Edition: 1590 (published in two parts). Criticism: Wilson (1969), Cartelli (2002).

works.title: Tara
works.shortDescription: Dattani's play about conjoined twins and the gender bias that leads to their separation, exploring themes of identity and gender discrimination.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Gender Theme – the play explores gender discrimination. (2) The Twins – the play focuses on the lives of conjoined twins. (3) The Family – the play explores the dynamics of a dysfunctional family. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the gender themes; the twins; the family dynamics.

works.title: Tender Is the Night
works.shortDescription: Fitzgerald's novel about the decline of a psychiatrist and his wife, set in the French Riviera.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Decline – the novel traces the decline of a marriage. (2) The Setting – the novel is set on the French Riviera. PYQ trends: asked about the decline of the marriage; the setting.

works.title: Tess of the d'Urbervilles
works.shortDescription: Hardy's novel about the tragic fate of Tess Durbeyfield, a pure woman who is destroyed by the forces of society and fate.[reference:23]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Tragic Heroine – Tess is one of the great tragic heroines of English literature. (2) The Critique of Society – the novel critiques Victorian social hypocrisy and the double standard. (3) The Subtitle – 'A Pure Woman Faithfully Presented' is a provocative subtitle. (4) The Famous Line – 'Justice was done, and the President of the Immortals had ended his sport with Tess.' PYQ trends: frequently asked about the tragic heroine; the social critique; the subtitle; the famous line.

works.title: The Edible Woman
works.shortDescription: Atwood's first novel, a funny and terrifying story about a young woman who feels consumed by society's expectations.[reference:37]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Novel – Atwood's debut novel. (2) The Feminist Theme – the novel explores the pressures on women in consumer society. (3) The Edible Woman – the title refers to the protagonist's feeling of being consumed. PYQ trends: asked about Atwood's first novel; the feminist themes.

works.title: The Act of Reading
works.shortDescription: Iser's foundational work of reader-response theory, which argues that meaning is created in the interaction between the reader and the text.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Reader-Response Theory – meaning is created in the interaction between the reader and the text. (2) The Implied Reader – the reader that the text anticipates. (3) The Repertoire – the familiar patterns and conventions the reader brings to the text. PYQ trends: frequently asked about reader-response theory; the implied reader; compared with other reader-response theorists.

works.title: The Advancement of Learning
works.shortDescription: Bacon's major philosophical work advocating for educational reform and the advancement of empirical science. It argues for the importance of knowledge and the need to reform the educational system.[reference:0]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Advocacy for Science – Bacon argues for the advancement of learning through empirical observation and experimentation. (2) Classification of Knowledge – Bacon classifies knowledge into history, poetry, and philosophy. (3) Idols of the Mind – introduces the concept of the 'Idols' (Idols of the Tribe, Cave, Marketplace, Theater) that hinder human understanding. (4) Influence – laid the foundation for the scientific method. PYQ trends: asked about Bacon's contribution to science and philosophy; the Idols; the classification of knowledge.

works.title: The Adventures of Augie March
works.shortDescription: A picaresque novel by Saul Bellow, published in 1953. It features the eponymous Augie March, who grows up during the Great Depression, and is an example of Bildungsroman.[reference:11]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Picaresque – the novel is a modern picaresque tale. (2) The Bildungsroman – the novel traces the development of an individual. (3) The Themes – explores the restless pursuit of meaning in the 20th century. PYQ trends: asked about the picaresque form; the Bildungsroman; the themes of identity and meaning.

works.title: The Affective Fallacy
works.shortDescription: A companion essay to 'The Intentional Fallacy', arguing that the reader's emotional response to a work is irrelevant to its objective interpretation.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Affective Fallacy – the fallacy of judging a work by its effect on the reader. (2) The Work as Objective – the work should be judged objectively. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the affective fallacy; compared with the intentional fallacy.
works.references: Edition: 1949 (in The Sewanee Review). Criticism: Wimsatt & Beardsley (2000).

works.title: The Age of Anxiety
works.shortDescription: Auden's Pulitzer Prize-winning long poem, a dramatic monologue set in a New York bar during World War II[reference:32].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Setting – a New York bar during wartime. (2) The Theme of Anxiety – the poem explores the psychological state of modern humanity. (3) The Pulitzer Prize – the poem won the Pulitzer Prize in 1948[reference:33]. PYQ trends: asked about Auden's later works; the Pulitzer Prize.

works.title: The Alchemist
works.shortDescription: Jonson's comedy about a trio of con artists who trick a series of gullible victims into believing they can turn base metals into gold. It is considered one of Jonson's masterpieces[reference:22][reference:23].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Satire of Human Folly – the play satirizes human greed, credulity, and the desire for easy wealth[reference:24]. (2) The Three Con Artists – Subtle (the alchemist), Doll Common (the prostitute), and Face (the housekeeper). (3) Unity of Time, Place, and Action – the play adheres to the classical unities, taking place in a single house over a few hours[reference:25]. (4) The Victim Types – each victim represents a different form of human folly: the gambler, the merchant, the Puritan, etc. PYQ trends: asked about the satire; the adherence to classical unities; compared with Volpone; the character types[reference:26][reference:27].
works.references: Editions: Norton Critical Edition. Criticism: Donaldson (2011), Jonson (1967).

works.title: The Ambassadors
works.shortDescription: James's novel about Lewis Lambert Strether, who travels to Paris to persuade his fiancée's son to return to America.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The International Theme – the novel explores the contrast between American and European cultures. (2) The 'Point of View' – the novel is told from Strether's limited perspective. (3) The Theme of Regret – Strether reflects on his wasted life. PYQ trends: asked about the international theme; the limited point of view; the theme of regret.

works.title: The American Scholar
works.shortDescription: Emerson's famous address, often called 'America's intellectual Declaration of Independence'. It argues for American intellectual independence from Europe.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The American Scholar – Emerson defines the role of the American intellectual. (2) The Declaration – the address is called 'America's intellectual Declaration of Independence'. (3) The Themes – includes self-reliance, nature, and the importance of action. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the 'American Scholar' as a declaration; the themes of self-reliance and nature.

works.title: The Archaeology of Knowledge
works.shortDescription: Foucault's methodological work, which introduces the concept of the 'archive' and the 'discursive formation' as the basis for analyzing knowledge and power.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Archive – the system that governs what can be said. (2) The Discursive Formation – the rules that structure discourse. (3) The Methodology – Foucault's 'archaeological' method analyzes the conditions of possibility for knowledge. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the archive; the discursive formation; the archaeological method.

works.title: The Atlantic
works.shortDescription: An American magazine founded in 1857, known for its literary and cultural criticism.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Magazine – a major American literary magazine. (2) The Contributions – published works by major American writers. (3) The Influence – significant influence on American literary culture. PYQ trends: asked about American literary magazines; The Atlantic's role; compared with other magazines.

works.title: The Bard
works.shortDescription: Gray's Pindaric ode, a dramatic and intense poem about a Welsh bard who curses Edward I and prophesies the future of Britain.[reference:8]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Pindaric Ode – the poem is written in the Pindaric ode form, characterized by its irregular meter and elevated tone. (2) The Sublime – the poem is a key example of the sublime in literature. (3) Welsh Nationalism – the poem celebrates Welsh history and culture. PYQ trends: asked about Gray's Pindaric odes; the sublime; the poem's historical context.

works.title: The Beaux' Stratagem
works.shortDescription: Farquhar's most famous play, a comedy of manners about two gentlemen who travel to the country in search of wealthy wives. It was written on his deathbed.[reference:15]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Last Play – Farquhar wrote the play while dying; it was his final work. (2) The 'Stratagem' – the two heroes pretend to be master and servant to gain access to wealthy families. (3) The Divorce – the play features a controversial happy ending that includes the separation of a married couple.[reference:16] (4) The Recruiting Officer – Farquhar drew on his own experiences as a recruiting officer for his earlier play. PYQ trends: frequently asked as a key Restoration comedy; the play's controversial ending; compared with other comedies.

works.title: The Bell Jar
works.shortDescription: Plath's only novel, a semi-autobiographical account of a young woman's mental breakdown and suicide attempt.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Semi-Autobiographical Novel – the novel draws on Plath's own experiences. (2) The Theme of Mental Illness – the novel explores the protagonist's descent into depression. (3) The Bell Jar – the title symbolizes the protagonist's feeling of being trapped. PYQ trends: frequently asked about The Bell Jar; the autobiographical elements; the theme of mental illness.
works.references: Edition: 1963 (under pseudonym Victoria Lucas). Criticism: Plath (2000).

works.title: The Birthday Party
works.shortDescription: Pinter's first full-length play, a comedy of menace about a man named Stanley who is visited by two mysterious strangers who seem to be after him.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Comedy of Menace – the play is a key example of Pinter's 'comedy of menace'. (2) The Absurd – the play is influenced by the Theatre of the Absurd. (3) The Mysterious Strangers – Goldberg and McCann represent unknown threats. (4) The Birthday Party – the party becomes a scene of psychological terror. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Pinter's comedies of menace; the Absurdist elements; the mysterious strangers.

works.title: The Blessed Damozel
works.shortDescription: Rossetti's early poem about a woman in heaven who longs for her lover on earth[reference:39].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Pre-Raphaelite Style – the poem is characterized by its rich, sensuous imagery[reference:40]. (2) The Theme of Love – the poem explores the power of love even beyond death. (3) The Narrative – the poem tells the story of a love that transcends death. PYQ trends: asked about the Pre-Raphaelite style; the theme of love; compared with other Pre-Raphaelite poems.

works.title: The Bluest Eye
works.shortDescription: Morrison's first novel, about a young black girl who longs for blue eyes because she believes they will make her beautiful.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Novel – Morrison's debut novel. (2) The Theme of Beauty – the novel explores the damaging effects of white beauty standards. (3) The Theme of Race – the novel explores the experience of growing up black in America. PYQ trends: asked about Morrison's first novel; the theme of beauty; the theme of race.

works.title: The Book of the Duchess
works.shortDescription: Chaucer's first major work, a dream-poem written in octosyllabic couplets. It is an elegy for Blanche of Lancaster, wife of John of Gaunt, who died in 1368.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Major Work – Chaucer's earliest major poem. (2) Dream Vision – follows the popular medieval genre of the dream vision. (3) Elegy – also known as 'the death of Blaunche'. (4) French Influence – heavily influenced by French courtly love poetry, particularly the Roman de la Rose. PYQ trends: asked in NET Jun 2012, Jun 2015; often compared with other dream visions like The House of Fame.
works.references: Editions: Benson (1988), Riverside Chaucer. Sources: Roman de la Rose, works of Machaut.

works.title: The Bothie of Tober-na-Vuolich
works.shortDescription: Clough's narrative poem about a group of Oxford scholars retreating to the Scottish Highlands[reference:58].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Narrative Poem – the poem is a long narrative in classical hexameters[reference:59]. (2) The Theme of Doubt – the poem reflects the religious doubt of the mid-19th century[reference:60]. (3) The Victorian Crisis of Faith – the poem is a key text of the Victorian crisis of faith[reference:61]. PYQ trends: asked about Clough's narrative poetry; the Victorian crisis of faith; the use of classical hexameters.

works.title: The Call of the Wild
works.shortDescription: A short adventure novel by Jack London, published in 1903 and set in Yukon, Canada, during the 1890s Klondike Gold Rush. The central character is a dog named Buck.[reference:4]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Naturalism – the novel is a key example of American Naturalism. (2) The Survival Instinct – the novel explores the struggle for survival in the wilderness. (3) The Animal Protagonist – the novel is told from the perspective of a dog. PYQ trends: asked about Naturalism; the survival instinct; the animal protagonist.

works.title: The Canterbury Tales
works.shortDescription: Chaucer's magnum opus, a collection of twenty-four stories in 17,000 lines told by 30 pilgrims. Composed in Middle English between 1387 and 1400. The work opens with pilgrims gathering at the Tabard Inn in London to visit the shrine of Saint Thomas Becket at Canterbury Cathedral.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Social Diversity – the work depicts the social classes, customs, and language of 14th-century England. Instead of revealing England's divisions, it revels in its diversity. (2) Frame Narrative – the pilgrims are asked to participate in a storytelling contest. (3) Incomplete Work – Chaucer intended 120 tales (4 from each pilgrim) but only completed 24. (4) Language – Chaucer chose East Midland as a base for English because it was his ancestor's language as well as the most spoken at that time. PYQ trends: most frequently asked work in NET; John Dryden's comment 'Here is God's Plenty'; Matthew Arnold's 'lack high seriousness'; the Wife of Bath's social mobility; the General Prologue as a social document.
works.references: MS: Multiple (Hengwrt, Ellesmere). Editions: Benson (1988), Riverside Chaucer (1987). Criticism: Kittredge (1912), Patterson (1991).

works.title: The Cantos
works.shortDescription: Pound's monumental epic, a vast and complex poem that spans history, economics, and culture. It is one of the most ambitious works of modernist poetry[reference:21].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Epic – the poem is an attempt to write a modern epic. (2) The Ideogrammic Method – Pound uses juxtaposition of images and ideas to create meaning[reference:22]. (3) The Themes – includes history, economics, politics, and cultural renewal. PYQ trends: asked about Pound's epic; the ideogrammic method; the poem's complexity.

works.title: The Caretaker
works.shortDescription: Pinter's most famous play, a three-character drama about two brothers and a tramp who becomes their caretaker. It explores power, identity, and the nature of communication.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Three Characters – Aston, Mick, and Davies. (2) The Theme of Power – the play explores the dynamics of power and control. (3) The Pinter Pause – the play is famous for its use of pauses. (4) The Open Ending – the play ends ambiguously. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the Pinter pause; the theme of power; the characters; compared with The Birthday Party.

works.title: The Castaway
works.shortDescription: Cowper's moving poem about a sailor washed overboard, often read as an allegory of his own spiritual despair.[reference:15]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Allegory – the poem is often read as an allegory of Cowper's own spiritual despair. (2) The Theme of Isolation – the poem explores the theme of isolation and abandonment. PYQ trends: asked about Cowper's later poems; the theme of despair.

works.title: The Charge of the Light Brigade
works.shortDescription: Tennyson's famous poem commemorating the Charge of the Light Brigade at the Battle of Balaclava during the Crimean War.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) War Poetry – the poem celebrates the heroism of the soldiers. (2) The Rhythm – the poem's rhythm mimics the galloping of horses. (3) The Famous Line – 'Theirs not to reason why, / Theirs but to do and die'. PYQ trends: asked about war poetry; the Crimean War; the poem's rhythm.

works.title: The Cocktail Party
works.shortDescription: Eliot's verse comedy, a modern adaptation of Euripides' Alcestis. It was a commercial success.[reference:40]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Greek Myth – based on Euripides' Alcestis. (2) The Comedy – the play is a comedy, unlike Eliot's earlier plays. (3) The Commercial Success – the play was a hit on the West End. PYQ trends: asked about Eliot's later plays; the comic tone; compared with Murder in the Cathedral.

works.title: The Collected Poems of A. D. Hope
works.shortDescription: A collection of poems by A. D. Hope, one of Australia's most important poets. His work is characterized by its classical form, wit, and exploration of love, death, and the human condition.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Australian Poetry – Hope is a major figure in Australian poetry. (2) Classical Form – Hope's poetry is known for its formal elegance and use of classical forms. (3) Thematic Range – explores love, death, and the human condition. PYQ trends: asked about Australian poetry; Hope's classical style; the thematic range.

works.title: The Color Purple
works.shortDescription: Walker's epistolary novel about Celie, a young black woman in the American South who overcomes abuse and oppression to find her voice and independence.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Epistolary Novel – the novel is written in the form of letters. (2) The Feminist Theme – the novel explores the empowerment of women. (3) The Racial Theme – the novel explores the experience of African Americans in the South. (4) The Pulitzer Prize – the novel won the Pulitzer Prize in 1983. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the epistolary form; the feminist themes; the Pulitzer Prize.

works.title: The Colossus and Other Poems
works.shortDescription: Plath's first major collection, published in England in 1960. It established her as a significant new voice in poetry.[reference:4]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Collection – Plath's debut collection, published two years before her death. (2) The Title Poem – 'The Colossus' is a meditation on the poet's father. (3) The Style – the poems are characterized by their intensity, control, and powerful imagery. PYQ trends: asked about Plath's first collection; the title poem; the early style.

works.title: The Comical Revenge
works.shortDescription: Etherege's first play, a comedy of intrigue that interweaves four separate plots. It was an immediate success.[reference:6]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Success – the play established Etherege's reputation. (2) Multiple Plots – the play interweaves four separate plots, including a serious love story and a comic intrigue. (3) Cavalier Gentry – the play offers a partisan portrait of the Cavalier gentry.[reference:7] PYQ trends: asked about Etherege's early works.

works.title: The Communist Manifesto
works.shortDescription: Marx and Engels's political pamphlet, which argues for the overthrow of the bourgeoisie by the proletariat. It is a foundational text of Marxist theory.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Class Struggle – history is the history of class struggles. (2) The Proletarian Revolution – the proletariat will overthrow the bourgeoisie. (3) The Critique of Capitalism – capitalism is exploitative. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the class struggle; the proletarian revolution; the critique of capitalism.

works.title: The Confederacy
works.shortDescription: Vanbrugh's later comedy, an adaptation of a French play by Dancourt. It is a satire on marital and financial intrigue.[reference:14]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Adaptation – Vanbrugh adapts Dancourt's Les Bourgeoises à la Mode. (2) Financial Satire – the play satirizes the pursuit of wealth and the complexities of marital contracts. PYQ trends: asked about Vanbrugh's later works.

works.title: The Countess Cathleen
works.shortDescription: Yeats's first poetic play, a verse drama about a noblewoman who sells her soul to save her tenants from starvation.[reference:32]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Poetic Play – Yeats's first major dramatic work. (2) The Mythological Subject – based on Irish mythology. (3) The Theme of Sacrifice – the Countess sacrifices herself for others. PYQ trends: asked about Yeats's early plays; the mythological element; the verse form.

works.title: The Countess of Pembroke's Arcadia
works.shortDescription: Sidney's pastoral romance, a prose work interspersed with poems. Written for his sister Mary, Countess of Pembroke. It exists in two versions: the 'Old Arcadia' and the revised 'New Arcadia'.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Pastoral Romance – combines prose narrative with poetry, following the classical pastoral tradition. (2) Two Versions – the Old Arcadia (c. 1580) is a complete romance; the New Arcadia (c. 1584) is an unfinished revision. (3) Influence – one of the most influential prose works of the Elizabethan age, influencing later pastoral and romance writing. PYQ trends: asked about Sidney's prose works; the pastoral mode; the relationship between the two versions.

works.title: The Country Wife
works.shortDescription: Wycherley's most famous play, a bawdy Restoration comedy that satirizes the hypocrisy of London society. It is known for its frank treatment of sexual desire.[reference:9]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Bawdy Comedy – the play is notorious for its sexual frankness. (2) The 'Country Wife' – Margery Pinchwife, a naive country girl who is brought to London and becomes the object of desire. (3) Horner – the rake who pretends to be a eunuch to gain access to married women. (4) The China Scene – a famous scene in which Horner's 'china' is used as a double entendre. (5) Wycherley's Style – the play reflects Wycherley's personal conflict between puritanism and sensuality.[reference:10] PYQ trends: frequently asked as a key Restoration comedy; the character of Horner; the china scene; compared with other Restoration comedies.

works.title: The Criterion
works.shortDescription: A literary periodical edited by T.S. Eliot from 1922 to 1939. It was a major venue for modernist literature.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Periodical – The Criterion was a major modernist periodical. (2) The Editorial Role – Eliot used the periodical to promote modernist literature. (3) The Contributors – included Virginia Woolf, Ezra Pound, and other modernist figures. PYQ trends: asked about The Criterion; Eliot's role; compared with other modernist periodicals.

works.title: The Critic as Artist
works.shortDescription: Wilde's dialogue arguing that criticism is a creative art form, equal to and independent of the work of art it interprets.[reference:73]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Criticism as Art – Wilde argues that criticism is a profoundly creative endeavor.[reference:74] (2) Equality of Criticism and Art – criticism is not subordinate to art but can precede it.[reference:75] (3) The Dialogue – the essay is written as a dialogue between Ernest and Gilbert.[reference:76] PYQ trends: frequently asked about the argument for criticism as art; the dialogue form; compared with other critical theories.

works.title: The Crucible
works.shortDescription: Miller's play about the Salem witch trials, written as an allegory for McCarthyism and the anti-communist hysteria of the 1950s.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Allegory – the play is an allegory for McCarthyism. (2) The Historical Setting – the play is set in Salem, Massachusetts in 1692. (3) The Theme of Hysteria – the play explores the dangers of mass hysteria and false accusations. (4) The Crucible – the title symbolizes the test of conscience. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the allegory for McCarthyism; the historical setting; the theme of hysteria; the title's meaning.

works.title: The Crying of Lot 49
works.shortDescription: Pynchon's novella about Oedipa Maas, a young woman who is named executrix of a deceased ex-lover's estate and discovers a secret organization.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Postmodern Novel – the novel is a key example of postmodern fiction. (2) The Paranoid Theme – the novel explores the theme of paranoia. (3) The Secret Organization – the Tristero organization is never fully explained. (4) The Open Ending – the novel ends ambiguously. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the postmodern elements; the theme of paranoia; the open ending.

works.title: The Dance of the Eunuchs
works.shortDescription: Das's famous poem, which explores themes of love, loss, and alienation through the image of eunuchs dancing.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Symbolism – the eunuchs symbolize alienation and loss. (2) The Feminist Theme – the poem explores the experience of being an outsider. (3) The Confessional Style – the poem is intensely personal and confessional. PYQ trends: frequently asked about this poem; the symbolism of the eunuchs; the feminist and confessional themes.
works.references: Edition: 1965 (in Summer in Calcutta). Criticism: Das (2000).

works.title: The Dark Holds No Terrors
works.shortDescription: Deshpande's novel about a woman's struggle to find her own identity. It was first published by Vikas Publishing House in 1980.[reference:75][reference:76]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Feminist Theme – the novel explores a woman's struggle for identity. (2) The Publication Year – the novel was first published in 1980.[reference:77] PYQ trends: asked about the novel's publication year (1980); and the feminist themes.
works.references: Edition: 1980 (New Delhi: Vikas Publishing House). Criticism: Deshpande (2000).

works.title: The Death of the Author
works.shortDescription: Barthes's seminal essay, which argues that the author's intention and biography are irrelevant to the interpretation of a text. It is a key text of poststructuralism.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Death of the Author – the author is not the source of meaning; meaning is produced by the reader. (2) The Birth of the Reader – the reader is the site of meaning. (3) The Refutation of Intentionalism – the author's intention is irrelevant. PYQ trends: one of the most frequently asked essays in NET; the death of the author; the birth of the reader.

works.title: The Decay of Lying
works.shortDescription: Wilde's dialogue on aesthetics, arguing for the supremacy of art over nature and the creative power of the imagination.[reference:70]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Art over Nature – Wilde argues that art is superior to nature. (2) Realism vs. Romanticism – Wilde argues against realism and for romanticism. (3) The Dialogue Form – the essay is written as a dialogue between Cyril and Vivian.[reference:71] (4) Famous Line – 'As a method, realism is a complete failure'.[reference:72] PYQ trends: frequently asked about the dialogue form; the argument for art over nature; the critique of realism.

works.title: The Defence of Poesy
works.shortDescription: Sidney's seminal work of literary criticism, also known as An Apology for Poetry. It is a response to Stephen Gosson's The School of Abuse (1579), defending poetry as a valuable human endeavor.[reference:9]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Three Kinds of Poetry – religious poetry (imitating God), philosophical poetry (moral instruction), and right poetry (imaginative creation).[reference:10] (2) Poetry as Superior to History and Philosophy – poetry combines the universality of philosophy with the particularity of history. (3) Classical and Renaissance Sources – draws on Aristotle, Horace, and Italian Renaissance theorists. (4) Response to Gosson – Sidney's treatise is a direct reply to Puritan attacks on poetry. (5) Defence of the English Language – argues for the capacity of English to achieve poetic greatness. (6) The Poet as Creator – Sidney argues that the poet creates a golden world, improving upon nature. (7) Refutation of Plato – Sidney refutes Plato's charge that poets are liars, arguing that the poet 'nothing affirms, and therefore never lieth.'[reference:11] PYQ trends: one of the most frequently asked critical texts in NET; asked about the three kinds of poetry; Sidney's arguments for poetry; compared with other Renaissance critical texts (e.g., Jonson's Timber, Puttenham's Arte of English Poesie).
works.references: Edition: 1595 (published as An Apology for Poetry). Criticism: Leitch (2001), Heninger (1999).

works.title: The Deserted Village
works.shortDescription: Goldsmith's pastoral poem, mourning the depopulation of the English countryside and the loss of traditional rural life.[reference:10]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Pastoral Poetry – the poem is a pastoral elegy for the lost rural community. (2) Social Critique – the poem critiques the enclosure movement and the commercialization of agriculture. (3) Heroic Couplets – the poem is written in heroic couplets. (4) The Character of the Village Preacher – a memorable portrait of the simple and virtuous village priest. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Goldsmith's The Deserted Village; the pastoral tradition; the social critique; the character of the village preacher.

works.title: The Dial
works.shortDescription: An American literary magazine, published in various forms from 1840 to 1929. It was a key publication for Transcendentalist and later modernist writers.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Magazine – a major American literary magazine. (2) Transcendentalism – the early Dial was a key Transcendentalist publication. (3) Modernism – the later Dial published modernist writers. PYQ trends: asked about American literary magazines; The Dial's role; compared with other magazines.

works.title: The Dialogic Imagination
works.shortDescription: A collection of Bakhtin's essays, which introduce his key concepts of dialogism, heteroglossia, carnivalesque, and chronotope. It is a foundational text of poststructuralist literary theory.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Dialogism – the idea that language is inherently dialogic and shaped by interaction. (2) Heteroglossia – the multiplicity of voices and languages within a text. (3) Carnivalesque – the subversive, liberating force of popular culture. (4) Chronotope – the relationship between time and space in literature. PYQ trends: one of the most frequently asked texts in NET; dialogism; heteroglossia; carnivalesque; chronotope.
works.references: Edition: 1975 (in Russian), 1981 (English translation). Criticism: Bakhtin (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: The Diary of John Evelyn
works.shortDescription: Evelyn's diary, covering the period from 1640 to 1706. It offers a gentlemanly and scholarly account of life in 17th-century England, including the Civil War, the Commonwealth, and the Restoration.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Historical Document – a valuable record of 17th-century England. (2) Gentlemanly Perspective – Evelyn was a Royalist and a virtuoso, and his diary reflects his social and intellectual interests. (3) Compared with Pepys – Evelyn's diary is more formal and less intimate than Pepys's. (4) First Publication – first published in 1818. PYQ trends: asked about Evelyn's diary; compared with Pepys's diary; the historical significance.

works.title: The Diary of Samuel Pepys
works.shortDescription: Pepys's famous diary, covering the period from 1660 to 1669. It offers a vivid and detailed account of life in Restoration London, including eyewitness accounts of the Great Plague (1665) and the Great Fire of London (1666).
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Historical Document – an invaluable record of Restoration London. (2) Personal and Intimate – the diary reveals Pepys's personal life, including his ambitions, fears, and private affairs. (3) Great Events – includes detailed accounts of the Great Plague, the Great Fire, and the Dutch War. (4) First Publication – the diary was first published in 1825, long after Pepys's death. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Pepys's diary; the historical significance; compared with Evelyn's diary.

works.title: The Double Dealer
works.shortDescription: Congreve's comedy about a scheming villain who attempts to ruin the happiness of two young lovers. It was less successful than his other plays.[reference:4]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Title Character – the 'double dealer' is Maskwell, a character who deceives everyone. (2) Darker Tone – the play has a darker tone than Congreve's other comedies. PYQ trends: asked about Congreve's comedies.

works.title: The Duchess of Malfi
works.shortDescription: Webster's masterpiece, a Jacobean revenge tragedy about a young widow who defies her brothers by secretly marrying. The play explores themes of corruption, power, and gender[reference:39][reference:40].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Source – based on a true story from the Italian novella by Matteo Bandello. (2) The Duchess – a strong female protagonist who asserts her autonomy against patriarchal authority[reference:41]. (3) Bosola – the cynic spy who serves as the play's moral compass[reference:42]. (4) The Echo Scene – a famous scene in which the Duchess hears echoes of her own voice. (5) The Play's Language – known for its lyrical and poetic dialogue[reference:43]. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the character of the Duchess; the theme of corruption; compared with The White Devil; the play's sources[reference:44][reference:45].
works.references: Sources: Bandello's Novelle. Editions: Norton Critical Edition. Criticism: Webster (2001), Schoenbaum (1987).

works.title: The Dunciad
works.shortDescription: Pope's satirical masterpiece, a mock-epic attack on dullness and bad writing. It was published in several versions, with the final four-book version appearing in 1743.[reference:2]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Satire of Dullness – the poem is a savage satire on literary hacks, bad poets, and the decline of taste. (2) The Goddess Dulness – the central figure of the poem, who presides over the kingdom of dullness. (3) The Hero – the poem's hero is a dunce, representing the worst of contemporary writers. (4) The Scriblerus Club – the poem grew out of Pope's association with the Scriblerus Club, a group of writers including Swift and Gay. PYQ trends: asked about Pope's satire; the character of Dulness; the Scriblerus Club.
works.references: Editions: 1728 (three books), 1743 (four books). Criticism: Pope (2000), Mack (1985).

works.title: The Edinburgh Review
works.shortDescription: A Scottish literary and political periodical founded in 1802, known for its Whig politics and its role in promoting Romantic literature.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Periodical – a major literary and political periodical. (2) The Politics – known for its Whig stance. (3) The Role – promoted Romantic literature. PYQ trends: asked about periodicals of the Romantic period; The Edinburgh Review's role; compared with other periodicals.

works.title: The Ego and the Id
works.shortDescription: Freud's work that fully articulates his structural model of the psyche: the id, ego, and superego.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Id – the unconscious, instinctual part of the psyche. (2) The Ego – the conscious, rational part of the psyche. (3) The Superego – the moral, internalized part of the psyche. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the id, ego, and superego; compared with other psychoanalytic models.

works.title: The Egoist
works.shortDescription: Meredith's comic masterpiece, a satire of egotism and the institution of marriage, centered on the character of Sir Willoughby Patterns.[reference:44]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Comic Novel – the novel is a brilliant satire of egotism. (2) The Title Character – Sir Willoughby Patterns is one of the great comic characters in English literature. (3) The Essay on Comedy – Meredith prefaced the novel with an essay on comedy. PYQ trends: asked about the comic novel; the character of Sir Willoughby; the essay on comedy.

works.title: The Emperor of Ice-Cream
works.shortDescription: A poem from Wallace Stevens's first collection of poetry, Harmonium (1923). It is a short, compact poem of sixteen lines, divided into two stanzas, connecting the reader to an ordinary funeral.[reference:6][reference:7]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Modernism – the poem is a key example of Modernist poetry. (2) The Imagery – the poem uses vivid, concrete imagery. (3) The Theme – the poem explores the relationship between reality and imagination. PYQ trends: asked about Modernism; the imagery; the theme of reality and imagination.

works.title: The English Patient
works.shortDescription: Ondaatje's novel set in an Italian villa at the end of World War II, exploring themes of love, identity, and the aftermath of war.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Postmodern Novel – the novel is a key example of postmodern fiction. (2) The Fragmented Narrative – the novel uses a fragmented, non-linear narrative. (3) The Themes – explores love, identity, and the aftermath of war. (4) The Booker Prize – the novel won the Booker Prize in 1992. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the postmodern elements; the fragmented narrative; the themes; the Booker Prize.
works.references: Edition: 1992 (Toronto: McClelland & Stewart). Criticism: Ondaatje (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: The Essays
works.shortDescription: Bacon's most famous work, a collection of short prose pieces on various topics such as truth, death, love, studies, and friendship. First published in 1597 with 10 essays, expanded to 38 in the 1612 edition, and finalized with 58 essays in the 1625 edition.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Aphoristic Style – Bacon's essays are characterized by their concise, epigrammatic, and sententious style. (2) Practical Wisdom – the essays offer practical advice on moral, political, and domestic matters. (3) Universal Themes – topics include 'Of Truth', 'Of Death', 'Of Studies', 'Of Friendship', 'Of Revenge', and 'Of Ambition'. (4) Influence – Bacon is considered the father of the English essay. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Bacon as an essayist; his aphoristic style; individual essays like 'Of Studies' and 'Of Friendship'.

works.title: The Eve of St. Agnes
works.shortDescription: Keats's narrative poem, a medieval romance about the love of Porphyro and Madeline on the eve of St. Agnes.[reference:38]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Narrative Poem – a romantic narrative in Spenserian stanzas. (2) The Medieval Setting – the poem is set in a medieval castle. (3) The Sensuous Imagery – the poem is noted for its rich, sensuous imagery. PYQ trends: asked about Keats's The Eve of St. Agnes; the Spenserian stanza; the sensuous imagery.

works.title: The Everyman
works.shortDescription: A series of cheap, accessible editions of literary classics, published by J.M. Dent and Sons from 1912 onwards.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Series – a series of classic literature in accessible editions. (2) The Mission – to make literature affordable and accessible. (3) The Influence – played a major role in the democratization of reading. PYQ trends: asked about the Everyman Library; the democratization of reading; compared with other publishing series.

works.title: The Faerie Queene
works.shortDescription: Spenser's magnum opus, an epic poem and fantastical allegory celebrating the Tudor dynasty and Queen Elizabeth I. Published in two editions: Books I-III in 1590 and Books IV-VI in 1596. It remains the longest poem in the English language.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Spenserian Stanza – a nine-line stanza (eight lines of iambic pentameter + one Alexandrine, rhyme scheme ABABBCBCC) invented by Spenser. (2) Allegory – operates on multiple levels: moral, religious, political, and personal. Each book represents a virtue (Holiness, Temperance, Chastity, Friendship, Justice, Courtesy). (3) Gloriana – Queen Elizabeth is allegorized as both Gloriana (the Faerie Queene) and Belphoebe (chastity). (4) Incomplete Work – Spenser intended twelve books but only completed six; Two Cantos of Mutability was published posthumously in 1609. (5) Renaissance Ideals – embodies humanism, imaginative exuberance, and innovation. PYQ trends: asked about the Spenserian stanza, the allegorical significance, the role of Queen Elizabeth, and the poem's unfinished status.
works.references: Editions: 1590 (Books I-III), 1596 (Books IV-VI). Criticism: Hamilton (1990), Heale (1999).

works.title: The Family Reunion
works.shortDescription: Eliot's verse drama, a modern adaptation of the Greek myth of Orestes, set in an English country house.[reference:38]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Greek Myth – the play is based on the Orestes myth. (2) The Verse Drama – written in blank verse. (3) The Chorus – the play features a chorus of uncles and aunts. (4) The Reception – the play was less popular than Murder in the Cathedral.[reference:39] PYQ trends: asked about the Greek myth adaptation; the verse form; compared with Murder in the Cathedral.

works.title: The Fates of the Apostles
works.shortDescription: A short 122-line versified martyrology describing the mission and death of each of the Twelve Apostles.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Brevity and Purpose – likely intended as a mnemonic or homiletic poem for oral recitation. (2) Runic Signature – contains the shortest version of his signature (spelling CYNWULF), and here it is explicitly linked to the theme of apostolic martyrdom, drawing a parallel between the apostles' sacrifice and the poet's own spiritual anxiety. (3) Source – based on the Latin Martyrologium Hieronymianum. PYQ trends: asked in context of the Vercelli Book's contents and Cynewulf's authorship corpus.
works.references: MS: Vercelli Book (Biblioteca Capitolare, Vercelli). Editions: Krapp (1932), Fulk (2010).

works.title: The French Lieutenant's Woman
works.shortDescription: Fowles's postmodern novel set in Victorian England, offering three different endings and exploring themes of freedom, love, and social convention.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Postmodern Novel – the novel is a key example of postmodern fiction. (2) The Three Endings – the novel offers three possible endings. (3) The Victorian Setting – the novel is set in 1867 Lyme Regis. (4) The Metafictional Element – the narrator intrudes to comment on the story. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the postmodern elements; the three endings; the Victorian setting.

works.title: The French Revolution: A History
works.shortDescription: Carlyle's three-volume historical work on the French Revolution, written in a dramatic and visionary prose style. It was highly influential and remains a classic.[reference:29][reference:30]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Dramatic History – Carlyle presents history in a dramatic, almost epic style.[reference:31] (2) Historical Vision – the work is a visionary interpretation of the Revolution. (3) Influence – the work was influential and is still a significant reference in studies of the French Revolution.[reference:32] PYQ trends: frequently asked about Carlyle's historical writing; the dramatic prose style; compared with other historical works.

works.title: The Garden Party and Other Stories
works.shortDescription: Mansfield's most famous collection of short stories, which established her as one of the great masters of the short story form. The stories explore themes of class, identity, and the inner lives of women.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Short Story – Mansfield is a master of the short story form. (2) The Modernist Style – the stories use modernist techniques such as stream of consciousness. (3) The Themes – explores class, identity, and the inner lives of women. (4) The Influence – Mansfield was a major influence on later writers, including Virginia Woolf. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the short story form; the modernist techniques; the themes of class and identity; Mansfield's influence.

works.title: The General
works.shortDescription: A short, satirical poem that exposes the incompetence and ignorance of the military leadership[reference:44].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Satire – the poem is a biting satire of the General's cheerful ignorance[reference:45]. (2) The Simple Language – the poem uses simple, one-syllable words to create a stark effect[reference:46]. (3) The Irony – the General's 'good-morning' contrasts with the grim reality of the trenches[reference:47]. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the satire; the simple language; the critique of military leadership.
works.references: Edition: 1917 (in The Old Huntsman). Criticism: Sassoon (2000).

works.title: The Glass Palace
works.shortDescription: A historical novel by Amitav Ghosh, set in Burma, Bengal, India, and Malaya. It spans a century from the Third Anglo-Burmese War and the consequent fall of the Konbaung Dynasty in Mandalay, through the Second World War. Commonwealth Writers Prize(2001).
works.analysis: Rajkumar, an American Dreamer in Burma, falls in love with Dolly, royal house helper.

works.title: The God of Small Things
works.shortDescription: Roy's debut novel, which won the Booker Prize in 1997. It explores themes of caste, love, and social taboos in Kerala.[reference:43][reference:44][reference:45]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Booker Prize – the novel won the Booker Prize in 1997.[reference:46] (2) The Themes – the novel explores caste, love, and social taboos in Kerala.[reference:47] (3) The Narrative Style – the novel is known for its captivating narrative.[reference:48] PYQ trends: frequently asked about the novel's publication year (1997); the Booker Prize; and the themes of caste and love.
works.references: Edition: 1997 (New Delhi: IndiaInk). Criticism: Roy (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: The Golden Notebook
works.shortDescription: Lessing's most famous novel, a complex and experimental work about the life of Anna Wulf, a writer who keeps four notebooks to separate different aspects of her life.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Experimental Novel – the novel uses notebooks to structure the narrative. (2) The Feminist Theme – the novel explores the lives of women in the 1950s. (3) The Political Theme – the novel engages with communism and the Cold War. (4) The Golden Notebook – the fifth notebook represents the unity of Anna's life. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the experimental structure; the feminist themes; the political engagement.

works.title: The Grapes of Wrath
works.shortDescription: An American realist novel by John Steinbeck, published in 1939. It tells the story of the Joad family, who are forced to travel west during the Dust Bowl in search of a better life.[reference:5]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Realism – the novel is a key example of American Realism. (2) The Dust Bowl – the novel depicts the plight of migrant workers during the Great Depression. (3) The Joad Family – the family represents the struggle of the working class. (4) The Awards – the novel won the National Book Award and the Pulitzer Prize. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Realism; the Dust Bowl setting; the Pulitzer Prize.

works.title: The Great Gatsby
works.shortDescription: Fitzgerald's masterpiece, a novel about the mysterious Jay Gatsby's obsessive love for Daisy Buchanan, exploring the American Dream and the Jazz Age.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The American Dream – the novel critiques the American Dream. (2) The Jazz Age – the novel captures the excesses of the 1920s. (3) The Symbolism – the green light represents Gatsby's hope. (4) The Narrator – Nick Carraway is a reliable narrator. (5) The Famous Line – 'So we beat on, boats against the current, borne back ceaselessly into the past.' PYQ trends: frequently asked about the American Dream; the Jazz Age; the symbolism of the green light; the narrator.

works.title: The Great Tradition
works.shortDescription: Leavis's most famous work, which argues for a tradition of English novelists including Jane Austen, George Eliot, Henry James, and Joseph Conrad. He rejects Dickens for his lack of moral seriousness.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Great Tradition – Leavis defines a tradition of moral seriousness in the English novel. (2) The Canon – includes Jane Austen, George Eliot, Henry James, and Joseph Conrad. (3) The Rejection of Dickens – Leavis excludes Dickens for his lack of moral seriousness. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the Great Tradition; the canon; the rejection of Dickens; compared with other critical traditions.
works.references: Edition: 1948 (London: Chatto & Windus). Criticism: Leavis (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: The Guardian
works.shortDescription: A British newspaper known for its liberal editorial stance and its coverage of politics, culture, and the arts.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Newspaper – a major British newspaper. (2) The Stance – known for its liberal and progressive stance. (3) The Influence – significant influence on British political and cultural life. PYQ trends: asked about modern British journalism; The Guardian's role; compared with other newspapers.

works.title: The Guide
works.shortDescription: Narayan's most famous novel, which describes the transformation of the protagonist, Raju, from a tour guide to a spiritual guide and eventually one of the greatest holy men of India.[reference:19][reference:20]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Transformation of Raju – the novel traces the protagonist's journey from a tour guide to a spiritual guide.[reference:21] (2) The Fictional Town of Malgudi – the novel is set in Narayan's iconic fictional town.[reference:22] (3) The Sahitya Akademi Award – the novel won the Sahitya Akademi Award in 1960. PYQ trends: asked about the novel's publication year (1958); the character of Raju; and the theme of transformation.[reference:23][reference:24][reference:25]

works.title: The Handmaid's Tale
works.shortDescription: Atwood's most famous novel, a dystopian work set in the Republic of Gilead, a totalitarian society where women are subjugated.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Dystopian Fiction – the novel is a landmark of dystopian fiction. (2) The Feminist Theme – the novel explores the subjugation of women. (3) The Handmaid – the title character is a woman forced to bear children for the ruling class. (4) The Television Adaptation – the novel was adapted into a successful television series. PYQ trends: frequently asked about The Handmaid's Tale; the dystopian genre; the feminist themes.

works.title: The Hawk in the Rain
works.shortDescription: Hughes's first major collection, which established his reputation for capturing the raw power of nature through striking imagery and dynamic verse.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Animal Imagery – Hughes explores the savagery and cunning of animal life in harsh, disjunctive lines. (2) The Title Poem – 'The Hawk in the Rain' depicts the hawk's struggle against the elements. (3) The Collection – the volume won the First Publication Award and announced a major new voice in British poetry. PYQ trends: asked about Hughes's early work; the animal imagery; the collection's significance.

works.title: The Heart of the Matter
works.shortDescription: Greene's novel about a police officer in a West African colony who is torn between his duty and his love for a woman.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Moral Dilemma – the novel explores a profound moral dilemma. (2) The Setting – the novel is set in West Africa. (3) The Theme of Guilt – the protagonist is consumed by guilt. PYQ trends: asked about the moral dilemma; the setting; the theme of guilt.

works.title: The History of England
works.shortDescription: Hume's six-volume history of England, which was a bestseller and established his reputation as a historian.[reference:40]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Historical Writing – Hume's History is a landmark in English historiography. (2) The Style – Hume's prose is clear and elegant. PYQ trends: asked about Hume's History; compared with other historians.

works.title: The History of Rasselas
works.shortDescription: Johnson's only novel, a philosophical fable about a prince who leaves the Happy Valley in search of happiness.[reference:30]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Philosophical Fable – the novel is a moral tale about the search for happiness. (2) The Happy Valley – the prince leaves the paradisal Happy Valley to explore the world. (3) The Theme of Choice – the novel explores the theme of choice and the limitations of human freedom. PYQ trends: asked about Johnson's Rasselas; the philosophical fable; compared with Voltaire's Candide.

works.title: The History of the World
works.shortDescription: Raleigh's ambitious historical work, written while imprisoned in the Tower of London. Intended as a teaching tool for Prince Henry, son of King James I.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Historical Magnum Opus – Raleigh's most substantial prose work, covering history from Creation to the Roman era. (2) Political Context – King James found the book 'too saucy in the censuring of princes' and revoked publishing rights. (3) Incomplete – left unfinished after Prince Henry's death in 1612. PYQ trends: asked as a major prose work of the Elizabethan/Jacobean period; compared with other historical works (e.g., Holinshed's Chronicles).

works.title: The Hollow Men
works.shortDescription: A poem about the spiritual emptiness of modern humanity, often seen as a companion piece to The Waste Land[reference:12].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Hollow Men – represent modern humanity's spiritual emptiness. (2) The Famous Lines – 'This is the way the world ends / Not with a bang but a whimper.' (3) The Epigraphs – the poem quotes Dante and Conrad's Heart of Darkness. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the theme of emptiness; the famous closing lines; the epigraphs.

works.title: The Holy War
works.shortDescription: Bunyan's allegorical work, published in 1682, telling the story of the battle for the soul of man, represented as the town of Mansoul.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Religious Allegory – a military allegory of the spiritual battle for the soul. (2) The Town of Mansoul – represents the human soul. (3) Themes – explores themes of sin, redemption, and spiritual warfare. PYQ trends: asked about Bunyan's other allegorical works; compared with The Pilgrim's Progress.

works.title: The House of Fame
works.shortDescription: A dream-vision poem in three books, over 2000 lines long, composed in octosyllabic couplets. It is considered one of Chaucer's earliest and most original works.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Parody – parodies Dante's Divine Comedy, particularly the Inferno and Paradiso. (2) Dream Vision – follows the medieval dream-vision convention. (3) Linguistic First – contains the earliest known uses in English of the term 'Galaxy' and 'Milky Way'. (4) Unfinished – like many of Chaucer's works, it is left incomplete. PYQ trends: asked in NET Dec 2009; frequently compared with The Book of the Duchess.
works.references: Editions: Benson (1988), Riverside Chaucer. Source: Dante's Divine Comedy.

works.title: The House of Life
works.shortDescription: Dante Gabriel Rossetti's sonnet sequence, a cycle of love sonnets that explores the nature of love and beauty[reference:34].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Sonnet Sequence – the sequence is a cycle of love sonnets[reference:35]. (2) The Theme of Love – the poems explore the nature of love and beauty[reference:36][reference:37]. (3) The Technical Skill – the sequence is celebrated for its technical skill[reference:38]. PYQ trends: asked about the sonnet sequence; compared with other Victorian sonnet sequences.
works.references: Edition: 1881 (in Ballads and Sonnets). Criticism: Rossetti (2000).

works.title: The Iceman Cometh
works.shortDescription: O'Neill's masterpiece, a long, sprawling play about a group of alcoholics in a New York bar who live on illusions. The title character, Hickey, arrives to shatter their dreams.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Illusions – the characters live on illusions to survive. (2) Hickey – the salesman who destroys their illusions. (3) The Bar Setting – the play is set in a Greenwich Village bar. (4) The Tragedy – the play is a tragedy of the human condition. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the theme of illusion; the character of Hickey; the bar setting; compared with other O'Neill plays.

works.title: The Importance of Being Earnest
works.shortDescription: Wilde's masterpiece, a farcical comedy of manners subtitled 'A Trivial Comedy for Serious People'. It satirizes Victorian social conventions through mistaken identities, witty epigrams, and the pursuit of marriage.[reference:8][reference:9]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Comedy of Manners – the play is the supreme example of the genre. (2) The Wit – Wilde's epigrams define the play: 'The truth is rarely pure and never simple.' (3) The Double Life – Jack and Algernon both lead double lives. (4) The Title – 'Earnest' is a pun on the name and the virtue. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the comedy of manners; Wilde's epigrams; the satire of Victorian society; the double life motif.

works.title: The Independent
works.shortDescription: A British newspaper founded in 1986, known for its liberal stance and innovative design.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Newspaper – a major British newspaper. (2) The Stance – known for its liberal and independent stance. (3) The Design – innovative and modern design. PYQ trends: asked about modern British journalism; the Independent's role; compared with other newspapers.

works.title: The Intentional Fallacy
works.shortDescription: A landmark essay in New Criticism, arguing that the author's intention is irrelevant to the interpretation of a literary work.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Intentional Fallacy – the fallacy of judging a work by the author's intention. (2) The Work as Autonomous – the work should be judged on its own merits. (3) Close Reading – the essay advocates for close reading without reference to the author. PYQ trends: one of the most frequently asked critical concepts in NET; the intentional fallacy; the work as autonomous.
works.references: Edition: 1946 (in The Sewanee Review). Criticism: Wimsatt & Beardsley (2000).

works.title: The Interpretation of Dreams
works.shortDescription: Freud's foundational work of psychoanalysis, which introduces his theory of dreams as the 'royal road to the unconscious'. It includes the concepts of the id, ego, and superego.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Unconscious – dreams reveal the unconscious mind. (2) The Id, Ego, and Superego – the three structures of the psyche. (3) The Oedipus Complex – the child's unconscious desire for the opposite-sex parent. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the id, ego, and superego; the Oedipus complex; compared with other psychoanalytic theories.

works.title: The Invisible Man
works.shortDescription: Wells's novel about a scientist who discovers how to make himself invisible, but descends into madness.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Theme of Power – the novel explores the corrupting influence of power. (2) The Scientific Theme – the novel reflects Wells's interest in science. PYQ trends: asked about the theme of power; the scientific theme.

works.title: The Jew of Malta
