works.shortDescription: Marlowe's play centered around Barabas, a cunning and vengeful Jewish merchant. A dark portrayal of religious and political hypocrisy[reference:14].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Source – the character of Barabas is based on the historical figure of Joseph Nassi. (2) Anti-Semitism – the play engages with Renaissance attitudes towards Jews, and is often compared with Shakespeare's The Merchant of Venice. (3) Machiavellian Villain – Barabas is a precursor to the Machiavellian stage villain. (4) The Prologue – spoken by Machiavel, introducing the play's cynical tone. PYQ trends: asked about the character of Barabas; the play's treatment of religion and hypocrisy; compared with Shakespeare's Shylock.
works.references: Editions: Norton Critical Edition. Criticism: Marlowe (2009), Cartelli (2002).

works.title: The Keeper of the Dead
works.shortDescription: Daruwalla's collection of poems, which explores themes of death, violence, and the human condition.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Dark Themes – the collection explores death, violence, and the human condition. (2) The Style – Daruwalla's poetry is characterized by its stark realism and powerful imagery. PYQ trends: asked about the dark themes; the style; the poet's engagement with history.
works.references: Edition: 1982 (New Delhi: Oxford University Press). Criticism: Daruwalla (2000).

works.title: The Lady of Shalott
works.shortDescription: Tennyson's lyrical ballad about a lady who is cursed to weave a web and never look directly at the outside world.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Ballad Form – the poem follows the traditional ballad form. (2) The Symbolism – the lady represents the artist who is isolated from the world. (3) The Curse – the lady is cursed to weave and never look at the outside world. PYQ trends: asked about the symbolism; the Pre-Raphaelite influence; compared with other Victorian poems.

works.title: The Lamb
works.shortDescription: Blake's poem from Songs of Innocence, celebrating the innocence and gentleness of the lamb.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Symbolism – the lamb represents innocence and the gentle aspects of creation. (2) The Question – 'Little Lamb, who made thee?' (3) The Answer – the poem answers its own question, identifying the creator as the Lamb of God. PYQ trends: asked about Blake's The Lamb; compared with The Tyger.
works.references: Edition: 1789 (in Songs of Innocence). Criticism: Blake (2000), Frye (1947).

works.title: The Land of Heart's Desire
works.shortDescription: Yeats's one-act play about a young married woman who is lured away by fairies.[reference:34]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Fairy Lore – the play draws on Irish fairy folklore. (2) The One-Act Form – a short poetic drama. PYQ trends: asked about Yeats's early one-act plays; the fairy element.

works.title: The Latest Decalogue
works.shortDescription: Clough's satirical poem that parodies the Ten Commandments, criticizing Victorian moral complacency[reference:70].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Satire – the poem is a sharp satire of Victorian moral complacency[reference:71]. (2) The Parody – the poem parodies the Ten Commandments[reference:72]. PYQ trends: asked about the satire; the parody form.

works.title: The Laugh of the Medusa
works.shortDescription: Cixous's essay, which argues for the importance of écriture féminine (women's writing) as a way of challenging patriarchal discourse.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Écriture Féminine – a form of writing that expresses the feminine body and desire. (2) The Challenge to Patriarchy – women's writing challenges patriarchal discourse. (3) The Medusa – the Medusa is used as a symbol of female power. PYQ trends: frequently asked about écriture féminine; the challenge to patriarchy; compared with other feminist theories.

works.title: The Legend of Good Women
works.shortDescription: A dream-vision poem recounting the stories of virtuous women in nine sections. It is considered the first significant work in English literature to use iambic pentameter consistently.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Dream Vision – follows the genre. (2) Iambic Pentameter – first significant work in English to use this meter systematically. (3) Palinode – also called a palinode, a poem in which a poet retracts a view expressed in an earlier poem (here, his earlier works that spoke ill of women). (4) Legend of Cupid – the Prologue introduces the God of Love. PYQ trends: asked in NET Dec 2014; the meter is a key identifying feature.
works.references: Editions: Benson (1988), Riverside Chaucer. Criticism: Mann (1991).

works.title: The Less Deceived
works.shortDescription: Larkin's second major collection, which established his reputation as a leading figure of the Movement.[reference:17]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Second Collection – Larkin's breakthrough collection. (2) The Movement – the collection is associated with the Movement, a group of poets who rejected romanticism. (3) The Title – 'The Less Deceived' reflects Larkin's ironic, unsentimental stance. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Larkin's The Less Deceived; the Movement; the ironic tone.

works.title: The Lie
works.shortDescription: A poem likely composed in the 1590s after Raleigh fell out of favor with Queen Elizabeth following his secret marriage. It is one of Raleigh's most famous poems.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Courtly Disillusionment – reflects Raleigh's fall from grace and his disillusionment with court life. (2) The 'Lie' Refrain – each stanza ends with 'Go, soul, the body's guest, / Upon a thankless errand; / Fear not to touch the best; / The truth shall be thy warrant: / Go, since I needs must die, / And give the world the lie.' (3) Historical Context – Raleigh secretly married one of Elizabeth's Maids-of-Honor in 1591 and was imprisoned in the Tower. PYQ trends: asked about Raleigh's court poetry; the theme of disillusionment; compared with other Elizabethan court poets.

works.title: The Life of Charlotte Brontë
works.shortDescription: Gaskell's biography of her friend Charlotte Brontë, first published in 1857, two years after Brontë's death.[reference:42][reference:43]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Literary Biography – the work is one of the most famous literary biographies in English. (2) The Friendship – Gaskell was a close friend of Charlotte Brontë. (3) The Controversy – the biography caused controversy for its frank portrayal of Brontë's life. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Gaskell's biography; the literary biography; compared with other biographies.

works.title: The Lion and the Jewel
works.shortDescription: Soyinka's comic play about the clash between traditional and modern values in Africa, centered on a young woman courted by both a traditional chief and a Western-educated man.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Comic Play – the play is a light-hearted comedy. (2) The Clash of Cultures – the play explores the tension between tradition and modernity. (3) The Characters – Lakunle (the modern man), Baroka (the traditional chief), and Sidi (the beautiful woman). PYQ trends: frequently asked about the clash of cultures; the comic tone; the characters; compared with other Soyinka plays.
works.references: Edition: 1959 (Ibadan: Oxford University Press). Criticism: Soyinka (2000).

works.title: The Location of Culture
works.shortDescription: Bhabha's foundational work of postcolonial theory, which introduces the concepts of hybridity, the third space, and the destabilization of colonial authority.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Hybridity – the mixing of cultures and identities. (2) The Third Space – a space of negotiation and translation. (3) The Mimicry – the colonized mimicking the colonizer. PYQ trends: frequently asked about hybridity; the third space; mimicry; compared with other postcolonial theorists.

works.title: The London Review of Books
works.shortDescription: A British literary periodical founded in 1979, known for its long-form essays and literary criticism.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Periodical – a major British literary periodical. (2) The Essays – known for its long-form essays. (3) The Influence – significant influence on British literary culture. PYQ trends: asked about modern literary periodicals; The London Review of Books' role; compared with other periodicals.

works.title: The Lotos-Eaters
works.shortDescription: Tennyson's poem about the mariners who eat the lotus fruit and lose all desire to return home.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Classical Source – based on Homer's Odyssey. (2) The Theme of Escape – the poem explores the desire to escape from the struggles of life. PYQ trends: asked about the classical sources; the theme of escape.

works.title: The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock
works.shortDescription: Eliot's landmark modernist poem, published in Poetry magazine. It is a dramatic monologue of a hesitant, neurotic man who is paralyzed by indecision[reference:10].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Dramatic Monologue – the poem is a modernist reworking of the dramatic monologue. (2) The Inaction – Prufrock is paralyzed by indecision and self-doubt. (3) The Fragmentation – the poem uses fragmented images and allusions. (4) The Famous Lines – 'Do I dare?' and 'I have measured out my life with coffee spoons'. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the dramatic monologue; the theme of inaction; the modernist techniques.

works.title: The Madwoman in the Attic
works.shortDescription: Gilbert and Gubar's study of the 19th-century women's novel, which argues that the figure of the 'madwoman' represents the anxiety and anger of women writers.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Madwoman – the figure represents women's anxiety and anger. (2) The Critique of Patriarchy – the study critiques patriarchal literary conventions. (3) The Female Gothic – the study explores the Gothic elements in women's novels. PYQ trends: one of the most frequently asked texts in NET; the madwoman; the critique of patriarchy; compared with other feminist literary critics.
works.references: Edition: 1979 (New Haven: Yale University Press). Criticism: Gilbert & Gubar (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: The Man of Mode
works.shortDescription: Etherege's masterpiece, a sparkling Restoration comedy that satirizes the fashionable world of London. The play features Sir Fopling Flutter, a character based on the fop stereotype.[reference:5]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Comedy of Manners – one of the greatest examples of the genre. (2) Sir Fopling Flutter – the archetypal Restoration fop, obsessed with fashion and appearance. (3) Dorimant – the witty and cynical hero who represents the Restoration rake. (4) The Influence of Molière – the play shows the influence of French comedy. PYQ trends: frequently asked as a key Restoration comedy; the character of Sir Fopling Flutter; compared with Congreve's The Way of the World.

works.title: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell
works.shortDescription: Blake's prose poem, a revolutionary work that challenges conventional morality and celebrates the energy of the devil.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Proverb of Hell – the work contains a series of aphorisms known as the 'Proverbs of Hell'. (2) The Marriage of Opposites – Blake argues that contraries are necessary for progress. (3) The Devil as Hero – Blake celebrates the devil as a figure of energy and liberation. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Blake's The Marriage of Heaven and Hell; the Proverbs of Hell; the revolutionary themes.

works.title: The Mayor of Casterbridge
works.shortDescription: Hardy's novel about Michael Henchard, a man who sells his wife at a fair and later rises to become mayor, only to fall again.[reference:26]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Tragic Hero – Henchard is a flawed but tragic figure. (2) The Theme of Fate – the novel explores the role of fate and coincidence. (3) The Setting – the novel is set in the fictional town of Casterbridge. PYQ trends: asked about the tragic hero; the theme of fate; the setting.

works.title: The Merchant of Venice
works.shortDescription: Shakespeare's play about a merchant who borrows money from a Jewish moneylender. The play explores themes of justice, mercy, prejudice, and the nature of love.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Source – based on Giovanni Fiorentino's Il Pecorone (1378) and Christopher Marlowe's The Jew of Malta[reference:3]. (2) The Merchant – Antonio, the merchant, is the title character but is overshadowed by Shylock. (3) Shylock – one of Shakespeare's most complex characters; often read as an anti-Semitic stereotype but also as a sympathetic figure of persecution. (4) The Trial Scene – Portia's 'quality of mercy' speech is one of Shakespeare's most famous passages. (5) The Caskets – the test of the three caskets (gold, silver, lead). PYQ trends: asked about the character of Shylock; the theme of justice vs mercy; the treatment of anti-Semitism; the casket test.
works.references: Sources: Il Pecorone, The Jew of Malta. Editions: Arden Shakespeare (3rd ed., Drakakis, 2010). Criticism: Bloom (1998), Shapiro (1996).

works.title: The Mill on the Floss
works.shortDescription: Eliot's most autobiographical novel, telling the story of Maggie Tulliver and her conflicted relationship with her brother Tom.[reference:20][reference:21]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Autobiographical Elements – the novel draws on Eliot's own childhood. (2) The Sibling Relationship – the relationship between Maggie and Tom is the emotional core of the novel. (3) The Tragic Ending – the novel ends with the death of the siblings. PYQ trends: asked about the autobiographical elements; the sibling relationship; the tragic ending.

works.title: The Moonstone
works.shortDescription: Collins's novel about the theft of a priceless diamond, often considered one of the first detective novels.[reference:39]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Detective Novel – often considered one of the first detective novels.[reference:40] (2) The Multiple Narrators – the novel uses multiple narrators to tell the story. (3) The Sensation Novel – the novel is a key example of the sensation novel genre. PYQ trends: asked about the detective novel; the multiple narrators.

works.title: The New Atlantis
works.shortDescription: Bacon's utopian fable, published posthumously in 1626. It describes a fictional island society, Bensalem, where scientific research is organized and supported by the state.[reference:1]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Utopian Genre – one of the classic utopian works in English literature. (2) Scientific Utopia – depicts a society organized around scientific research and the pursuit of knowledge. (3) Salomon's House – the central institution of the island, a research institute dedicated to the advancement of science. PYQ trends: asked about Bacon's utopian vision; compared with More's Utopia.

works.title: The New Criticism
works.shortDescription: Ransom's collection of essays that helped to define and name the New Criticism movement. It includes essays on I.A. Richards, T.S. Eliot, and other critics.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The New Criticism – the movement that emphasized close reading and the autonomy of the text. (2) The Name – Ransom coined the term 'The New Criticism'. (3) The Essays – the collection includes essays on major critics. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the New Criticism; Ransom's role in naming the movement; compared with other critical movements.

works.title: The New Yorker
works.shortDescription: An American magazine founded in 1925, known for its literary journalism, satire, and cultural criticism.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Magazine – a major American literary magazine. (2) The Contributions – published works by major American writers. (3) The Influence – significant influence on American literary culture. PYQ trends: asked about American literary magazines; The New Yorker's role; compared with other magazines.

works.title: The North Ship
works.shortDescription: Larkin's first volume of poetry, containing 33 poems.[reference:16]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Collection – Larkin's debut collection. (2) The Yeats Influence – the poems show the influence of W.B. Yeats. (3) The Early Style – the collection is less characteristic of Larkin's mature work. PYQ trends: asked about Larkin's first collection; the Yeats influence.

works.title: The Old Bachelour
works.shortDescription: Congreve's first play, a comedy of intrigue that was an immediate success. It tells the story of an old bachelor who is tricked into marriage.[reference:3]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Play – Congreve's debut, written at the age of 23. (2) Restoration Comedy Conventions – the play follows the conventions of the genre with its witty dialogue, sexual intrigue, and satirical portrayal of society. PYQ trends: asked about Congreve's early works.

works.title: The Old Huntsman
works.shortDescription: Sassoon's early poetry collection, containing some of his first war poems[reference:43].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Early War Poems – the collection includes some of Sassoon's first war poems. (2) The Satirical Voice – Sassoon's satirical critique of the war begins to emerge. PYQ trends: asked about Sassoon's early collections; the development of his satirical voice.

works.title: The Old Man and the Sea
works.shortDescription: Hemingway's novella about an aging Cuban fisherman's struggle with a giant marlin. It won the Pulitzer Prize and was cited for his Nobel Prize.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Symbolism – the old man represents human endurance. (2) The Struggle – the struggle with the marlin is a symbol of life's challenges. (3) The Iceberg Theory – the story is told with Hemingway's characteristic economy. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the symbolism; the theme of endurance; the iceberg theory.

works.title: The Ordeal of Richard Feverel
works.shortDescription: Meredith's early bildungsroman about the education and emotional development of Richard Feverel.[reference:45]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Bildungsroman – the novel traces Richard's development from youth to maturity. (2) The Theme of Education – the novel critiques the system of education. PYQ trends: asked about the bildungsroman; the theme of education.

works.title: The Partisan Review
works.shortDescription: An American literary magazine founded in 1934, known for its left-wing politics and its role in promoting modernist literature.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Magazine – a major American literary magazine. (2) The Politics – known for its left-wing stance. (3) The Role – promoted modernist literature and criticism. PYQ trends: asked about American literary magazines; Partisan Review's role; compared with other magazines.

works.title: The Picture of Dorian Gray
works.shortDescription: Wilde's only novel, a philosophical fantasy about a young man who sells his soul for eternal youth and beauty.[reference:69]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Aestheticism – the novel is a key text of Aestheticism. (2) The Preface – the famous preface contains Wilde's aesthetic manifesto: 'All art is quite useless'. (3) The Portrait – the portrait represents Dorian's soul, aging as he remains young. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the novel's aesthetic philosophy; the preface; the symbolism of the portrait; compared with other works of Aestheticism.

works.title: The Pilgrim's Progress
works.shortDescription: Bunyan's allegorical masterpiece, one of the most famous works of English literature. It tells the story of Christian, who journeys from the City of Destruction to the Celestial City. Part I was published in 1678, Part II in 1684.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Religious Allegory – the work is a powerful allegory of the Christian journey of faith. (2) Plain Style – Bunyan's prose is simple, direct, and vivid, making the allegory accessible to ordinary readers. (3) Characters – the work features memorable characters like Christian, Faithful, Hopeful, and Mr. Worldly Wiseman. (4) Part II – follows Christian's wife and children on their journey. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Bunyan's Pilgrim's Progress; the allegorical meaning; the plain style; compared with other allegories (e.g., Spenser's Faerie Queene).
works.references: Edition: 1678 (Part I), 1684 (Part II). Criticism: Bunyan (2000), Forrest (2005).

works.title: The Plain Dealer
works.shortDescription: Wycherley's last play, a dark and cynical comedy that satirizes hypocrisy and pretension. It is often considered his finest work.[reference:11]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Dark Satire – a more cynical and bitter satire than his earlier works. (2) The Plain Dealer – the title character, Manly, is a blunt and honest man who is betrayed by those he trusts. (3) Influence of Molière – the play is based on Molière's Le Misanthrope. PYQ trends: asked about Wycherley's later works; compared with Molière.

works.title: The Playboy of the Western World
works.shortDescription: Synge's most famous play, a tragicomedy about Christy Mahon, a young man who becomes a local hero after claiming to have killed his father. The play caused riots at its Dublin premiere.[reference:15][reference:16]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Controversy – the play caused riots at the Abbey Theatre in 1907 due to its unsentimental portrayal of Irish peasants. (2) The Hero – Christy is a 'playboy' who is idolized for his supposed crime. (3) The Language – Synge's dialogue is a rich blend of Hiberno-English and Irish syntax. (4) The Peasantry – the play is a complex portrait of Irish peasant life.[reference:17] PYQ trends: frequently asked about the 1907 riots; the character of Christy; Synge's use of Irish dialect; the play's treatment of heroism.

works.title: The Plough and the Stars
works.shortDescription: O'Casey's play set during the 1916 Easter Rising. It aroused such hostility at its premiere that O'Casey moved to England.[reference:28][reference:29]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Easter Rising – the play is set during the 1916 rebellion. (2) The Controversy – the play was seen as unpatriotic by some audiences. (3) The Plough and the Stars – the title refers to the flag of the Irish Citizen Army. PYQ trends: asked about the Easter Rising; the controversy; compared with Juno and the Paycock.

works.title: The Poems of Emily Dickinson
works.shortDescription: Dickinson's complete poems, published posthumously. She wrote nearly 1,800 poems, exploring themes of death, immortality, nature, and the inner life.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Themes – death, immortality, nature, and the inner life. (2) The Style – Dickinson's poetry is characterized by its brevity, dashes, and unconventional capitalization. (3) The Slant Rhyme – she often used slant rhyme. (4) The Posthumous Publication – most of her poems were published after her death. (5) Poem 465 ('I heard a Fly buzz') and Poem 712 ('Because I could not stop for Death') are frequently anthologized. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the themes of death and immortality; the use of dashes and slant rhyme; the posthumous publication.

works.title: The Poetical Works
works.shortDescription: Traherne's poetry, first published in 1903 in an edition by Bertram Dobell. His poems, like his prose, celebrate the wonder of creation and the presence of God.[reference:33]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Religious Poetry – Traherne's poetry explores themes of divine love, creation, and the soul's relationship with God. (2) Celebratory Style – his poetry is characterized by its joy, wonder, and simplicity. (3) Influence – Traherne's poetry was rediscovered in the 20th century and has influenced modern poets. PYQ trends: asked about Traherne's poetry; the mystical element; compared with other metaphysical poets.
works.references: Edition: 1903 (published by Bertram Dobell). Criticism: Stewart (2006), Smith (2000).

works.title: The Political Unconscious
works.shortDescription: Jameson's foundational work of Marxist criticism, which argues that all literature is a symptom of the political unconscious.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Political Unconscious – literature is a symptom of the political unconscious. (2) The Three Levels – political, social, and historical. (3) The Interpretation of Literature – literature must be interpreted in its historical context. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the political unconscious; the three levels; compared with other Marxist critics.
works.references: Edition: 1981 (Ithaca: Cornell University Press). Criticism: Jameson (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: The Poplar Field
works.shortDescription: Cowper's short lyric poem mourning the cutting down of a grove of poplar trees.[reference:16]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Nature Poetry – the poem is a meditation on the loss of nature. (2) The Theme of Transience – the poem reflects on the transience of life and nature. PYQ trends: asked about Cowper's lyrics.

works.title: The Portrait of a Lady
works.shortDescription: James's novel about Isabel Archer, a young American woman who inherits a fortune and becomes the victim of a scheming husband.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The International Theme – the novel explores the contrast between American innocence and European sophistication. (2) The 'House of Fiction' – James's metaphor for the novel. (3) The Theme of Freedom – Isabel's struggle for independence. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the international theme; the character of Isabel; compared with other James novels.

works.title: The Post Office
works.shortDescription: Tagore's play about a sick boy who longs for the outside world. It is a deeply moving and symbolic work about human longing and the search for connection.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Symbolism – the play is a deeply symbolic work. (2) The Themes – explores longing, hope, and the search for connection. (3) The Child Protagonist – the play is told from the perspective of a child. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the play; the symbolism; the themes of longing and hope.

works.title: The Postmodern Condition
works.shortDescription: Lyotard's foundational text of postmodern theory, which defines postmodernism as 'incredulity toward metanarratives'.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Metanarratives – large-scale narratives like progress, reason, and liberation. (2) The Postmodern Condition – the collapse of metanarratives. (3) The Nature of Knowledge – knowledge is fragmented and localized. PYQ trends: one of the most frequently asked texts in NET; the definition of postmodernism; the collapse of metanarratives.

works.title: The Power and the Glory
works.shortDescription: Greene's novel about a 'whisky priest' in Mexico during a time of religious persecution. It is a profound exploration of sin, faith, and redemption.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Catholic Novel – the novel explores Catholic themes of sin and redemption. (2) The 'Whisky Priest' – the protagonist is a flawed but heroic figure. (3) The Setting – the novel is set in Mexico. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the Catholic themes; the character of the whisky priest; the setting.

works.title: The Prelude
works.shortDescription: Wordsworth's autobiographical epic, subtitled 'Growth of a Poet's Mind'. It was begun in 1798, completed in its first version in 1805, and published posthumously in 1850.[reference:6]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Autobiographical Epic – the poem traces Wordsworth's development as a poet and his relationship with nature.[reference:7] (2) Three Versions – composed in 1799 (two-part), 1805 (thirteen-book), and 1850 (fourteen-book). NTA frequently tests the dates.[reference:8] (3) The Spots of Time – key moments of revelation and insight. (4) The Arab Dream – in Book V, Wordsworth dreams of an Arab in the desert.[reference:9] PYQ trends: frequently asked about the dates; the theme of the poet's growth; the 'spots of time'; compared with other autobiographies.

works.title: The Professor
works.shortDescription: Charlotte Brontë's first completed novel, published posthumously in 1857. It tells the story of William Crimsworth, an Englishman who becomes a teacher in Brussels.[reference:13]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Novel – the first novel Brontë completed, though it was published last. (2) The Male Protagonist – unlike her other novels, the protagonist is a man. (3) The Brussels Setting – the novel draws on Brontë's experiences in Brussels. PYQ trends: asked about Brontë's first completed novel; the male protagonist; the Brussels setting.

works.title: The Progress of Poesy
works.shortDescription: Gray's Pindaric ode tracing the history of poetry from ancient Greece to modern England.[reference:9]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Pindaric Ode – the poem is written in the Pindaric ode form. (2) The History of Poetry – the poem traces the development of poetry through the ages. PYQ trends: asked about Gray's Pindaric odes.

works.title: The Provoked Wife
works.shortDescription: Vanbrugh's second comedy, a play about a woman who is driven to adultery by her abusive husband. It is a more serious and sympathetic treatment of marriage.[reference:13]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Marriage and Abuse – the play explores the theme of marital unhappiness and abuse. (2) Lady Brute – a sympathetic heroine who is driven to consider adultery. (3) The Play's Reception – the play was successful but also controversial for its treatment of marriage. PYQ trends: asked about Vanbrugh's comedies; compared with The Relapse.

works.title: The Psychopathology of Everyday Life
works.shortDescription: Freud's work on the psychological causes of everyday errors, slips of the tongue, and forgetfulness.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Unconscious – everyday errors reveal the unconscious. (2) The Slip of the Tongue – the 'Freudian slip' is a manifestation of unconscious desires. PYQ trends: asked about the Freudian slip; the role of the unconscious in everyday life.

works.title: The Quarterly Review
works.shortDescription: A British literary periodical founded in 1809, known for its Tory politics and its conservative literary criticism.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Periodical – a major literary periodical. (2) The Politics – known for its Tory stance. (3) The Role – offered conservative literary criticism. PYQ trends: asked about periodicals of the Romantic period; The Quarterly Review's role; compared with The Edinburgh Review.

works.title: The Rainbow
works.shortDescription: Lawrence's novel about three generations of the Brangwen family, exploring themes of love, sexuality, and the search for fulfillment.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Generational Structure – the novel traces three generations of the Brangwen family. (2) The Theme of Sexuality – the novel explores the complexities of sexual relationships. (3) The Censorship – the novel was suppressed for obscenity. PYQ trends: asked about the generational structure; the theme of sexuality; the censorship.

works.title: The Rambler
works.shortDescription: Johnson's periodical essays, published twice weekly from 1750 to 1752. They are known for their moral and literary reflections.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Periodical Essay – Johnson's essays are in the tradition of The Spectator. (2) Moral Reflections – the essays offer moral and philosophical reflections on life and literature. (3) The Style – Johnson's prose is characterized by its balance and Latinate syntax. PYQ trends: asked about Johnson's periodical essays; the moral reflections; the prose style.

works.title: The Rape of the Lock
works.shortDescription: Pope's mock-epic masterpiece, a satirical poem in heroic couplets that treats a trivial social incident — the cutting of a lock of hair — with the elevated style of classical epic.[reference:0]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Mock-Epic – Pope uses the conventions of epic poetry (invocation, supernatural machinery, epic similes) to treat a trivial subject, creating a brilliant satire of high society. (2) The Sylphs – Pope introduces a supernatural machinery of sylphs, gnomes, nymphs, and salamanders, drawing on Rosicrucian mythology. (3) The Heroic Couplet – Pope's mastery of the heroic couplet is on full display, with balanced antitheses and epigrammatic wit. (4) The Lock – the central symbol of the poem, representing female vanity and the triviality of aristocratic concerns. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the mock-epic form; Pope's use of the heroic couplet; the social satire; the character of Belinda.
works.references: Editions: 1712 (two cantos), 1714 (five cantos). Criticism: Pope (2000), Mack (1985).

works.title: The Raven
works.shortDescription: Poe's most famous poem, a musical and melancholic work about a man who is visited by a raven that repeats the word 'Nevermore'.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Gothic – the poem is a key example of Gothic literature. (2) The Musicality – the poem is famous for its rhythmic and musical quality. (3) The Symbolism – the raven symbolizes death and loss. (4) The Themes – explores grief, loss, and the supernatural. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the Gothic elements; the musicality; the symbolism of the raven; compared with other Poe works.

works.title: The Recruiting Officer
works.shortDescription: Farquhar's comedy based on his own experiences as a recruiting officer during the War of Spanish Succession.[reference:17]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Autobiographical Elements – the play draws on Farquhar's experiences as a recruiting officer.[reference:18] (2) Satire of the Army – the play satirizes the army and the recruitment process. PYQ trends: asked about Farquhar's life and works.

works.title: The Red Badge of Courage
works.shortDescription: Stephen Crane's masterpiece about the nightmare of war, first published in 1895. It portrays the initial cowardice and later courage of a young Union soldier in the Civil War.[reference:2][reference:3]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Naturalism – the novel is a key example of American Naturalism. (2) The Psychology of War – the novel explores the inner turmoil of a soldier. (3) The Themes – explores maturation, heroism, cowardice, and the indifference of nature. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Naturalism; the psychology of war; the themes of heroism and cowardice.

works.title: The Relapse
works.shortDescription: Vanbrugh's first comedy, a sequel to Colley Cibber's Love's Last Shift. It is a bawdy and witty play that subverts the sentimental moralizing of its predecessor.[reference:12]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Sequel and Parody – Vanbrugh's play is a direct parody of Cibber's sentimental comedy. (2) Lord Foppington – one of the great Restoration fops, a character who is vain, foolish, and obsessed with fashion. (3) Sir Novelty Fashion – another fop character who represents the excesses of the age. (4) The Play's Title – 'Virtue in Danger' is the subtitle, ironically suggesting that virtue is threatened by vice. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Vanbrugh's comedies; the character of Lord Foppington; compared with other Restoration comedies.

works.title: The Remains of the Day
works.shortDescription: Ishiguro's novel about Stevens, an English butler who reflects on his life of service and the missed opportunities for love and personal fulfillment.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Unreliable Narrator – Stevens is a classic unreliable narrator. (2) The Theme of Regret – the novel explores the theme of missed opportunities. (3) The English Setting – the novel is set in the English countryside. (4) The Booker Prize – the novel won the Booker Prize in 1989. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the unreliable narrator; the theme of regret; the Booker Prize.

works.title: The Republic
works.shortDescription: A Socratic dialogue by Plato, in which Socrates discusses the nature of justice and the ideal state. In Book X, Plato famously banishes poets from the republic for their imitative nature and their capacity to corrupt citizens.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Banishment of Poets – Plato argues that poets should be banished because they imitate appearances rather than truth. (2) The Theory of Forms – poets are thrice removed from truth because they imitate objects, which are themselves imitations of Forms. (3) The Moral Critique – poetry appeals to the irrational part of the soul and can corrupt citizens. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the banishment of poets; the theory of Forms; the moral critique of poetry.
works.references: Edition: c. 375 BCE. Criticism: Plato (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: The Return of the Native
works.shortDescription: Hardy's novel set on Egdon Heath, exploring the conflict between characters and the elemental forces of nature.[reference:25]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Setting – Egdon Heath is a character in itself, representing nature's indifference to human desires. (2) The Tragedy – the novel is a tragedy of miscommunication and failed love. PYQ trends: asked about the setting; the tragic elements; compared with other Hardy novels.

works.title: The Rime of the Ancient Mariner
works.shortDescription: Coleridge's longest major poem, first published in Lyrical Ballads (1798). It tells the story of a mariner who kills an albatross and suffers supernatural consequences.[reference:11]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Supernatural – the poem explores themes of guilt, punishment, and redemption. (2) The Albatross – the killing of the albatross is a symbolic act. (3) The Moral – 'He prayeth best, who loveth best / All things both great and small'. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the poem's symbolism; the supernatural elements; compared with other Romantic poems.
works.references: Edition: 1798 (in Lyrical Ballads). Criticism: Coleridge (2000), Abrams (1973).

works.title: The Ring and the Book
works.shortDescription: Browning's longest and most ambitious work, a dramatic narrative poem based on a 17th-century Roman murder trial.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Dramatic Narrative – the poem tells the story of a murder trial from multiple perspectives. (2) The Structure – the poem is divided into twelve books, each narrated by a different character. (3) The Ring and the Book – the title refers to the device of using a ring to authenticate a document. PYQ trends: asked about Browning's longer works; the dramatic narrative; compared with other Victorian epics.

works.title: The Road Not Taken
works.shortDescription: A narrative poem by Robert Frost, first published in the August 1915 issue of the Atlantic Monthly and later as the first poem in the 1916 collection, Mountain Interval.[reference:9]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Modernism – the poem is a key example of Modernist poetry. (2) The Narrative – the poem tells the story of a traveler who chooses the less-traveled road. (3) The Irony – the poem is often misunderstood as a celebration of individualism; it is actually more ambiguous. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the poem's meaning; the irony; the Modernist elements.
works.references: Edition: 1916 (in Mountain Interval). Criticism: Frost (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: The Rover
works.shortDescription: Behn's most famous play, a Restoration comedy set in Naples during the carnival. It tells the story of a group of English cavaliers and their romantic adventures.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Restoration Comedy – one of the most successful examples of Restoration comedy. (2) Female Playwright – Behn was one of the first professional female playwrights in England. (3) Themes – explores themes of love, sexuality, gender, and class. (4) The Carnival Setting – the carnival setting allows for social inversion and romantic intrigue. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Behn's The Rover; the Restoration comedy genre; Behn's role as a female playwright.

works.title: The Satanic Verses
works.shortDescription: Rushdie's fourth novel, which won the Whitbread Prize but also led to a lot of controversy. Its satirical depictions of the Prophet Muhammad ignited a furor and led to a fatwa.[reference:38][reference:39]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Controversy – the novel is famous for the controversy it generated.[reference:40] (2) The Whitbread Prize – the novel won the Whitbread Prize.[reference:41] PYQ trends: asked about the novel's publication year (1988); the controversy; and the Whitbread Prize.[reference:42]

works.title: The Scarlet Letter
works.shortDescription: Hawthorne's masterpiece, a novel set in Puritan Boston about Hester Prynne, who is forced to wear a scarlet 'A' for adultery.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Symbolism – the scarlet letter is a powerful symbol. (2) The Puritan Setting – the novel critiques Puritan society. (3) The Theme of Sin – the novel explores the nature of sin and redemption. (4) The Characters – includes Hester, Dimmesdale, and Chillingworth. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the symbolism of the scarlet letter; the Puritan setting; the theme of sin; the characters.

works.title: The School of Abuse
works.shortDescription: Gosson's polemical work attacking poetry, plays, and other forms of entertainment. It is a Puritanical attack on the arts and is famous as the work that provoked Sir Philip Sidney's Defence of Poesy.[reference:7]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Puritan Attack on Poetry – Gosson argues that poets and playwrights mislead readers and spread immorality.[reference:8] (2) Polemical Style – the work is a passionate invective against the arts. (3) Response to Sidney – the work is famous as the target of Sidney's Defence of Poesy. PYQ trends: frequently asked as the work that provoked Sidney's Defence; the Puritan attack on poetry.

works.title: The Sea, The Sea
works.shortDescription: Murdoch's novel about Charles Arrowby, a retired theatre director who retreats to a house by the sea and becomes obsessed with his first love. It won the Booker Prize.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Obsessive Love – the novel explores the theme of obsessive love. (2) The Setting – the novel is set by the sea. (3) The Ego – the novel explores the nature of the ego and self-deception. (4) The Booker Prize – the novel won the Booker Prize in 1978. PYQ trends: asked about the theme of obsessive love; the Booker Prize; the philosophical themes.

works.title: The Second Coming
works.shortDescription: One of Yeats's most famous poems, written in the aftermath of World War I. It envisions a chaotic world spinning out of control and the imminent arrival of a new, terrifying era.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Falcon – the falcon turning in 'widening gyre' symbolizes the loss of control and the breakdown of order[reference:0]. (2) The Rough Beast – the 'rough beast' slouching towards Bethlehem symbolizes the rise of a new, violent era marking the end of the Christian age[reference:1]. (3) The Gyres – Yeats's theory of history as a series of 2,000-year cycles. (4) The Vision – the poem reflects Yeats's own mystical system outlined in A Vision[reference:2]. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the symbols of the falcon and the rough beast; the apocalyptic vision; Yeats's theory of history.
works.references: Edition: 1920 (in Michael Robartes and the Dancer). Criticism: Yeats (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: The Second Sex
works.shortDescription: de Beauvoir's foundational work of feminist philosophy, which argues that 'one is not born, but rather becomes, a woman'. It is a key text of existentialist feminism.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Distinction – sex vs. gender. (2) The Woman as Other – woman is defined as the Other in relation to man. (3) The Existentialist Framework – woman's condition is a matter of existential choice. PYQ trends: one of the most frequently asked texts in NET; the distinction between sex and gender; woman as Other; compared with other feminist texts.

works.title: The Seven Lamps of Architecture
works.shortDescription: Ruskin's treatise on architecture, presenting seven moral principles or 'lamps' that guide architectural creation: Sacrifice, Truth, Power, Beauty, Life, Memory, and Obedience.[reference:7]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Seven Lamps – each lamp represents a moral principle: Sacrifice, Truth, Power, Beauty, Life, Memory, and Obedience. (2) Gothic Architecture – Ruskin praises Gothic architecture for its honesty and connection to nature. (3) Moral Aesthetics – architecture is evaluated on moral, not merely aesthetic, grounds. PYQ trends: asked about the seven lamps; Ruskin's views on Gothic architecture; the moral dimension of aesthetics.

works.title: The Shadow of a Gunman
works.shortDescription: O'Casey's first successful play, set in Dublin during the Irish War of Independence. It explores the lives of the poor and the political turmoil of the period.[reference:30]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The War of Independence – the play is set during the conflict. (2) The Slum Setting – the play is set in a tenement building. (3) The Theme of Violence – the play explores the impact of violence on ordinary people. PYQ trends: asked about O'Casey's first success; the War of Independence setting.

works.title: The Shepheardes Calendar
works.shortDescription: Spenser's first major work, a pastoral poem consisting of twelve eclogues (one for each month of the year). It established Spenser as a major poet and marked a turning point in English poetry.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Pastoral Convention – follows the classical pastoral tradition of Theocritus and Virgil. (2) Archaic Language – deliberately uses archaic and rustic diction to evoke an earlier English poetic tradition. (3) Allegorical Themes – each eclogue addresses political, religious, or personal themes. (4) E.K.'s Gloss – the poem was published with an extensive commentary by 'E.K.', whose identity remains debated. PYQ trends: asked as Spenser's first significant work; compared with Sidney's Arcadia; the pastoral mode in Elizabethan poetry.
works.references: Edition: 1579 (published under the pseudonym 'Immerito'). Criticism: King (2002).

works.title: The Shield of Achilles
works.shortDescription: Auden's poem in response to Homer's description of the shield of Achilles in the Iliad[reference:26]. It contrasts the idealized world of Homer with the brutal reality of the modern world[reference:27].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Ekphrasis – the poem responds to Homer's detailed description of the shield[reference:28]. (2) The Contrast – the poem contrasts the Homeric world of beauty and harmony with the modern world of war and impersonality[reference:29]. (3) The Irony – the shield of Achilles, which should be a thing of beauty, is depicted as a symbol of modern warfare. PYQ trends: asked about Auden's response to Homer; the theme of war; the contrast between ancient and modern.

works.title: The Silver Tassie
works.shortDescription: O'Casey's anti-war play, which was rejected by the Abbey Theatre and caused him to break with W.B. Yeats.[reference:31]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Anti-War Theme – the play is a powerful critique of war. (2) The Expressionist Element – the play incorporates expressionist techniques. (3) The Rejection – the play's rejection by the Abbey Theatre was a turning point in O'Casey's career. PYQ trends: asked about O'Casey's later works; the expressionist style; the break with Yeats.

works.title: The Soldier
works.shortDescription: Brooke's most famous poem, a sonnet that celebrates dying for one's country and expresses a deep love for England[reference:52].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Sonnet Form – a Petrarchan sonnet of fourteen lines[reference:53]. (2) The Theme of Patriotism – the poem expresses a deep love for England. (3) The Idealized Death – the poem presents death in war as beautiful and meaningful[reference:54]. (4) The Contrast – the poem is often contrasted with Owen's Dulce et Decorum Est[reference:55]. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the sonnet form; the theme of patriotism; contrasted with Owen's war poetry.

works.title: The Sot-Weed Factor
works.shortDescription: A 1960 novel by John Barth that marks the beginning of his literary postmodernism. It is a parody of the historical novel and a picaresque satire.[reference:13]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Postmodernism – the novel is a key example of Postmodernist fiction. (2) The Parody – the novel parodies the historical novel. (3) The Picaresque – the novel is a picaresque satire. (4) The Anatomy – the novel is what Northrop Frye called an 'anatomy.' PYQ trends: frequently asked about Postmodernism; the parody of the historical novel; the picaresque form.

works.title: The Sound and the Fury
works.shortDescription: Faulkner's novel about the decline of the Compson family, told from four different perspectives, including the stream-of-consciousness narrative of the mentally disabled Benjy.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Four Sections – the novel is divided into four parts, each with a different narrator. (2) Stream of Consciousness – Benjy's section is a landmark of stream of consciousness. (3) The Theme of Decline – the novel explores the decline of the Southern aristocracy. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the structure; the stream of consciousness; the theme of decline.

works.title: The Spanish Tragedy
works.shortDescription: Kyd's masterpiece, the play that established the revenge tragedy genre in English drama. It tells the story of Hieronimo, a Spanish knight who seeks revenge for the murder of his son[reference:51][reference:52].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Revenge Tragedy Genre – the play established the conventions of the revenge tragedy: a protagonist who seeks revenge, a ghost, a play-within-a-play, and a body count[reference:53]. (2) Hieronimo – the archetypal revenger who descends into madness[reference:54]. (3) The Play-within-a-Play – the play features a performance of 'Soliman and Perseda' that mirrors the plot. (4) Influence on Shakespeare – influenced Shakespeare's Hamlet and other revenge tragedies[reference:55]. (5) The Ghost of Andrea – the play opens with the ghost of Andrea and the personification of Revenge, framing the tragedy. PYQ trends: frequently asked as the first revenge tragedy; compared with Hamlet; the influence on later drama[reference:56].
works.references: Editions: Norton Critical Edition. Criticism: Kyd (2001), Schoenbaum (1987).

works.title: The Spectator
works.shortDescription: A daily periodical founded by Joseph Addison and Richard Steele, published from 1711 to 1712. It was one of the most influential publications of the 18th century, shaping English prose style and social commentary.[reference:25]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Periodical Essay – The Spectator established the periodical essay as a popular literary form.[reference:26] (2) The Spectator Club – the publication featured a cast of recurring characters, including Sir Roger de Coverley. (3) Prose Style – Addison and Steele's prose is characterized by its clarity, elegance, and wit. (4) Social Commentary – The Spectator offered commentary on manners, literature, and politics. (5) The Tatler – the precursor to The Spectator, also founded by Steele and Addison.[reference:27] PYQ trends: frequently asked about The Spectator; the periodical essay; the prose style; the character of Sir Roger de Coverley.

works.title: The Spectator
works.shortDescription: A daily periodical founded by Joseph Addison and Richard Steele, published from 1711 to 1712. It was one of the most influential publications of the 18th century, shaping English prose style and social commentary.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Periodical Essay – The Spectator established the periodical essay as a popular literary form. (2) The Spectator Club – the publication featured a cast of recurring characters, including Sir Roger de Coverley. (3) Prose Style – Addison and Steele's prose is characterized by its clarity, elegance, and wit. (4) Social Commentary – The Spectator offered commentary on manners, literature, and politics. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the periodical essay; the Spectator Club; the prose style; the character of Sir Roger de Coverley.

works.title: The Spirit of the Age
works.shortDescription: Hazlitt's collection of character sketches of twenty-five contemporary figures, including Wordsworth, Coleridge, Byron, and Bentham.[reference:49]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Character Sketch – the work is a series of portraits of notable contemporaries. (2) The Spirit of the Age – the title reflects Hazlitt's attempt to capture the intellectual and cultural spirit of the age. (3) The Subjects – includes Wordsworth, Coleridge, Byron, and Bentham. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Hazlitt's The Spirit of the Age; the character sketches; compared with other critical works.

works.title: The Spy Who Came in from the Cold
works.shortDescription: Le Carré's breakthrough novel, a Cold War spy thriller that subverts the conventions of the genre by presenting a bleak, morally ambiguous world.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Spy Thriller – the novel is a classic of the genre. (2) The Cold War Setting – the novel is set against the backdrop of the Cold War. (3) The Morally Ambiguous Hero – Alec Leamas is a disillusioned spy. (4) The Bleak Ending – the novel ends on a tragic note. PYQ trends: asked about the spy thriller genre; the Cold War setting; the morally ambiguous hero.

works.title: The Stones of Venice
works.shortDescription: Ruskin's three-volume study of Venetian architecture, celebrating the Gothic style and arguing that the decline of Venice was tied to the decline of its architectural integrity.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Nature of Gothic – the chapter 'The Nature of Gothic' is the most famous, celebrating Gothic architecture as honest, moral, and connected to nature. (2) Venetian Decline – Ruskin argues that the decline of Venice was tied to the decline of its architectural integrity. (3) Political Economy – the work contains Ruskin's early social and economic critiques. PYQ trends: frequently asked about 'The Nature of Gothic'; the connection between architecture and society.

works.title: The Story of Rimini
works.shortDescription: Hunt's narrative poem, telling the story of the love between Paolo and Francesca, based on Dante's Inferno.[reference:50]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Narrative Poetry – the poem is a romantic narrative. (2) The Dante Source – the poem is based on Dante's story of Paolo and Francesca. (3) The Cockney School – Hunt was a member of the 'Cockney School' of poets, which included Keats and Shelley. PYQ trends: asked about Hunt's narrative poetry; the Cockney School.

works.title: The Striders
works.shortDescription: Ramanujan's first major collection of poetry, which showcases his unique style of combining Indian themes with modernist techniques.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Indian Context – Ramanujan draws on Indian mythology, folklore, and everyday life. (2) The Modernist Techniques – the poet uses complex imagery and irony. (3) The Title – 'The Striders' refers to water insects that can walk on water. PYQ trends: asked about Ramanujan's style; the Indian themes; the modernist techniques.
works.references: Edition: 1966 (London: Oxford University Press). Criticism: Ramanujan (2000), King (1987).

works.title: The Study of Poetry
works.shortDescription: Arnold's critical essay on how poetry should be judged and what role it should play in human life. It introduces the 'touchstone method'.[reference:64]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Touchstone Method – Arnold's method of judging poetry by comparing passages with the best passages of the great poets. (2) The Function of Poetry – Arnold argues that poetry will replace religion and philosophy. (3) The 'High Seriousness' – Arnold values 'high seriousness' in poetry. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the touchstone method; the function of poetry; compared with other critical methods.
works.references: Edition: 1880 (in Essays in Criticism, Second Series). Criticism: Arnold (2000).

works.title: The Subjection of Women
works.shortDescription: Mill's feminist treatise arguing for the equality of women. It is a key text in the history of feminism.[reference:50][reference:51]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Feminist Argument – Mill argues for the equality of women. (2) Critique of Marriage – Mill critiques the legal subordination of women in marriage.[reference:52] (3) Social and Political Equality – Mill argues for women's social and political equality. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Mill's feminist arguments; the critique of marriage; compared with other feminist texts.

works.title: The Sun Also Rises
works.shortDescription: Hemingway's first successful novel, about a group of American and British expatriates in Paris and Spain, exploring themes of disillusionment and the 'Lost Generation'.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Lost Generation – the novel depicts the disillusioned expatriate generation. (2) The Iceberg Theory – Hemingway's style emphasizes what is not said. (3) The Bullfighting – the novel features bullfighting as a symbol of life and death. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the Lost Generation; the iceberg theory; the theme of disillusionment.

works.title: The Task
works.shortDescription: Cowper's masterpiece, a long poem in blank verse that celebrates the simple pleasures of rural life and anticipates the Romantic movement.[reference:14]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Nature Poetry – Cowper's poem is a celebration of the natural world and the simple life. (2) Blank Verse – the poem is written in blank verse, a departure from the heroic couplet. (3) Anticipation of Romanticism – the poem anticipates the Romantic movement with its focus on nature, emotion, and individual experience. (4) The Sofa – the poem begins with a humorous reflection on the invention of the sofa. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Cowper's The Task; the nature poetry; the anticipation of Romanticism; the blank verse.

works.title: The Tatler
works.shortDescription: A periodical founded by Richard Steele in 1709, with contributions from Joseph Addison. It was the precursor to The Spectator.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Precursor – The Tatler was the precursor to The Spectator. (2) The Format – the periodical was published three times a week. (3) The Content – covered politics, literature, and social life. PYQ trends: asked about The Tatler's role as a precursor; compared with The Spectator.

works.title: The Tell-Tale Heart
works.shortDescription: Poe's famous short story about a murderer who is haunted by the sound of his victim's beating heart.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Unreliable Narrator – the narrator is insane and unreliable. (2) The Gothic – the story is a classic Gothic tale. (3) The Guilt – the story explores the theme of guilt. PYQ trends: asked about the unreliable narrator; the Gothic elements; the theme of guilt.

works.title: The Temple
works.shortDescription: Herbert's major collection of poetry, published posthumously in 1633. The collection is divided into three sections: 'The Church Porch', 'The Church', and 'The Church Militant'. It is a literary depiction of the individual's relationship with God.[reference:19][reference:20]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Religious Poetry – Herbert's poetry is deeply devotional, exploring the spiritual conflicts and joys of the Christian life.[reference:21] (2) Plain Style – Herbert's style is characterized by its simplicity, clarity, and directness, in contrast to Donne's complexity. (3) Shape Poems – Herbert uses concrete poetry (e.g., 'Easter Wings', 'The Altar') to visually represent his themes.[reference:22] (4) The Temple as Metaphor – the collection is structured as a journey through the church, with each poem representing a part of the spiritual life. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Herbert's religious poetry; the shape poems; the plain style; compared with Donne.

works.title: The Tenant of Wildfell Hall
works.shortDescription: Anne Brontë's second novel, considered one of the first feminist novels. It tells the story of Helen Graham, who escapes her abusive husband and supports herself through art.[reference:18]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Feminist Novel – the novel is a powerful critique of marriage and the legal subordination of women. (2) The Epistolary Form – the novel uses letters and diary entries. (3) The Theme of Independence – Helen's determination to support herself is a key theme. PYQ trends: asked about the feminist themes; the epistolary form; compared with Jane Eyre.

works.title: The Theory of the Novel
works.shortDescription: Lukács's early work, which argues that the novel is the epic form of a world that has lost its meaning. It is a key text of Marxist literary criticism.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Novel as Epic – the novel is the epic form of the modern world. (2) The Loss of Totality – the novel reflects the fragmentation of modern life. (3) Reflection Realism – literature reflects social reality. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Lukács's theory of the novel; reflection realism; compared with other Marxist critics.

works.title: The Time Machine
works.shortDescription: Wells's first novel, a science fiction classic about a time traveler who journeys to the distant future and encounters the Eloi and the Morlocks.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Science Fiction – the novel is a foundational work of science fiction. (2) Social Critique – the novel critiques the class divisions of Victorian society. (3) The Future – the novel envisions a future in which humanity has evolved into two separate species. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the science fiction genre; the social critique; the future vision.

works.title: The Times
works.shortDescription: A British newspaper founded by John Walter in 1785, known for its authoritative journalism.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Newspaper – one of the oldest and most respected British newspapers. (2) The Authority – known for its authoritative and serious journalism. (3) The Influence – significant influence on British political life. PYQ trends: asked about the history of British journalism; The Times' role; compared with other newspapers.

works.title: The Tower
works.shortDescription: The title poem of Yeats's 1928 collection, exploring themes of aging, creativity, and the legacy of the poet.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Tower – the tower symbolizes the poet's legacy and his creative powers. (2) The Theme of Aging – the poem reflects on the poet's old age and his fear of losing creative power. (3) The Irish Context – the poem is set in the context of the Irish Civil War. PYQ trends: asked about the symbolism of the tower; the theme of aging; Yeats's later poetry.

works.title: The Traveller
works.shortDescription: Goldsmith's philosophical poem in heroic couplets, reflecting on the political and social conditions of different European countries.[reference:11]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Philosophical Poetry – the poem explores the relationship between government, climate, and national character. (2) Heroic Couplets – the poem is written in heroic couplets. PYQ trends: asked about Goldsmith's The Traveller; the poem's themes.

works.title: The Tree of Man
works.shortDescription: White's novel about the life of a pioneer family in the Australian bush, exploring the relationship between humanity and the natural world.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Pioneer Theme – the novel follows the lives of pioneers in the Australian bush. (2) The Australian Landscape – the novel explores the relationship between humans and the natural world. (3) The Mythic Quality – the novel has a mythic quality. PYQ trends: asked about the pioneer theme; the Australian landscape; the mythic quality.

works.title: The Turn of the Screw
works.shortDescription: James's novella about a governess who believes the children she cares for are being haunted by ghosts. The work is famously ambiguous about whether the ghosts are real or imagined.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Ambiguity – the novella is famously ambiguous about the reality of the ghosts. (2) The Frame Narrative – the story is told by a governess to a group of listeners. (3) The Theme of Innocence – the children represent innocence threatened by corruption. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the ambiguity; the frame narrative; the theme of innocence.

works.title: The Twice Born Fiction
works.shortDescription: Mukherjee's study of the Indian English novel, which explores the way Indian novelists have engaged with the Western tradition.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Twice Born – the Indian English novel as a 'twice born' genre. (2) The Western Tradition – the engagement with the Western tradition. (3) The Indian Context – the Indian context of the novel. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the twice-born fiction; the engagement with Western tradition; compared with other studies.

works.title: The Tyger
works.shortDescription: Blake's most famous poem from Songs of Experience, exploring the nature of creation and the existence of evil.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Symbolism – the tyger represents the fearsome and powerful aspects of creation. (2) The Question – 'Did he who made the Lamb make thee?' (3) The Meter – the poem is written in trochaic meter. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Blake's The Tyger; compared with The Lamb; the theme of creation.
works.references: Edition: 1794 (in Songs of Experience). Criticism: Blake (2000), Frye (1947).

works.title: The Typology of Detective Fiction
works.shortDescription: Todorov's essay, which classifies detective fiction into two main types: the 'whodunit' and the 'thriller'. It is a key text of structuralist genre theory.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Two Types – Todorov identifies the 'whodunit' and the 'thriller'. (2) The Structure – the detective story has a dual structure. PYQ trends: asked about Todorov's typology; the structure of detective fiction; compared with other genre theories.

works.title: The Unfinished Man
works.shortDescription: Ezekiel's second collection of poems, which explores the existential struggles of modern man and the search for identity and meaning.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Existential Themes – the collection explores alienation, loneliness, and the search for meaning. (2) Urban Poetry – Ezekiel captures the experience of urban India. (3) The Title – 'The Unfinished Man' suggests a person in a constant process of becoming. PYQ trends: asked about Ezekiel's urban poetry; the existential themes; the collection's significance.
works.references: Edition: 1960 (Bombay: Strand Bookshop). Criticism: Ezekiel (2000), King (1987).

works.title: The Unfortunate Traveller
works.shortDescription: Nashe's picaresque novel, the first of its kind in English. It tells the story of Jack Wilton, a page who travels through Europe witnessing various adventures and historical events[reference:78][reference:79].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First English Picaresque Novel – the first example of the picaresque genre in English literature[reference:80][reference:81]. (2) Historical Events – the novel includes references to historical events such as the Siege of Münster and the court of Henry VIII. (3) Satirical Tone – the novel is characterized by its satirical and often grotesque humor[reference:82][reference:83]. (4) Influence – influenced later prose fiction, including the works of Defoe and Smollett. PYQ trends: asked about Nashe's prose; the picaresque novel; the play's satirical elements[reference:84].
works.references: Editions: Norton Critical Edition. Criticism: Nashe (2002), Kinney (2005).

works.title: The Uses of Literacy
works.shortDescription: Hoggart's foundational work of cultural studies, which examines the lives of the British working class and the impact of mass culture.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Cultural Studies – the work is a foundational text of cultural studies. (2) The Working Class – the work examines the lives of the British working class. (3) Mass Culture – the work explores the impact of mass culture on working-class life. PYQ trends: frequently asked about cultural studies; Hoggart's role; compared with other cultural studies theorists.
works.references: Edition: 1957 (London: Chatto & Windus). Criticism: Hoggart (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: The Vanity of Human Wishes
works.shortDescription: Johnson's most famous poem, a moral satire in heroic couplets on the emptiness of worldly ambitions.[reference:29]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Moral Satire – the poem is a Juvenalian satire on the vanity of human desires. (2) The Theme of Ambition – the poem explores the futility of ambition and the pursuit of worldly success. (3) The Heroic Couplet – Johnson uses the heroic couplet with great skill. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Johnson's The Vanity of Human Wishes; the moral satire; compared with Pope's An Essay on Man.

works.title: The Vicar of Wakefield
works.shortDescription: Goldsmith's only novel, a sentimental and comic story of the vicar Dr. Primrose and his family.[reference:12]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Sentimental Novel – the novel is a key example of the sentimental novel. (2) The Vicar – Dr. Primrose is a memorable character, a model of Christian virtue and resignation. (3) The Plot – the novel follows the vicar's misfortunes and eventual restoration. PYQ trends: asked about Goldsmith's The Vicar of Wakefield; the sentimental novel; compared with other 18th-century novels.

works.title: The War of the Worlds
works.shortDescription: Wells's novel about an invasion of Earth by Martians, a work that established the alien invasion genre.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Alien Invasion – the novel established the alien invasion genre. (2) The Critique of Imperialism – the novel critiques the arrogance of imperialism. (3) The Famous Radio Adaptation – Orson Welles's 1938 radio adaptation caused panic. PYQ trends: asked about the alien invasion genre; the critique of imperialism; the radio adaptation.

works.title: The Waste Land
works.shortDescription: Eliot's masterpiece, the single most important modernist poem[reference:3]. It reflects the disillusionment and fragmentation of post-World War I Europe through a collage of voices, allusions, and cultural fragments[reference:4].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Five Sections – 'The Burial of the Dead,' 'A Game of Chess,' 'The Fire Sermon,' 'Death by Water,' and 'What the Thunder Said'[reference:5]. (2) Mythical Method – Eliot uses myth to structure the poem and comment on modern decay[reference:6]. (3) Tiresias – the seer who narrates across genders and time[reference:7]. (4) Themes – lust, emptiness, moral decay, spiritual crisis[reference:8]. (5) Allusions – the poem is dense with literary and cultural allusions. PYQ trends: the single most important modernist poem for exams[reference:9]; the five sections; the mythical method; the theme of disillusionment.
works.references: Edition: 1922 (in The Criterion). Criticism: Eliot (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: The Waves
works.shortDescription: Woolf's most experimental novel, composed of soliloquies delivered by six characters from childhood to old age.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Experimental Form – the novel is a series of soliloquies. (2) The Six Characters – the characters represent different aspects of consciousness. (3) The Interludes – the soliloquies are interspersed with descriptions of the sea. PYQ trends: asked about the experimental form; the soliloquies; compared with other Woolf novels.

works.title: The Way of All Flesh
works.shortDescription: Butler's semi-autobiographical novel, published posthumously in 1903. It is a savage satire on Victorian family life and the institution of the Church.[reference:48][reference:49]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Semi-Autobiographical – the novel draws on Butler's own life. (2) Satire of Victorian Family – the novel is a savage critique of Victorian family life. (3) The Posthumous Publication – the novel was published after Butler's death.[reference:50] PYQ trends: asked about the semi-autobiographical elements; the satire of Victorian family; the posthumous publication.

works.title: The Way of the World
works.shortDescription: Congreve's masterpiece and one of the greatest Restoration comedies. It is a brilliant satire on the marriage market, social pretensions, and the war of the sexes in late 17th-century London society.[reference:0]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Comedy of Manners – the play epitomizes the genre with its witty dialogue, intricate plotting, and satirical portrayal of social affectations.[reference:1] (2) The Prologue – spoken by a character representing the author, it defends the play against criticism. (3) Mirabell and Millamant – one of the greatest comic couples in English drama; their 'proviso scene' is a masterpiece of comic negotiation. (4) The Double-Dealing – the characters of Fainall and Mrs. Marwood represent the darker side of Restoration society. PYQ trends: frequently asked as the pinnacle of Restoration comedy; the proviso scene; compared with other comedies of manners.

works.title: The Well of the Saints
works.shortDescription: Synge's first three-act play, a tragicomedy about a blind couple who are cured of their blindness and then lose their sight again.[reference:22][reference:23]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Three-Act Form – Synge's first full-length play. (2) The Theme of Illusion – the play explores the relationship between illusion and reality. PYQ trends: asked about Synge's development as a playwright.

works.title: The Well Wrought Urn: Studies in the Structure of Poetry
works.shortDescription: Brooks's most famous work, a collection of essays that demonstrate the method of New Criticism. He argues for the importance of paradox and irony in poetry.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Paradox and Irony – Brooks argues that paradox and irony are central to poetry. (2) The Heresy of Paraphrase – the idea that a poem cannot be reduced to a paraphrase. (3) Close Reading – the work is a model of close reading. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the heresy of paraphrase; the importance of paradox and irony; the New Criticism method.
works.references: Edition: 1947 (New York: Reynal & Hitchcock). Criticism: Brooks (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: The Westminster Review
works.shortDescription: A British literary and political periodical founded in 1824, known for its radical politics and its support for utilitarianism.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Periodical – a major literary and political periodical. (2) The Politics – known for its radical stance. (3) The Role – supported utilitarianism and reform. PYQ trends: asked about periodicals of the Romantic period; The Westminster Review's role; compared with other periodicals.

works.title: The White Devil
works.shortDescription: Webster's first major tragedy, based on the true story of Vittoria Accoramboni, an Italian woman who was murdered for her adulterous relationships. The play is a dark exploration of corruption, lust, and power[reference:46][reference:47].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Source – based on the true story of Vittoria Accoramboni, who was murdered in 1585. (2) Vittoria Corombona – a complex female protagonist who is both victim and survivor. (3) Theme of Corruption – the play depicts a corrupt Italian court where everyone is motivated by self-interest[reference:48]. (4) The Play's Title – the 'white devil' refers to the deceptive nature of appearances. (5) The Play's Reception – not initially popular, but now regarded as one of the great Jacobean tragedies[reference:49]. PYQ trends: asked about the character of Vittoria; compared with The Duchess of Malfi; the play's treatment of gender and power[reference:50].
works.references: Sources: True story of Vittoria Accoramboni. Editions: Norton Critical Edition. Criticism: Webster (2001), Schoenbaum (1987).

works.title: The White Goddess
works.shortDescription: Graves's controversial study of poetic myth-making, arguing that the true source of poetic inspiration is the White Goddess, a figure of love, destructiveness, and poetic inspiration[reference:60].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The White Goddess – a figure who combines the powers of love, destructiveness, and poetic inspiration[reference:61]. (2) Matriarchal vs. Patriarchal – Graves argues that the White Goddess ruled during a matriarchal period before being deposed by patriarchal gods[reference:62]. (3) Poetic Inspiration – the work is a 'historical grammar of the language of poetic myth'[reference:63]. PYQ trends: asked about Graves's mythological work; the concept of the White Goddess; the influence on poetry.

works.title: The Whitsun Weddings
works.shortDescription: Larkin's third major collection, which includes some of his most famous poems. The title poem describes a train journey and the observation of wedding parties.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Third Collection – Larkin's most celebrated collection. (2) The Title Poem – 'The Whitsun Weddings' describes a train journey and the observation of wedding parties. (3) The Themes – explores themes of love, marriage, mortality, and the passage of time. PYQ trends: frequently asked about The Whitsun Weddings; the title poem; Larkin's mature style.

works.title: The Wild Swans at Coole
works.shortDescription: A lyric poem reflecting on aging, change, and the permanence of nature, set at Coole Park, the estate of Lady Gregory.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Swans – the swans represent beauty, permanence, and the ideal. (2) The Passage of Time – the poem reflects on the poet's aging and the changes in his life. (3) The Setting – Coole Park is a real place associated with Yeats's patron Lady Gregory. PYQ trends: asked about the symbolism of the swans; the theme of aging; the elegiac tone.
works.references: Edition: 1919 (in The Wild Swans at Coole). Criticism: Yeats (2000).

works.title: The Windhover
works.shortDescription: Hopkins's sonnet dedicated to Christ, celebrating the beauty and power of a kestrel in flight[reference:46].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Sonnet – the poem is a Petrarchan sonnet. (2) The Theme of Christ – the poem is dedicated to Christ[reference:47]. (3) The Sprung Rhythm – the poem is written in Hopkins's sprung rhythm[reference:48]. (4) The Inscape – the poem captures the 'inscape' of the bird[reference:49]. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Hopkins's poetry; the sprung rhythm; the theme of Christ; the concept of inscape.

works.title: Windsor Forest
works.shortDescription: Pope's topographical poem celebrating the English countryside and the peace of the Treaty of Utrecht.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Topographical Poetry – the poem is a celebration of the English landscape. (2) Political Context – the poem celebrates the Treaty of Utrecht (1713) and the peace it brought. (3) The Georgic Tradition – the poem draws on Virgil's Georgics. PYQ trends: asked about Pope's topographical poetry.

works.title: The Woman in White
