works.shortDescription: Collins's most famous novel, often regarded as one of the first mystery novels. It tells the story of Walter Hartright's encounter with a mysterious woman in white.[reference:36]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Mystery Novel – often considered one of the first mystery novels.[reference:37] (2) The Sensation Novel – the novel is a key example of the sensation novel genre.[reference:38] (3) The Multiple Narrators – the novel uses multiple narrators to tell the story. (4) The Gothic Elements – the novel uses Gothic conventions. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the mystery novel; the sensation novel; the multiple narrators.

works.title: The Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction
works.shortDescription: Benjamin's influential essay, which argues that mechanical reproduction destroys the 'aura' of the work of art and changes its political function.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Aura – the unique presence of the work of art. (2) Mechanical Reproduction – reproduction destroys the aura. (3) The Political Function – reproduction changes the political function of art. PYQ trends: one of the most frequently asked essays in NET; the aura; mechanical reproduction; the political function of art.

works.title: The World's Wife
works.shortDescription: Duffy's most famous collection, in which historic male characters are presented from the wife's viewpoint. The collection retells famous stories and fables from the perspective of women.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Feminist Re-Visioning – the collection retells stories from a female perspective. (2) The Poems – includes 'Mrs Midas', 'Mrs Darwin', and 'Queen Kong'. (3) The Wry Tone – the poems are characterized by their wry, exuberant, and feminist tone. PYQ trends: frequently asked about The World's Wife; the feminist re-visioning; individual poems.

works.title: The Wreck of the Deutschland
works.shortDescription: Hopkins's long ode commemorating the drowning of five Franciscan nuns in a shipwreck[reference:56].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Ode – the poem is a long ode in irregular meter. (2) The Theme of Faith – the poem explores the theme of faith in the face of tragedy. (3) The Sprung Rhythm – the poem is written in Hopkins's sprung rhythm[reference:57]. PYQ trends: asked about the ode form; the theme of faith; the sprung rhythm.

works.title: The Wretched of the Earth
works.shortDescription: Fanon's most influential work, which argues that decolonization is a violent process and that the colonized must reclaim their identity through revolutionary struggle.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Violence of Decolonization – decolonization is inherently violent. (2) The National Consciousness – the colonized must develop a national consciousness. (3) The Third World – the role of the Third World in global politics. PYQ trends: one of the most frequently asked texts in NET; the violence of decolonization; the concept of national consciousness; the third world.

works.title: Theory of Literature
works.shortDescription: Wellek and Warren's comprehensive textbook of literary theory, which covers a wide range of topics from literary history and criticism to genre theory.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Comprehensive Approach – the work covers a wide range of literary topics. (2) The Influence – the work was a standard textbook for generations. (3) The Intrinsic vs. Extrinsic Approach – distinguishes between internal and external approaches to literature. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the intrinsic vs. extrinsic approach; the influence of the textbook; compared with other theoretical works.
works.references: Edition: 1949 (New York: Harcourt, Brace). Criticism: Wellek & Warren (2000).

works.title: Theory of the Formal Method
works.shortDescription: Eichenbaum's essay that provides a history and defense of the Russian Formalist method, arguing for the autonomy of literary form.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Formalist Method – Eichenbaum defends the Formalist approach. (2) The Autonomy of Form – form is independent of content. (3) The History – the essay provides a history of the Formalist movement. PYQ trends: asked about the Formalist method; Eichenbaum's defense of Formalism; compared with other formalist essays.

works.title: Things Fall Apart
works.shortDescription: Achebe's most famous novel, a response to colonial narratives. It tells the story of Okonkwo, a proud Igbo warrior, and the destruction of his community by British colonialism.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Igbo Society – a richly detailed depiction of pre-colonial Igbo culture. (2) The Title – from W.B. Yeats's 'The Second Coming', symbolizing the collapse of traditional society. (3) The Tragedy – Okonkwo is a tragic hero who is destroyed by his own flaws and by colonial forces. (4) The Language – Achebe uses English to convey Igbo proverbs and rhythms. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the depiction of Igbo society; the title's meaning; the character of Okonkwo; the novel's response to colonialism.

works.title: Third Theatre
works.shortDescription: Sircar's manifesto and practice of a new form of theatre that breaks away from the proscenium stage and engages directly with the audience.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Third Theatre – Sircar's concept of a theatre that breaks away from conventional stages. (2) The Engagement – the theatre engages directly with the audience. (3) The Innovation – the form is a significant innovation in Indian theatre. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the Third Theatre concept; the innovations; compared with other theatrical movements.

works.title: This Side of Paradise
works.shortDescription: Fitzgerald's first novel, about the life of Amory Blaine, a young man who seeks love and meaning in the post-World War I world.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Novel – Fitzgerald's debut novel. (2) The Lost Generation – the novel reflects the disillusionment of the post-war generation. PYQ trends: asked about Fitzgerald's first novel; the Lost Generation.

works.title: Thyrsis
works.shortDescription: Arnold's pastoral elegy for his friend Arthur Hugh Clough.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Pastoral Elegy – the poem follows the classical tradition of pastoral mourning[reference:23]. (2) The Subject – the poem mourns the death of Clough[reference:24]. (3) The Famous Line – 'Ah, what a dust is here'. PYQ trends: asked about the pastoral elegy; compared with other elegies.

works.title: Timber or Discoveries
works.shortDescription: Jonson's collection of thoughts, impressions, and comments on various issues including life, dramatic theory, and poetics. It is a common-place book that reflects his critical views.[reference:12]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Critical Views – Jonson's opinions on dramatic theory, poetry, and literature. (2) Classical Influence – Jonson advocates for order and principle in writing, following Greek and Roman models.[reference:13] (3) Common-place Book – the work is a collection of notes and observations gathered over his career.[reference:14] (4) Influence – a significant work of Renaissance literary criticism. PYQ trends: asked about Jonson as a critic; his views on drama and poetry; compared with Sidney's Defence.

works.title: Lines Composed a Few Miles above Tintern Abbey
works.shortDescription: One of Wordsworth's most famous poems, written during a walking tour with his sister Dorothy. It reflects on the poet's relationship with nature and the passage of time.[reference:4]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Nature and Memory – the poem explores how memories of nature sustain the poet in adulthood.[reference:5] (2) The 'Presence' – Wordsworth describes a 'presence' in nature that connects all living things. (3) The Sister – the poem addresses Dorothy, who represents the poet's former self. PYQ trends: asked about the theme of nature; the poem's structure; compared with other Romantic nature poems.
works.references: Edition: 1798 (in Lyrical Ballads). Criticism: Wordsworth (2000), Abrams (1973).

works.title: To a Mouse
works.shortDescription: Burns's famous poem addressed to a mouse whose nest he has destroyed while ploughing. It contains the famous line 'The best-laid schemes o' mice an' men / Gang aft agley'.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Poem's Occasion – Burns wrote the poem after accidentally destroying a mouse's nest. (2) The Theme of Sympathy – the poem expresses sympathy for the mouse and reflects on the fragility of life. (3) The Famous Line – 'The best-laid schemes o' mice an' men / Gang aft agley' has become a proverb. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Burns's 'To a Mouse'; the famous line; the theme of sympathy.

works.title: To a Skylark
works.shortDescription: Shelley's ode to the skylark, celebrating the bird's joyful song and its symbolic power.[reference:28]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Ode Form – a short lyric ode. (2) The Skylark – the bird represents the ideal of pure, joyful creativity. (3) The Famous Line – 'Hail to thee, blithe Spirit!'[reference:29] PYQ trends: asked about Shelley's To a Skylark; the symbolism of the bird; compared with other Romantic nature poems.

works.title: To the Lighthouse
works.shortDescription: Woolf's novel about the Ramsay family and their visits to the Isle of Skye, exploring themes of time, memory, and the nature of art.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Three Sections – 'The Window', 'Time Passes', and 'The Lighthouse'. (2) The Passage of Time – the novel explores the destructive power of time. (3) The Artist Figure – Lily Briscoe is an artist who struggles to complete her painting. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the structure; the passage of time; the artist figure.

works.title: Tom Jones
works.shortDescription: Fielding's masterpiece, a comic epic in prose about the adventures of a foundling who is expelled from his adoptive home and travels through England.[reference:31]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Comic Epic in Prose – Fielding's term for his novel, which combines epic scope with comic sensibility. (2) The Foundling – Tom Jones is a foundling who is raised by a wealthy squire. (3) The Plot – the novel follows Tom's adventures as he travels through England, encountering a series of comic and satirical episodes. (4) The Narrator – the narrator is omniscient and often comments on the action. (5) The Moral – the novel explores themes of virtue, hypocrisy, and the nature of goodness. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Fielding's Tom Jones; the 'comic epic in prose'; the narrator; compared with Richardson's Clarissa.

works.title: Too Black, Too Strong
works.shortDescription: Zephaniah's collection of poems that address issues of race, identity, and social justice.[reference:47]
works.analysis:
# Themes
of race, identity, and social justice.
# Style
written in the dub poetry

works.title: Top Girls
works.shortDescription: Churchill's play about Marlene, a successful businesswoman, and her relationships with other women. It explores feminist themes, Thatcherism, and the price of success.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Feminist Play – the play is a key work of feminist theatre. (2) The Dinner Scene – the play opens with a surreal dinner party with women from history. (3) The Theme of Success – the play explores the price of success for women. (4) The Thatcher Context – the play reflects the era of Margaret Thatcher. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the feminist themes; the dinner scene; the play's political context.

works.title: Towards a Poetics of the Indian English Novel
works.shortDescription: Paranjape's work on the poetics of the Indian English novel, exploring the formal and thematic characteristics of the genre.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Poetics – the formal and thematic characteristics of the genre. (2) The Indian English Novel – the specific characteristics of the Indian English novel. (3) The Thematic Framework – the thematic structure of the genre. PYQ trends: asked about the poetics of the Indian English novel; compared with other studies of the genre.

works.title: Towards an Aesthetic of Dalit Literature
works.shortDescription: Limbale's manifesto for Dalit literature, arguing for an aesthetic that emerges from the experience of caste oppression.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Dalit Literature – a literature of the oppressed. (2) The Aesthetic – an aesthetic based on the experience of oppression. (3) The Manifesto – the work is a manifesto for Dalit literature. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Dalit literature; the aesthetic of oppression; compared with other marginal literatures.

works.title: Tradition and the Individual Talent
works.shortDescription: Eliot's seminal critical essay, arguing that a poet must be aware of the literary tradition and that poetry is an escape from personality[reference:14].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Tradition – the poet must be aware of the entire tradition of literature. (2) Impersonal Theory – poetry is not an expression of personality but an escape from it. (3) The Historical Sense – the poet must have a sense of the past and the present. PYQ trends: one of the most frequently asked critical texts in NET; the concept of tradition; the impersonal theory of poetry.

works.title: Train to Pakistan
works.shortDescription: Singh's most famous novel, which recounts the Partition of India in August 1947 through the perspective of a fictional border village.[reference:92][reference:93]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Partition – the novel is set during the Partition of India.[reference:94] (2) The Village Perspective – the novel is told through the perspective of a border village.[reference:95] PYQ trends: frequently asked about the novel's publication year (1956); the Partition setting; and the village perspective.

works.title: Tristram Shandy
works.shortDescription: Sterne's experimental novel, a comic masterpiece that defies conventional narrative structure. The novel is a series of digressions and associations, with the narrator attempting to tell the story of his life.[reference:32]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Experimental Novel – the novel breaks all the conventions of the novel form. (2) Digressive Structure – the narrative is full of digressions, with the narrator frequently getting sidetracked. (3) The Black Page – the novel includes a black page to mark the death of Parson Yorick. (4) The Marbled Page – the novel includes a marbled page, inviting the reader to interpret it. (5) The Nose and the Window – the novel features comic obsessions with noses and windows. (6) Influence – Sterne's novel has influenced countless later writers, including James Joyce and Virginia Woolf. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Sterne's Tristram Shandy; the experimental narrative; the digressions; compared with other novels.

works.title: Troilus and Criseyde
works.shortDescription: A tragic love story of Troilus and Criseyde written in rhyme royal stanza (seven-line stanza, ABABBCC). It is a psychological exploration of love and betrayal.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Rhyme Royal – seven-line stanza with the scheme ABABBCC, a significant metrical contribution. (2) Adaptation – an adaptation of Boccaccio's Il Filostrato. (3) Theme – gives the message that women cannot be trusted, though this is complicated by Criseyde's circumstances. (4) Influence – approved by Edward III and used by King James I of Scotland in The King's Quair (1424). (5) Boethian Philosophy – influenced by Boethius's Consolation of Philosophy. PYQ trends: frequently asked; Scottish Chaucerians (King James-I, Robert Henryson, William Dunbar); compared with Shakespeare's Troilus and Cressida.
works.references: Editions: Benson (1988), Riverside Chaucer. Source: Boccaccio's Il Filostrato. Influence: Boethius's Consolation of Philosophy.

works.title: Tughlaq
works.shortDescription: Karnad's first play, a historical drama about the 14th-century Sultan of Delhi. It is a powerful exploration of power, idealism, and political failure.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Historical Drama – the play is set in 14th-century India. (2) The Political Theme – the play explores the nature of power and political failure. (3) The Protagonist – Tughlaq is a complex and idealistic ruler who fails to realize his vision. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the play; the historical setting; the political themes; the character of Tughlaq.
works.references: Edition: 1965 (Delhi: Oxford University Press). Criticism: Karnad (2000), Dharwadker (2005).

works.title: Tulips and Chimneys
works.shortDescription: The first collection of poetry by E. E. Cummings, published in 1923. It showcases his experimental style, including his innovative use of typography, grammar, and form.[reference:8]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Experimental Poetry – Cummings is known for his linguistic and typographic inventions. (2) The Avant-Garde – the collection is a key example of avant-garde poetry. (3) The Themes – explores romance, death, and nature with a modern twist. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Cummings's experimental style; the use of typography; the avant-garde.

works.title: Twelfth Night
works.shortDescription: Shakespeare's comedy of mistaken identity and romantic confusion. The play tells the story of Viola, who is shipwrecked and disguises herself as a page, leading to a complex love triangle.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Genre – one of Shakespeare's most perfect comedies, often called his 'last great comedy'. (2) Theme of Identity – Viola's disguise as Cesario creates confusion about gender and identity[reference:4]. (3) Malvolio – the comic subplot of the puritanical steward who is tricked into believing Olivia loves him. (4) The Opening Line – 'If music be the food of love, play on' is a famous opening. PYQ trends: asked about the theme of identity; the character of Malvolio; the play's treatment of love; the comic subplots.
works.references: Editions: Arden Shakespeare (3rd ed., Elam, 2008). Criticism: Bloom (1998), Orgel (1996).

works.title: Ulysses
works.shortDescription: Joyce's modernist masterpiece, chronicling the events of a single day (June 16, 1904) in Dublin through the consciousness of Leopold Bloom, Stephen Dedalus, and Molly Bloom. It is a landmark of modernist literature.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Stream of Consciousness – the novel uses interior monologue to represent the characters' thoughts. (2) Mythological Parallels – parallels Homer's Odyssey. (3) Experimental Language – the novel uses a variety of styles, from parodies to a final monologue without punctuation. (4) The Structure – the novel is divided into 18 episodes. (5) The Censorship – the novel was banned for obscenity. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the stream of consciousness technique; the Homeric parallels; the final Molly Bloom monologue; the censorship.
works.references: Edition: 1922 (Paris, Shakespeare and Company). Criticism: Joyce (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Under Milk Wood
works.shortDescription: Thomas's radio play, a lyrical and humorous depiction of a day in the life of a small Welsh fishing village[reference:74].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Radio Play – a play written for radio broadcast. (2) The Welsh Setting – the play is set in the fictional village of Llareggub. (3) The Lyrical Language – the play is noted for its poetic and lyrical language. PYQ trends: asked about Thomas's prose works; the radio play; the Welsh setting.

works.title: Unto This Last
works.shortDescription: Ruskin's essays on political economy, originally published in the Cornhill Magazine in 1860. It was controversial for its critique of classical economics and its advocacy for social justice.[reference:9]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Critique of Political Economy – Ruskin attacks the 'science' of political economy for its inhumanity. (2) The 'Unto This Last' – the title is a reference to the Parable of the Labourers, emphasizing fairness and compassion. (3) Influence – Gandhi read the essays in 1904 and was profoundly influenced by them. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Ruskin's social and economic thought; the influence on Gandhi; compared with other Victorian social critics.

works.title: Untouchable
works.shortDescription: Anand's debut novel, which established him as a leading Indian English author. It follows a day in the life of Bakha, a young sweeper, and exposes the inhumanity of the caste system.[reference:0][reference:1]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Social Realism – a raw portrayal of the harsh realities of untouchability.[reference:2] (2) The Influence of Gandhi – the novel ends with Bakha's encounter with Gandhi.[reference:3] (3) E.M. Forster's Preface – the novel carries a preface by the renowned English novelist.[reference:4] (4) The First Indian English Novel to Deal with Untouchability – a landmark work.[reference:5] PYQ trends: asked about the novel's publication year (1935); the character of Bakha; the influence of Gandhi; and its status as a foundational text of Indian English literature.[reference:6][reference:7]
works.references: Edition: 1935 (London: Wishart Books). Criticism: Anand (2000), King (1987).

works.title: Up-Hill
works.shortDescription: Rossetti's short poem about the journey of life and the hope of rest[reference:32].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Dialogue Form – the poem is a dialogue between a traveler and a guide. (2) The Theme of Life – the poem explores the journey of life and the hope of rest[reference:33]. PYQ trends: asked about the dialogue form; the theme of life.

works.title: Utilitarianism
works.shortDescription: Mill's defence of utilitarianism, the ethical theory that actions are right in proportion as they tend to promote happiness.[reference:48][reference:49]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Greatest Happiness Principle – Mill argues that actions are right in proportion as they tend to promote happiness. (2) Higher and Lower Pleasures – Mill distinguishes between higher (intellectual) and lower (physical) pleasures. (3) Rule Utilitarianism – Mill's version of utilitarianism is often seen as a rule-based rather than act-based theory. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Mill's utilitarianism; the higher and lower pleasures; compared with Bentham's utilitarianism.

works.title: Vanity Fair
works.shortDescription: Thackeray's most famous novel, subtitled 'A Novel without a Hero'. It is a social satire exposing the greed, hypocrisy, and moral corruption of early 19th-century British society.[reference:32][reference:33]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Social Satire – the novel is a savage satire of British society. (2) The Subtitle – 'A Novel without a Hero' reflects the novel's cynical view of humanity. (3) The Characters – includes Becky Sharp and Amelia Sedley. (4) The Napoleonic Setting – the novel is set against the backdrop of the Napoleonic Wars. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the social satire; the subtitle; the characters; the Napoleonic setting.

works.title: Villette
works.shortDescription: Charlotte Brontë's third novel, about the protagonist Lucy Snowe who travels to the fictional city of Villette to work as a teacher.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Heroine – Lucy Snowe is a more introverted and complex heroine than Jane Eyre. (2) The Theme of Isolation – the novel explores themes of loneliness and isolation. (3) The Autobiographical Elements – the novel draws on Brontë's own experiences in Brussels. PYQ trends: asked about the character of Lucy; the theme of isolation; compared with Jane Eyre.

works.title: Volpone
works.shortDescription: Jonson's satirical comedy about a wealthy Venetian who feigns death to trick his greedy acquaintances into giving him gifts. It is one of Jonson's greatest comedies[reference:18][reference:19].
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Satire of Greed – the play is a savage satire on greed and avarice[reference:20]. (2) Animal Names – Volpone (fox), Mosca (fly), Voltore (vulture), Corbaccio (raven), Corvino (crow) – the characters are named after animals to reflect their predatory natures. (3) The Subplot – the subplot involving Sir Politic Would-Be and Peregrine satirizes English travelers. (4) Commedia dell'arte – the play draws on the Italian comic tradition. (5) The Ending – the punishments are harsh: Volpone is imprisoned and Mosca is whipped. PYQ trends: asked about the satire of greed; the use of animal names; the play's structure; compared with The Alchemist[reference:21].
works.references: Editions: Norton Critical Edition. Criticism: Donaldson (2011), Jonson (1967).

works.title: Voss
works.shortDescription: White's novel about a German explorer's expedition into the Australian outback, exploring themes of ambition, failure, and the relationship between man and the natural world.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Exploration Theme – the novel follows an expedition into the Australian outback. (2) The Australian Landscape – the novel explores the harsh Australian landscape. (3) The Philosophical Themes – the novel explores themes of ambition and failure. PYQ trends: asked about the exploration theme; the Australian landscape; the philosophical themes.
works.references: Edition: 1957 (London: Eyre & Spottiswoode). Criticism: White (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Waiting for Godot
works.shortDescription: Beckett's most famous play, a landmark of the Theatre of the Absurd. It depicts two tramps, Vladimir and Estragon, waiting for a man named Godot who never arrives.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Absurd – the play is a key example of the Theatre of the Absurd. (2) The Waiting – the central action is waiting, which symbolizes the human condition. (3) The Characters – Vladimir and Estragon are archetypal figures of human futility. (4) Godot – Godot never arrives, representing the absence of meaning or God. (5) The Structure – the play is in two acts, with the second act repeating the first. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the Theatre of the Absurd; the symbolism of Godot; the play's structure; the characters of Vladimir and Estragon.

works.title: Walden
works.shortDescription: Thoreau's account of his two years of simple living in a cabin by Walden Pond. It is a meditation on self-reliance, nature, and the meaning of life.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Simple Life – Thoreau advocates for simple living. (2) Nature – the work celebrates nature and the natural world. (3) Self-Reliance – the work is a key text of Transcendentalism. (4) The Protest – includes Thoreau's justification for not paying taxes. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the theme of simple living; the Transcendentalist ideas; the nature writing; compared with Emerson.

works.title: Waverley
works.shortDescription: Scott's first novel, subtitled ''Tis Sixty Years Since'. It is a historical novel set in Scotland during the Jacobite rebellion of 1745.[reference:42]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) Historical Novel – Scott is considered the inventor of the historical novel. (2) The Jacobite Rebellion – the novel is set during the 1745 rebellion. (3) The Waverley Novels – the series of novels Scott published under the name 'Waverley'. PYQ trends: frequently asked about Scott's Waverley; the historical novel genre; compared with other historical novels.

works.title: White Noise
works.shortDescription: DeLillo's novel about Jack Gladney, a professor of Hitler studies, and his family's encounter with a catastrophic chemical spill. It is a postmodern exploration of consumerism, media, and mortality.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Postmodern Novel – the novel is a key example of postmodern fiction. (2) The Consumerism – the novel critiques American consumer culture. (3) The Theme of Mortality – the novel explores the fear of death. (4) The Chemical Spill – the central event of the novel. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the postmodern elements; the critique of consumerism; the theme of mortality.

works.title: Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf?
works.shortDescription: Albee's most famous play, a dark comedy about the dysfunctional marriage of George and Martha, who engage in psychological warfare with their younger guests.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Dysfunctional Marriage – the play explores the destructive relationship of George and Martha. (2) The Illusion – the play's title refers to the illusion of their child. (3) The American Dream – the play critiques the American Dream. (4) The Pulitzer Prize – the play won the Tony Award and was nominated for the Pulitzer. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the dysfunctional marriage; the illusion of the child; the critique of the American Dream.

works.title: Wide Sargasso Sea
works.shortDescription: Rhys's novel, a prequel to Charlotte Brontë's Jane Eyre, telling the story of Antoinette Cosway, the 'mad woman in the attic' from the perspective of her Creole upbringing in the Caribbean.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Prequel – the novel is a prequel to Jane Eyre. (2) The Colonial Theme – the novel explores the legacy of colonialism in the Caribbean. (3) The Mad Woman – Antoinette is a sympathetic figure who is driven to madness. (4) The Narrative – the novel uses multiple narrators. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the prequel to Jane Eyre; the colonial themes; the character of Antoinette; the multiple narrators.
works.references: Edition: 1966 (London: Andre Deutsch). Criticism: Rhys (2000), Bloom (1998).

works.title: Wodwo
works.shortDescription: Hughes's fourth collection, which differs in form from his other works, comprising three sections: poems, short stories, and more poems.[reference:2]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Title – 'Wodwo' is a creature from Anglo-Saxon mythology, half-man, half-beast. (2) The Mixed Form – the collection includes poems, short stories, and prose pieces. (3) The Themes – explores themes of identity, nature, and the primitive. PYQ trends: asked about Hughes's experimental collection; the mixed form; the mythological element.

works.title: Women in Love
works.shortDescription: Lawrence's sequel to The Rainbow, focusing on the relationships of two sisters, Ursula and Gudrun Brangwen, with their lovers, Rupert Birkin and Gerald Crich.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Relationships – the novel explores different models of love and relationships. (2) The Themes – explores themes of death, sexuality, and industrial society. (3) The 'Blood-Brotherhood' – Birkin's desire for a male relationship. PYQ trends: asked about the relationships; the themes of death and sexuality; compared with The Rainbow.

works.title: World Englishes
works.shortDescription: Kachru's work on world Englishes, introducing the model of inner, outer, and expanding circles to describe the spread of English.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Three Circles – inner, outer, and expanding circles of English. (2) World Englishes – the diversity of English in the world. (3) The Indian Context – Kachru's focus on Indian English. PYQ trends: frequently asked about the three circles; world Englishes; compared with other linguistic models.
works.references: Edition: 1985 (in the journal World Englishes). Criticism: Kachru (2000).

works.title: Wuthering Heights
works.shortDescription: Emily Brontë's only novel, a Gothic masterpiece about the obsessive love between Heathcliff and Catherine Earnshaw, spanning two generations and two houses.[reference:14][reference:15]
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) The Gothic Novel – the novel uses Gothic conventions of the supernatural and the sublime. (2) The Dual Narrative – the novel is narrated by Lockwood and Nelly Dean. (3) The Two Houses – Wuthering Heights and Thrushcross Grange represent contrasting worlds. (4) The Theme of Obsessive Love – the love between Heathcliff and Catherine transcends death. (5) The Pseudonym – Emily published under the pseudonym Ellis Bell.[reference:16] PYQ trends: frequently asked about the dual narrative; the Gothic elements; the theme of obsessive love; the character of Heathcliff; published in 1847.[reference:17]

works.title: Wycliffe's Bible
works.shortDescription: The first complete translation of the Bible into English, translated directly from the Latin Vulgate. It is probable that Wycliffe personally translated the Gospels of Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John, while others in his circle translated the rest.
works.analysis: NET/SET Focus: (1) First Complete English Bible – a landmark in English religious and literary history, making scripture accessible to the laity. (2) Vernacular Translation – translated into vernacular English in 1382. (3) Later Versions – additional updated versions were done by Wycliffe's assistant John Purvey and others in 1388 and 1395 (the Later Version). (4) Reformist Context – Wycliffe was an early dissident in the Roman Catholic Church; his translation was condemned at the Council of Oxford (1408). (5) Linguistic Contribution – contributed significantly to the standardization of English prose. PYQ trends: asked about Wycliffe's role as a translator and reformer; his place in the Age of Chaucer; compared with later translations (Tyndale, King James).
works.references: Edition: Forshall & Madden (1850). Criticism: Deanesly (1920), Hudson (1988).

authors.name: A. D. Hope
authors.bio: An Australian poet, Hope is one of the most important poets in Australian literature, known for his classical form and witty verse. His major works include The Collected Poems (1972). He died in 2000.

authors.name: A. K. Ramanujan
authors.bio: A renowned poet, translator, and scholar, Ramanujan is known for his work combining Indian themes with modernist techniques. His major works include The Striders (1966) and his translations of Tamil and Kannada poetry.

authors.name: A. S. Byatt
authors.bio: An English novelist, poet, and critic, Byatt is best known for her historical and academic novels. Born in Sheffield, England in 1936, her major works include Possession (1990), The Children's Book (2009), and The Virgin in the Garden (1978). She was awarded the Booker Prize for Possession. She died in 2023.

authors.name: Joseph Addison & Richard Steele
authors.bio: Joseph Addison (1672-1719) and Richard Steele (1672-1729) were English essayists and journalists who co-founded The Spectator (1711-1712) and The Tatler (1709-1711). They established the periodical essay as a popular literary form and shaped English prose style.

authors.name: Adil Jussawalla
authors.bio: An Indian poet, known for his contribution to the modernization of Indian English poetry. His major works include Land's End (1962).

authors.name: Aimé Césaire
authors.bio: A French-Martinican poet, playwright, and politician, Césaire was a founder of the Négritude movement. His major work is Discourse on Colonialism (1955). He died in 2008.

authors.name: Aldous Huxley
authors.bio: An English writer known for his novels and essays, including the dystopian classic Brave New World (1932). His other works include Crome Yellow (1921), Antic Hay (1923), and Point Counter Point (1928)[reference:23]. He died in 1963.

authors.name: Alexander Pope
authors.bio: The supreme master of the heroic couplet and the dominant poetic voice of the Augustan age.[reference:41] Born in London in 1688, Pope was a poet and satirist of the English Augustan period, best known for his poems An Essay on Criticism (1711), The Rape of the Lock (1712–14), The Dunciad (1728), and An Essay on Man (1733–34).[reference:42] He was a central figure of the Scriblerus Club and a friend of Swift, Gay, and Arbuthnot. He died in 1744.

authors.name: Alfred Lord Tennyson
authors.bio: The dominant poetic voice of the Victorian era, Tennyson served as Poet Laureate for much of Queen Victoria's reign[reference:73]. Born in Lincolnshire in 1809, he published his first collection, Poems, Chiefly Lyrical, in 1830[reference:74]. His major works include In Memoriam (1850), Idylls of the King (1859-1885), Ulysses (1842), and The Charge of the Light Brigade (1854). He was raised to the peerage in 1884[reference:75]. He died in 1892.

authors.name: Algernon Charles Swinburne
authors.bio: A major Victorian poet known for his pagan themes, sensuous imagery, and metrical mastery. Born in London in 1837, his first major collection, Poems and Ballads (1866), caused a scandal for its pagan themes. His other works include Atalanta in Calydon (1865). He was associated with the Pre-Raphaelite movement[reference:108]. He died in 1909.

authors.name: Alice Munro
authors.bio: A Canadian short story writer, Munro is widely regarded as one of the greatest short story writers of all time. Her major works include Lives of Girls and Women (1971). She was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 2013.

authors.name: Alice Walker
authors.bio: An American novelist, poet, and activist, Walker is best known for her novel The Color Purple (1982), which explores the lives of African American women in the South. Born in Eatonton, Georgia in 1944, her major works include The Color Purple (1982), Meridian (1976), and The Third Life of Grange Copeland (1970). She was awarded the Pulitzer Prize for The Color Purple.

authors.name: Allen Ginsberg
authors.bio: An American poet whose epic poem Howl (1956) is considered one of the most significant products of the Beat movement. Born in Newark, New Jersey in 1926, he was a central figure of the Beat Generation. His major works include Howl (1956), Kaddish (1961), and The Fall of America (1973). He died in New York in 1997.

authors.name: Amitav Ghosh
authors.bio: An Indian novelist, known for his historical fiction.

authors.name: Anita Desai
authors.bio: An Indian novelist, known for her psychological realism. Her major work includes Clear Light of Day (1980).[reference:113]

authors.name: Anne Brontë
authors.bio: The youngest of the Brontë sisters, Anne is the author of Agnes Grey (1847) and The Tenant of Wildfell Hall (1848). Born in Thornton, Yorkshire in 1820, she is often considered the most underrated of the Brontë sisters.[reference:55] Her work is notable for its realism and feminist themes. She died in 1849.

authors.name: Anthony Trollope
authors.bio: A prolific Victorian novelist, Trollope is known for his detailed characterizations and his insightful depictions of society.[reference:62] Born in London in 1815, he is best known for the Chronicles of Barsetshire series, which includes The Warden (1855), Barchester Towers (1857), and Doctor Thorne (1859).[reference:63][reference:64] He also wrote the Palliser novels. He died in 1882.

authors.name: Antonio Gramsci
authors.bio: An Italian Marxist philosopher, Gramsci is known for his concept of cultural hegemony. He died in 1937.

authors.name: Aphra Behn
authors.bio: One of the first professional female playwrights in England and a significant figure of the Restoration period. Born in Kent around 1640, she worked as a spy for Charles II in the Netherlands before turning to writing. Her major works include the play The Rover (1677) and the novella Oroonoko (1688). She is known for her witty comedies, her exploration of gender and sexuality, and her pioneering role as a woman writer. She died in 1689.

authors.name: Aristotle
authors.bio: A Greek philosopher and student of Plato, Aristotle is one of the most influential figures in the history of Western thought. His Poetics (c. 335 BCE) is a foundational text of literary criticism. He died in 322 BCE.

authors.name: Aristotle Onassis
authors.bio: A Greek businessman and shipping magnate, Onassis was also a publisher of the Greek newspaper. He died in 1975.

authors.name: Arthur Hugh Clough
authors.bio: A poet whose work reflects the perplexity and religious doubt of mid-19th century England[reference:103]. Born in Liverpool in 1819[reference:104], he was a friend of Matthew Arnold[reference:105]. His major works include The Bothie of Tober-na-Vuolich (1848) and Amours de Voyage (1858)[reference:106]. He died in Florence in 1861[reference:107].

authors.name: Arthur Miller
authors.bio: An American playwright, essayist, and screenwriter, Miller is one of the most important figures in 20th-century American drama. His works explore themes of morality, social responsibility, and the American Dream. His major plays include Death of a Salesman (1949), The Crucible (1953), A View from the Bridge (1955), and All My Sons (1947). He died in 2005.

authors.name: Arun Kolatkar
authors.bio: An Indian poet and painter, known for his collection Jejuri (1976), which won the Commonwealth Poetry Prize in 1977.

authors.name: Arundhati Roy
authors.bio: An Indian novelist and activist, Roy won the Booker Prize for her debut novel The God of Small Things (1997).[reference:106]

authors.name: Badal Sircar
authors.bio: An Indian playwright, known for his development of the 'Third Theatre' movement. His major works include Evam Indrajit (1963) and his manifesto Third Theatre (1972). He was awarded the Sangeet Natak Akademi Award in 1968.

authors.name: Ben Jonson
authors.bio: Generally regarded as the second most important English dramatist after Shakespeare during the reign of James I. Born in London in 1572, he was educated at Westminster School under William Camden. He is best known for his satirical comedies including Every Man in His Humour (1598), Volpone (1606), The Alchemist (1610), and Bartholomew Fair (1614). He introduced the 'comedy of humours' to English drama. He was the first English writer to publish his plays as 'Workes' (1616), and he led the 'Tribe of Ben' at the Mermaid Tavern. His critical writings, collected in Timber or Discoveries (1641), are an important source of Renaissance literary criticism.[reference:37] He died in 1637.

authors.name: Benjamin Zephaniah
authors.bio: British author jamaican descent worked in the series peaky blinders, received Bbc Young playwright AwardIn 1988.

authors.name: Bessie Head
authors.bio: A South African novelist and short story writer, Head is known for her exploration of themes of racism, madness, and identity. Her major works include A Question of Power (1973). She died in 1986.

authors.name: Bhabani Bhattacharya
authors.bio: An Indian novelist, known for his social realism. His major work includes So Many Hungers! (1947).[reference:116]

authors.name: Boris Eichenbaum
authors.bio: A Russian literary critic and theorist, Eichenbaum is a key figure of Russian Formalism. His essay 'Theory of the Formal Method' (1926) is a defense of the Formalist approach. He died in 1959.

authors.name: Braj Kachru
authors.bio: An Indian linguist, Kachru is known for his work on world Englishes. His major contribution is the three-circle model of English. He died in 2016.

authors.name: Caedmon
authors.bio: The earliest English poet whose name is known, according to the 8th-century historian Bede in his Historia Ecclesiastica (IV.24). He was an illiterate cowherd at the monastery of Whitby who received a divine vision commanding him to sing of creation. His miraculous gift was recognized by Abbess Hilda, and he became a monk. Only his nine-line Hymn survives. He represents the critical bridge from oral to written poetic tradition in England.

authors.name: Carol Ann Duffy
authors.bio: An English poet and playwright, Duffy was appointed Poet Laureate in 2009, the first woman to hold the position. Born in Glasgow in 1955, her major works include Standing Female Nude (1985), The World's Wife (1999), Mean Time (1993), and Rapture (2005). She is known for her feminist perspective and her use of dramatic monologue.

authors.name: Caryl Churchill
authors.bio: An English playwright, known for her feminist and politically engaged plays. Her work is characterized by its formal experimentation and its exploration of gender, power, and social issues. Her major plays include Cloud Nine (1979), Top Girls (1982), Serious Money (1987), and A Number (2002). She continues to write.

authors.name: Charles Dickens
authors.bio: One of the most popular and influential novelists of the Victorian era, Dickens is known for his vivid characters, social criticism, and masterful storytelling. Born in Portsmouth in 1812, he is the author of such classics as Oliver Twist (1837), David Copperfield (1850), Bleak House (1853), Hard Times (1854), A Tale of Two Cities (1859), and Great Expectations (1861). He called David Copperfield his 'favourite child'.[reference:51] He died in 1870.

authors.name: Charles Lamb
authors.bio: An English essayist, poet, and critic, best known for his Essays of Elia (1823) and his collaborative work Tales from Shakespeare (1807) with his sister Mary. Born in London in 1775, he was a member of the Romantic circle that included Coleridge and Wordsworth. He died in 1834.[reference:62]

authors.name: Charlotte Brontë
authors.bio: The eldest of the Brontë sisters, Charlotte is best known for her novel Jane Eyre (1847), a bildungsroman that has become a classic of English literature. Born in Thornton, Yorkshire in 1816, she published under the pseudonym Currer Bell.[reference:53] Her other major works include Shirley (1849), Villette (1853), and The Professor (1857). She died in 1855.

authors.name: Chinua Achebe
authors.bio: A Nigerian novelist, poet, and critic, Achebe is widely regarded as the father of African literature. His first novel, Things Fall Apart (1958), is a foundational text of postcolonial literature. He was awarded the Man Booker International Prize in 2007. He died in 2013.

authors.name: Christina Rossetti
authors.bio: One of the most significant poets of the Victorian period, Rossetti is known for her rich imagery, emotional intensity, and exploration of spiritual and moral themes[reference:89]. Born in London in 1830, she was the sister of Dante Gabriel Rossetti and was associated with the Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood[reference:90]. Her major works include Goblin Market and Other Poems (1862) and The Prince's Progress (1866)[reference:91]. She died in 1894[reference:92].

authors.name: Christopher Marlowe
authors.bio: A towering figure of Elizabethan drama, a contemporary of William Shakespeare, and one of the earliest dramatists to bring the Renaissance spirit into English theatre[reference:93]. Despite his short life (he died at 29 in a tavern brawl), Marlowe left an indelible mark on English literature[reference:94]. He is known for his 'mighty lines' and his revolutionary use of blank verse[reference:95]. His major works include Tamburlaine the Great, Doctor Faustus, The Jew of Malta, and Edward II[reference:96][reference:97]. His focus on ambitious protagonists who strive beyond human limits laid the groundwork for Shakespeare and later Jacobean drama[reference:98].

authors.name: Claude Lévi-Strauss
authors.bio: A French anthropologist, Lévi-Strauss is a key figure in structuralism. His major works include Structural Anthropology (1958) and The Savage Mind (1962). He died in 2009.

authors.name: Cleanth Brooks
authors.bio: An American literary critic, Brooks is a key figure in the New Criticism movement. His major works include The Well Wrought Urn (1947). He died in 1994.

authors.name: Cynewulf
authors.bio: One of only four Old English poets known by name. His name, meaning 'royal wolf' (cyne = royal, wulf = wolf), is known entirely from runic acrostic signatures woven into the epilogues of four surviving poems. He was almost certainly a learned cleric, deeply versed in Latin hagiography and Christian doctrine. His corpus represents the high point of Christian Old English poetry, blending Germanic alliterative craftsmanship with continental European source material. In his epilogues, he presents himself as an aging man fearful of Judgment Day, requesting prayers for his soul.

authors.name: D. H. Lawrence
authors.bio: An English novelist, poet, playwright, essayist and literary critic[reference:3], Lawrence is known for his novels exploring human sexuality and the unconscious. His major novels include Sons and Lovers (1913), The Rainbow (1915), and Women in Love (1920)[reference:4]. He died in 1930[reference:5].

authors.name: Daniel Defoe
authors.bio: One of the earliest and most influential English novelists, known for his realistic and detailed narratives. Born in London in 1660, he was a journalist, pamphleteer, and businessman. His major novels include Robinson Crusoe (1719), Moll Flanders (1722), A Journal of the Plague Year (1722), and Roxana (1724)[reference:62]. He is often regarded as one of the first modern novelists[reference:63]. He died in 1731.

authors.name: Dante Gabriel Rossetti
authors.bio: A pivotal English poet and painter, renowned for his leadership in the Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood[reference:93]. Born in London in 1828[reference:94], he founded the Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood in 1848[reference:95]. His literary works include The House of Life, The Blessed Damozel, and Sister Helen[reference:96]. His work is characterized by its rich, sensuous imagery and mystical themes[reference:97]. He died in 1882[reference:98].

authors.name: David Hume
authors.bio: A Scottish thinker who made substantial contributions to the fields of epistemology, metaphysics, religion, mind, aesthetics, morals, politics, and history.[reference:63] Born in Edinburgh in 1711, his major philosophical works include A Treatise of Human Nature (1739-1740), the Enquiries concerning Human Understanding (1748) and concerning the Principles of Morals (1751), as well as the posthumously published Dialogues concerning Natural Religion (1779).[reference:64] He also wrote a six-volume History of England (1754-1762).[reference:65] He died in 1776.

authors.name: David Mamet
authors.bio: An American playwright, screenwriter, and director, Mamet is known for his distinctive dialogue and his critique of capitalism. His major plays include Glengarry Glen Ross (1983), American Buffalo (1975), and Speed-the-Plow (1988). He continues to write.

authors.name: Derek Walcott
authors.bio: A West Indian poet and playwright noted for works that explore the Caribbean cultural experience. Born in Castries, Saint Lucia in 1930, his major works include Dream on Monkey Mountain (1967) and the Homeric epic poem Omeros (1990). He received the 1992 Nobel Prize in Literature. He died in 2017.

authors.name: Dom Moraes
authors.bio: An Indian poet and writer, known for his lyrical poetry. His first collection, A Beginning (1957), won the Hawthornden Prize when he was just nineteen.

authors.name: Don DeLillo
authors.bio: An American novelist, playwright, and essayist, DeLillo is one of the most important contemporary American novelists. Born in New York City in 1936, his major works include White Noise (1985), Libra (1988), Mao II (1991), and Underworld (1997). He has won numerous awards, including the National Book Award.

authors.name: Doris Lessing
authors.bio: A British novelist, playwright, poet, and short-story writer, Lessing is one of the most important writers of the 20th century. Born in Iran in 1919, she grew up in Southern Rhodesia (now Zimbabwe). Her major works include The Grass Is Singing (1950), the five-volume Children of Violence series, and The Golden Notebook (1962). She was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 2007. She died in 2013.

authors.name: Dylan Thomas
authors.bio: A Welsh poet and writer, known for his intense, complex, and richly romantic poetry[reference:112]. Born in Swansea, Wales in 1914, his major collections include 18 Poems (1934), Twenty-five Poems (1936), and Deaths and Entrances (1946)[reference:113]. His most famous poems include 'Do not go gentle into that good night,' 'Fern Hill,' and 'And death shall have no dominion'[reference:114]. He also wrote the radio play Under Milk Wood (1954)[reference:115]. He died in New York in 1953[reference:116].

authors.name: E. E. Cummings
authors.bio: An American poet, painter, and playwright, known for his experimental poetry. He pioneered linguistic and typographic inventions, often using lowercase letters and unconventional syntax. His first collection was Tulips and Chimneys (1923). He died in 1962.[reference:21]

authors.name: E. M. Forster
authors.bio: An English writer, essayist, and librettist known for his novels that questioned the norms of Victorian society[reference:14]. His major works include Where Angels Fear to Tread (1905), The Longest Journey (1907), A Room with a View (1908), Howards End (1910), and A Passage to India (1924)[reference:15][reference:16]. He died in 1970[reference:17].

authors.name: Edgar Allan Poe
authors.bio: An American writer, poet, editor, and literary critic, Poe is best known for his poetry and short stories, particularly his tales of mystery and the macabre. His major works include 'The Raven' (1845) and short stories like 'The Tell-Tale Heart' (1843) and 'The Fall of the House of Usher' (1839). He died in 1849.

authors.name: Edmund Burke
authors.bio: A British statesman, parliamentary orator, and political thinker prominent in public life from 1765 to about 1795 and important in the history of political theory.[reference:59] Born in Dublin in 1729, his most famous work is Reflections on the Revolution in France (1790), a critique of the French Revolution.[reference:60] His other notable works include A Philosophical Enquiry into the Origin of Our Ideas of the Sublime and Beautiful (1757).[reference:61] He died in 1797.[reference:62]

authors.name: Edmund Spenser
authors.bio: One of the premier craftsmen of nascent Modern English verse, Spenser is considered one of the greatest poets in the English language. He was born in London around 1552 and educated at Cambridge. He is best known for The Faerie Queene, an epic poem and fantastical allegory celebrating the Tudor dynasty and Elizabeth I. He introduced the Spenserian stanza and the Spenserian sonnet, and his works embody Renaissance ideals of humanism, imaginative exuberance, and innovation. He died in 1599.

authors.name: Edward Albee
authors.bio: An American playwright, known for his dark comedies and psychological dramas. His major plays include Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf? (1962), A Delicate Balance (1966), and Three Tall Women (1994). He died in 2016.

authors.name: Edward Said
authors.bio: A Palestinian-American literary critic, philosopher, and political activist, Said is a foundational figure in postcolonial theory. His major work is Orientalism (1978). He died in 2003.

authors.name: Elaine Showalter
authors.bio: An American literary critic, Showalter is a key figure in feminist literary criticism. Her major works include A Literature of Their Own (1977) and her concept of gynocriticism.

authors.name: Elizabeth Barrett Browning
authors.bio: One of the most popular poets of the Victorian era, Barrett Browning is best known for her Sonnets from the Portuguese (1850) and the verse novel Aurora Leigh (1857)[reference:80]. Born in Durham in 1806[reference:81], she married Robert Browning in 1846[reference:82]. Her enduring fame has rested on these two works[reference:83]. She died in Florence in 1861[reference:84].

authors.name: Elizabeth Gaskell
authors.bio: A Victorian novelist and short story writer, Gaskell is known for her social commentary and her biography of Charlotte Brontë. Born in London in 1810, her major works include Mary Barton (1848), Cranford (1853), North and South (1855), and The Life of Charlotte Brontë (1857).[reference:66][reference:67] She died in 1865.

authors.name: Emily Brontë
authors.bio: The author of the Gothic masterpiece Wuthering Heights (1847), Emily Brontë is one of the most original voices in English literature. Born in Thornton, Yorkshire in 1818, she published under the pseudonym Ellis Bell.[reference:54] She died in 1848.

authors.name: Emily Dickinson
authors.bio: An American poet, Dickinson is one of the most important figures in American literature. She lived a reclusive life and wrote nearly 1,800 poems, only a few of which were published during her lifetime. Her poetry is characterized by its brevity, dashes, and unconventional capitalization. She died in 1886.

authors.name: Ernest Hemingway
authors.bio: An American novelist and short story writer, known for his economical and understated style (the 'Iceberg Theory'). His major works include The Sun Also Rises (1926), A Farewell to Arms (1929), For Whom the Bell Tolls (1940), and The Old Man and the Sea (1952)[reference:31][reference:32][reference:33]. He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1954. He died in 1961.

authors.name: Eugene O'Neill
authors.bio: An American playwright, O'Neill is considered the father of American drama. He is known for his tragic, realistic, and often experimental plays. His major works include The Iceman Cometh (1946), Long Day's Journey Into Night (1956), and The Emperor Jones (1920). He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1936. He died in 1953.

authors.name: Eunice de Souza
authors.bio: An Indian poet, novelist, and critic, known for her sharp wit and feminist themes. Her major works include Fix (1979) and Dangerous Women (1990).

authors.name: Evelyn Waugh
authors.bio: An English novelist known for his satirical comedies. His major works include Decline and Fall (1928), A Handful of Dust (1934), Brideshead Revisited (1945), and the Sword of Honour trilogy[reference:24][reference:25]. He died in 1966.

authors.name: Ezra Pound
authors.bio: A poet and critic, Pound was a supremely discerning and energetic entrepreneur of the arts who did more than any other single figure to advance a 'modern' movement in English and American literature[reference:80]. Born in Hailey, Idaho in 1885[reference:81], he was a central figure of the Imagist movement and a mentor to T. S. Eliot. His major works include Personae (1909), Hugh Selwyn Mauberley (1920), and The Cantos (1925-1969)[reference:82]. He also wrote critical essays, including A Retrospect (1918)[reference:83]. He died in Venice in 1972[reference:84].

authors.name: F. R. Leavis
authors.bio: A British literary critic, Leavis is a major figure in the development of New Criticism in Britain. He edited the periodical Scrutiny (1932-1953). He died in 1978.

authors.name: F. Scott Fitzgerald
authors.bio: An American novelist and short story writer, best known for his novel The Great Gatsby (1925), which has often been called 'the great American novel'[reference:34]. His other works include This Side of Paradise (1920) and Tender Is the Night (1934)[reference:35][reference:36]. He died in 1940[reference:37].

authors.name: Fanny Burney
authors.bio: A pioneering English novelist and diarist, known for her satirical novels of manners. Born in 1752[reference:78], she published her first novel, Evelina, anonymously in 1778[reference:79]. Her major novels include Evelina (1778), Cecilia (1782), Camilla (1796), and The Wanderer (1814)[reference:80]. Jane Austen admired her novels[reference:81]. She died in 1840.[reference:82]

authors.name: Ferdinand de Saussure
authors.bio: A Swiss linguist, Saussure is the founder of structuralism. His Course in General Linguistics (1916), published posthumously, introduced the key concepts of langue and parole, the signifier and signified, and the arbitrary nature of the linguistic sign. He died in 1913.

authors.name: Francis Bacon
authors.bio: One of the most influential philosophers, scientists, and essayists in English history. Born in London in 1561, Bacon served as Lord Chancellor of England under James I. He is known as the father of empiricism and the scientific method. His major works include The Essays (1597, 1612, 1625), The Advancement of Learning (1605), Novum Organum (1620), and The New Atlantis (1626). He is considered the father of the English essay. He died in 1626.

authors.name: Frantz Fanon
authors.bio: A French-Algerian psychiatrist, philosopher, and revolutionary, Fanon is a key figure in postcolonial theory. His major works include Black Skin, White Masks (1952) and The Wretched of the Earth (1961). He died in 1961.

authors.name: Fredric Jameson
authors.bio: An American literary critic and Marxist theorist, Jameson is a key figure in contemporary criticism. His major works include The Political Unconscious (1981) and Postmodernism (1991).

authors.name: G. N. Devy
authors.bio: An Indian literary critic and cultural theorist, Devy is known for his work on the critical tradition in Indian English literature. His major work is After Amnesia (1992).

authors.name: G. V. Desani
authors.bio: An Indian novelist and philosopher, best known for his novel All About H. Hatterr (1948).[reference:121]

authors.name: Gauri Viswanathan
authors.bio: An Indian literary critic and historian, Viswanathan is known for her work on colonial education. Her major work is Masks of Conquest (1989).

authors.name: Gayatri Chakravorty Spivak
authors.bio: An Indian literary theorist and philosopher, Spivak is a key figure in postcolonial theory. Her major essay is 'Can the Subaltern Speak?' (1988).

authors.name: Geoffrey Chaucer
authors.bio:
Born to John and Agnes Chaucer. Known as the 'Father of English Literature'. John Dryden called him the 'Father of English Poetry'. 
# Three periods of his works
**French Period**- Translated 'Roman De La Rose', original poem 'The Book of The Duchess'
**Italian Period** -Under influence of Dante wrote 'The House of Fame', wrote the 'Parliament of Fowls',  Translated 'Consolation of Philosophy' by Boethius, 'Troilus and Criseyde', 'The Legends of Good Women'
**English Period** -'Canterbury Tales'

authors.name: Georg Lukács
authors.bio: A Hungarian philosopher and literary critic, Lukács is a key figure in Marxist literary criticism. His major works include The Theory of the Novel (1916) and History and Class Consciousness (1923). He died in 1971.

authors.name: George Bernard Shaw
authors.bio: An Irish playwright, critic, and political activist, Shaw is considered the leading dramatist of his generation. Born in Dublin in 1856, he wrote more than sixty plays, including major works such as Man and Superman (1902), Pygmalion (1913), and Saint Joan (1923). He was a leader of the Fabian Society and used his plays to disseminate his political, social, and religious ideas. He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1925.[reference:41] He died in 1950.

authors.name: George Chapman
authors.bio: An English dramatist, translator and poet[reference:121]. Born near Hitchin in Hertfordshire around 1559[reference:122], he is known for his translations of Homer's Iliad and Odyssey, completed in 1616, which are among the greatest English translations[reference:123][reference:124]. As a dramatist, his best-known tragedy is Bussy D'Ambois (1607)[reference:125][reference:126], and he collaborated with Ben Jonson and John Marston on Eastward Ho! (1605)[reference:127][reference:128]. He completed Marlowe's unfinished Hero and Leander (1598)[reference:129]. He has been associated with the 'School of Night', a group of free-thinkers including Marlowe and Raleigh[reference:130]. He died in 1634[reference:131].

authors.name: George Eliot
authors.bio: The pen name of Mary Ann Evans, George Eliot is one of the greatest novelists of the Victorian era. Born in Warwickshire in 1819, she is known for her psychological depth, moral seriousness, and detailed realism. Her major works include Adam Bede (1859), The Mill on the Floss (1860), Silas Marner (1861), Middlemarch (1871-1872), and Daniel Deronda (1876).[reference:56] She died in 1880.

authors.name: George Etherege
authors.bio: A pioneer of Restoration comedy, Etherege is best known for his witty and sophisticated plays. He wrote three major comedies: The Comical Revenge (1664), She Would If She Could (1668), and The Man of Mode (1676)[reference:49]. The Man of Mode is considered his masterpiece and one of the greatest Restoration comedies. He died in 1692.

authors.name: George Farquhar
authors.bio: A Restoration dramatist, known for his cheerful and humane comedies. Born in Ireland in 1677, he was educated at Trinity College, Dublin. His major plays include Love and a Bottle (1699), The Recruiting Officer (1706)[reference:58], and The Beaux' Stratagem (1707)[reference:59], which he wrote on his deathbed[reference:60]. His plays are notable for their warmth and their sympathetic portrayal of ordinary life. He died in 1707.[reference:61]

authors.name: George Herbert
authors.bio: One of the greatest religious poets in the English language, a key figure in the metaphysical school. Born in Montgomery, Wales in 1593, he was educated at Cambridge and served as a Member of Parliament before taking holy orders and becoming a rector in Bemerton, Wiltshire. His major work is The Temple (1633), a collection of devotional poetry published shortly after his death.[reference:40] His poetry is known for its simplicity, clarity, and deep spirituality, and he is often contrasted with Donne's more complex style. He died in 1633.

authors.name: George Lamming
authors.bio: A Barbadian novelist, essayist, and poet, Lamming is known for his exploration of colonial identity and the Caribbean experience. His major works include In the Castle of My Skin (1953).

authors.name: George Meredith
authors.bio: A Victorian novelist and poet, Meredith is known for his wit, brilliant dialogue, and innovative narrative prose.[reference:68] Born in Portsmouth in 1828, his major novels include The Ordeal of Richard Feverel (1859), Evan Harrington (1860), and The Egoist (1879).[reference:69][reference:70] He died in 1909.

authors.name: George Orwell
authors.bio: An English novelist, essayist, and critic famous for his novels Animal Farm (1945) and Nineteen Eighty-Four (1949), which examine the dangers of totalitarian rule[reference:21]. He died in 1950[reference:22].

authors.name: Gerard Manley Hopkins
authors.bio: An English poet and Jesuit priest, whose posthumous fame places him among the leading English poets[reference:99]. Born in London in 1844, his prosody – notably his concept of sprung rhythm – established him as an innovator[reference:100]. His major works include God's Grandeur, The Windhover, and The Wreck of the Deutschland[reference:101][reference:102]. He died in 1889.

authors.name: Girish Karnad
authors.bio: A renowned Indian playwright, actor, and director, Karnad is known for his historical and mythological plays. His major works include Tughlaq (1965), Hayavadana (1971), and Nagamandala (1988). He was awarded the Jnanpith Award in 1998.

authors.name: Graham Greene
authors.bio: An English novelist regarded as one of the greatest writers of the 20th century[reference:26]. His major works include Brighton Rock (1938), The Power and the Glory (1940), The Heart of the Matter (1948), and The End of the Affair (1951)[reference:27]. He died in 1991.

authors.name: H. G. Wells
authors.bio: An English writer, best known for his science fiction novels. His notable works include The Time Machine (1895), The Island of Doctor Moreau (1896), The Invisible Man (1897), and The War of the Worlds (1898)[reference:18][reference:19]. He also wrote realistic novels[reference:20]. He died in 1946.

authors.name: Harish Trivedi
authors.bio: An Indian literary critic and translator, Trivedi is known for his work on colonial literary exchange. His major work is Colonial Transactions (1993).

authors.name: Harold Pinter
authors.bio: An English playwright, screenwriter, director, and actor. He is one of the most influential modern British dramatists, known for his 'comedies of menace' and the 'Pinter pause'. His major plays include The Birthday Party (1957), The Caretaker (1960), The Homecoming (1965), and Betrayal (1978). He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 2005. He died in 2008.

authors.name: Hélène Cixous
authors.bio: A French philosopher, playwright, and literary critic, Cixous is a key figure in French feminism. Her essay 'The Laugh of the Medusa' (1975) introduced the concept of écriture féminine.

authors.name: Henry David Thoreau
authors.bio: An American naturalist, essayist, poet, and philosopher, Thoreau is a leading figure of Transcendentalism. His most famous work is Walden (1854), an account of his simple life in a cabin by Walden Pond. He also wrote the essay 'Civil Disobedience' (1849). He died in 1862.

authors.name: Henry Fielding
authors.bio: One of the founders of the English novel, known for his comic and satirical works. Born in Somerset in 1707[reference:68], he was also a playwright and a magistrate. His major novels include Shamela (1741), Joseph Andrews (1742), Tom Jones (1749), and Amelia (1751)[reference:69]. He is considered, with Samuel Richardson, a founder of the English novel[reference:70]. He died in 1754.

authors.name: Henry James
authors.bio: An American novelist and naturalized English citizen[reference:10], James is known for his exploration of the contrast between American and European character. His major works include The Portrait of a Lady (1881), The Ambassadors (1903), and The Turn of the Screw (1898)[reference:11][reference:12]. He died in 1916[reference:13].

authors.name: Henry Luce
authors.bio: An American publisher, Luce founded Time (1923), Fortune (1930), and Life (1936). He died in 1967.

authors.name: Henry Vaughan
authors.bio: An Anglo-Welsh poet and mystic, considered one of the finest of the metaphysical poets.[reference:41] Born in Llansantffraed, Breconshire, Wales in 1621, he was educated at Oxford and later practiced medicine. His major work is Silex Scintillans (1650-1655), a collection of religious poetry that acknowledges his debt to George Herbert.[reference:42] His poetry is characterized by its mystical vision, its use of nature imagery, and its exploration of spiritual longing. He died in 1695.

authors.name: Herman Melville
authors.bio: An American novelist, short story writer, and poet of the American Renaissance period. His most famous work, Moby-Dick (1851), is considered one of the greatest novels in English. His other works include Typee (1846) and Billy Budd, Sailor (1924). He died in 1891.

authors.name: Homi K. Bhabha
authors.bio: An Indian literary theorist and philosopher, Bhabha is a key figure in postcolonial theory. His major work is The Location of Culture (1994).

authors.name: Horace
authors.bio: A Roman poet and literary critic at the time of Augustus. Born in 65BCE and died in 8 BCE. Suetonius' work 'Life of the Poets'. As a poet wrote Iambus or Iambic poetry and fought as a Knight

authors.name: I. A. Richards
authors.bio: A British literary critic and rhetorician, Richards is a key figure in the development of New Criticism. His major works include Principles of Literary Criticism (1924) and Practical Criticism (1929). He died in 1979.

authors.name: Ian McEwan
authors.bio: An English novelist whose works are characterized by their psychological depth, moral complexity, and often unsettling subject matter. Born in Aldershot, England in 1948, his major works include Atonement (2001), Saturday (2005), Amsterdam (1998), and Enduring Love (1997). He has won numerous awards, including the Booker Prize for Amsterdam. He continues to write and is a leading figure in contemporary English literature.

authors.name: Immanuel Kant
authors.bio: A German philosopher whose comprehensive and systematic work in epistemology, ethics, and aesthetics greatly influenced all subsequent philosophy. Born in Königsberg, Prussia (now Kaliningrad, Russia) in 1724, his major works are the three 'Critiques': Critique of Pure Reason (1781), Critique of Practical Reason (1788), and Critique of Judgment (1790). He died in 1804.[reference:65]

authors.name: Imtiaz Dharker
authors.bio: An Indian poet, artist, and film-maker, known for her exploration of themes of displacement and the experience of women. Her major works include Purdah (1989) and The Terrorist at My Table (2006).

authors.name: Iris Murdoch
authors.bio: An English novelist and philosopher, Murdoch is known for her philosophical novels exploring themes of love, morality, and the nature of the self. Born in Dublin in 1919, she grew up in England. Her major works include The Sea, The Sea (1978), Under the Net (1954), and The Black Prince (1973). She died in 1999.

authors.name: J. M. Coetzee
authors.bio: A South African novelist and essayist, Coetzee is known for his spare, allegorical prose and his exploration of the legacy of colonialism and apartheid. Born in Cape Town, South Africa in 1940, his major works include Disgrace (1999), Waiting for the Barbarians (1980), and Life & Times of Michael K (1983). He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 2003.

authors.name: J. M. Dent
authors.bio: A British publisher, Dent founded J.M. Dent and Sons and launched the Everyman's Library series in 1912. He died in 1926.

authors.name: J. M. Synge
authors.bio: A leading figure in the Irish Literary Renaissance, Synge is known for his poetic dramas depicting the harsh rural life of the Aran Islands. Born near Dublin in 1871, he was inspired by W.B. Yeats to go to the Aran Islands and draw material from life.[reference:43] His major plays include Riders to the Sea (1904), The Playboy of the Western World (1907), and the unfinished Deirdre of the Sorrows (1910). He died in 1909.

authors.name: Jack London
authors.bio: An American novelist, journalist, and social activist, a leading figure of American Naturalism. He is best known for his novels The Call of the Wild (1903) and White Fang (1906). He died in 1916.[reference:18]

authors.name: Jacques Derrida
authors.bio: A French philosopher, Derrida is the founder of deconstruction. His major works include Of Grammatology (1967) and 'Structure, Sign, and Play in the Discourse of the Human Sciences' (1966). He died in 2004.

authors.name: Jacques Lacan
authors.bio: A French psychoanalyst, Lacan is a key figure in poststructuralist psychoanalysis. His major concepts include the mirror stage and the symbolic order. He died in 1981.

authors.name: Jamaica Kincaid
authors.bio: An Antiguan-American novelist and essayist, Kincaid is known for her exploration of themes of identity, colonialism, and the relationship between mother and daughter. Her major works include Annie John (1985).

authors.name: James Joyce
authors.bio: One of the most influential and innovative writers of the 20th century, James Joyce was the author of the short story collection Dubliners (1914) and the novels A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man (1916), Ulysses (1922), and Finnegans Wake (1939)[reference:0]. He is known for his stream-of-consciousness technique, his use of mythological parallels, and his experimental language. He died in Zurich in 1941.

authors.name: Jane Austen
authors.bio: A central figure of English literature, Jane Austen is celebrated for her wit, social observation, and mastery of the novel of manners. Born in Hampshire in 1775, she wrote six major novels: Sense and Sensibility (1811), Pride and Prejudice (1813), Mansfield Park (1814), Emma (1816), Northanger Abbey (1817), and Persuasion (1817). Her works are frequently tested in character identification, thematic questions, and factual recall.[reference:52] She died in 1817.

authors.name: Jayanta Mahapatra
authors.bio: An Indian English poet, known for his exploration of themes of love, death, and the human condition. His major works include Relationship (1980). He was awarded the Sahitya Akademi Award in 1981.

authors.name: Jean-François Lyotard
authors.bio: A French philosopher, Lyotard is a key figure in postmodern theory. His major work, The Postmodern Condition (1979), defines postmodernism as 'incredulity toward metanarratives'. He died in 1998.

authors.name: Jean Rhys
authors.bio: A Dominican-English novelist, Rhys is best known for her novel Wide Sargasso Sea (1966), a prequel to Jane Eyre. She died in 1979.

authors.name: John Barth
authors.bio: An American novelist and short story writer, a leading figure of Postmodernist fiction. He is best known for his novel The Sot-Weed Factor (1960). He died in 2024.[reference:26]

authors.name: John Betjeman
authors.bio: An English poet, writer, and broadcaster, Betjeman was known for his wit, nostalgia, and social commentary. His poetry resonated with themes of nostalgia and social commentary, often appealing to those familiar with the nuances of English class structure and cultural heritage. His major works include Mount Zion (1931), Continual Dew (1937), A Few Late Chrysanthemums (1954), and Summoned by Bells (1960). He was Poet Laureate from 1972 until his death in 1984.

authors.name: John Bunyan
authors.bio: One of the most famous English writers, known for his allegorical masterpiece The Pilgrim's Progress (1678). Born in Bedfordshire in 1628, he was a Baptist preacher who was imprisoned for his nonconformist beliefs. His works include The Pilgrim's Progress, The Holy War, and The Life and Death of Mr Badman. His prose is characterized by its simplicity, vividness, and powerful allegorical imagination. He died in 1688.

authors.name: John Crowe Ransom
authors.bio: An American poet and critic, Ransom is a central figure in the New Criticism movement. His collection of essays The New Criticism (1941) helped to define and name the movement. He died in 1974.

authors.name: John Donne
authors.bio: The leading figure of the metaphysical school of poetry and one of the greatest English poets.[reference:38] Born in London in 1572 to a Catholic family, Donne converted to Anglicanism and became a priest, eventually serving as Dean of St. Paul's Cathedral. His poetry is remarkable for its concentrated passion, intellectual agility, and dramatic power.[reference:39] His major works include Songs and Sonnets, the Holy Sonnets, and his prose works such as Devotions upon Emergent Occasions. He is known for his elaborate conceits and his exploration of love, death, and religion. He died in 1631.

authors.name: John Dryden
authors.bio: The dominant literary figure of the Restoration period, known for his poetry, plays, and criticism. Born in Northamptonshire in 1631, he was educated at Cambridge and became Poet Laureate in 1668. His major works include the satirical poem Absalom and Achitophel (1681), the tragedy All for Love (1677), and the critical essay Of Dramatick Poesie (1668). He is known for his mastery of the heroic couplet and his influential literary criticism. He died in 1700.

authors.name: John Evelyn
authors.bio: An English writer, gardener, and diarist, known for his Diary (first published in 1818). Born in Surrey in 1620, he was a Royalist and a virtuoso with wide-ranging intellectual interests. His diary covers the period from 1640 to 1706 and offers a gentlemanly and scholarly account of 17th-century England. He died in 1706.

authors.name: John Fowles
authors.bio: An English novelist and essayist, Fowles is known for his postmodern, experimental works. Born in Leigh-on-Sea, England in 1926, his major works include The Collector (1963), The Magus (1965), and The French Lieutenant's Woman (1969). He died in 2005.

authors.name: John Gower
authors.bio: A medieval English poet in the tradition of courtly love and moral allegory, whose reputation once matched that of his contemporary and friend Geoffrey Chaucer. He was probably born to an affluent family from Kent and is remembered primarily for three major works written in French, Latin, and English respectively: Mirour de l'Omme (French), Vox Clamantis (Latin), and Confessio Amantis (English). He was trilingual and addressed themes of social upheaval, political morality, and divine providence.

authors.name: John Keats
authors.bio: One of the greatest Romantic poets, Keats is known for his sensuous imagery, his exploration of beauty and mortality, and his mastery of the ode form.
