Born in London in 1795, he trained as a surgeon before turning to poetry. His major works include 'Ode to a Nightingale' (1819), 'Ode on a Grecian Urn' (1819), Endymion (1818), and Hyperion (1820). He died of tuberculosis in Rome in 1821.[reference:58]

authors.name: John le Carré
authors.bio: An English novelist, le Carré is widely regarded as one of the greatest spy novelists in history. Born in Poole, Dorset in 1931, he worked for MI5 and MI6 before becoming a writer. His major works include The Spy Who Came in from the Cold (1963), Tinker Tailor Soldier Spy (1974), and Smiley's People (1979). He died in 2020.

authors.name: John Lyly
authors.bio: An English writer, playwright, courtier, and parliamentarian, considered the first English prose stylist to leave an enduring impression upon the language[reference:132][reference:133]. Born in Kent around 1554, he achieved fame with Euphues: The Anatomy of Wit (1578) and Euphues and His England (1580), which established the ornate prose style known as euphuism[reference:134]. As a dramatist, he wrote eight surviving plays, at least six of which were performed before Queen Elizabeth I[reference:135]. He is often called the 'Father of English Prose Comedy'[reference:136]. He died in 1606[reference:137].

authors.name: John Milton
authors.bio: One of the greatest poets in the English language, known for his epic masterpiece Paradise Lost (1667). Born in London in 1608, he was educated at Cambridge and became a prominent pamphleteer and defender of the Commonwealth. His works include Paradise Lost, Paradise Regained, Samson Agonistes, Lycidas, and Areopagitica. He is known for his grand style, his use of blank verse, and his exploration of theological and political themes. He died in 1674.

authors.name: John Osborne
authors.bio: An English playwright, best known for his play Look Back in Anger (1956), which revolutionized English theatre and defined the 'Angry Young Men' movement. Born in London in 1929, his other works include The Entertainer (1957) and Inadmissible Evidence (1964). He died in 1994.

authors.name: John Ruskin
authors.bio: An English critic of art, architecture, and society. His major works include Modern Painters (1843-1860) and The Stones of Venice (1851-1853). He died in 1900.

authors.name: John Steinbeck
authors.bio: An American novelist, a leading figure of American Realism. He is best known for his novels The Grapes of Wrath (1939), East of Eden (1952), and Of Mice and Men (1937). He was awarded the Pulitzer Prize and the Nobel Prize in Literature. He died in 1968.[reference:19]

authors.name: John Stuart Mill
authors.bio: A British philosopher, economist, and exponent of utilitarianism.[reference:91] Born in London in 1806, he is prominent as a logician and an ethical theorist.[reference:92] His major works include A System of Logic (1843), Principles of Political Economy (1848), On Liberty (1859), Considerations on Representative Government (1861), Utilitarianism (1863), and The Subjection of Women (1869).[reference:93] He could be regarded as a reluctant democrat, a pluralist, a cooperative socialist, an elitist and a feminist.[reference:94] He died in Avignon in 1873.[reference:95]

authors.name: John Suckling
authors.bio: A leading Cavalier poet, known for his wit, elegance, and sophisticated verse. Born in Norfolk in 1609, he was educated at Cambridge and became a courtier to Charles I. He is best known for his posthumous collection Fragmenta Aurea (1646), which includes 'Ballad upon a Wedding' and 'A Session of the Poets'. He is also credited with inventing the game of cribbage. He died in 1642.

authors.name: John Vanbrugh
authors.bio: A Restoration dramatist and architect, known for his witty and bawdy comedies. Born in London in 1664[reference:54], he is best known for two original plays: The Relapse (1696) and The Provoked Wife (1697)[reference:55]. He also wrote The Confederacy (1705)[reference:56]. Vanbrugh was also a celebrated architect, designing Blenheim Palace and Castle Howard. He was knighted in 1714[reference:57]. He died in 1726.

authors.name: John Webster
authors.bio: A celebrated English Jacobean dramatist, known primarily for his two great tragedies The White Devil (pub. 1612) and The Duchess of Malfi (pub. 1623)[reference:107][reference:108][reference:109]. With these two tragedies, Webster has achieved a reputation second only to Shakespeare's among Jacobean tragedians[reference:110]. His plays are distinguished by their intricate female characters, political themes, and poignant, poetic dialogue[reference:111]. Little is known of his life, but his work is characterized by its dark, brooding atmosphere and its exploration of corruption, power, and gender[reference:112].

authors.name: John Wycliffe
authors.bio: An English theologian, philosopher, church reformer, and promoter of the first complete translation of the Bible into English. Born in Yorkshire around the 1320s–1330s, he was an Oxford scholar who became an early dissident in the Roman Catholic Church, advocating for reform and challenging papal authority. His followers, the Lollards, continued his reformist ideas after his death in 1384. His translation of the Bible into the vernacular was a revolutionary act that laid the groundwork for the English Reformation.

authors.name: Jonathan Culler
authors.bio: An American literary theorist, Culler is a leading figure in structuralism and poststructuralism. His major works include Structuralist Poetics (1975) and Literary Theory: A Very Short Introduction (1997).

authors.name: Jonathan Swift
authors.bio: One of the greatest satirists in the English language. Born in Dublin in 1667, he was a poet, essayist, and political writer. His major works include A Tale of a Tub (1704), Gulliver's Travels (1726), and A Modest Proposal (1729)[reference:64]. He served as Dean of St. Patrick's Cathedral in Dublin. He died in 1745.

authors.name: Joseph Addison and Richard Steele
authors.bio: The co-founders of The Spectator (1711-1712) and The Tatler (1709-1711), two of the most influential periodicals of the 18th century.[reference:54] Joseph Addison (1672-1719) was a poet, playwright, and essayist. Richard Steele (1672-1729) was a playwright and essayist. Together, they established the periodical essay as a popular literary form and shaped English prose style.[reference:55]

authors.name: Joseph Conrad
authors.bio: A Polish author who wrote in English after settling in England[reference:6], Conrad is regarded as one of the greatest novelists in English[reference:7]. His major works include Heart of Darkness (1899), Lord Jim (1900), and Nostromo (1904)[reference:8][reference:9]. He died in 1924.

authors.name: Joseph Pulitzer
authors.bio: An American journalist and publisher, Pulitzer is known for his role in the development of yellow journalism and for establishing the Pulitzer Prizes. He died in 1911.

authors.name: Joyce Carol Oates
authors.bio: An American novelist, short story writer, and essayist. She is known for her prolific output and her exploration of dark themes. Her novella Black Water (1992) was a finalist for the Pulitzer Prize.[reference:25]

authors.name: Judith Butler
authors.bio: An American philosopher and gender theorist, Butler is a key figure in contemporary feminist theory. Her major work, Gender Trouble (1990), introduces the concept of gender performativity.

authors.name: Julia Kristeva
authors.bio: A French-Bulgarian philosopher and literary critic, Kristeva is a key figure in poststructuralism. Her major works include Revolution in Poetic Language (1974).

authors.name: K. R. Srinivasa Iyengar
authors.bio: An Indian literary critic and historian, Iyengar is a pioneering figure in the study of Indian English literature. His major work is Indian Writing in English (1962). He died in 1999.

authors.name: Kamala Das
authors.bio: A pioneering Indian English poet and writer, known for her confessional style and feminist themes. Her major works include My Story (1976) and 'The Dance of the Eunuchs' (1965).

authors.name: Kamala Markandaya
authors.bio: An Indian novelist, known for her depictions of rural India. Her major work includes Nectar in a Sieve (1954).[reference:115]

authors.name: Karl Marx
authors.bio: A German philosopher and economist, Marx is the founder of Marxist theory. His major works include The Communist Manifesto (1848) and Das Kapital (1867). He died in 1883.

authors.name: Katherine Mansfield
authors.bio: A New Zealand-born short story writer, Mansfield is widely considered one of the masters of the short story. Her major works include The Garden Party and Other Stories (1922). She died in 1923.

authors.name: Kazuo Ishiguro
authors.bio: An English novelist of Japanese origin, Ishiguro is known for his novels exploring memory, identity, and the nature of time. Born in Nagasaki, Japan in 1954, he moved to England as a child. His major works include The Remains of the Day (1989), Never Let Me Go (2005), and The Buried Giant (2015). He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 2017.

authors.name: Keki N. Daruwalla
authors.bio: An Indian poet and short story writer, known for his stark realism and powerful imagery. His major works include The Keeper of the Dead (1982). He was awarded the Sahitya Akademi Award in 1984.

authors.name: Khushwant Singh
authors.bio: An Indian novelist and journalist, best known for his novel Train to Pakistan (1956).[reference:119][reference:120]

authors.name: Kingsley Amis
authors.bio: An English novelist, poet, and critic, Amis was a leading figure of the Movement. Born in London in 1922, he is best known for his comic novel Lucky Jim (1954), which satirized academic life. His other works include The Anti-Death League (1966) and The Old Devils (1986). He died in 1995.

authors.name: Kurt Vonnegut
authors.bio: An American novelist, a leading figure of Postmodernist fiction. He is best known for his anti-war novel Slaughterhouse-Five (1969), which blends science fiction with black humor. He died in 2007.[reference:27]

authors.name: Laurence Sterne
authors.bio: An innovative novelist known for his experimental and digressive narrative style. Born in Ireland in 1713[reference:71], he was an Anglican clergyman. His major works are The Life and Opinions of Tristram Shandy, Gentleman (1759-1767) and A Sentimental Journey Through France and Italy (1768)[reference:72]. Tristram Shandy is considered one of the most innovative works in English literature[reference:73]. He died in 1768.

authors.name: Leigh Hunt
authors.bio: An English essayist, critic, journalist, and poet, known for his influential periodicals and his support of younger Romantic poets like Keats and Shelley. Born in 1784, his major works include The Story of Rimini (1816) and his periodical essays. He died in 1859.[reference:64]

authors.name: Longinus
authors.bio: A Greek rhetorician and philosopher, attributed as the author of On the Sublime (c. 1st century CE), a treatise on elevated thought and language. His exact date of death is unknown.

authors.name: Lord Byron
authors.bio: A leading figure of the Romantic movement, Byron is known for his narrative poems, his satirical epic Don Juan, and the creation of the 'Byronic hero'. Born in London in 1788, he achieved fame with Childe Harold's Pilgrimage (1812-1818). His major works include Don Juan (1819-1824), 'She Walks in Beauty' (1814), and the plays Sardanapalus and Cain. He died in Greece in 1824 while fighting for Greek independence.[reference:56]

authors.name: Louis Althusser
authors.bio: A French Marxist philosopher, Althusser is known for his concepts of ideological state apparatuses and interpellation. He died in 1990.

authors.name: Luce Irigaray
authors.bio: A French philosopher and psychoanalyst, Irigaray is a key figure in French feminism. Her major work, Speculum of the Other Woman (1974), critiques phallocentrism in Western philosophy.

authors.name: M. K. Naik
authors.bio: An Indian literary critic, Naik is a leading authority on Indian English literature. His major work is A History of Indian English Literature (1982). He died in 2006.

authors.name: Mahesh Dattani
authors.bio: An Indian playwright, director, and actor, Dattani is known for his exploration of contemporary social issues. His major works include Final Solutions (1993) and Tara (1990). He was awarded the Sahitya Akademi Award in 1998.

authors.name: Makarand Paranjape
authors.bio: An Indian literary critic and novelist, Paranjape is known for his work on the poetics of the Indian English novel. His major work is Towards a Poetics of the Indian English Novel (1992).

authors.name: Manju Kapur
authors.bio: An Indian novelist, known for her novels set around the time of Partition. Her major work includes Difficult Daughters (1998).[reference:117]

authors.name: Margaret Atwood
authors.bio: A Canadian poet, novelist, and critic, Atwood is one of the most celebrated writers of the contemporary period. Her major works include the dystopian novel The Handmaid's Tale (1985), The Edible Woman (1969), and Alias Grace (1996). She has won numerous awards, including the Booker Prize. She continues to write and is a prominent voice in contemporary literature.

authors.name: Mark Twain
authors.bio: An American writer, humorist, and essayist, born Samuel L. Clemens. He is best known for his novels The Adventures of Tom Sawyer (1876) and Adventures of Huckleberry Finn (1884). He is often called the 'father of American literature.' He died in 1910.[reference:15]

authors.name: Mary Shelley
authors.bio: The author of Frankenstein (1818), Mary Shelley is often credited with writing the first science fiction novel. Born in London in 1797, she was the daughter of the feminist Mary Wollstonecraft and the philosopher William Godwin.[reference:58] She conceived Frankenstein during a summer spent with Lord Byron and Percy Bysshe Shelley at Lake Geneva.[reference:59] She died in 1851.

authors.name: Mary Wollstonecraft
authors.bio: An English writer and philosopher, Wollstonecraft is a pioneering feminist. Her major work, A Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1792), is one of the earliest works of feminist philosophy. She died in 1797.

authors.name: Matthew Arnold
authors.bio: A leading Victorian poet, critic, and educator, renowned for works like Dover Beach, Culture and Anarchy, and Thyrsis.[reference:96] Born in Laleham, Middlesex in 1822, his reflections on culture, faith, and modernity are essential for literary studies. His major prose works include Essays in Criticism (1865), Culture and Anarchy (1869), and The Study of Poetry (1880).[reference:97][reference:98] He died in 1888.[reference:99]

authors.name: Maud Bodkin
authors.bio: An English literary critic, Bodkin is best known for her work Archetypal Patterns in Poetry (1934), which applied Jungian psychology to the study of literature. She died in 1967.

authors.name: Meena Alexander
authors.bio: An Indian-American poet and scholar, known for her exploration of themes of memory, loss, and the experience of women. Her major works include Illiterate Heart (2002).

authors.name: Meenakshi Mukherjee
authors.bio: An Indian literary critic, Mukherjee is known for her work on the Indian English novel. Her major work is The Twice Born Fiction (1971). She died in 2015.

authors.name: Michael Drayton
authors.bio: An English poet, the first to write odes in English in the manner of Horace. Born in 1563 in Warwickshire, he spent his early years in the service of Sir Henry Goodere and celebrated his daughter Anne as 'Idea' in his poems. His most ambitious work is Poly-Olbion (1612-1622), a topographical poem on England, among the longest in English. His other notable works include Englands Heroicall Epistles (1597), Nymphidia (1627), and The Muses Elizium (1630). He died in 1631.

authors.name: Michael Ondaatje
authors.bio: A Canadian novelist and poet, Ondaatje is known for his poetic, fragmented style. His major works include The English Patient (1992), which won the Booker Prize.

authors.name: Michel Foucault
authors.bio: A French philosopher and historian, Foucault is a key figure in poststructuralism. His major works include The Archaeology of Knowledge (1969) and Discipline and Punish (1975). He died in 1984.

authors.name: Mikhail Bakhtin
authors.bio: A Russian philosopher and literary critic, Bakhtin is known for his theories of dialogism, heteroglossia, carnivalesque, and chronotope. His major work, The Dialogic Imagination, was published posthumously in 1975. He died in 1975.

authors.name: Mulk Raj Anand
authors.bio: One of the pioneers of Indian English literature, Anand is known for his social realist novels that exposed the injustices of the caste system and British rule. His major works include Untouchable (1935) and Coolie (1936).[reference:99][reference:100]

authors.name: Nadine Gordimer
authors.bio: A South African novelist and short story writer, Gordimer is known for her opposition to apartheid. Her major works include July's People (1981). She was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1991. She died in 2014.

authors.name: Nathaniel Hawthorne
authors.bio: An American novelist and short story writer, Hawthorne is a central figure of the American Renaissance. His works explore themes of sin, guilt, and the Puritan legacy. His major works include The Scarlet Letter (1850), The House of the Seven Gables (1851), and Twice-Told Tales (1837). He died in 1864.

authors.name: Nayantara Sahgal
authors.bio: An Indian novelist, known for her political and historical fiction. Her major work includes Rich Like Us (1985).[reference:111][reference:112]

authors.name: Ngũgĩ wa Thiong'o
authors.bio: A Kenyan novelist, playwright, and essayist, Ngũgĩ is known for his political and social commentary. His major non-fiction work is Decolonising the Mind (1986).

authors.name: Nirad C. Chaudhuri
authors.bio: An Indian writer and historian, known for his autobiography Autobiography of an Unknown Indian (1951) and his exploration of Indian culture and history.

authors.name: Nissim Ezekiel
authors.bio: A pioneering figure in Indian English poetry, Ezekiel is known for his urban poetry and his exploration of existential themes. His major works include The Unfinished Man (1960) and 'Night of the Scorpion' (1965). He was awarded the Sahitya Akademi Award in 1983.

authors.name: Northrop Frye
authors.bio: A Canadian literary critic and theorist, Frye is best known for his work Anatomy of Criticism (1957), which argues for a systematic, archetypal approach to literature. He died in 1991.

authors.name: Oliver Goldsmith
authors.bio: An Anglo-Irish novelist, playwright, and poet, best known for his novel The Vicar of Wakefield (1766), his pastoral poem The Deserted Village (1770), and his plays The Good-Natur'd Man (1768) and She Stoops to Conquer (1771).[reference:45] Born in Ireland in 1728, he was a member of Dr. Johnson's Literary Club. He died in 1774.[reference:46]

authors.name: Oscar Wilde
authors.bio: An Irish poet, playwright, and aesthetic philosopher, widely recognized as a leading figure of the Aesthetic movement. Born in Dublin in 1854, he dominated London theatre with his scathing social comedies: Lady Windermere's Fan (1892), A Woman of No Importance (1893), An Ideal Husband (1895), and The Importance of Being Earnest (1895).[reference:42] He died in Paris in 1900.

authors.name: Patrick White
authors.bio: An Australian novelist and poet, White is considered one of the most important Australian writers. His major works include Voss (1957) and The Tree of Man (1955). He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1973. He died in 1990.

authors.name: Percy Bysshe Shelley
authors.bio: A major Romantic poet, Shelley is known for his lyrical genius, his radical politics, and his advocacy of liberty and love. Born in Sussex in 1792, he was expelled from Oxford for atheism. His major works include Prometheus Unbound (1820), 'Ode to the West Wind' (1819), 'Ozymandias' (1818), and 'Adonais' (1821). He drowned in Italy in 1822.[reference:57]

authors.name: Philip Larkin
authors.bio: An English poet and novelist, Larkin is considered one of the most important poets of the post-war period. He was a leading figure of the Movement, a group of poets who rejected romanticism. His major collections include The North Ship (1945), The Less Deceived (1955), The Whitsun Weddings (1964), and High Windows (1974). He died in Hull in 1985.

authors.name: Philip Roth
authors.bio: An American novelist, a leading figure of Postmodernist fiction. He is best known for his novel Portnoy's Complaint (1969), which was controversial for its explicit treatment of sexuality. He died in 2018.[reference:23]

authors.name: Pico Iyer
authors.bio: An Indian-American travel writer and essayist, known for his exploration of themes of identity, displacement, and the experience of globalization.

authors.name: Plato
authors.bio: A Greek philosopher, student of Socrates, and teacher of Aristotle. He is considered one of the most important figures in Western philosophy. His most famous work, The Republic (c. 380 BCE), includes a critique of poetry and a proposal to banish poets from the ideal state. He died in 348 BCE.

authors.name: R. K. Narayan
authors.bio: One of the most celebrated Indian English novelists, Narayan is known for his creation of the fictional town of Malgudi. His major works include Swami and Friends (1935) and The Guide (1958).[reference:101][reference:102]

authors.name: R. Parthasarathy
authors.bio: An Indian poet and translator, known for his long poem Rough Passage (1977), which is considered a landmark in Indian English poetry.

authors.name: Rabindranath Tagore
authors.bio: A Bengali poet, novelist, and playwright, Tagore is one of the most important figures in Indian literature. He was the first non-European to win the Nobel Prize in Literature (1913). His major works include Gitanjali (1910), Chandalika (1933), and The Post Office (1912).

authors.name: Raja Rao
authors.bio: A pioneering figure in Indian English literature, Rao is best known for his novel Kanthapura (1938), which is considered a foundational text of the tradition.[reference:103][reference:104]

authors.name: Ralph Waldo Emerson
authors.bio: An American essayist, lecturer, philosopher, and poet, Emerson is a leading figure of Transcendentalism. His major works include the essays 'Self-Reliance' (1841), 'The American Scholar' (1837), and the book Nature (1836). He died in 1882.

authors.name: Raymond Williams
authors.bio: A Welsh literary critic and cultural theorist, Williams is a key figure in cultural studies. His major works include Culture and Society (1958) and Marxism and Literature (1977). He died in 1988.

authors.name: René Wellek & Austin Warren
authors.bio: René Wellek (1903-1995) and Austin Warren (1899-1986) were American literary critics who co-authored Theory of Literature (1949), a comprehensive textbook of literary theory.

authors.name: Richard Crashaw
authors.bio: An English poet of the metaphysical school, known for his intensely religious and sensual poetry. Born in London in 1613 to a Puritan family, he converted to Catholicism and moved to Italy. His major works include Steps to the Temple (1646) and Carmen Deo Nostro (1652).[reference:43][reference:44] He was strongly influenced by George Herbert, but his style is more ornate, passionate, and baroque.[reference:45] He died in 1649.

authors.name: Richard Hoggart
authors.bio: An English cultural critic and academic, Hoggart is a foundational figure in cultural studies. His major work is The Uses of Literacy (1957). He died in 2014.

authors.name: Richard Hooker
authors.bio: One of the most influential Anglican theologians and prose stylists of the Elizabethan age. Born in Exeter in 1554, he is best known for his monumental work Of the Laws of Ecclesiastical Polity (1593-1597), a defense of the Church of England against Puritan attacks. He is considered one of the founding fathers of Anglicanism and one of the greatest prose stylists of the Elizabethan age.[reference:34] He died in 1600.

authors.name: Richard Lovelace
authors.bio: A leading Cavalier poet, known for his elegant and refined verse. Born in Kent in 1617, he was educated at Oxford and fought for the Royalist cause during the English Civil War. He was imprisoned twice for his loyalty to Charles I. His most famous collection is Lucasta (1649), which includes 'To Althea, from Prison' and 'To Lucasta, Going to the Wars'. His poetry celebrates honour, freedom, and love. He died in 1657.

authors.name: Robert Browning
authors.bio: A master of the dramatic monologue, Browning is a cornerstone of Victorian poetry[reference:76]. Born in London in 1812, he is best known for his dramatic monologues, including My Last Duchess, Porphyria's Lover, and The Ring and the Book[reference:77]. His work is noted for its irony, characterization, dark humour, and challenging vocabulary[reference:78]. He married Elizabeth Barrett Browning in 1846[reference:79]. He died in 1889.

authors.name: Robert Burns
authors.bio: The national poet of Scotland, who wrote lyrics and songs in Scots and in English.[reference:49] Born in Alloway, Ayrshire in 1759, he is widely regarded as the national poet of Scotland and is celebrated worldwide.[reference:50] His major works include Poems, Chiefly in the Scottish Dialect (1786), 'Auld Lang Syne' (1788), and 'To a Mouse' (1785). He died in 1796.[reference:51]

authors.name: Robert Frost
authors.bio: An American poet, a leading figure of American Modernism. He is known for his realistic depictions of rural life and his use of American colloquial speech. His major works include 'The Road Not Taken' (1916) and 'Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening.' He died in 1963.[reference:22]

authors.name: Robert Graves
authors.bio: An English poet, novelist, critic, and classical scholar[reference:106]. Born in London in 1895[reference:107], he is best known for his World War I memoir Good-Bye to All That (1929), his historical novel I, Claudius (1934), and his controversial study of poetic myth, The White Goddess (1948)[reference:108][reference:109]. He produced more than 140 works in his lifetime[reference:110]. He died in Majorca, Spain in 1985[reference:111].

authors.name: Rohinton Mistry
authors.bio: An Indian-born Canadian novelist, known for his novels set in India. His major work includes A Fine Balance (1995).[reference:110]

authors.name: Roland Barthes
authors.bio: A French literary critic, semiotician, and philosopher, Barthes is a key figure in structuralism and poststructuralism. His major works include Mythologies (1957), 'The Death of the Author' (1967), and S/Z (1970). He died in 1980.

authors.name: Roman Jakobson
authors.bio: A Russian linguist and literary theorist, Jakobson is a key figure in the development of structuralism. He made significant contributions to linguistics and poetics. He died in 1982.

authors.name: Rupert Brooke
authors.bio: An English poet whose early death in World War I contributed to his idealized image in the interwar period[reference:101]. Born in Rugby, England in 1887[reference:102], he is best known for his sonnet sequence 1914, which includes 'The Soldier'[reference:103]. His poetry reflects an early idealism about the war[reference:104]. He died of blood poisoning in Greece in 1915[reference:105].

authors.name: Rupert Murdoch
authors.bio: An Australian-American media mogul, Murdoch is the founder and CEO of News Corporation, which owns a vast array of media outlets, including The Times, The Sun, and the Wall Street Journal.

authors.name: Salman Rushdie
authors.bio: An Indian-English novelist and essayist, Rushdie is known for his magical realist works. In addition to his novels, he is also a prolific essayist.

authors.name: Samuel Beckett
authors.bio: An Irish novelist, playwright, short story writer, theatre director, poet, and literary translator. He is widely regarded as one of the most important writers of the 20th century. His work is known for its bleak, tragicomic outlook and its exploration of the human condition. His major plays include Waiting for Godot (1953), Endgame (1957), and Krapp's Last Tape (1958). He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1969. He died in Paris in 1989.

authors.name: Samuel Butler
authors.bio: A Victorian novelist, essayist, and critic, Butler is best known for his satirical utopian novel Erewhon (1872) and the posthumously published The Way of All Flesh (1903).[reference:71][reference:72] Born in Nottinghamshire in 1835, he was a critic of Victorian morality and religion.[reference:73] He died in 1902.

authors.name: Samuel Johnson
authors.bio: Dr. Samuel Johnson is best remembered for his monumental 'Dictionary of the English Language' (1755). He also wrote periodical essays for The Rambler (1750-1752) and The Idler (1758-1760). He died in 1784.

authors.name: Samuel Pepys
authors.bio: An English diarist and naval administrator, celebrated for his Diary (first published in 1825). Born in London in 1633, he served as Chief Secretary to the Admiralty under Charles II and James II. His diary, written in shorthand, offers a fascinating and intimate picture of Restoration London, including eyewitness accounts of the Great Plague (1665) and the Great Fire of London (1666). He died in 1703.

authors.name: Samuel Richardson
authors.bio: A pioneering English novelist who invented the epistolary novel. Born in Derbyshire in 1689[reference:65], he was a printer by trade. His major novels are Pamela (1740), Clarissa (1747-1748), and Sir Charles Grandison (1753)[reference:66]. He expanded the dramatic possibilities of the novel through his use of the letter form[reference:67]. He died in 1761.

authors.name: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
authors.bio: A poet, critic, and philosopher, Coleridge was a founder of the Romantic movement in England. Born in 1772, he collaborated with Wordsworth on Lyrical Ballads (1798). His major poems include 'The Rime of the Ancient Mariner' (1798), 'Kubla Khan' (1816), and 'Christabel' (1816). His prose work Biographia Literaria (1817) is the most significant work of literary criticism produced in the English Romantic period. He died in 1834.[reference:55]

authors.name: Sandra Gilbert & Susan Gubar
authors.bio: Sandra M. Gilbert (b. 1936) and Susan Gubar (b. 1944) are American literary critics who co-authored The Madwoman in the Attic (1979), a foundational work of feminist literary criticism.

authors.name: Sarah Kane
authors.bio: An English playwright, Kane is one of the most important figures of 'In-Yer-Face' theatre. Her plays are known for their graphic violence and their exploration of love, sex, and death. Her major plays include Blasted (1995), Phaedra's Love (1996), and 4.48 Psychosis (2000). She died in 1999.

authors.name: Saul Bellow
authors.bio: An American novelist, a leading figure of Postmodernist fiction. He is best known for his picaresque novel The Adventures of Augie March (1953). He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1976. He died in 2005.[reference:24]

authors.name: Seamus Heaney
authors.bio: An Irish poet whose work is notable for its evocation of Irish rural life and events in Irish history as well as for its allusions to Irish myth. Born near Castledàwson, County Londonderry, Northern Ireland in 1939, his major works include Death of a Naturalist (1966), Door into the Dark (1969), and North (1975). He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1995. He died in Dublin in 2013.

authors.name: Sean O'Casey
authors.bio: An Irish playwright and author, O'Casey is known for his plays about the Irish poor during the political troubles. Born in Dublin in 1880, his major works include The Shadow of a Gunman (1923), Juno and the Paycock (1924), and The Plough and the Stars (1926).[reference:44] He moved to England after the controversy surrounding The Plough and the Stars and died in Devon in 1964.[reference:45]

authors.name: Sharankumar Limbale
authors.bio: An Indian Dalit writer and critic, Limbale is known for his manifesto for Dalit literature. His major work is Towards an Aesthetic of Dalit Literature (2001).

authors.name: Shashi Deshpande
authors.bio: An Indian novelist, known for her feminist themes. Her major work includes The Dark Holds No Terrors (1980).[reference:114]

authors.name: Siegfried Sassoon
authors.bio: An English poet and soldier, Sassoon was one of the leading poets of the First World War[reference:95]. Born in Kent, England in 1886[reference:96], he was decorated for bravery on the Western Front but became a vocal critic of the war[reference:97]. His major poetry collections include The Old Huntsman (1917) and Counter-Attack (1918)[reference:98]. He also wrote the autobiographical Memoirs of a Fox-Hunting Man (1928)[reference:99]. He died in 1967[reference:100].

authors.name: Sigmund Freud
authors.bio: An Austrian neurologist, Freud is the founder of psychoanalysis. His major works include The Interpretation of Dreams (1900), The Psychopathology of Everyday Life (1901), and The Ego and the Id (1923). He died in 1939.

authors.name: Simone de Beauvoir
authors.bio: A French philosopher and writer, de Beauvoir is a key figure in existentialist and feminist philosophy. Her major work, The Second Sex (1949), is a foundational text of feminism. She died in 1986.

authors.name: Sir Philip Sidney
authors.bio: An English poet, courtier, scholar, and soldier, remembered as one of the most prominent figures of the Elizabethan age.[reference:36] Born in Kent in 1554, he was a model of the Renaissance ideal of the courtier. His works include the sonnet sequence Astrophel and Stella, the treatise The Defence of Poesy, and the pastoral romance The Countess of Pembroke's Arcadia. He was praised by Spenser as a noble poet, and his works had a profound influence on English literature. He died in battle at Zutphen in 1586.

authors.name: Sir Walter Raleigh
authors.bio: An English aristocrat, writer, poet, soldier, courtier, spy, and explorer. Born around 1552, he was one of the most notable figures of the Elizabethan era. He was a favorite of Queen Elizabeth until he secretly married one of her Maids-of-Honor and fell from grace. He is known for his court poetry (including 'The Lie'), his commendatory verse for Spenser's Faerie Queene, and his monumental prose work The History of the World, written while imprisoned in the Tower of London. He was executed in 1618.

authors.name: Somerset Maugham
authors.bio: An English playwright, novelist, and short-story writer, Maugham was one of the most popular and widely read authors of the early 20th century. Born in Paris in 1874, his major works include the semi-autobiographical novel Of Human Bondage (1915) and the short story 'Rain'. He also wrote many plays. He died in 1965.

authors.name: Stanley Fish
authors.bio: An American literary theorist, Fish is a key figure in reader-response theory. His major works include Is There a Text in This Class? (1980).

authors.name: Stephen Crane
authors.bio: An American poet, novelist, and short story writer, a leading figure of American Naturalism. His most famous work is The Red Badge of Courage (1895). He also wrote the short stories 'The Blue Hotel' and 'The Open Boat.' He died in 1900.[reference:17]

authors.name: Stephen Gosson
authors.bio: An English satirist and playwright, best known for his Puritanical attack on poetry and drama, The School of Abuse (1579). Gosson's work is famous as the target of Sir Philip Sidney's Defence of Poesy.[reference:35] Born in Kent around 1554, he was educated at Oxford and Cambridge. He wrote several plays and pamphlets, but is now remembered primarily for his attack on the arts. He died in 1624.

authors.name: Stevie Smith
authors.bio: An English poet and novelist, born Florence Margaret Smith. She is known for her distinctive, ironic, and offbeat style. Her major works include A Good Time Was Had by All (1937), Not Waving but Drowning (1957), and her novel Novel on Yellow Paper (1936). She won the Cholmondeley Award and was awarded the Queen's Gold Medal for Poetry. She died in 1971.

authors.name: Stuart Hall
authors.bio: A Jamaican-born English cultural theorist, Hall is a key figure in cultural studies. His major works include 'Encoding and Decoding' (1973) and 'Cultural Identity and Diaspora' (1990). He died in 2014.

authors.name: Sylvia Plath
authors.bio: An American poet and novelist, Plath is a leading figure of confessional poetry. Born in Boston, Massachusetts in 1932, she published her first major collection, The Colossus, in 1960. Her most famous collection, Ariel, was published posthumously in 1965. She also wrote the semi-autobiographical novel The Bell Jar (1963). In 1982, she became the first poet to win a Pulitzer Prize posthumously for The Collected Poems. She died in London in 1963.

authors.name: T. S. Eliot
authors.bio: A poet, playwright, and critic, Eliot was a leader of the Modernist movement. He edited the literary periodical The Criterion (1922-1939). He died in London in 1965.

authors.name: Ted Hughes
authors.bio: An English poet whose most characteristic verse is without sentimentality, emphasizing the cunning and savagery of animal life in harsh, sometimes disjunctive lines. Born in Mytholmroyd, Yorkshire in 1930, he was Poet Laureate from 1984 until his death. His major works include The Hawk in the Rain (1957), Lupercal (1960), Wodwo (1967), Crow (1972), and Birthday Letters (1998). He was married to Sylvia Plath. He died in London in 1998.

authors.name: Tennessee Williams
authors.bio: An American playwright, one of the most important figures in 20th-century American theatre. His works explore themes of desire, madness, and the decline of the Old South. His major plays include A Streetcar Named Desire (1947), The Glass Menagerie (1944), and Cat on a Hot Tin Roof (1955). He died in 1983.

authors.name: Terry Eagleton
authors.bio: An English literary theorist and critic, Eagleton is a leading figure in Marxist literary criticism. His major work is Literary Theory: An Introduction (1983).

authors.name: The Beowulf Poet
authors.bio: Anonymous poet who composed Beowulf sometime between the 8th and early 11th centuries. Modern scholarship posits a single, highly learned author who masterfully synthesized diverse heroic legends, biblical typology, and Germanic oral traditions into a unified epic. The poet's perspective is Christian, yet his subject is pagan Scandinavia, creating the poem's defining ideological tension. His identity, region, and precise date remain unknown.

authors.name: The Pearl Poet
authors.bio: The unidentified author of Sir Gawain and the Green Knight and three other poems (Pearl, Patience, Cleanness). The poet was a contemporary of Chaucer, Gower, and Langland, and was likely a native of east Cheshire or west Staffordshire, based on dialectal evidence. All four works are known from a single surviving manuscript, the Cotton MS Nero A.x. The poet is celebrated for sophisticated alliterative verse, intricate numerical structures, and profound theological and philosophical depth.

authors.name: Theodor W. Adorno
authors.bio: A German philosopher and sociologist, Adorno is a key figure in the Frankfurt School. His major works include Aesthetic Theory (1970) and his critique of the culture industry. He died in 1969.

authors.name: Theodore Dreiser
authors.bio: An American novelist, a leading figure of the Naturalist movement. His first novel, Sister Carrie (1900), was controversial for its frank depiction of sexuality. He is also known for An American Tragedy (1925). He died in 1945.[reference:16]

authors.name: Thomas Carew
authors.bio: A leading Cavalier poet and one of the 'Tribe of Ben'. Born in Kent in 1595, he was educated at Oxford and served as a courtier to Charles I. He is known for his polished and elegant verse, his erotic poem 'A Rapture', and his masque Coelum Britannicum (1634). His poems were published posthumously in 1640. He died in 1640.

authors.name: Thomas Carlyle
authors.bio: A Scottish historian, essayist, and satirical writer of the Victorian era.[reference:86] Born in Ecclefechan, Scotland in 1795, his major works include Sartor Resartus (1833-1834), The French Revolution (1837), On Heroes, Hero-Worship, and the Heroic in History (1841), and The History of Friedrich II of Prussia (1858-1865).[reference:87][reference:88] He initiated the debate on 'the condition of England question'.[reference:89] He died in London in 1881.[reference:90]

authors.name: Thomas De Quincey
authors.bio: An English essayist and critic, best known for his autobiographical Confessions of an English Opium Eater (1821). Born in Manchester in 1785, he was a close friend of Wordsworth and Coleridge. His other works include Suspiria de Profundis (1845) and the essays 'On the Knocking at the Gate in Macbeth' (1823). He died in 1859.[reference:61]

authors.name: Thomas Gray
authors.bio: An English poet whose 'Elegy Written in a Country Church Yard' is one of the best known of English lyric poems.[reference:43] Born in London in 1716, he was educated at Cambridge and became a professor of modern history there. His major works include 'Ode on a Distant Prospect of Eton College' (1747), 'Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard' (1751), 'The Progress of Poesy' (1754), and 'The Bard' (1757). He died in 1771.[reference:44]

authors.name: Thomas Hardy
authors.bio: A novelist and poet of the Victorian era, Hardy is known for his tragic vision and his portrayal of rural life in Wessex. Born in Dorset in 1840, his major novels include Far from the Madding Crowd (1874), The Return of the Native (1878), The Mayor of Casterbridge (1886), Tess of the d'Urbervilles (1891), and Jude the Obscure (1895).[reference:57] He died in 1928.

authors.name: Thomas Keneally
authors.bio: An Australian novelist, Keneally is best known for his novel Schindler's Ark (1982), which won the Booker Prize.

authors.name: Thomas Kyd
authors.bio: One of the most important figures in the development of Elizabethan drama[reference:113][reference:114]. Born in London in 1558, he is best known for The Spanish Tragedy (1592), a great popular success that established the genre of 'revenge tragedies' and greatly influenced the course of English drama[reference:115][reference:116]. The play influenced Shakespeare's Hamlet and other revenge tragedies[reference:117]. Kyd is also attributed with the lost Ur-Hamlet, a play that may have been a source for Shakespeare[reference:118][reference:119]. He died in 1594[reference:120].

authors.name: Thomas Malory
authors.bio: An English writer, the author or compiler of Le Morte d'Arthur, the classic English-language chronicle of the Arthurian legend. He was born around 1415–1416 into a family with lands in Warwickshire, Leicestershire, and Northamptonshire. The work was completed in prison around 1469–1470 and first published by William Caxton in 1485. His identity has been debated, but the most widely accepted candidate is Sir Thomas Malory of Newbold Revel, Warwickshire.

authors.name: Thomas Nashe
authors.bio: A pamphleteer, poet, dramatist, and author of The Unfortunate Traveller (1594), the first picaresque novel in English[reference:138][reference:139]. Born in Lowestoft, Suffolk in 1567, he was educated at Cambridge and became associated with Robert Greene and other professional writers in London[reference:140]. His prose is characterized by its excoriating and brilliant wit[reference:141], and he was a master of satire. His most famous works include The Unfortunate Traveller and Pierce Penilesse His Supplication to the Divell (1592)[reference:142]. He was involved in a famous feud with Gabriel Harvey[reference:143]. He died around 1601[reference:144].

authors.name: Thomas Pynchon
authors.bio: An American novelist, Pynchon is known for his dense, complex, and often parodic works that explore the dark undercurrents of American history and culture. Born in Glen Cove, New York in 1937, his major works include V. (1963), The Crying of Lot 49 (1966), and Gravity's Rainbow (1973). He is notoriously reclusive.

authors.name: Thomas Sackville
authors.bio: An English statesman, poet, and dramatist, remembered largely for his share in two achievements of significance in the development of Elizabethan poetry and drama: the collection A Myrrour for Magistrates (1563) and the tragedy Gorboduc (1561). Born in 1536 in Sussex, he was a barrister and Member of Parliament, later becoming Baron Buckhurst and eventually Earl of Dorset. His 'Induction' to the Myrrour is considered the best poetry between Chaucer and Spenser. He died in 1608.

authors.name: Thomas Traherne
authors.bio: An English poet, Anglican cleric, and religious writer, whose works were rediscovered and published in the early 20th century.[reference:46] Born in Hereford in 1637, he was educated at Oxford and served as a rector in Herefordshire. His most famous work is Centuries of Meditations, a collection of spiritual reflections celebrating the wonder of creation and the presence of God.[reference:47] His poetry and prose are characterized by their joy, simplicity, and visionary quality.[reference:48] He died in 1674.

authors.name: Tobias Smollett
authors.bio: A Scottish novelist known for his picaresque and satirical works. Born in Scotland in 1721[reference:74], he was a surgeon by training. His major novels include Roderick Random (1748), Peregrine Pickle (1751), Ferdinand Count Fathom (1753), and Humphry Clinker (1771)[reference:75]. He also wrote a Complete History of England.[reference:76] He died in 1771.[reference:77]

authors.name: Tom Stoppard
authors.bio: A British playwright of Czech origin, Stoppard is one of the most important contemporary dramatists in the English language. His work is characterized by its wit, intellectual rigour, and metatheatricality. His major plays include Rosencrantz and Guildenstern Are Dead (1966), Jumpers (1972), Travesties (1974), and The Coast of Utopia (2002). He continues to write.

authors.name: Toni Morrison
authors.bio: An American novelist, editor, and educator, Morrison is widely regarded as one of the greatest American writers. Born in Lorain, Ohio in 1931, she explored the African American experience through powerful and lyrical prose. Her major works include The Bluest Eye (1970), Song of Solomon (1977), Beloved (1987), and Jazz (1992). She was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1993. She died in 2019.

authors.name: Tzvetan Todorov
authors.bio: A French-Bulgarian literary theorist and philosopher, Todorov is a key figure in structuralism. His major works include The Typology of Detective Fiction (1966). He died in 2017.

authors.name: Upamanyu Chatterjee
authors.bio: An Indian novelist and civil servant, known for his comic novels. His major work includes English, August (1988).[reference:118]

authors.name: V. S. Naipaul
authors.bio: A Trinidadian-English novelist and essayist, known for his exploration of identity, displacement, and the experience of diaspora. His major works include A House for Mr. Biswas (1961) and An Area of Darkness (1964). He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 2001.

authors.name: Vijay Tendulkar
authors.bio: An Indian playwright, known for his powerful political and social dramas. His most famous play is Ghashiram Kotwal (1972).

authors.name: Vikram Seth
authors.bio: An Indian novelist and poet, best known for his epic novel A Suitable Boy (1993).[reference:109]

authors.name: Viktor Shklovsky
authors.bio: A Russian literary critic and theorist, Shklovsky is a key figure of Russian Formalism. He is best known for his concept of defamiliarization (ostranenie), introduced in his essay 'Art as Technique' (1917). He died in 1984.

authors.name: Virginia Woolf
authors.bio: An English novelist and essayist, Woolf is a key figure of Modernism and feminist literature. Her essay A Room of One's Own (1929) is a foundational text of feminist literary criticism. She died in 1941.

authors.name: Vladimir Nabokov
authors.bio: A Russian-American novelist, poet, and lepidopterist, Nabokov is one of the great stylists of the English language. Born in Saint Petersburg, Russia in 1899, he fled the Russian Revolution and eventually settled in the United States. His major works include Lolita (1955), Pale Fire (1962), and Speak, Memory (1951). He died in 1977.

authors.name: W. B. Yeats
authors.bio: A towering figure of 20th-century literature, Yeats was a poet, dramatist, and a driving force behind the Irish Literary Revival. Born in Dublin in 1865, he co-founded the Abbey Theatre and served as its chief during its early years.[reference:46] His plays include The Countess Cathleen (1892), Cathleen ni Houlihan (1902), and The Land of Heart's Desire (1894). He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1923.[reference:47] He died in 1939.

authors.name: W. H. Auden
authors.bio: An English-born poet and man of letters who achieved early fame in the 1930s as a hero of the left during the Great Depression[reference:85]. Born in York, England in 1907[reference:86], he is considered one of the most important English-speaking poets born in the twentieth century[reference:87]. His major works include Poems (1930), Another Time (1940), The Age of Anxiety (1947), and The Shield of Achilles (1952)[reference:88]. He died in Vienna in 1973[reference:89].

authors.name: W. K. Wimsatt & Monroe Beardsley
authors.bio: W. K. Wimsatt (1907-1975) and Monroe Beardsley (1915-1985) were American literary critics who collaborated on the essays 'The Intentional Fallacy' (1946) and 'The Affective Fallacy' (1949), which became foundational texts of New Criticism.

authors.name: Wallace Stevens
authors.bio: An American Modernist poet, known for his complex and philosophical verse. His first collection of poetry was Harmonium (1923), which includes 'The Emperor of Ice-Cream.' He died in 1955.[reference:20]

authors.name: Walt Whitman
authors.bio: An American poet, essayist, and journalist, Whitman is considered one of the most influential poets in the American canon. His collection Leaves of Grass (1855) is a landmark of American literature. He is known for his free verse and his celebration of the self and the American spirit. He died in 1892.

authors.name: Walter Benjamin
authors.bio: A German philosopher and cultural critic, Benjamin is a key figure in the Frankfurt School. His major works include 'The Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction' (1936). He died in 1940.

authors.name: Walter Pater
authors.bio: An English critic, essayist, and humanist whose advocacy of 'art for art's sake' became a cardinal doctrine of Aestheticism. His best-known work is Studies in the History of the Renaissance (1873). He died in 1894.

authors.name: Walter Scott
authors.bio: A Scottish novelist, poet, and historian, Scott is considered the inventor of the historical novel. Born in Edinburgh in 1771, his major works include the 'Waverley' novels, Ivanhoe (1819), Rob Roy (1817), and the narrative poems Marmion (1808) and The Lady of the Lake (1810). He died in 1832.[reference:60]

authors.name: Wilfred Owen
authors.bio: One of the leading poets of the First World War, Owen is known for his brutal and realistic depictions of trench warfare[reference:90]. Born in Oswestry, England in 1893[reference:91], he wrote almost all of his poems between August 1917 and September 1918[reference:92]. His most famous works include Dulce et Decorum Est, Anthem for Doomed Youth, Strange Meeting, and Futility[reference:93]. He was killed in action on November 4, 1918, just one week before the Armistice. His poems were published posthumously[reference:94].

authors.name: Wilkie Collins
authors.bio: A Victorian novelist and playwright, Collins is best known for his mystery and sensation novels. Born in London in 1824, his major works include The Woman in White (1860) and The Moonstone (1868).[reference:65] He is often regarded as one of the founders of the mystery and detective novel genres. He died in 1889.

authors.name: William Blake
authors.bio: An English poet, painter, and printmaker, Blake is known for his visionary and symbolic poetry, his illuminated printing, and his radical politics. Born in London in 1757, his major works include Songs of Innocence and of Experience (1794), The Marriage of Heaven and Hell (1790), and the prophetic books Milton and Jerusalem. He died in 1827.[reference:59]

authors.name: William Congreve
authors.bio: One of the greatest playwrights of the Restoration period, Congreve is best known for his brilliant comedies of manners. Born in Yorkshire in 1670[reference:44], he was educated at Trinity College, Dublin. His major plays include The Old Bachelour (1693), The Double Dealer (1693), Love for Love (1695), and The Way of the World (1700)[reference:45]. He also wrote a tragedy, The Mourning Bride (1697)[reference:46]. He is regarded by literary critics as one of the greatest playwrights of the Restoration period[reference:47]. He died in 1729.[reference:48]

authors.name: William Cowper
authors.bio: One of the most widely read English poets of his day, whose most characteristic work, as in The Task or the melodious short lyric 'The Poplar Trees,' brought a new directness to 18th-century nature poetry.[reference:47] Born in Hertfordshire in 1731, he suffered from bouts of depression and religious despair. His major works include The Task (1785), 'The Castaway' (1799), and his hymns. He died in 1800.[reference:48]

authors.name: William Empson
authors.bio: A British literary critic and poet, Empson is best known for his work Seven Types of Ambiguity (1930), a key text of New Criticism. He died in 1984.

authors.name: William Faulkner
authors.bio: An American novelist and short story writer, known for his stream-of-consciousness technique and his depiction of the American South. His notable works include The Sound and the Fury (1929), As I Lay Dying (1930), Light in August (1932), and Absalom, Absalom! (1936)[reference:28][reference:29]. He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1949. He died in 1962[reference:30].

authors.name: William Hazlitt
authors.bio: An English essayist, critic, and social commentator, known for his penetrating literary and art criticism. Born in Kent in 1778, his major works include The Spirit of the Age (1825) and Lectures on the English Poets (1818). He died in 1830.[reference:63]

authors.name: William Langland
authors.bio: Believed to have been born around 1330–1332 in Shropshire, Langland is the presumed author of Piers Plowman, one of the greatest examples of Middle English alliterative poetry. He was probably educated at the monastery of Great Malvern and trained to be a priest, but due to the death of his patrons, he only took Minor Orders. His poem reflects deep social concern, religious reformism, and a critique of clerical corruption. He died around 1400.

authors.name: William Makepeace Thackeray
authors.bio: A Victorian novelist and satirist, Thackeray is best known for his novel Vanity Fair (1848), a panoramic survey of English manners and human frailties.[reference:60][reference:61] Born in Calcutta in 1811, he is also the author of The History of Henry Esmond (1852) and The Book of Snobs. He died in 1863.

authors.name: William Randolph Hearst
authors.bio: An American publisher, Hearst is known for his role in the development of yellow journalism and his ownership of a major newspaper chain. He died in 1951.

authors.name: William Shakespeare
authors.bio: Known as the 'Bard of Avon', Shakespeare is the most important and frequently asked writer in the UGC NET examination. Born in Stratford-upon-Avon in 1564, he wrote 37 plays, 154 sonnets, and two long narrative poems. He is credited with introducing over 1,700 words to the English language and is considered the greatest writer in the English language. His works span comedies, histories, tragedies, and romances. John Dryden said of him, 'He was the man who of all modern, and perhaps ancient poets, had the largest and most comprehensive soul'. Ben Jonson wrote the famous epitaph: 'He was not of an age, but for all time'. His plays are foundational to the UGC NET syllabus, appearing across multiple units including Elizabethan drama, literary history, genres, dramatic devices, and thematic analysis[reference:92].

authors.name: William Wordsworth
authors.bio: One of the central figures of the Romantic movement, Wordsworth is known for his celebration of nature, the common man, and the power of memory. Born in Cockermouth, England in 1770, he collaborated with Coleridge on Lyrical Ballads (1798), which heralded the Romantic era. His major works include The Prelude (1850), 'Tintern Abbey' (1798), and 'Ode: Intimations of Immortality' (1807). He served as Poet Laureate from 1843 until his death in 1850.[reference:54]

authors.name: William Wycherley
authors.bio: A leading Restoration dramatist, known for his bawdy and satirical comedies. Born in 1641[reference:50], he was educated in France and at Oxford. His major plays include Love in a Wood (1671), The Gentleman Dancing-Master (1673), The Country Wife (1675), and The Plain Dealer (1676)[reference:51]. His plays reflect a personal conflict between puritanism and an ardent physical nature[reference:52]. He died in 1716.[reference:53]

authors.name: Wole Soyinka
authors.bio: A Nigerian playwright, poet, and essayist, Soyinka is the first African to be awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature (1986). His major plays include A Dance of the Forests (1960) and The Lion and the Jewel (1959).

authors.name: Wolfgang Iser
authors.bio: A German literary theorist, Iser is a key figure in reader-response theory. His major work is The Act of Reading (1976). He died in 2007.

concepts.name: Aestheticism
concepts.description: A 19th-century literary and artistic movement that advocated for 'art for art's sake', valuing aesthetic beauty above moral or social utility. Walter Pater is the most important English theorist of Aestheticism, with his Studies in the History of the Renaissance (1873) considered a manifesto of the movement. PYQ note: frequently asked about Aestheticism; Pater's role; compared with other literary movements.

concepts.name: African Literature
concepts.description: A body of literature from the African continent, encompassing works in English, French, Portuguese, and African languages. It often explores themes of colonialism, nationalism, identity, and post-independence disillusionment. Key figures include Chinua Achebe, Wole Soyinka, Ngũgĩ wa Thiong'o, and Bessie Head. PYQ note: frequently asked about African literature; its themes; and key authors and works.

concepts.name: Alliterative Revival
concepts.description: A term used to describe a resurgence of alliterative verse in Middle English during the 14th century, particularly in the West Midlands of England. Poets of this revival abandoned the French-influenced rhymed stanza forms (used by Chaucer and Gower) in favor of the native Germanic alliterative line, characterized by four stressed syllables and a strong medial caesura. Key works include Sir Gawain and the Green Knight (Pearl Poet) and Piers Plowman (Langland). The Gawain poet is considered the greatest master of this revival. PYQ note: asked to contrast with Chaucer's syllabic/rhymed style; the regional and social contexts of the revival.

concepts.name: American Modernism
concepts.description: A literary movement that emerged in the early 20th century, characterized by a break with traditional forms and a sense of fragmentation, disillusionment, and experimentation. Key features include free verse, imagery, and a focus on the inner self. Key figures include Wallace Stevens, E. E. Cummings, and Robert Frost. PYQ note: frequently asked about Modernism; its characteristics; compared with Romanticism and Realism.

concepts.name: American Naturalism
concepts.description: A literary movement that emerged in the late 19th century, an extension of Realism that emphasizes the role of environment, heredity, and social conditions in shaping human character. Naturalist writers often depict the struggle for survival. Key figures include Theodore Dreiser (Sister Carrie), Stephen Crane (The Red Badge of Courage), and Jack London (The Call of the Wild). PYQ note: frequently asked about Naturalism; its characteristics; compared with Realism.

concepts.name: American Postmodernism
concepts.description: A literary movement that emerged in the mid-20th century, characterized by a rejection of modernist conventions, a blurring of boundaries between fiction and reality, and a use of parody, irony, and metafiction. Key figures include Philip Roth, John Barth, and Kurt Vonnegut. PYQ note: frequently asked about Postmodernism; its characteristics; compared with Modernism.

concepts.name: American Realism
concepts.description: A literary movement that emerged in the late 19th century, characterized by a focus on everyday life, ordinary people, and the accurate depiction of reality. Realist writers rejected romanticism and idealism. John Steinbeck's The Grapes of Wrath (1939) is a key example of American Realism. PYQ note: frequently asked about Realism; its characteristics; compared with Romanticism.

concepts.name: Archetypal Criticism
concepts.description: A literary approach that focuses on the identification and analysis of recurring archetypes, myths, and symbols in literature. Northrop Frye's Anatomy of Criticism (1957) is the foundational text of archetypal criticism, arguing for a systematic, scientific approach based on the structure of literature. Maud Bodkin's Archetypal Patterns in Poetry (1934) is another key text. PYQ note: frequently asked about archetypal criticism; Frye's contributions; compared with other critical approaches.

concepts.name: Arnold's Touchstone Method
concepts.description: Matthew Arnold's method of literary criticism, as outlined in The Study of Poetry[reference:118]. The method involves comparing passages of poetry with the best passages of the great poets (the touchstones) to judge their quality. Arnold believed that this method would help readers develop a sense of literary excellence. PYQ note: frequently asked about Arnold's touchstone method; the function of criticism; compared with other critical methods.

concepts.name: Art for Art's Sake
concepts.description: The aesthetic doctrine that art has its own intrinsic value and should not be judged by moral or utilitarian criteria. Walter Pater's advocacy of this doctrine became a cardinal principle of the Aesthetic movement.[reference:106] Oscar Wilde also championed the doctrine. PYQ note: frequently asked about 'art for art's sake'; its key advocates; compared with other aesthetic theories.

concepts.name: Austen's Comedy of Manners
concepts.description: Jane Austen is the supreme master of the comedy of manners, a genre that satirizes the social conventions and pretensions of a particular social class. Austen's novels are characterized by their wit, irony, and precise observation of social behavior. Pride and Prejudice and Emma are prime examples of the genre.[reference:75] PYQ note: frequently asked about Austen's comedy of manners; the social satire; compared with other comic genres.

concepts.name: Bacon's Aphoristic Essay Style
concepts.description: Francis Bacon is credited with introducing the essay form into English literature. His essays are characterized by their aphoristic, epigrammatic style, combining practical wisdom with philosophical insight.[reference:49] They are concise, sententious, and often use paradox and antithesis. Bacon's essays cover a wide range of topics, including truth, death, love, studies, friendship, and ambition. His style has been described as 'of the earth, earthy', and his essays are known for their 'wisdom of the world' and their 'cunning observation of men and things'. PYQ note: frequently asked about Bacon as the father of the English essay; his aphoristic style; individual essays like 'Of Studies' and 'Of Friendship'.

concepts.name: Blake's Illuminated Printing
concepts.description: William Blake created his own method of illuminated printing, in which he combined text and image on a single plate. His Songs of Innocence and of Experience (1794) are among his most famous illuminated works. Blake's illuminated printing allowed him to control every aspect of the publication process and to create works that are both literary and visual. PYQ note: frequently asked about Blake's illuminated printing; the Songs; the combination of text and image.

concepts.name: Blank Verse
concepts.description: Unrhymed iambic pentameter. Introduced into English by Henry Howard, Earl of Surrey, in his translation of Virgil's Aeneid (c. 1554). The first English play to employ blank verse was Gorboduc (1562) by Thomas Norton and Thomas Sackville. Blank verse became the dominant form of English dramatic and epic poetry, used by Shakespeare, Milton (Paradise Lost), and many others. PYQ note: asked about the introduction of blank verse in English drama; Gorboduc as the first blank verse play.

concepts.name: Marlowe's 'Mighty Lines' (Blank Verse Revolution)
concepts.description: Christopher Marlowe revolutionized English drama by introducing what was called 'mighty lines' – a powerful and elevated form of blank verse (unrhymed iambic pentameter)[reference:145]. Before Marlowe, English drama used a more irregular and less dignified verse. Marlowe's verse was characterized by its sonority, its use of classical allusions, and its ability to convey intense emotion and ambition. His 'mighty lines' in Tamburlaine the Great set a new standard for dramatic poetry and profoundly influenced Shakespeare and later dramatists[reference:146]. PYQ note: often asked about Marlowe's contribution to the development of blank verse and his influence on Shakespeare.

concepts.name: Bunyan's Allegorical Style
concepts.description: John Bunyan's The Pilgrim's Progress is one of the greatest allegories in English literature. Bunyan uses a simple, direct prose style to create a powerful allegory of the Christian journey. The characters and places are personifications of abstract qualities (Christian, Faithful, Mr. Worldly Wiseman, the Slough of Despond). Bunyan's allegory is notable for its vividness, its use of everyday language, and its ability to convey profound spiritual truths. PYQ note: frequently asked about Bunyan's allegory; the characters and their symbolic meanings; compared with other allegories (e.g., Spenser's Faerie Queene).

concepts.name: Burns's Use of the Scots Dialect
concepts.description: Robert Burns wrote in the Scots language, celebrating Scottish culture and identity. His Poems, Chiefly in the Scottish Dialect (1786) established him as the national poet of Scotland.[reference:70] Burns's use of Scots dialect gives his poetry a distinctive voice and connects it to the Scottish folk tradition. PYQ note: frequently asked about Burns's use of Scots dialect; the national poet of Scotland.

concepts.name: Caribbean Literature
concepts.description: A body of literature from the Caribbean region, often exploring themes of colonialism, identity, displacement, and cultural hybridity. Key figures include George Lamming, Jean Rhys, and Jamaica Kincaid. PYQ note: frequently asked about Caribbean literature; its themes; and key authors and works.

concepts.name: Cavalier Poetry
concepts.description: A school of English poets of the 17th century, who were supporters of King Charles I during the English Civil War. The Cavalier poets include Robert Herrick, Richard Lovelace, Thomas Carew, and John Suckling. They were influenced by Ben Jonson and the classical tradition. Their poetry is characterized by its elegance, wit, lyricism, and celebration of love, beauty, and the pleasures of life. The carpe diem theme is prominent in their work. PYQ note: frequently asked about the Cavalier poets; their themes and style; compared with the Metaphysical poets.

concepts.name: Classical Literary Criticism
concepts.description: A body of literary theory that emerged in ancient Greece and Rome, characterized by a focus on the nature of poetry, its effects, and its moral value. Key figures include Plato, Aristotle, Horace, and Longinus. PYQ note: frequently asked about classical criticism; the key figures; the key concepts such as mimesis, catharsis, and the sublime.

concepts.name: Clough's Hexameter Experiments
concepts.description: Arthur Hugh Clough experimented with classical hexameters in English poetry, a form that was unusual in Victorian literature. His major works, The Bothie of Tober-na-Vuolich (1848) and Amours de Voyage (1858), are written in classical hexameters[reference:121]. This metrical experiment reflects Clough's innovative approach to poetry and his engagement with classical models. PYQ note: asked about Clough's use of hexameters; the classical influence; compared with other metrical experiments.

concepts.name: Coleridge's Imagination vs. Fancy
concepts.description: In Biographia Literaria (1817), Coleridge distinguishes between primary and secondary imagination, and between imagination and fancy. The primary imagination is the 'living Power and prime Agent' of human perception, a creative power shared by all humans. The secondary imagination is the creative power of the artist, which dissolves, diffuses, and dissipates in order to recreate. Fancy, by contrast, is a lower faculty that merely combines and associates pre-existing ideas. PYQ note: one of the most frequently asked critical concepts in NET; the distinction between imagination and fancy; compared with Wordsworth's theory.

concepts.name: Colonial and Postcolonial Identity
concepts.description: A central theme in postcolonial literature, exploring the formation of identity in the context of colonial rule and its aftermath. Achebe's Things Fall Apart and Rhys's Wide Sargasso Sea are key examples of works that explore this theme. PYQ note: frequently asked about the theme of identity in postcolonial literature; compared with other themes.

concepts.name: Comedy of Manners
concepts.description: A genre of comedy that flourished in Restoration England, characterized by witty dialogue, satirical portrayal of social affectations, and exploration of the war of the sexes. The plays typically focus on the intrigues of the upper classes and are known for their sophisticated and often bawdy humor. Major exponents include William Congreve (The Way of the World), George Etherege (The Man of Mode), and William Wycherley (The Country Wife). PYQ note: frequently asked about the genre; its characteristics; compared with other comic genres.

concepts.name: Commendatory Verse
concepts.description: Poems written to praise and recommend another author's work, often published as prefatory material (e.g., at the beginning of a book). Sir Walter Raleigh wrote a commendatory sonnet for Spenser's The Faerie Queene (1590), titled 'A Vision Upon this Concept of the Faery Queene'. PYQ note: asked about the literary circle around Spenser; Raleigh's role as a poet and patron.

concepts.name: Confessional Poetry
concepts.description: A poetic movement that emerged in the United States in the late 1950s and 1960s. Confessional poets write about intensely personal experiences, including mental illness, sexuality, and suicide. The movement is associated with poets like Robert Lowell, Sylvia Plath, and Anne Sexton. Plath's Ariel (1965) is a landmark collection of confessional poetry. PYQ note: frequently asked about confessional poetry; Plath's role; compared with other poetic movements.

concepts.name: Cultural Identity
concepts.description: Stuart Hall's concept of cultural identity, which argues that identity is not fixed but is a process of becoming. Hall distinguishes between identity as a shared culture and identity as a process of becoming, shaped by experience and history. PYQ note: frequently asked about cultural identity; diaspora; compared with other identity theorists.

concepts.name: Cultural Studies
concepts.description: A theoretical approach that emerged in the mid-20th century, examining the relationship between culture, power, and society. Key figures include Richard Hoggart and Stuart Hall. PYQ note: frequently asked about cultural studies; the key concepts such as encoding/decoding and cultural identity; the key figures.

concepts.name: E. E. Cummings's Experimental Poetry
concepts.description: E. E. Cummings is known for his pioneering experiments with typography, grammar, and syntax. His poetry often uses lowercase letters, unconventional punctuation, and visual arrangements to create meaning. Tulips and Chimneys (1923) showcases his experimental style. PYQ note: frequently asked about Cummings's experimental style; the use of typography; the avant-garde.

concepts.name: Deconstruction
concepts.description: A method of reading that challenges binary oppositions and the stability of meaning, associated with the philosophy of Jacques Derrida. It argues that meaning is endlessly deferred and that texts contain internal contradictions. PYQ note: one of the most frequently asked concepts in NET; deconstruction; différance; compared with other critical methods.

concepts.name: Defoe's Realistic Narrative
concepts.description: Daniel Defoe's innovative use of realistic detail and first-person narration in his novels. Defoe's novels are characterized by their detailed and plausible descriptions of everyday life, creating a sense of authenticity. This technique is used to great effect in Robinson Crusoe and Moll Flanders. Defoe's realism was a major influence on the development of the novel. PYQ note: frequently asked about Defoe's realism; compared with other novelists.

concepts.name: Dickensian Characters
concepts.description: Charles Dickens is known for his vivid, larger-than-life characters, often with memorable names and exaggerated traits. His characters are often caricatures who represent particular social types or moral qualities. Examples include Uriah Heep (David Copperfield), Fagin (Oliver Twist), and Miss Havisham (Great Expectations). PYQ note: frequently asked about Dickens's characters; the technique of caricature; compared with other novelists.

concepts.name: Dickinson's Poetic Style
concepts.description: Emily Dickinson's poetry is characterized by its brevity, its use of dashes, its unconventional capitalization, and its frequent use of slant rhyme. She wrote nearly 1,800 poems, exploring themes of death, immortality, nature, and the inner life. Her style was highly individual and influential. PYQ note: frequently asked about Dickinson's style; the use of dashes and slant rhyme; the themes of death and immortality.

concepts.name: Dramatic Monologue
concepts.description: A poem written in the form of a speech delivered by an individual character[reference:109]. It is a narration of a vivid scene or an event of the narrator's life, designed to reveal the narrator's history and psychological insight[reference:110]. Robert Browning is the supreme master of the dramatic monologue, with works like My Last Duchess, Porphyria's Lover, and Fra Lippo Lippi[reference:111]. Tennyson also used the form in Ulysses. PYQ note: frequently asked about the dramatic monologue; Browning's use of the form; compared with other poetic forms.

concepts.name: Duffy's Use of Dramatic Monologue
concepts.description: Carol Ann Duffy frequently uses the dramatic monologue form to give voice to historical and mythical figures, often from a feminist perspective. Her collection The World's Wife (1999) features dramatic monologues from the wives of famous men, retelling stories from a female viewpoint. PYQ note: frequently asked about Duffy's dramatic monologues; The World's Wife; the feminist re-visioning.

concepts.name: Dream Vision
concepts.description: A popular medieval genre in which the narrator falls asleep and experiences a dream that reveals allegorical or philosophical truths. The dreamer often meets personified abstractions (e.g., Lady Venus, Lady Philosophy) who guide them through moral or spiritual instruction. Geoffrey Chaucer used this genre extensively in his early works: The Book of the Duchess, The House of Fame, The Legend of Good Women, and (in part) The Canterbury Tales. The Pearl Poet's Pearl is also a classic example. PYQ note: frequently asked about Chaucer's use of the dream vision and its sources (e.g., Roman de la Rose, Dante).

concepts.name: Dryden's Mastery of the Heroic Couplet
concepts.description: John Dryden perfected the use of the heroic couplet (rhymed pairs of iambic pentameter lines) in English poetry. His use of the couplet is characterized by its balance, clarity, and epigrammatic wit. Dryden used the heroic couplet in his satirical poems (Absalom and Achitophel) and his translations. His mastery of the form influenced later poets, particularly Alexander Pope. PYQ note: frequently asked about Dryden's use of the heroic couplet; compared with Pope; the satirical effect.

concepts.name: Dub Poetry
concepts.description: A form of performance poetry that originated in Jamaica and is influenced by reggae music. Dub poets often address political and social issues, using a rhythmic, spoken-word style. Benjamin Zephaniah is one of the most prominent dub poets in Britain. His collection Pen Rhythm (1980) established his reputation as a dub poet. PYQ note: asked about dub poetry; Zephaniah's role; compared with other performance poetry traditions.

concepts.name: Dystopian Fiction
concepts.description: A genre of fiction that depicts an undesirable, oppressive society. Dystopian novels often explore themes of totalitarianism, surveillance, and the loss of individual freedom. Key examples include George Orwell's Nineteen Eighty-Four and Aldous Huxley's Brave New World. PYQ note: frequently asked about dystopian fiction; compared with utopian fiction; the themes of totalitarianism and surveillance.

concepts.name: Atwood's Dystopian Fiction
concepts.description: Margaret Atwood is a leading writer of dystopian fiction, with The Handmaid's Tale (1985) being her most famous work in the genre. The novel is set in the Republic of Gilead, a totalitarian society where women are subjugated. Atwood's dystopian fiction explores themes of feminism, power, and totalitarianism. PYQ note: frequently asked about Atwood's dystopian fiction; The Handmaid's Tale; compared with other dystopian works.

concepts.name: Eliot's Impersonal Theory of Poetry
concepts.description: T. S. Eliot's critical theory, articulated in Tradition and the Individual Talent (1919), which holds that poetry is not an expression of the poet's personality but an escape from it. Eliot argues that the poet must be aware of the tradition and that the poem should be judged on its own merits, not on the poet's biography[reference:125]. PYQ note: one of the most frequently asked critical concepts in NET; the impersonal theory; compared with other critical theories.

concepts.name: Encoding and Decoding
concepts.description: Stuart Hall's model of communication, which argues that meaning in media is not simply transmitted but is encoded by producers and decoded by audiences in different ways. Hall identifies three positions: dominant-hegemonic, negotiated, and oppositional readings. PYQ note: frequently asked about encoding and decoding; the three positions; compared with other communication models.

concepts.name: Epistolary Novel
concepts.description: A novel written in the form of letters exchanged between characters. Samuel Richardson pioneered the form in English literature with Pamela (1740) and Clarissa (1747-1748).
