The form allows for multiple perspectives and a sense of immediacy. Other notable examples include Fanny Burney's Evelina (1778) and Tobias Smollett's Humphry Clinker (1771). PYQ note: frequently asked about the epistolary form; Richardson's contribution; compared with other novel forms.

concepts.name: Euphuism
concepts.description: An ornate prose style introduced by John Lyly in his Euphues: The Anatomy of Wit (1578)[reference:155][reference:156]. The style is characterized by alliteration, balanced antithetical phrases, classical allusions, and 'unnatural natural history' (fanciful and often inaccurate claims about nature)[reference:157]. The style became wildly fashionable; 'that beautie in court which could not parley Euphuism was as little regarded as she which nowe there speakes not French'[reference:158]. The style influenced Elizabethan prose and was parodied by later writers. PYQ note: frequently asked as a stylistic term; Lyly's contribution to English prose; the influence on Shakespeare.

concepts.name: Feminist Criticism
concepts.description: A critical approach that applies feminist theory to the interpretation of literature, emphasizing gender inequality and the representation of women. Key figures include Mary Wollstonecraft, Virginia Woolf, Simone de Beauvoir, Elaine Showalter, and Judith Butler. PYQ note: one of the most frequently asked topics in NET; the key concepts such as écriture féminine, gynocriticism, and gender performativity; the key figures.

concepts.name: Fielding's 'Comic Epic in Prose'
concepts.description: Henry Fielding's term for his novelistic style, as articulated in the preface to Joseph Andrews and practiced in Tom Jones. The term combines the epic's scope and grandeur with the comic's humor and satire. Fielding's novels are characterized by their omniscient narrators, their digressive structures, and their moral seriousness beneath the comic surface. PYQ note: frequently asked about Fielding's 'comic epic in prose'; the narrator; compared with Richardson's novels.

concepts.name: Hardy's Wessex
concepts.description: Thomas Hardy's fictional region of Wessex, based on the rural counties of southwestern England, serves as the setting for most of his novels. Wessex is not merely a backdrop but an active force in the novels, representing nature's indifference to human desires. The Mayor of Casterbridge, The Return of the Native, and Tess of the d'Urbervilles are set in Wessex.[reference:76] PYQ note: frequently asked about Hardy's Wessex; the significance of the setting; compared with other regional novelists.

concepts.name: Herbert's Shape Poems (Technopaegnia)
concepts.description: George Herbert is known for his use of concrete or shape poetry, also known as technopaegnia. In poems such as 'Easter Wings' and 'The Altar', the visual arrangement of the words on the page mirrors the subject of the poem.[reference:54] 'Easter Wings' is shaped like a pair of wings, while 'The Altar' is shaped like an altar. This visual element adds another layer of meaning to the poem and reflects Herbert's interest in the relationship between form and content. PYQ note: frequently asked about Herbert's shape poems; the visual element in The Temple; technopaegnia as a poetic technique.

concepts.name: Hooker's Natural Law Tradition
concepts.description: Richard Hooker's Of the Laws of Ecclesiastical Polity is based on the natural law tradition, which holds that there is a universal moral law that can be discerned through reason.[reference:55] Hooker argues that this natural law is the foundation of all human law and that it is the basis for the Church of England's authority. He draws on Aristotelian philosophy and the Thomistic tradition to defend the Elizabethan settlement against Puritan attacks. Hooker's work is a significant contribution to political and legal theory, as well as to English prose style. PYQ note: asked about Hooker's natural law theory; his defence of the Church of England; his influence on political thought.

concepts.name: Hopkins's Inscape and Instress
concepts.description: Hopkins's key poetic concepts: 'inscape' is the unique, essential individuality of a thing, its inner design or pattern[reference:119]; 'instress' is the force or energy that conveys the inscape to the observer[reference:120]. Hopkins sought to capture the inscape of natural objects in his poetry, using sprung rhythm and vivid imagery. PYQ note: frequently asked about Hopkins's inscape and instress; the concepts of individuality and perception; compared with other poetic concepts.

concepts.name: Hume's Empiricism
concepts.description: David Hume is a key figure in the empiricist tradition. In A Treatise of Human Nature (1739-1740) and An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding (1748), Hume argues that all knowledge comes from experience.[reference:69] He distinguishes between impressions (sensations) and ideas (thoughts), and argues that causation is not a necessary connection but a habit of the mind. He also famously argues that the self is a bundle of perceptions. PYQ note: frequently asked about Hume's philosophy; the empiricist tradition; the theory of causation; the self.

concepts.name: Hybridity and Third Space
concepts.description: Homi Bhabha's concepts of hybridity and the third space. Hybridity refers to the mixing of cultures and identities, while the third space is a space of negotiation and translation where new identities and meanings are created. PYQ note: frequently asked about hybridity and the third space; Bhabha's contribution to postcolonial theory; compared with other postcolonial concepts.

concepts.name: Iambus
concepts.description: or Iambic Poetrya genre of ancient Greek poetry, not specifically uses iambic meter but uses insulting and obscene language for satire also called blame poetry.

concepts.name: Imagism
concepts.description: A modernist poetic movement that advocated for the use of precise, clear images and the economy of language. Ezra Pound was a central figure of the Imagist movement, with his poem 'In a Station of the Metro' serving as a quintessential example[reference:117]. Pound's essays, including A Retrospect (1918), defined the principles of Imagism[reference:118]. PYQ note: frequently asked about Imagism; its principles; Pound's role.

concepts.name: In-Yer-Face Theatre
concepts.description: A term coined by critic Aleks Sierz to describe a movement in British theatre in the 1990s that was characterized by graphic violence, explicit sexuality, and shocking subject matter. Sarah Kane's Blasted (1995) is a key example of the movement. PYQ note: frequently asked about 'In-Yer-Face' theatre; Kane's role; compared with other theatrical movements.

concepts.name: Indian English Drama
concepts.description: A body of drama written in English by Indian authors. It emerged in the 20th century and has developed into a significant theatrical tradition. Key figures include Girish Karnad, Mahesh Dattani, Badal Sircar, and Vijay Tendulkar. PYQ note: frequently asked about the major playwrights; the themes; and the development of the tradition.

concepts.name: Indian English Literature
concepts.description: A body of literature written in English by Indian authors. It emerged in the early 20th century and has grown to become a significant part of world literature. Key figures include Mulk Raj Anand, R. K. Narayan, Raja Rao, and Salman Rushdie.[reference:122][reference:123] PYQ note: frequently asked about the foundational texts of Indian English literature; the chronology of major works; and the major authors.

concepts.name: Indian English Poetry
concepts.description: A body of poetry written in English by Indian authors. It emerged in the 20th century and has developed into a significant literary tradition. Key figures include Nissim Ezekiel, A. K. Ramanujan, Kamala Das, Jayanta Mahapatra, and Arun Kolatkar. PYQ note: frequently asked about the major poets; the development of the tradition; and the key themes.

concepts.name: Jnanpith Award
concepts.description: the oldest and the highest Indian literary award presented annually by the Bharatiya Jnanpith. First recipient Malayalam writer G. Sankara Kurup in 1965 for his collection of poems, Odakkuzhal (The Bamboo Flute), published in 1950. In 1976, Bengali novelist Ashapoorna Devi became the first woman to win the award and was honoured for the 1965 novel Prothom Protishruti (The First Promise)

concepts.name: Johnson's Dictionary
concepts.description: Samuel Johnson's A Dictionary of the English Language (1755) was the pre-eminent English dictionary until the Oxford English Dictionary. It set a new standard for English lexicography with its definitions, quotations, and etymologies.[reference:67] Johnson's definitions are often witty and personal, and the dictionary includes quotations from English literature, making it a literary anthology as well as a dictionary. The preface is a masterpiece of English prose. PYQ note: frequently asked about Johnson's Dictionary; the lexicography; the definitions; the preface.

concepts.name: Jonson's Timber or Discoveries
concepts.description: Ben Jonson's Timber or Discoveries (1641) is a collection of his critical thoughts on literature, drama, and life. It is a common-place book that reflects his views on classical models, the importance of order and discipline in writing, and his judgments on his contemporaries.[reference:56][reference:57] Jonson advocates for the imitation of Greek and Roman writers and criticizes the excesses of contemporary drama.[reference:58] The work is an important source of Renaissance literary criticism and offers insights into Jonson's own practice as a dramatist. PYQ note: asked about Jonson as a critic; his views on drama and poetry; compared with Sidney's Defence.

concepts.name: Kant's Categorical Imperative
concepts.description: The central concept of Immanuel Kant's ethics, as presented in the Critique of Practical Reason (1788). The categorical imperative is a universal moral law that is binding on all rational beings. The most famous formulation is: 'Act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law.' The categorical imperative is contrasted with hypothetical imperatives, which are conditional on desires or goals. PYQ note: frequently asked about Kant's ethics; the categorical imperative; compared with other ethical theories.

concepts.name: Kant's Sublime
concepts.description: In the Critique of Judgment (1790), Kant explores the concept of the sublime. The sublime is an experience of awe and terror in the face of nature's vastness or power. Unlike the beautiful, which pleases directly, the sublime pleases indirectly, through the experience of our own reason's superiority over nature. The sublime became a key concept in Romantic aesthetics. PYQ note: frequently asked about Kant's aesthetics; the sublime and the beautiful; the influence on Romanticism.

concepts.name: Keats's Negative Capability
concepts.description: A concept introduced by John Keats in a letter to his brothers (1817). Negative capability is the ability to remain in 'uncertainties, mysteries, doubts, without any irritable reaching after fact and reason'. It is the capacity to accept ambiguity and to appreciate beauty without imposing logical or philosophical systems. Keats admired Shakespeare for his possession of this quality. PYQ note: frequently asked about Keats's Negative Capability; compared with other Romantic concepts.

concepts.name: Kenning
concepts.description: A distinctive figurative device used extensively in Old Norse, Old English, and other Germanic poetry. It is a compound, two-word metaphorical phrase that replaces a simple, everyday noun (e.g., 'whale-road' = sea; 'bone-house' = body). Skilled poets could stack kennings into multiple layers of abstraction: 'Gunnr's fire' (sword) → 'sword's storm' (battle) → 'battle's sweat' (blood). In Old English poetry, kennings served metrical (alliterative) purposes, elevated diction, and showcased the poet's verbal riddling wit. Cædmon's Hymn and Beowulf are dense with kennings for God, sea, and warriors. PYQ note: often tested with examples and asked to distinguish from other metaphorical devices.

concepts.name: Magical Realism
concepts.description: A literary genre in which magical elements are blended with realistic settings and events. Salman Rushdie's Midnight's Children (1981) is a key example of magical realism, in which the narrator's magical powers are intertwined with the history of India. PYQ note: frequently asked about magical realism; Rushdie's use of the genre; compared with other magical realist writers.

concepts.name: Malgudi
concepts.description: A fictional town created by R. K. Narayan, which serves as the setting for many of his novels and short stories. It is a microcosm of Indian life. PYQ note: frequently asked about Malgudi; its significance; and the novels set in it.

concepts.name: Mamet-Speak
concepts.description: A term used to describe the distinctive dialogue of David Mamet's plays, characterized by its rhythm, profanity, and elliptical quality. Mamet's dialogue often reflects the power struggles and desperation of his characters. Glengarry Glen Ross (1983) is a prime example of Mamet-speak. PYQ note: frequently asked about Mamet-speak; the distinctive dialogue; compared with other playwrights.

concepts.name: Marxist Criticism
concepts.description: A critical approach that applies the theories of Karl Marx to the interpretation of literature, emphasizing the role of class struggle, ideology, and economic determinism. Key figures include Georg Lukács, Louis Althusser, Fredric Jameson, and Raymond Williams. PYQ note: frequently asked about Marxist criticism; the key concepts such as surplus value, interpellation, and the political unconscious; the key figures.

concepts.name: Mass Culture
concepts.description: A term used to describe the culture produced and consumed by large numbers of people, often through mass media. The rise of periodicals and mass-circulation newspapers contributed to the development of mass culture. PYQ note: asked about mass culture; the role of periodicals; compared with high culture.

concepts.name: Metaphysical Conceit
concepts.description: A distinctive feature of metaphysical poetry, particularly associated with John Donne. A metaphysical conceit is an extended, elaborate, and often paradoxical metaphor that draws on knowledge from diverse fields such as philosophy, science, theology, and geography. Unlike the Petrarchan conceit, which is often conventional and decorative, the metaphysical conceit is intellectually challenging and aims to startle the reader into a new understanding. Examples include Donne's compass in 'A Valediction: Forbidding Mourning' and his flea in 'The Flea'. PYQ note: frequently asked as a defining feature of metaphysical poetry; Donne's use of conceits; compared with the Petrarchan conceit.

concepts.name: Milton's Use of Blank Verse
concepts.description: John Milton's use of blank verse (unrhymed iambic pentameter) in Paradise Lost is considered one of the most accomplished and influential in English poetry. Milton's blank verse is characterized by its flexibility, its use of enjambment, and its ability to convey a wide range of tones and emotions. Milton defended his use of blank verse in the introductory note to Paradise Lost, arguing that it was a more natural and dignified form than rhyme. PYQ note: frequently asked about Milton's blank verse; compared with Shakespeare's blank verse; the influence on later poets.

concepts.name: Milton's Epic Simile
concepts.description: Milton's use of the epic simile in Paradise Lost is a defining feature of his style. Drawing on the classical tradition of Homer and Virgil, Milton uses extended similes that compare the actions of his characters to various natural, mythological, and historical phenomena. These similes serve to elevate the epic tone and to provide commentary on the action. PYQ note: asked about Milton's use of the epic simile; compared with Homer and Virgil.

concepts.name: Mock-Heroic Satire
concepts.description: A satirical genre that uses the elevated style and conventions of epic poetry to treat trivial or contemptible subjects. Samuel Butler's Hudibras is one of the most famous examples of mock-heroic satire, using the epic style to satirize the Puritans and the Civil War. The genre was later perfected by Alexander Pope in The Rape of the Lock. PYQ note: frequently asked about Butler's Hudibras; the mock-heroic style; compared with Pope's The Rape of the Lock.

concepts.name: Modernism
concepts.description: A literary and artistic movement that emerged in the early 20th century, characterized by a break with traditional forms and a sense of fragmentation, disillusionment, and experimentation. Key features include stream of consciousness, fragmentation, allusiveness, and a rejection of realism. Major modernist poets include T. S. Eliot, Ezra Pound, and W. B. Yeats. PYQ note: frequently asked about Modernism; its characteristics; compared with other literary movements.

concepts.name: The Modernist Novel
concepts.description: A literary movement in the early 20th century that broke with traditional narrative forms. Modernist novels are characterized by experimental techniques such as stream of consciousness, fragmentation, multiple perspectives, and a focus on the subjective experience of characters. Key authors include James Joyce, Virginia Woolf, William Faulkner, and Ernest Hemingway. PYQ note: frequently asked about the Modernist novel; its characteristics; compared with the Victorian novel.

concepts.name: Muckraking
concepts.description: A style of journalism that exposes corruption, injustice, and social problems. Muckraking journalists played a significant role in the Progressive Era in the United States. PYQ note: asked about muckraking; its role in social reform; compared with other journalistic styles.

concepts.name: Négritude
concepts.description: A literary and political movement that emerged in the 1930s, asserting the value of black culture and identity. Aimé Césaire was a founder of the movement. PYQ note: frequently asked about Négritude; Césaire's role; compared with other postcolonial movements.

concepts.name: New Criticism
concepts.description: A movement in literary criticism that emerged in the early 20th century, emphasizing close reading and the autonomy of the literary work. It argues that the author's intention and the reader's response are irrelevant to interpretation. Key figures include I.A. Richards, William Empson, F.R. Leavis, Cleanth Brooks, and John Crowe Ransom. PYQ note: one of the most frequently asked topics in NET; the key figures; the key concepts such as the intentional and affective fallacies; the method of close reading.

concepts.name: Orientalism
concepts.description: Edward Said's concept of the Western construction of the Orient as a system of knowledge that serves the interests of colonial power. Said argues that Orientalism is a form of discourse that constructs the Orient as the 'Other' to the West. PYQ note: one of the most frequently asked concepts in NET; Orientalism; knowledge and power; the Western gaze.

concepts.name: Pastoral Elegy
concepts.description: A poetic genre that mourns a death within the conventions of pastoral poetry (shepherds, rural settings, nature imagery). The genre originates with Theocritus and was developed by Virgil. In English literature, major examples include Spenser's Astrophel (for Sidney), Milton's Lycidas, and Shelley's Adonais. The genre typically features the mourning of a shepherd-poet, the participation of nature in mourning, and a final consolation or apotheosis. PYQ note: asked about Spenser's Astrophel as a pastoral elegy; compared with Milton's Lycidas.

concepts.name: Paterian Aestheticism
concepts.description: Walter Pater's philosophy of aestheticism, as articulated in Studies in the History of the Renaissance (1873). Pater argues for an intense appreciation of art and life as a series of heightened aesthetic moments. The famous 'Conclusion' urges readers to live with a 'hard, gem-like flame'.[reference:108] PYQ note: frequently asked about Pater's aestheticism; the 'Conclusion'; the 'hard, gem-like flame'; compared with other aesthetic theories.

concepts.name: Picaresque Novel
concepts.description: A genre of prose fiction that follows the adventures of a roguish hero who travels through various social settings. The genre originated in Spain and was adopted by English novelists such as Daniel Defoe (Moll Flanders), Henry Fielding (Joseph Andrews, Tom Jones), and Tobias Smollett (Roderick Random, Peregrine Pickle). The form is characterized by its episodic structure, satirical tone, and focus on the lower classes. PYQ note: frequently asked about the picaresque genre; its characteristics; compared with other novel forms.

concepts.name: Postcolonial Literature
concepts.description: A body of literature that emerged from former colonies, responding to the experience of colonialism and its aftermath. It is characterized by a focus on identity, cultural hybridity, and the critique of colonial narratives. Key figures include Chinua Achebe, Ngũgĩ wa Thiong'o, and Wole Soyinka. PYQ note: frequently asked about postcolonial literature; its characteristics; and key authors and works.

concepts.name: Postcolonial Theory
concepts.description: A theoretical approach that emerged in the late 20th century, exploring the effects of colonialism on culture, identity, and knowledge. It examines the power dynamics between colonizer and colonized and the legacy of colonial rule. Key figures include Edward Said, Gayatri Spivak, Homi Bhabha, and Frantz Fanon. PYQ note: one of the most frequently asked topics in NET; the key concepts such as Orientalism, hybridity, the subaltern, and the third space.

concepts.name: Poststructuralism
concepts.description: A theoretical approach that emerged in the late 1960s, challenging the assumptions of structuralism and emphasizing the instability of meaning. Key figures include Jacques Derrida, Michel Foucault, and Julia Kristeva. PYQ note: frequently asked about poststructuralism; the key concepts such as deconstruction, différance, and intertextuality; the key figures.

concepts.name: Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood
concepts.description: An artistic and literary movement founded in 1848 by Dante Gabriel Rossetti, William Holman Hunt, and John Everett Millais[reference:113][reference:114]. The Brotherhood aimed to recapture the purity and simplicity of early Renaissance art[reference:115]. In literature, the movement is associated with rich, sensuous imagery, medieval themes, and mystical symbolism[reference:116]. Christina Rossetti and Algernon Swinburne were also associated with the movement[reference:117]. PYQ note: frequently asked about the Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood; its artistic and literary principles; compared with other Victorian movements.

concepts.name: Psychoanalytic Criticism
concepts.description: A critical approach that applies the theories of psychoanalysis to the interpretation of literature. Key figures include Sigmund Freud and Jacques Lacan. PYQ note: frequently asked about psychoanalytic criticism; Freud's concepts of the id, ego, and superego, and the Oedipus complex; Lacan's mirror stage and symbolic order.

concepts.name: Reader-Response Theory
concepts.description: A theoretical approach that emerged in the 1960s and 1970s, emphasizing the role of the reader in the construction of meaning. Key figures include Wolfgang Iser and Stanley Fish. PYQ note: frequently asked about reader-response theory; the key concepts such as the implied reader and interpretive communities; the key figures.

concepts.name: Restoration Comedy
concepts.description: A genre of comedy that flourished in England during the Restoration period (1660-1710). Restoration comedies are characterized by their witty dialogue, sexual intrigue, satirical portrayal of manners, and complex plots. Aphra Behn's The Rover is one of the most successful examples of the genre. The genre was influenced by French comedy and the comedies of Ben Jonson. PYQ note: frequently asked about Restoration comedy; the characteristics of the genre; Behn's contribution; compared with other comic genres.

concepts.name: Rhyme Royal
concepts.description: A seven-line stanza with the rhyme scheme ABABBCC. Also known as the 'Troilus stanza' because Chaucer used it in his Troilus and Criseyde. It was first used in English by Chaucer and later adopted by Scottish Chaucerians (King James I of Scotland in The King's Quair, Robert Henryson, William Dunbar). The stanza originated from the French ballade form but Chaucer adapted it. PYQ note: often asked as a technical poetic term in NET; Chaucer's innovation with this stanza is a key point.

concepts.name: Runic Acrostic Signature
concepts.description: A groundbreaking poetic technique unique to Cynewulf in Old English literature. Near the end of each of his four signed poems (Elene, Christ II, Juliana, Fates of the Apostles), Cynewulf wove a sequence of Old English runic characters into the text. When read, these runes spell out his name (CYNEWULF or CYNWULF). This signature functions on three simultaneous levels: (1) an acrostic authorship claim, (2) an Old English riddle (each rune also represents a word, e.g., the rune for 'C' means 'torch'), and (3) a spiritual meditation, as the runic passage is integrated into a prayerful epilogue concerning the Last Judgment and the transience of life. This fusion of native Germanic runic tradition with Latin Christian acrostic practice is unprecedented and a key exam topic.

concepts.name: Ruskin's Critique of Political Economy
concepts.description: John Ruskin's Unto This Last (1860) is a powerful critique of classical political economy. Ruskin argues that economics must be governed by moral principles, not just by the laws of supply and demand. The work influenced Gandhi and others. PYQ note: frequently asked about Ruskin's social and economic thought; the influence on Gandhi; compared with other Victorian social critics.

concepts.name: Russian Formalism
concepts.description: A school of literary criticism that emerged in Russia in the early 20th century, emphasizing the autonomy of literary form and the function of defamiliarization. Key figures include Viktor Shklovsky, Boris Eichenbaum, and Roman Jakobson. PYQ note: frequently asked about Russian Formalism; the key figures; the concept of defamiliarization.

concepts.name: Senecan Tragedy
concepts.description: A style of tragedy based on the plays of the Roman philosopher Seneca (c. 4 BC – 65 AD). Characteristics include: five-act structure, rhetorical speeches, a chorus, scenes of violence and bloodshed, a moralizing tone, and themes of revenge, tyranny, and fate. English Senecan tragedy was influential in the development of Elizabethan drama, with Gorboduc being the first English tragedy to follow the Senecan model. PYQ note: asked about the Senecan influence on Gorboduc and later Elizabethan revenge tragedies (e.g., Kyd's The Spanish Tragedy, Shakespeare's Hamlet).

concepts.name: Sentimental Novel
concepts.description: A genre of fiction that emphasizes emotion and moral sentiment. Samuel Richardson's Pamela (1740) and Clarissa (1747-1748) are early examples of the sentimental novel. Laurence Sterne's A Sentimental Journey (1768) is a later example. The genre was a reaction against the cold rationality of the Augustan age and sought to evoke sympathy and moral feeling in the reader. PYQ note: asked about the sentimental novel; Richardson's contribution; compared with the picaresque novel.

concepts.name: Shavian Drama
concepts.description: A term used to describe the plays of George Bernard Shaw, characterized by intellectual debate, social critique, and the use of drama as a vehicle for ideas. Shaw's plays are often 'discussion plays' in which characters argue about philosophical, political, or social issues. He introduced a new realism into English-language drama, influenced by Henrik Ibsen.[reference:50] PYQ note: frequently asked about Shaw's dramatic techniques; the 'discussion play'; his influence on modern drama.

concepts.name: Sidney's Defence of Poesy
concepts.description: Sir Philip Sidney's Defence of Poesy (1595) is one of the most important works of literary criticism in English literature.[reference:50] Written as a response to Stephen Gosson's Puritan attack on poetry, Sidney argues for the value and superiority of poetry.[reference:51] He identifies three kinds of poetry: religious, philosophical, and 'right' poetry (imaginative creation).[reference:52] He argues that poetry is superior to history and philosophy because it combines universality with particularity. He also refutes Plato's charge that poets are liars, arguing that the poet 'nothing affirms, and therefore never lieth.'[reference:53] PYQ note: one of the most frequently asked critical texts in NET; the three kinds of poetry; Sidney's arguments for poetry; compared with other Renaissance critical texts.

concepts.name: Sonnet
concepts.description:
A fourteen line short lyrical poem developed in Italy around middle ages.
# Three styles of Sonnet
**Petrarchan Sonnet**- most common, consists of an Octave and a Sestet
**Spencerian Sonnet**- Four quatrain and a couplet (abab bcbc cdcd ee)
**Shakespearean Sonnet**- Four quatrain and a couplet (abab cdcd efef gg)
Thomas Wyatt and Earl of Surrey brought Sonnet to English with translation of Petrarch.

concepts.name: Southern Gothic
concepts.description: A subgenre of Gothic fiction set in the American South, characterized by a grotesque or darkly comic tone. William Faulkner is a key figure of Southern Gothic, with novels such as The Sound and the Fury and Absalom, Absalom! exploring the decay of the Southern aristocracy and the region's racial tensions. PYQ note: asked about Southern Gothic; Faulkner's use of the genre; compared with other Gothic traditions.

concepts.name: Spenserian Sonnet
concepts.description: A sonnet form developed by Edmund Spenser, used in his Amoretti sequence. It has the rhyme scheme ABAB BCBC CDCD EE, featuring interlocking quatrains (the rhyme of the previous quatrain carries over into the next). This linking of quatrains creates a sense of logical progression and thematic continuity. PYQ note: asked to distinguish from the Petrarchan (octave-sestet) and Shakespearean (three quatrains + couplet) sonnet forms.

concepts.name: Spenserian Stanza
concepts.description: A nine-line stanza invented by Edmund Spenser for The Faerie Queene. It consists of eight lines of iambic pentameter followed by a ninth line of iambic hexameter (an Alexandrine), with the rhyme scheme ABABBCBCC. The final Alexandrine provides a closing effect, often expanding or concluding the thought of the stanza. PYQ note: frequently asked as a technical poetic term; the stanza was later used by Byron (Childe Harold's Pilgrimage), Keats (The Eve of St. Agnes), and Shelley (The Revolt of Islam).

concepts.name: Sprung Rhythm
concepts.description: A poetic rhythm developed by Gerard Manley Hopkins, characterized by a variable number of syllables per line and a strong stress pattern[reference:112]. Hopkins used sprung rhythm to create a more natural and expressive poetic line. The rhythm is based on the number of stressed syllables, with unstressed syllables being counted less. PYQ note: frequently asked about Hopkins's prosody; the sprung rhythm; compared with traditional meter.

concepts.name: Sterne's Digressive Narrative
concepts.description: Laurence Sterne's innovative narrative technique in Tristram Shandy, in which the narrator constantly digresses from the main story. The novel is structured around the narrator's associations and digressions, rather than a linear plot. This technique allows Sterne to explore the nature of narrative, time, and memory. PYQ note: frequently asked about Sterne's narrative technique; the digressions; compared with other novelists.

concepts.name: Stream of Consciousness
concepts.description: A narrative technique that seeks to capture the continuous flow of a character's thoughts, feelings, and sensations. It attempts to represent the 'stream' of consciousness as it flows, often without logical order or punctuation. Key practitioners include James Joyce (Ulysses), Virginia Woolf (Mrs Dalloway, To the Lighthouse), and William Faulkner (The Sound and the Fury). PYQ note: frequently asked about the stream of consciousness technique; its characteristics; compared with interior monologue.

concepts.name: Structuralism
concepts.description: A theoretical approach that emerged in the mid-20th century, emphasizing the underlying structures of language, culture, and society. Key figures include Ferdinand de Saussure, Claude Lévi-Strauss, and Roland Barthes. PYQ note: frequently asked about structuralism; the key concepts such as langue and parole, the signifier and signified; the key figures.

concepts.name: Swift's Satirical Irony
concepts.description: Jonathan Swift's distinctive use of irony in his satirical works. Swift often adopts a persona who argues for a position that is the opposite of what the author intends, creating a powerful satirical effect. This technique is used to devastating effect in A Modest Proposal, where the speaker rationally argues for eating Irish babies. In Gulliver's Travels, Swift uses the ironic contrast between the narrator's perceptions and the reader's understanding. PYQ note: frequently asked about Swift's irony; compared with other satirists.

concepts.name: Synge's Dramatic Language
concepts.description: J.M. Synge created a new, musical dramatic idiom, spoken in English but vitalized by Irish syntax, ways of thought, and imagery.[reference:54] His dialogue blends Hiberno-English with the rich, colourful sayings of the Irish peasantry. PYQ note: asked about Synge's language; the Hiberno-English style; compared with other Irish dramatists.

concepts.name: The Affective Fallacy
concepts.description: A concept introduced by W.K. Wimsatt and Monroe Beardsley in their 1949 essay. It argues that the reader's emotional response to a work is irrelevant to its objective interpretation. PYQ note: frequently asked about the affective fallacy; compared with the intentional fallacy.

concepts.name: The American Dream
concepts.description: A national ethos of the United States, the set of ideals in which freedom includes the opportunity for prosperity and success. F. Scott Fitzgerald's The Great Gatsby is a powerful critique of the American Dream, exposing its corruption and emptiness. PYQ note: frequently asked about the American Dream in Fitzgerald; the critique of the Jazz Age; compared with other American novels.

concepts.name: The American Renaissance
concepts.description: A period of American literature in the mid-19th century (roughly 1830-1865) that produced some of the greatest works in American literature. Key figures include Nathaniel Hawthorne (The Scarlet Letter), Herman Melville (Moby-Dick), Edgar Allan Poe, Walt Whitman (Leaves of Grass), and Emily Dickinson. PYQ note: frequently asked about the American Renaissance; its key figures and works; compared with other periods.

concepts.name: The Angry Young Men
concepts.description: A group of British writers in the 1950s who expressed their disillusionment with the establishment and the class system. John Osborne's Look Back in Anger (1956) is the defining work of the movement. The plays often feature a working-class or lower-middle-class protagonist who rages against the system. PYQ note: frequently asked about the Angry Young Men; Osborne's role; compared with other social movements.

concepts.name: The Animal Poem
concepts.description: A poetic tradition in which animals are used to explore human nature and the natural world. Ted Hughes is one of the most important poets of the animal poem, with his works exploring the savagery and cunning of animal life in harsh, disjunctive lines. His collections The Hawk in the Rain and Crow are central to this tradition. PYQ note: frequently asked about Hughes's animal poetry; the use of animal imagery; compared with other nature poets.

concepts.name: The Beat Generation
concepts.description: A literary movement of the 1950s that rejected conventional values and embraced non-conformity, experimentation, and Eastern spirituality. Allen Ginsberg's Howl (1956) is the defining work of the Beat Generation. The poem is a free verse epic that critiques American society and celebrates madness, sexuality, and drug use. PYQ note: frequently asked about the Beat Generation; Howl; the obscenity trial.

concepts.name: The Bildungsroman
concepts.description: A genre of the novel that focuses on the psychological and moral growth of the protagonist from youth to adulthood. The Adventures of Augie March (1953) is an example of a Bildungsroman. PYQ note: frequently asked about the Bildungsroman; its characteristics; compared with other novel forms.

concepts.name: The Byronic Hero
concepts.description: A character type introduced by Lord Byron, particularly in Childe Harold's Pilgrimage. The Byronic hero is a brooding, rebellious, and melancholy figure, often with a mysterious past. He is intelligent, passionate, and defiant of social norms, but also haunted by guilt and a sense of isolation. The Byronic hero became a defining archetype of Romantic literature and influenced later literary figures. PYQ note: frequently asked about the Byronic hero; compared with other Romantic heroes.

concepts.name: The Campus Novel
concepts.description: A genre of fiction set in a university or college campus. Kingsley Amis's Lucky Jim (1954) is one of the earliest and most famous examples of the campus novel. The genre often satirizes academic life and the pretensions of intellectuals. PYQ note: asked about the campus novel; Amis's role; compared with other campus novels.

concepts.name: The Comedy of Humours
concepts.description: A genre of comedy introduced by Ben Jonson, based on the medieval/Renaissance medical theory that personality is determined by the balance of four bodily fluids (humours): blood, phlegm, choler, and melancholy[reference:152]. In Jonson's comedies, characters are ruled by a dominant humour that determines their behavior, leading to comic situations. The genre satirizes human folly and pretension. Jonson's Every Man in His Humour (1598) is the first major example[reference:153][reference:154]. PYQ note: asked about Jonson's theory of humours; compared with Shakespeare's comedies; the satirical intent.

concepts.name: The Confessional Mode
concepts.description: A literary mode in which the writer reveals intensely personal experiences and emotions. Kamala Das is a key figure in Indian English literature known for her confessional style, both in her poetry and in her autobiography My Story (1976). PYQ note: frequently asked about the confessional mode; Das's use of it; and its influence on Indian English literature.

concepts.name: The Diasporic Novel
concepts.description: A genre of fiction that deals with the experience of migration and diaspora. Rohinton Mistry's A Fine Balance (1995) is a key example, exploring the lives of Indians during the Emergency.[reference:126] PYQ note: asked about the diasporic novel; its characteristics; and key examples.

concepts.name: The Double Standard
concepts.description: A theme explored by many Victorian novelists, particularly in relation to gender. The double standard refers to the different moral standards applied to men and women, particularly in matters of sexuality. Thomas Hardy's Tess of the d'Urbervilles is a powerful critique of the double standard, as Tess is condemned for her sexual experience while men are not. PYQ note: frequently asked about the double standard; the critique of Victorian morality; compared with other social critiques.

concepts.name: The Epistolary Novel
concepts.description: A novel written in the form of letters. Alice Walker's The Color Purple (1982) is an epistolary novel, written in the form of letters from Celie to God and to her sister Nettie. PYQ note: asked about the epistolary form; Walker's use of the form; compared with other epistolary novels.

concepts.name: The Familiar Essay
concepts.description: A type of essay characterized by its personal, intimate, and conversational style. Charles Lamb is considered the master of the familiar essay, particularly in his Essays of Elia (1823). The familiar essay often reflects on everyday life, personal memories, and the writer's individual sensibility. PYQ note: frequently asked about Lamb's Essays of Elia; the familiar essay; compared with the periodical essay.

concepts.name: The Fourth Estate
concepts.description: A term used to describe the press as a political force in its own right, separate from the three traditional estates of the realm (the clergy, the nobility, and the commoners). The term emphasizes the role of journalism in holding power to account. PYQ note: frequently asked about the fourth estate; the role of journalism in democracy; compared with other political concepts.

concepts.name: The Framed Narrative
concepts.description: A narrative technique in which a story is told within a story. Mary Shelley's Frankenstein uses a framed narrative structure: the novel is framed by letters from Walton, who encounters Victor Frankenstein, who tells his story, which includes the monster's narrative. PYQ note: frequently asked about the framed narrative; its characteristics; compared with other narrative techniques.

concepts.name: The Gothic in American Literature
concepts.description: A tradition of Gothic literature in America, characterized by dark, mysterious, and supernatural elements. Edgar Allan Poe is the master of the American Gothic, with works like 'The Raven' and 'The Tell-Tale Heart'. Nathaniel Hawthorne's The Scarlet Letter also contains Gothic elements. PYQ note: frequently asked about the American Gothic; Poe's role; compared with other Gothic traditions.

concepts.name: The Gothic Novel
concepts.description: A genre of fiction characterized by mystery, horror, and the supernatural. The Gothic novel was popular in the late 18th and 19th centuries and influenced many Victorian novelists. Examples include Emily Brontë's Wuthering Heights, Charlotte Brontë's Jane Eyre, and Mary Shelley's Frankenstein. PYQ note: frequently asked about the Gothic novel; its characteristics; compared with other genres.

concepts.name: The Graveyard School
concepts.description: A group of 18th-century poets who focused on mortality, melancholy, and the contemplation of death. Thomas Gray's 'Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard' is the most famous example of the Graveyard School. Other poets in this tradition include Edward Young (Night Thoughts) and Robert Blair (The Grave). The Graveyard School anticipates the Gothic and Romantic movements. PYQ note: frequently asked about Gray's Elegy; the Graveyard School; compared with other literary movements.

concepts.name: Carlyle's Great Man Theory
concepts.description: Thomas Carlyle's theory that history is shaped by the actions of great individuals, or 'heroes'. In On Heroes, Hero-Worship, and the Heroic in History (1841), Carlyle identifies different types of heroes: the Prophet, the Poet, the Priest, the Man of Letters, and the King.[reference:107] PYQ note: frequently asked about the Great Man theory; the types of heroes; compared with other theories of history.

concepts.name: The Harm Principle
concepts.description: A concept introduced by John Stuart Mill in On Liberty (1859), which holds that the only purpose for which power can be exercised over an individual against his will is to prevent harm to others.[reference:104] Mill argues that human actions fall into two categories: self-regarding actions (which concern only the individual) and other-regarding actions (which concern others).[reference:105] PYQ note: frequently asked about the Harm Principle; the distinction between self-regarding and other-regarding actions; compared with other political theories.

concepts.name: The Heresy of Paraphrase
concepts.description: A concept introduced by Cleanth Brooks in The Well Wrought Urn (1947). It argues that a poem cannot be reduced to a paraphrase or summary, because its meaning is inseparable from its form and structure. PYQ note: frequently asked about the heresy of paraphrase; Brooks's contribution to New Criticism.

concepts.name: The Heroic Couplet
concepts.description: A rhyming pair of iambic pentameter lines. Alexander Pope perfected the use of the heroic couplet in English poetry, using it in his mock-epics, satires, and philosophical poems. Pope's heroic couplets are characterized by their balance, clarity, epigrammatic wit, and use of antithesis and caesura. His mastery of the form influenced later poets, particularly Samuel Johnson. PYQ note: frequently asked about Pope's use of the heroic couplet; compared with Dryden; the satirical effect.

concepts.name: The Homeric Epic
concepts.description: A tradition of epic poetry that originated with Homer's Iliad and Odyssey. Derek Walcott's Omeros (1990) is a Caribbean reworking of the Homeric epic, many critics view as Walcott's major achievement. Walcott uses the epic form to explore the Caribbean cultural experience and the legacy of colonialism. PYQ note: frequently asked about Walcott's Omeros; the Homeric tradition; the postcolonial epic.

concepts.name: The Ibis Trilogy
concepts.description: A trilogy of historical novels by Amitav Ghosh, consisting of Sea of Poppies (2008), River of Smoke (2011), and Flood of Fire (2015). The trilogy is set in the 1830s and follows a group of characters on board a ship called the Ibis.[reference:127] PYQ note: asked about the Ibis trilogy; its historical setting; and the key themes.

concepts.name: Hemingway's Iceberg Theory
concepts.description: Ernest Hemingway's theory of writing, which emphasizes the omission of unnecessary detail. Hemingway believed that the deeper meaning of a story should not be evident on the surface, but should be implied, like an iceberg where only one-eighth is visible above water. The theory is exemplified in his novels and short stories. PYQ note: frequently asked about Hemingway's Iceberg Theory; the economy of his style; compared with other modernist styles.

concepts.name: The Intentional Fallacy
concepts.description: A concept introduced by W.K. Wimsatt and Monroe Beardsley in their 1946 essay. It argues that the author's intention is irrelevant to the interpretation of a literary work, and that the work should be judged on its own merits. PYQ note: one of the most frequently asked concepts in NET; the intentional fallacy; compared with the affective fallacy.

concepts.name: The International Theme
concepts.description: A recurring theme in the novels of Henry James, exploring the contrast between American and European cultures. James often depicts innocent, naive Americans who are corrupted or transformed by their encounters with sophisticated, often decadent Europeans. The Portrait of a Lady and The Ambassadors are key examples. PYQ note: frequently asked about the international theme; compared with other transatlantic themes.

concepts.name: The Irish Literary Renaissance
concepts.description: A literary movement of the late 19th and early 20th centuries that sought to revive Irish culture, language, and identity. Key figures include W.B. Yeats, J.M. Synge, and Lady Gregory, who co-founded the Abbey Theatre. The movement drew on Irish mythology, folklore, and the rural life of the Irish peasantry.[reference:51][reference:52] PYQ note: frequently asked about the Irish Literary Renaissance; the Abbey Theatre; its key figures and themes.

concepts.name: The Language Debate
concepts.description: A central debate in postcolonial literature about whether African writers should write in English or in their native languages. Ngũgĩ wa Thiong'o's Decolonising the Mind (1986) is a key text in this debate, arguing for the importance of writing in African languages. PYQ note: frequently asked about the language debate; Ngũgĩ's arguments; compared with other postcolonial theorists.

concepts.name: The Lost Generation
concepts.description: A term used to describe the generation of writers who came of age during World War I and were disillusioned by the war's brutality. The term was popularized by Ernest Hemingway in The Sun Also Rises. Key figures include Hemingway, F. Scott Fitzgerald, and Gertrude Stein. PYQ note: frequently asked about the Lost Generation; its characteristics; compared with other literary generations.

concepts.name: The Mock-Epic
concepts.description: A satirical genre that uses the elevated style and conventions of epic poetry to treat trivial or contemptuous subjects. Alexander Pope's The Rape of the Lock is the supreme example of the mock-epic in English. Pope uses epic conventions (invocation, supernatural machinery, epic similes, catalogues) to treat a trivial social incident, creating a brilliant satire of high society. PYQ note: frequently asked about Pope's The Rape of the Lock; the mock-epic form; compared with other mock-epics (e.g., Butler's Hudibras).

concepts.name: The Movement
concepts.description: A group of English poets who emerged in the 1950s, characterized by their ironic, unsentimental, and formal poetry. The Movement rejected the excesses of romanticism and modernism. Philip Larkin is the leading figure of the Movement. His collection The Less Deceived (1955) is a key text of the movement. PYQ note: asked about The Movement; Larkin's role; compared with other poetic movements.

concepts.name: The Mythical Method
concepts.description: A modernist technique in which myths and classical stories are used to structure and give meaning to contemporary experience. T. S. Eliot used the mythical method in The Waste Land, weaving together myth and modernity to comment on the spiritual crisis of the modern world[reference:119]. PYQ note: frequently asked about Eliot's mythical method; compared with other modernist techniques.

concepts.name: The Omniscient Narrator
concepts.description: A narrative technique in which the narrator knows everything about the characters and events, and often comments on the action. The omniscient narrator was a common feature of Victorian novels. George Eliot's Middlemarch is a masterful example of the use of an omniscient narrator who offers moral judgments and psychological insights. PYQ note: frequently asked about the omniscient narrator; its characteristics; compared with other narrative techniques.

concepts.name: The Partition Novel
concepts.description: A genre of fiction that deals with the Partition of India in 1947. Key examples include Khushwant Singh's Train to Pakistan (1956) and Anita Desai's Clear Light of Day (1980).[reference:124][reference:125] PYQ note: frequently asked about the Partition novel; its characteristics; and key examples.

concepts.name: The Periodical Essay
concepts.description: A short prose essay published in a periodical, often on social, moral, or literary topics. Joseph Addison and Richard Steele established the periodical essay as a popular literary form with The Tatler (1709-1711) and The Spectator (1711-1712). The form was later used by Samuel Johnson in The Rambler (1750-1752). PYQ note: frequently asked about the periodical essay; Addison and Steele's role; compared with other literary forms.

concepts.name: The Picaresque Novel
concepts.description: A genre of prose fiction that follows the adventures of a roguish hero who travels through various social settings. The genre originated in Spain and was adopted by American novelists such as Mark Twain (Adventures of Huckleberry Finn) and Saul Bellow (The Adventures of Augie March). PYQ note: frequently asked about the picaresque genre; its characteristics; compared with other novel forms.

concepts.name: The Pindaric Ode
concepts.description: A form of ode characterized by its irregular meter and elevated tone, modeled on the odes of the Greek poet Pindar. Thomas Gray's 'The Progress of Poesy' (1754) and 'The Bard' (1757) are key examples of the Pindaric ode in English. The form was also used by other poets, including Cowley and Dryden. PYQ note: asked about Gray's Pindaric odes; the sublime; compared with the Horatian ode.

concepts.name: The Pinter Pause
concepts.description: A distinctive feature of Harold Pinter's plays, characterized by the use of pauses and silences to create tension and ambiguity. The pause often reveals what is not said, and it is a key element of Pinter's 'comedy of menace'. PYQ note: frequently asked about the Pinter pause; Pinter's use of silence; compared with other playwrights.

concepts.name: The Postmodern Novel
concepts.description: A literary genre characterized by self-reflexivity, irony, and a questioning of traditional narrative forms. John Fowles's The French Lieutenant's Woman (1969), Don DeLillo's White Noise (1985), and Thomas Pynchon's The Crying of Lot 49 (1966) are key examples of the postmodern novel. PYQ note: frequently asked about the postmodern novel; its characteristics; compared with other literary movements.

concepts.name: The Revenge Tragedy
concepts.description: A genre of drama that originated with Thomas Kyd's The Spanish Tragedy (1592)[reference:147][reference:148]. The genre is characterized by a protagonist who seeks revenge for a wrong, a ghost who demands vengeance, a play-within-a-play, feigned madness, and a high body count[reference:149]. The genre was enormously influential and shaped later revenge tragedies including Shakespeare's Hamlet and the works of John Webster[reference:150][reference:151]. PYQ note: frequently asked as a genre; Kyd's The Spanish Tragedy as the first revenge tragedy; compared with Hamlet and The Duchess of Malfi.

concepts.name: The Roman à Clef
concepts.description: A novel that uses fictional characters to represent real people and events. Joyce Carol Oates's Black Water (1992) is a roman à clef based on the Chappaquiddick incident. PYQ note: asked about the roman à clef; its characteristics; compared with historical fiction.

concepts.name: The Sensation Novel
concepts.description: A genre of fiction that flourished in the 1860s, characterized by sensational plots involving crime, mystery, and scandal. Wilkie Collins is the master of the sensation novel, with works such as The Woman in White (1860) and The Moonstone (1868).[reference:74] The genre was popular with Victorian readers and influenced later detective fiction. PYQ note: frequently asked about the sensation novel; Collins's contributions; compared with other Victorian genres.

concepts.name: The Short Story
concepts.description: A literary genre that focuses on a single incident or character, often with a compressed narrative structure. Katherine Mansfield and Alice Munro are two of the greatest masters of the short story form. PYQ note: frequently asked about the short story; its characteristics; and key practitioners.

concepts.name: The Social Novel
concepts.description: A genre of fiction that addresses social issues and critiques social institutions. The social novel flourished in the Victorian era, addressing issues such as poverty, industrialization, class conflict, and the condition of women. Examples include Charles Dickens's Oliver Twist and Hard Times, Elizabeth Gaskell's Mary Barton and North and South, and George Eliot's Middlemarch. PYQ note: frequently asked about the social novel; the Victorian social novel; compared with other novel forms.

concepts.name: The Subaltern
concepts.description: Gayatri Spivak's concept of the oppressed and marginalized subject whose voice is always mediated by colonial and patriarchal power structures. Spivak argues that the subaltern cannot speak because their voice is always filtered through dominant discourses. PYQ note: one of the most frequently asked concepts in NET; the subaltern; the problem of representation; the Sati example.

concepts.name: The Sublime and the Beautiful
concepts.description: Edmund Burke's A Philosophical Enquiry into the Origin of Our Ideas of the Sublime and Beautiful (1757) is a key text in aesthetics.[reference:68] Burke defines the sublime as that which inspires awe, terror, and astonishment, while the beautiful is that which inspires love and pleasure. The work anticipates the Romantic movement's interest in the sublime and influenced later writers such as Kant and Wordsworth. PYQ note: frequently asked about Burke's Enquiry; the sublime and the beautiful; the influence on Romanticism.

concepts.name: The Theatre of the Absurd
concepts.description: A theatrical movement that emerged in the 1950s, characterized by a sense of meaninglessness, a rejection of conventional plot and character, and a focus on the absurdity of the human condition. Samuel Beckett's Waiting for Godot (1953) is the defining work of the Theatre of the Absurd. PYQ note: frequently asked about the Theatre of the Absurd; Beckett's role; compared with other theatrical movements.

concepts.name: The Three Circles Model
concepts.description: Braj Kachru's model of world Englishes, which divides the world into three circles: the inner circle (countries where English is the native language), the outer circle (countries where English is used as a second language), and the expanding circle (countries where English is used as a foreign language). PYQ note: frequently asked about the three circles; Kachru's contribution to linguistics; compared with other linguistic models.

concepts.name: The Tribe of Ben
concepts.description: A group of poets and playwrights who were influenced by and admired Ben Jonson. They met at the Mermaid Tavern in London and followed Jonson's classical principles of poetry, emphasizing order, discipline, and craftsmanship. The group included Robert Herrick, Thomas Carew, and Richard Lovelace. PYQ note: asked about the influence of Ben Jonson on the Cavalier poets.

concepts.name: The Unreliable Narrator
concepts.description: A narrator whose credibility is compromised or questionable. Kazuo Ishiguro's The Remains of the Day (1989) features a classic unreliable narrator in Stevens, the butler. Vladimir Nabokov's Lolita (1955) also features the unreliable narrator Humbert Humbert. PYQ note: frequently asked about the unreliable narrator; Ishiguro's use of the technique; compared with other narrators.

concepts.name: Eliot's Verse Drama
concepts.description: T.S. Eliot sought to revive poetic drama for the modern stage. He wrote his plays in a flexible blank verse, in which the metrical effect is not apprehended apart from the sense.[reference:53] His most successful verse drama is Murder in the Cathedral (1935), which uses a Greek-style chorus. PYQ note: frequently asked about Eliot's verse drama; the use of blank verse; the chorus; compared with other poetic dramatists.

concepts.name: The Victorian Sage
concepts.description: A type of writer who seeks to cause widespread cultural and social change through polemical prose. John Ruskin is a key example of the Victorian Sage, with works such as Modern Painters and The Stones of Venice. PYQ note: frequently asked about the Victorian Sage; Ruskin's role; compared with other sage writers.

concepts.name: The Villanelle
concepts.description: A nineteen-line poem consisting of five tercets followed by a quatrain, with a specific rhyme scheme and two repeating refrains. Dylan Thomas's 'Do not go gentle into that good night' is one of the most famous villanelles in English[reference:124]. PYQ note: frequently asked about the villanelle form; Thomas's use of the form; compared with other poetic forms.

concepts.name: The War Poets
concepts.description: A group of poets who wrote about their experiences of the First World War. The war poets can be divided into two phases: the idealistic early poets (like Rupert Brooke) and the disillusioned later poets (like Wilfred Owen and Siegfried Sassoon). Brooke's 'The Soldier' reflects the early idealism, while Owen's 'Dulce et Decorum Est' exposes the horrors of war[reference:120][reference:121]. PYQ note: frequently asked about the war poets; the contrast between idealism and disillusionment; compared with other war literature.

concepts.name: The Well-Made Play
concepts.description: A dramatic structure popularized by French playwrights like Eugène Scribe, characterized by a tightly constructed plot with exposition, rising action, climax, and denouement. Oscar Wilde used the well-made play structure in his comedies, but subverted it with his wit and social satire. PYQ note: asked about the well-made play; Wilde's use of the form; compared with other dramatic structures.

concepts.name: Third Theatre
concepts.description: A theatrical movement developed by Badal Sircar, which breaks away from the conventional proscenium stage and engages directly with the audience. The movement seeks to make theatre more accessible and interactive. PYQ note: frequently asked about the Third Theatre movement; its innovations; and its influence on Indian theatre.

concepts.name: Topographical Poetry
concepts.description: A genre of poetry that describes a particular landscape or region, often with historical, legendary, or political commentary. Michael Drayton's Poly-Olbion is the most ambitious example in English, a poetic survey of England's geography, history, and legends. The genre anticipates later works like John Denham's Cooper's Hill. PYQ note: asked about Poly-Olbion as a topographical poem; the genre's development.

concepts.name: Arnold's Touchstone Method
concepts.description: Matthew Arnold's method of literary criticism, as outlined in The Study of Poetry. The method involves comparing passages of poetry with the best passages of the great poets (the touchstones) to judge their quality. Arnold believed that this method would help readers develop a sense of literary excellence. PYQ note: frequently asked about Arnold's touchstone method; the function of criticism; compared with other critical methods.

concepts.name: Transcendentalism
concepts.description: A philosophical and literary movement that emerged in New England in the 1830s and 1840s. Transcendentalists believed in the inherent goodness of people and nature, and they emphasized self-reliance, intuition, and the importance of individual conscience. Key figures include Ralph Waldo Emerson, Henry David Thoreau, and Walt Whitman. PYQ note: frequently asked about Transcendentalism; its key figures; compared with other philosophical movements.

concepts.name: Victorian Crisis of Faith
concepts.description: A period of intense religious doubt and questioning in Victorian England, brought about by scientific discoveries (Darwin's Origin of Species, 1859), biblical criticism, and social changes. The crisis is reflected in the poetry of Matthew Arnold (Dover Beach), Arthur Hugh Clough (The Bothie), and Alfred Tennyson (In Memoriam). PYQ note: frequently asked about the Victorian crisis of faith; the response of Victorian poets; compared with other periods of religious doubt.

concepts.name: Wildean Epigram
concepts.description: A characteristic feature of Oscar Wilde's plays, the epigram is a witty, paradoxical, and often cynical statement that subverts conventional wisdom. Wilde's epigrams define his plays: 'The truth is rarely pure and never simple' (The Importance of Being Earnest) and 'We are all in the gutter, but some of us are looking at the stars' (Lady Windermere's Fan). PYQ note: frequently asked about Wilde's epigrams; the comedy of manners; the wit.

concepts.name: Wollstonecraft's Feminist Arguments
concepts.description: Mary Wollstonecraft's pioneering feminist arguments in A Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1792). Wollstonecraft argues for the education and rights of women, criticizing the educational system that trained women to be frivolous and dependent. She also critiques marriage as 'legal prostitution' and advocates for women's independence. Her work is one of the earliest and most influential works of feminism. PYQ note: frequently asked about Wollstonecraft's feminist arguments; compared with other feminist writers.

concepts.name: Wordsworth's 'Spontaneous Overflow of Powerful Feelings'
concepts.description: Wordsworth's famous definition of poetry from the Preface to Lyrical Ballads (1800): 'Poetry is the spontaneous overflow of powerful feelings: it takes its origin from emotion recollected in tranquillity.' This definition emphasizes the emotional and experiential basis of poetry, and the role of memory and reflection in the creative process. Wordsworth also advocated for the use of 'language really used by men' in poetry. PYQ note: frequently asked about Wordsworth's theory of poetry; the definition of poetry; the concept of 'emotion recollected in tranquillity'.

concepts.name: Yeats's Theory of History
concepts.description: W. B. Yeats developed a cyclical theory of history, based on the concept of the 'gyre' (a spiral). He believed that history moves in 2,000-year cycles, with each cycle representing the rise and fall of a civilization. This theory is reflected in his poem 'The Second Coming', which envisions the end of the Christian era and the rise of a new, violent age[reference:122][reference:123]. PYQ note: frequently asked about Yeats's theory of history; the gyres; the apocalyptic vision.

concepts.name: Yellow Journalism
concepts.description: A style of journalism that emphasizes sensationalism, exaggeration, and scandalous reporting to attract readers. It was associated with Joseph Pulitzer and William Randolph Hearst in the late 19th century. PYQ note: frequently asked about yellow journalism; Pulitzer and Hearst's role; compared with other journalistic styles.

history.name: Black Death
history.description:
ended in 1349 but revived in 1361, 1362, 1369. It finally ended in the 17th century when black rats were driven out by brown rats and improvement in medicine.
# Impact
increase in demand of labor due to high mortality thing high wages
from feudal system to pay for services

history.name: Hundred Year War
history.description:
Ended in 1453 with the French victory at the Battle of Castillon.
# Impact
Emerged two nation states- France and England
Rise of living conditions of English people due to pillage loot and high pay to soldiers
changed the old code of chivalry to local militia and modern infantry
Increase in laborer's pay amid Black death

history.name: Norman Conquest
history.description:
Normans (Vikings settled in France) under William attacked England. Fought battle of Hastings and shot Richard II in the eye. William crowned on Christmas of 1066.
# Impact
English Nobility replaced by French Nobility
French became the language of courts
Introduction of French literary tradition including Romances
Normans bought their Viking ancestral culture of Heroism and Pagan Gods

history.name: Peasant's Revolt
history.description:
'heroit' or mortuary tax, poll tat to ease the financial burden of Hundred Year War. Rebellion under Wat Tyler, attacked London, killed Archbishop of Canterbury. Suppressed by King Richard II.
# Imapcts
Rise of Guilds, Ex. Wool Industry
Increase in labor wages

history.name: Thirty Year War
history.description:
Ended in 1648 under the peace treaty of Westphalia. Europe was decided in two hostile religious groups- France and Poland were catholic while Britain, Holland and Scandinavia were protestants.
# Impact
Faith of people were shrunken by religious war.
